topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Pelton Wheel (Load test and best speed)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Pelton Wheel (Load test and best speed)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Pelton Wheel** is a **tangential-flow impulse turbine** in which one or more free jets of water impinge on a series of bucket-shaped blades mounted on the periphery of a rotating disc. Energy transfer occurs entirely through the **change in momentum (impulse)** of the high-velocity jet, with **no pressure change** across the runner. It is the preferred prime-mover for **high-head (above ~300 m)** and **low-to-medium discharge** hydroelectric installations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Pelton wheel is classified as an *impulse turbine* because the working fluid (water) is at atmospheric pressure throughout its passage over the runner buckets. All the available head is first converted into *kinetic energy* by a nozzle before striking the buckets.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Splash Bucket" Intuition

Imagine holding a large spoon under a kitchen-tap jet striking it sideways so the water deflects backward. The spoon is pushed forward — that push is **impulse force**. A Pelton wheel is essentially this idea engineered precisely:

- The **tap** → the **nozzle** (accelerates water to a high-velocity jet).
- The **spoon** → the **double-cupped bucket** (splits and reverses the jet through ~165°–170°).
- Your **wrist** → the **runner disc** (carries buckets in a circle).
- The water's *change in direction* is what *pushes* the wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the jet must possess high kinetic energy to give a meaningful push, a Pelton wheel **must operate under a high head**. For the same power, low-head sites use **Kaplan/Francis** turbines instead.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity coefficient (nozzle) | $C_v$ | **0.97 – 0.99** |
| Coefficient of friction (bucket) | $k$ | **0.85 – 0.98** (often taken ≈ 0.9) |
| Bucket deflection angle (exit) | $\beta$ | **10° – 15°** (so turning angle = 180° − β) |
| Best speed ratio | $K_u = u/V_1$ | **≈ 0.45 – 0.48** |
| Maximum hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_{h,max}$ | theoretical ≈ **100 %** (at $u = V_1/2$) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why "best speed"?** For any given head and discharge, there is exactly **one runner speed** at which hydraulic efficiency is maximum. This is the **best speed** for that operating head, and the laboratory test is designed to find it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Head Conversion:** Water from the penstock enters the spear nozzle. Pressure head $H$ is converted to kinetic head by the nozzle, producing a jet of velocity

$$V_1 = C_v\,\sqrt{2gH}$$

2. **Jet Strikes Bucket:** The free jet, moving at $V_1$, impinges tangentially on a bucket moving with peripheral (tangential) velocity

$$u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

where $D$ is the mean bucket diameter (jet circle diameter) and $N$ is the runner speed in rpm.

3. **Momentum Change → Force:** The bucket deflects the jet through angle $(180° - \beta)$ so the relative-velocity vector exits at angle $\beta$ to the tangent, but **opposite to the direction of bucket motion**. This reversal of momentum produces the tangential thrust.

4. **Energy Transfer:** The tangential thrust acting through the bucket's peripheral motion does useful **work** on the shaft.

5. **Output Measurement:** The shaft drives a **rope-brake / Prony brake / hydraulic dynamometer**; load is varied and speed is recorded to plot the characteristic curves.

### 2.2 Velocity Triangle Logic (Why the math works)

- **Whirl velocity at entry:** $V_{w1} = V_1$ (the jet has *only* tangential component relative to a stationary observer).
- **Relative velocity at entry:** $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ (vector subtraction along the tangent).
- **Relative velocity at exit:** $V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1}$ (the bucket surface friction reduces the relative speed).
- **Whirl velocity at exit:** $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta = u - k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **sign convention**: $V_{w2}$ is measured positive in the *original direction of $V_1$*. Because the jet is reversed, the algebraic term carries a minus sign.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s |
| 2 | Bucket peripheral speed | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Relative velocity at entry | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ | m/s |
| 4 | Relative velocity at exit | $V_{r2} = k\,V_{r1}$ | m/s |
| 5 | Whirl velocity at exit | $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta$ | m/s |
| 6 | Force on bucket | $F_B = \rho Q\,(V_{w1} - V_{w2}) = \rho Q\,(V_1 - V_{w2})$ | N |
| 7 | Work done per second (shaft power, ideal) | $P_{ideal} = F_B \cdot u = \rho Q u (V_1 - V_{w2})$ | W |
| 8 | Input power | $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$ | W |
| 9 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^{2}}$ | — |
| 10 | Best speed condition (for $\eta_{h,max}$) | $u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}$ → $K_u = 0.5$ | — |
| 11 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (SI) |
| 12 | Unit speed | $N_{11} = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm/m^(1/2) |
| 13 | Unit discharge | $Q_{11} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s per √m |
| 14 | Unit power | $P_{11} = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | kW per m^(3/2) |
| 15 | Brake power (rope brake) | $BP = \dfrac{(S_1 - S_2)\,g \cdot R \cdot 2\pi N}{60}$ | W |
| 16 | Net head (with losses) | $H = H_{gross} - h_f$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the laboratory, **net head $H$** is measured using a **piezometer + pressure gauge at the nozzle inlet + a venturi at the supply line** — not assumed equal to the tank-overrun-jet level difference. Always use $H = \dfrac{p_{inlet}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_{inlet}^{2}}{2g} - h_{f,pipe}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Micro-hydro and high-head power plants** in Himalayan / Western-Ghat catchments (KTU-Kerala context: Idukki, Idamalayar).
- **Marine auxiliary applications** — high-head Pelton wheels historically powered **hydraulic deck machinery** on warships (e.g., gun turrets, anchor windlasses) where reliable impulse turbines outperformed reciprocating pumps.
- **Pump-as-turbine (PAT)** test rigs in academic laboratories use the Pelton wheel as a calibration benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Force Exerted by Jet on a Single Pelton Bucket

Consider a jet of velocity $V_1$ striking a bucket moving at $u$ in the same direction. Let mass flow rate be $\dot{m} = \rho Q$.

**Step 1:** Initial momentum flux (tangential direction)

$$\dot{m}\,V_{w1} = \rho Q V_1$$

**Step 2:** Final momentum flux

$$\dot{m}\,V_{w2} = \rho Q \left[u - k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta\right]$$

**Step 3:** Rate of change of momentum (Newton's second law in tangential direction) equals the force exerted *on the water*. The force exerted *on the bucket* is equal and opposite in the direction of motion:

$$F_B = \rho Q\,(V_{w1} - V_{w2})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B &= \rho Q \left[V_1 - u + k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta\right] \\
F_B &= \rho Q (V_1 - u)\left[1 + k\cos\beta\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Work done per second on the bucket = Force × velocity (the bucket moves at $u$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ideal} &= F_B \cdot u \\
&= \rho Q\,u\,(V_1 - u)\,(1 + k\cos\beta)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation — Hydraulic Efficiency and the "Best Speed" Condition

**Step 1:** Hydraulic efficiency = output (kinetic energy given to runner) ÷ input (jet kinetic energy)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_h &= \frac{P_{ideal}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^{2}} \\
&= \frac{\rho Q\,u\,(V_1 - u)\,(1+k\cos\beta)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^{2}} \\
\eta_h &= \frac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** For a *given* head, $V_1$ is constant. Hence $\eta_h$ is a function of $u$ alone. To find the maximum, set $\dfrac{d\eta_h}{du}=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\eta_h}{du} &= \frac{2(1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^{2}}\,\frac{d}{du}\bigl[u(V_1 - u)\bigr] \\
&= \frac{2(1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^{2}}\,(V_1 - 2u) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve the bracketed term:

$$V_1 - 2u = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad u = \frac{V_1}{2}$$

**Step 4:** The **best-speed ratio**:

$$K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = 0.5 \quad \text{(ideal);}\quad \text{actual}\ \approx 0.45 - 0.48$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $u = V_1/2$ back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{h,\max} &= \frac{2\cdot \dfrac{V_1}{2}\left(V_1 - \dfrac{V_1}{2}\right)(1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^{2}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{2} \cdot (1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^{2}} \\
\eta_{h,\max} &= \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** If $k = 0.9$ and $\beta = 15°$, then $\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 + 0.9\cos 15°}{2} = \dfrac{1 + 0.9 \times 0.9659}{2} = \dfrac{1.8693}{2} \approx 0.9347$ i.e. **93.5 %**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Computation Flow for a Typical KTU Lab Reading

Given a *row* of observations: Head $H$, Discharge $Q$, Rope-balance readings $S_1$ & $S_2$, Brake-drum radius $R$, Tachometer reading $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input power}\ P_{in} &= \rho g Q H \quad &\text{[W]} \\
\text{Load } (T_1 - T_2) &= (S_1 - S_2)\,g \quad &\text{[N]} \\
\text{Force, tangential } F &= (S_1 - S_2)\,g \quad &\text{[N]} \\
\text{Torque } \tau &= F \cdot R \quad &\text{[N·m]} \\
\text{Brake power } BP &= \dfrac{2\pi N \tau}{60} \quad &\text{[W]} \\
\text{Overall efficiency}\ \eta_{ov} &= \dfrac{BP}{P_{in}} \quad &\text{[-]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** The *rope-balance spring readings* $S_1$ and $S_2$ are in **kgf** (not Newtons). Convert via $\times 9.81$ to obtain force in N, or — more commonly — compute BP in kgf·m/s and then convert to kW via $\times 9.81 / 1000$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pelton Wheel Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Tank] --> B[Supply Pipe with Valve]
    B --> C[Pressure Gauge at Nozzle Inlet]
    C --> D[Spear Nozzle: Cv and Q control]
    D --> E[High-Velocity Jet V1]
    E --> F[Runner Buckets on Disc]
    F --> G[Shaft to Brake Drum]
    G --> H[Rope with Spring Balances S1 and S2]
    F --> I[Tachometer for N rpm]
    F --> J[Voltmeter-Alternator Load Bank]

    subgraph Measurements
        H
        I
        J
        C
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Procedure (Load Test)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Prime system, set constant head H] --> S2[Step 2: Adjust spear to give rated Q]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Open bypass to load to zero and record no-load N0]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Increase load step by step via brake rope tension]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Record S1, S2, N for each load step]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute BP, Pinput, and efficiency]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Plot efficiency vs speed and BP vs speed]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Identify best speed Nbest and max efficiency]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Performance-Curve Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Shaft Speed N rpm] --> Y1[Output: Brake Power BP]
    X --> Y2[Output: Efficiency eta overall]
    X --> Y3[Output: Discharge Q]
    X --> Y4[Output: Head H at constant supply]

    Y1 --> Z1[Characteristic Curve: BP vs N]
    Y2 --> Z2[Characteristic Curve: eta vs N]
    Y3 --> Z3[Characteristic Curve: Q vs N]
    Y4 --> Z4[Constant Head line]

    Z1 --> R[Best Operating Point: d eta/dN = 0]
    Z2 --> R
    R --> P[Point of Maximum Efficiency eta max]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Velocity Triangle at Bucket Exit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Tangential direction, original V1"] --> B["Subtract u along the same direction"]
    B --> C["Relative velocity at entry Vr1 = V1 minus u"]
    C --> D["Friction reduces magnitude to Vr2 = k Vr1"]
    D --> E["Deflect through angle beta at exit"]
    E --> F["Whirl component at exit Vw2 opposes V1"]
    F --> G["Net momentum change yields tangential thrust on bucket"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram instead of a true CAD drawing?** A precise blade-shape CAD of a Pelton bucket cannot be drawn in Mermaid. The block-flow above conveys the *cause → effect* chain — which is what the KTU lab viva expects — using the velocity-triangle variables as plain alphanumeric labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the condition for maximum hydraulic efficiency of a Pelton wheel and derive the corresponding best-speed ratio.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For maximum hydraulic efficiency, $\dfrac{d\eta_h}{du}=0$, where $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$.

Differentiating with respect to $u$:

$$\frac{d\eta_h}{du} = \frac{2(1+k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}(V_1 - 2u) = 0$$

Hence $u = V_1/2$, giving the **best-speed ratio**:

$$K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = 0.5 \quad \text{(ideal);}\ 0.45 \text{–} 0.48 \text{ (actual)}$$

At this condition, $\eta_{h,\max} = (1 + k\cos\beta)/2$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is a Pelton wheel classified as an "impulse" turbine and not a "reaction" turbine?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a Pelton wheel, the **entire available head is first converted into kinetic energy** by the nozzle, producing a **free jet at atmospheric pressure**. Across the runner buckets, the fluid pressure remains constant at atmospheric and **no pressure drop occurs** while passing over the blades. Energy transfer is due purely to the **change in momentum (impulse)** of the jet.

In contrast, a reaction turbine (Francis, Kaplan) operates with the runner **fully submerged** and energy transfer involves both **pressure and kinetic-energy changes**. Hence a Pelton wheel is an *impulse* turbine. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Always convert spring-balance readings from **kgf to N** before computing torque.
> 2. The brake-drum **effective radius** $R$ must be taken to the **centre of the rope**, not the drum's outer radius.
> 3. Many students forget that rope tension differences alone give the *net tangential force*. $S_1$ alone is **not** the load.
> 4. Units of discharge — use **m³/s**, not litres per second, when computing input power in Watts.

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a velocity-triangle sketch, derive the expression for the force exerted by a jet on a single Pelton bucket, and hence obtain an expression for the work done per second. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pelton wheel has a mean bucket diameter of **1.20 m** and is supplied with water under a net head of **400 m** at a rate of **0.15 m³/s**. The nozzle velocity coefficient is **0.97**, the bucket-friction coefficient is **0.92**, and the bucket exit angle is **15°**. Calculate (i) the best speed of the wheel in rpm, and (ii) the maximum hydraulic efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the jet velocity be $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ and the bucket speed be $u = \pi DN/60$.

Step 1 — Relative velocity at entry: $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$

Step 2 — Relative velocity at exit (after friction and deflection through $180° - \beta$):

$$V_{r2} = k\,V_{r1}$$

Step 3 — Whirl velocity at exit: $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta = u - k(V_1-u)\cos\beta$

Step 4 — Force on bucket (rate of change of tangential momentum):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_B &= \rho Q (V_{w1} - V_{w2}) \\
&= \rho Q\bigl[V_1 - u + k(V_1-u)\cos\beta\bigr] \\
F_B &= \rho Q (V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Work done per second:

$$P = F_B \cdot u = \rho Q\,u\,(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)$$

**(7 marks — full marks for clear derivation with all 5 steps labelled.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Jet velocity:

$$V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 400}$$

$$V_1 = 0.97 \times \sqrt{7848} = 0.97 \times 88.59 = 85.93 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2 — Best speed (linear):

$$u_{best} = \frac{V_1}{2} = \frac{85.93}{2} = 42.97 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 3 — Convert to rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{best} &= \frac{60 \cdot u_{best}}{\pi D} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 42.97}{\pi \times 1.20} \\
&= \frac{2578.2}{3.7699} \\
N_{best} &\approx 683.9 \text{ rpm} \;\;(\approx 684 \text{ rpm})
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Maximum hydraulic efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{h,\max} &= \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2} \\
&= \frac{1 + 0.92 \times \cos 15°}{2} \\
&= \frac{1 + 0.92 \times 0.9659}{2} \\
&= \frac{1 + 0.8886}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.8886}{2} \\
\eta_{h,\max} &\approx 0.9443 \;\text{or}\; 94.43 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $N_{best} \approx 684 \text{ rpm}$; $\eta_{h,\max} \approx 94.4\%$. **(7 marks — 2 for jet velocity, 2 for best speed, 3 for efficiency.)**

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Describe, with a labelled sketch, the construction and working of a Pelton wheel test rig used in the KTU laboratory for conducting a load test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a load test on a Pelton wheel, the following observations were recorded at a constant net head of **60 m** and supply discharge of **0.025 m³/s**:

| Trial | Spring Balance $S_1$ (kgf) | Spring Balance $S_2$ (kgf) | Speed $N$ (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8.0 | 0.5 | 700 |
| 2 | 9.5 | 1.2 | 670 |
| 3 | 11.0 | 2.5 | 620 |
| 4 | 12.5 | 4.0 | 560 |
| 5 | 13.5 | 5.5 | 490 |

The effective brake-drum radius is **0.20 m**. Determine for **each trial**: (i) the brake power in kW, and (ii) the overall efficiency. Identify the trial giving **maximum efficiency** and quote the corresponding speed as the *best speed* of the wheel for this head. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Description (7 marks):**

The KTU Pelton-wheel test rig consists of the following components:

1. **Overhead tank** providing a constant head.
2. **Supply pipe** with a control valve and a **pressure gauge** at the nozzle inlet to measure net head $H$.
3. **Spear nozzle** to control jet velocity $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$ and discharge $Q$.
4. **Pelton runner** with double-cup buckets mounted on a horizontal shaft.
5. **Brake drum** rigidly fixed to the shaft, around which a **rope** is wound; the rope carries a **tension spring balance** $S_1$ on the tight side and a **compression / load cell / second spring balance** $S_2$ on the slack side.
6. **Tachometer** (digital / mechanical) to record speed $N$ in rpm.
7. **Supply tank with measuring notch / venturimeter** for discharge measurement.
8. **Bypass / drain valve** to unload the wheel at will.

Working: As load is increased via the rope tension, the speed drops. The friction torque at the drum equals $(S_1 - S_2)\,g\,R$. Speed is read on the tachometer, and discharge is held constant by adjusting the spear.

**(7 marks — 2 for sketch description, 3 for component list, 2 for working.)**

---

**Part (b) — Tabulated Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Convert each row.

Load (kgf) $W_i = S_{1,i} - S_{2,i}$; Net force $F_i = W_i \times 9.81$ N; Torque $\tau_i = F_i \times 0.20$ N·m; Brake power $BP_i = 2\pi N_i \tau_i / 60$ (W); then convert to kW.

Input power: $P_{in} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 60 = 14\,715$ W = **14.715 kW** (constant for all trials).

| Trial | $W$ (kgf) | $F$ (N) | $\tau$ (N·m) | $BP$ (W) | $BP$ (kW) | $\eta_{ov} = BP/P_{in}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7.5 | 73.58 | 14.72 | 1079.4 | 1.079 | 0.0733 = 7.33% |
| 2 | 8.3 | 81.42 | 16.28 | 1141.7 | 1.142 | 0.0776 = 7.76% |
| 3 | 8.5 | 83.39 | 16.68 | 1081.9 | 1.082 | 0.0735 = 7.35% |
| 4 | 8.5 | 83.39 | 16.68 | 977.7 | 0.978 | 0.0664 = 6.64% |
| 5 | 8.0 | 78.48 | 15.70 | 805.0 | 0.805 | 0.0547 = 5.47% |

> **Sample calculation (Trial 2):**
>
> $$F = 8.3 \times 9.81 = 81.42 \text{ N}$$
>
> $$\tau = 81.42 \times 0.20 = 16.28 \text{ N·m}$$
>
> $$BP = \frac{2\pi \times 670 \times 16.28}{60} = 1141.7 \text{ W} = 1.142 \text{ kW}$$
>
> $$\eta_{ov} = \frac{1.142}{14.715} = 0.0776 = 7.76\%$$

Step 2 — **Maximum efficiency** occurs in **Trial 2** at $\eta_{ov} \approx 7.76\%$.

Step 3 — **Best speed for $H = 60$ m:** $N_{best} \approx 670 \text{ rpm}$.

> **Examiner's Note:** The absolute efficiency values look low because the test rig operates at a *fractional head* compared with the design head (60 m vs. hundreds of metres). What matters is the **shape** of the $\eta$–$N$ curve, not the magnitude. **(7 marks — 2 for $P_{in}$, 3 for tabular BP, 2 for identification of best speed.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Impulse turbine** → energy transfer purely by **momentum change**; pressure across buckets = atmospheric.
- ✅ **Jet velocity** $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH}$; **bucket speed** $u = \pi DN/60$.
- ✅ **Relative velocity at entry** $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$; **at exit** $V_{r2} = k V_{r1}$ where $k$ is bucket-friction coefficient.
- ✅ **Whirl velocity at exit** $V_{w2} = u - k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$ — sign is **opposite** to original jet direction.
- ✅ **Force on bucket** $F_B = \rho Q (V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)$.
- ✅ **Work done per second** $P = \rho Q\,u\,(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)$.
- ✅ **Best speed condition** = $u = V_1/2$ → **best speed ratio** $K_u = 0.5$ (ideal), $0.45$–$0.48$ (actual).
- ✅ **Maximum hydraulic efficiency** $\eta_{h,\max} = (1 + k\cos\beta)/2$.
- ✅ **Rope-brake load test:** $BP = 2\pi N (S_1 - S_2) g R / 60$; remember **kgf → N** conversion.
- ✅ **Input power** $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$; **overall efficiency** $\eta_{ov} = BP / P_{in}$.
- ✅ **Specific speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ is **low** for Pelton (impulse) compared to Francis / Kaplan.
- ✅ **Performance curves** to plot: (i) $N$ vs $Q$, (ii) $N$ vs $BP$, (iii) $N$ vs $\eta_{ov}$; best speed = point of max $\eta_{ov}$ on the constant-head curve.
- ✅ **Lab safety:** ensure rope is **properly lapped** on the drum, water hammer is avoided by slow valve closure, and the brake-drum cooling water (if provided) is flowing before load is applied.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** confusing $C_v$ (nozzle velocity coefficient) with the bucket-friction coefficient $k$ — they are **different** parameters and must be applied at **different stages** of the calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Francis Turbine (Load test and best gate opening)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Francis Turbine (Load test and best gate opening)

# Performance Characteristic Tests on Francis Turbine

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Francis Turbine** is a reaction-type, mixed-flow hydraulic turbine in which water enters the runner radially and leaves axially. The **Performance Characteristic Test** is a standardized experimental procedure conducted on a Francis turbine test rig to determine the relationship between the **efficiency (η)** and the **load (brake power output)** or **gate opening**, at a **constant rated head (H)** and **constant synchronous/operating speed (N)**.

The **Load Test** (also called the **Brake Load Test** or **Variable Load Test**) involves progressively applying a mechanical braking force to the turbine shaft using a rope-brake / band-brake dynamometer while recording:
- Head (H) — measured by a piezometer / U-tube manometer
- Discharge (Q) — measured by a venturi / orifice meter
- Speed (N) — measured by a tachometer
- Spring balance readings (S₁, S₂) and dead weight (W) on the brake drum

The **Best Gate Opening Test** involves holding the head and speed constant, but varying the **guide vane / spear valve opening** to determine the **gate opening at which peak efficiency occurs** for that operating head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For a KTU Board examination in PCNSL507, you are expected to: (i) Tabulate observed and calculated readings, (ii) Plot **η vs Load (kW)** and **η vs Gate Opening (%)**, (iii) Compute the **Overall Efficiency η₀**, **Hydraulic Efficiency ηₕ**, **Mechanical Efficiency ηₘ**, **Volumetric Efficiency ηᵥ**, and **Specific Speed Nₛ**, and (iv) Identify the **best gate opening** from the characteristic curve.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bicycle dynamo (the kind that lights your cycle lamp). When you pedal slowly, the bulb glows dimly. When you pedal fast, it glows brightly. The **conversion efficiency** of the dynamo (mechanical energy → electrical energy) is **maximum at one specific speed** — neither too slow nor too fast.

A Francis turbine behaves identically. If you let in **too little water** (small gate opening), the water rushes through without doing much work — like pedalling in too high a gear at low cadence. If you let in **too much water**, the runner gets flooded, the water slips past the blades without transferring energy, and the turbine struggles — like pedalling in too low a gear at high cadence. There is **one "sweet spot" gate opening** where the water interacts with the blades most efficiently, and the **overall efficiency is maximum**.

The **load test** answers: *"For my fixed water supply, how much electrical power can I draw without wasting water?"* The **best gate opening test** answers: *"At my fixed water head, how wide should I open the inlet valve to get the most electricity per litre of water?"*

**Key physical constants used:**
- Density of water **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Acceleration due to gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> **NEP 2020 / OBE Mapping:** This experiment directly satisfies **CO2** (*"Apply performance analysis of reaction turbines for marine and hydro applications"*) and **PSO1** (*"Hydrodynamic analysis of marine propulsion systems"*). KTU expects students to draw smooth characteristic curves and identify operating zones.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a Francis Turbine

A Francis turbine is a **reaction turbine** — meaning the water is under **pressure** as it passes through the runner (unlike an impulse turbine like a Pelton wheel where water is at atmospheric pressure). The energy conversion happens in **two stages**:

1. **Pressure → Kinetic Energy** at the spiral casing and guide vane assembly
2. **Kinetic Energy → Mechanical (Rotational) Energy** at the runner (mixed-flow radial-axial)

The guide vanes (wicket gates) control the **discharge Q** entering the runner by changing the flow area. As the gate opening changes:
- **Discharge Q** changes (more opening → more Q)
- **Inlet velocity V₁** changes
- **Whirl component Vw1** changes
- **Tangential force on buckets** changes
- **Brake power output** changes

### 2.2 Why Do We Need These Tests?

In real engineering (especially in **marine hydrodynamics**, where auxiliary turbo-generators and emergency hydraulic drives use small Francis turbines), designers must guarantee:
- The turbine meets its **rated output** at the **design head**
- The turbine operates at **high efficiency** across a range of loads (not just at one point)
- The turbine is **safe from cavitation** at part-load conditions

These tests generate the **performance characteristic curves** used for:
- Selecting turbines for power stations
- Predicting performance at off-design heads
- Marine applications: shipboard micro-hydro generators, tidal turbine integration

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are stated with **SI units** as required by KTU 2024 valuation. Note carefully that **vertical bars are written as `\vert`** to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | **Water Power (Hydraulic Power Input)** | $P_{water} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H$ | ρ = density, g = gravity, Q = discharge, H = net head | W (Watts) |
| 2 | **Shaft Power / Brake Power (Output)** | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | N = speed, T = torque | W |
| 3 | **Torque via Rope-Brake Dynamometer** | $T = (W - S) \, R_{eff}$ | W = dead weight, S = spring balance, R_eff = brake drum radius | N·m |
| 4 | **Effective Brake Drum Radius** | $R_{eff} = (D + d)/2$ | D = outer diameter of rope, d = diameter of brake drum | m |
| 5 | **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{o} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60 \, \rho \, g \, Q \, H}$ | Ratio of output to input | dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | **Hydraulic Efficiency** | $\eta_{h} = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g H}$ | Vw1, Vw2 = whirl components, u1, u2 = blade velocities | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{runner}} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water} \times \eta_{h} \times \eta_{v}}$ | Accounts for bearing & friction losses | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{Q_{theoretical} - Q_{leakage}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Accounts for clearance leakage | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Specific Speed** | $N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{shaft}}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Identifies turbine type (Francis ≈ 60–400) | (SI) |
| 10 | **Unit Speed** | $N_{u} = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | For constant head similarity | rpm |
| 11 | **Unit Discharge** | $Q_{u} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s |
| 12 | **Unit Power** | $P_{u} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{H^{3/2}}$ | W |
| 13 | **Runaway Speed (Safety Check)** | $N_{r} \approx 1.8 \, N_{synchronous}$ | Critical for Francis turbines | rpm |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydroelectric power plants** (Idukki, Mullaperiyar in Kerala) — performance tests validate vendor guarantees
- **Pumped-storage schemes** — turbines run in reverse as pumps; load tests verify dual-mode efficiency
- **Marine hydrodynamics** — emergency power take-off from ship propeller shaft using miniature Francis turbines
- **Small hydro / pico-hydro** in remote coastal installations — best gate opening determines optimal generator loading
- **Tidal energy converters** — Francis-type bulbs in low-head tidal barrages

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power from Rope-Brake Dynamometer

The brake drum is rigidly mounted on the turbine shaft. A rope (with dead weight **W** on one side and spring balance **S** on the other) wraps around the drum. Friction between rope and drum produces a **net tangential braking force**.

**Step 1: Net Tangential Force on the Drum**

The rope is pulled down by dead weight **W** (Newton) and pulled up by spring balance **S** (Newton, where S < W).

$$
F_{net} = W - S
$$

The unit is **Newton**. The factor of 2 does not appear because there is **one continuous rope** passing over the drum, not a two-strap pulley.

**Step 2: Net Braking Torque**

The force is applied at the **effective radius** $R_{eff} = (D + d)/2$, where **D** is the rope diameter and **d** is the drum outer diameter.

$$
T = F_{net} \times R_{eff} = (W - S) \, R_{eff}
$$

**Step 3: Angular Velocity in rad/s**

If the shaft rotates at **N** revolutions per minute, the angular velocity is:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}
$$

**Step 4: Brake (Shaft) Power**

Power = Torque × Angular velocity:

$$
P_{shaft} = T \times \omega = (W - S) \, R_{eff} \times \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

The **2 in the denominator comes from 60 s/min ÷ 2π** logic — we are simply converting the rate of rotation from per-minute to per-second while simultaneously converting revolutions to radians.

$$
\boxed{P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R_{eff}}{60} \quad \text{(Watts)}}
$$

If the brake drum has a **diameter d** and the rope has **thickness D**, then $R_{eff} = (d + D)/2$ (this is the mean radius at which friction acts). Sometimes an **effective diameter** $D_{eff} = d + D$ is directly given.

### 3.2 Derivation of Water Power (Input)

The water enters the turbine with a **gross head H** and leaves with a residual (tail-race) head. The **net head** is:

$$
H_{net} = H_{gross} - H_{tail-race} - h_{friction}
$$

For most KTU lab rigs, the **piezometer reading at inlet and outlet** directly gives $H_{net}$ in metres of water.

The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ (kg/s). The energy available per kg of water is $g H_{net}$ (J/kg).

$$
P_{water} = \dot{m} \times g \times H_{net} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H_{net}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times Q \times H_{net} = 9810 \, Q \, H_{net} \quad \text{(Watts)}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Overall Efficiency

By definition, **efficiency is output divided by input**:

$$
\eta_{o} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}}
$$

Substituting the derived expressions:

$$
\eta_{o} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R_{eff}}{60 \times \rho \, g \, Q \, H_{net}}
$$

Substituting $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
\eta_{o} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R_{eff}}{60 \times 9810 \, Q \, H_{net}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R_{eff}}{588600 \, Q \, H_{net}}
$$

In percentage:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{o} \, (\%) = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R_{eff}}{60 \times 9810 \, Q \, H_{net}} \times 100}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Specific Speed

The **specific speed** is a dimensionless number that uniquely identifies a turbine's geometry. It is defined as the speed at which a **geometrically similar turbine** would run while producing **1 unit of power under 1 unit of head**.

Starting from the dimensional relationship:
- $N \propto P^{1/2} / H^{5/4}$

Therefore:

$$
N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{shaft}}}{H_{net}^{5/4}}
$$

For **Francis turbines**, the typical range is:

$$
60 \leq N_{s} \leq 400 \quad \text{(in SI units, with P in kW and H in metres)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** If the question gives shaft power in **kW** instead of **Watts**, remember to **multiply Nₛ by √1000 = 31.62** when comparing with the textbook range. Most lab manuals compute Nₛ with power in **kW** and head in **metres**, so stick to that convention.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify the Best Gate Opening

The **best gate opening** is determined **graphically**, not analytically. The procedure is:

| Step | Action | Expected Observation |
|------|--------|----------------------|
| 1 | Hold **H** and **N** constant | Set H via inlet valve, hold N near synchronous by adjusting brake |
| 2 | Vary gate opening in **6–8 steps** from low to high | Q increases monotonically |
| 3 | At each step, record **Q, W, S, N** | Note: W must be chosen so S ≥ 0 (no negative spring reading) |
| 4 | Compute **P_shaft**, **P_water**, **η₀** for each step | η₀ first rises, then falls |
| 5 | Plot **η₀ (%) vs Gate Opening (%)** | A characteristic **inverted-U** curve |
| 6 | The **peak of the curve** gives the **best gate opening** | Read corresponding Q and P_shaft |

**Why is the curve inverted-U shaped?**
- At **low gate opening**: very little Q, so P_shaft is small. Friction and windage losses dominate → low η₀.
- At **optimum gate opening**: the flow angle matches the blade angle, shock losses are minimum → η₀ peaks.
- At **high gate opening**: Q is large, but flow cannot follow blade curvature, **shock losses** rise sharply and **leakage losses** increase → η₀ falls.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Test Rig Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Tank] --> B[Sump Pump]
    B --> C[Inlet Pipe]
    C --> D[Valve for Head Control]
    D --> E[Pressure Gauge at Inlet]
    E --> F[Spiral Casing of Francis Turbine]
    F --> G[Guide Vane Wicket Gate]
    G --> H[Francis Runner]
    H --> I[Draft Tube]
    I --> J[Tail Race Tank]
    J --> K[Volumetric Measuring Tank]
    K --> L[Stop Watch]
    H --> M[Brake Drum on Shaft]
    M --> N[Dead Weight W]
    M --> O[Spring Balance S]
    H --> P[Tachometer]
    P --> Q[Speed Display N rpm]
    subgraph Calculations
        X[Brake Power] --> Y[Eta o percent]
        Z[Water Power] --> Y
    end
```

### 4.2 Performance Characteristic Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Constant H and N] --> A2[Increase Load Step 1]
    A2 --> A3[Increase Load Step 2]
    A3 --> A4[Increase Load Step 3]
    A4 --> A5[Increase Load Step 4]
    A5 --> A6[Increase Load Step 5]
    A6 --> A7[Increase Load Step 6]
    A7 --> B1[Plot Eta o versus Load kW]
    A7 --> B2[Plot Eta o versus Gate Opening percent]
    B1 --> C1[Identify Best Efficiency Point]
    B2 --> C2[Identify Best Gate Opening]
    C1 --> D[Result Eta o max and P rated]
    C2 --> D
```

### 4.3 Energy Flow in a Francis Turbine (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water at Head H] --> B[Pressure Energy at Spiral Casing]
    B --> C[Kinetic Energy at Guide Vanes]
    C --> D[Rotational KE at Runner Buckets]
    D --> E[Shaft Mechanical Power]
    E --> F[Electrical Output at Generator]
    B -.Loss.-> G[Hydraulic Losses]
    D -.Loss.-> H[Friction and Windage]
    E -.Loss.-> I[Mechanical Losses]
    F --> J[Useful Output]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style F fill:#ccffcc
    style G fill:#ffcccc
    style H fill:#ffcccc
    style I fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.4 Component Layout of the Test Rig (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Suction_Side
        S1[Sump Tank] --> S2[Centrifugal Pump]
    end
    S2 --> S3[Delivery Pipe with Control Valve]
    S3 --> S4[Inlet Pressure Gauge]
    S4 --> S5[Spiral Casing with Guide Vanes]
    subgraph Turbine_Assembly
        S5 --> S6[Francis Runner]
        S6 --> S7[Draft Tube]
    end
    S7 --> S8[Tail Race Tank]
    S8 --> S9[Measuring Tank with Stop Watch]
    subgraph Power_Measurement
        S6 --> S10[Brake Drum]
        S10 --> S11[Dead Weight and Spring Balance]
        S6 --> S12[Tachometer]
    end
    S5 --> S13[Inlet Venturi for Q Measurement]
    S13 --> S14[U Tube Manometer]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define specific speed of a turbine. State its significance and typical range for a Francis turbine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Specific speed $N_s$ is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would run if it were to develop unit power (1 kW) under unit head (1 m). Mathematically,
$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$
where N is in rpm, P in kW, and H in metres.
- **Significance (1 mark):** It is used to classify turbines (Pelton, Francis, Kaplan) and select a turbine type for a given head and power requirement. Two geometrically similar turbines have the same Nₛ.
- **Range (0.5 mark):** For Francis turbines, $N_s = 60$ to $400$ (in SI metric units).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is meant by "best gate opening" in a Francis turbine? How is it determined experimentally?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The best gate opening is the opening of the guide vanes (wicket gate) at which the turbine operates with **maximum overall efficiency** for a given constant head and rated speed.
- **Determination (1.5 marks):** With the head H and speed N held constant, the gate opening is varied in 6–8 steps. At each step, the discharge Q, brake load (W−S)R, and speed N are noted. The overall efficiency η₀ is calculated and plotted against gate opening (%). The gate opening corresponding to the **peak of the η₀ curve** is the best gate opening.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Evaluation]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
*(a) Describe with a neat sketch the experimental setup for conducting the load test on a Francis turbine. List the instruments used and the readings to be recorded. (7 marks)*
*(b) The following readings were observed during a load test on a Francis turbine: Net head H = 8 m, Speed N = 750 rpm, Brake drum diameter = 40 cm, Rope diameter = 2 cm, Dead weight W = 25 kg, Spring balance S = 5 kg, Discharge Q = 0.06 m³/s. Calculate the shaft power, water power, and overall efficiency. Take g = 9.81 m/s². (7 marks)*

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The setup consists of:
1. **Sump tank** at floor level (water source).
2. **Centrifugal pump** to deliver water at the required head.
3. **Delivery pipe** with a **control valve** to set the head.
4. **Pressure gauge / piezometer** at the turbine inlet to measure gross head.
5. **Spiral casing** with **guide vane assembly** (the gate).
6. **Francis runner** (the moving part) coupled to a **brake drum**.
7. **Draft tube** to recover residual kinetic energy.
8. **Tail race tank** leading to a **measuring tank** with **stop watch** to compute Q volumetrically.
9. **Rope-brake dynamometer**: dead weight W and spring balance S.
10. **Tachometer** (digital or mechanical) to measure shaft speed N.

Readings recorded: **H, N, Q, W, S, time for a known volume of water, brake drum diameter, rope diameter**.

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks (Step-by-Step):**

**Given:**
- H = 8 m
- N = 750 rpm
- Brake drum diameter d = 40 cm = 0.40 m
- Rope diameter D = 2 cm = 0.02 m
- W = 25 kg, S = 5 kg → net load = 20 kg
- Q = 0.06 m³/s

**Step 1: Effective radius of brake drum**

$$
R_{eff} = \dfrac{d + D}{2} = \dfrac{0.40 + 0.02}{2} = \dfrac{0.42}{2} = 0.21 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Net force on rope**

$$
F = (W - S) \times g = (25 - 5) \times 9.81 = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Braking torque**

$$
T = F \times R_{eff} = 196.2 \times 0.21 = 41.202 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Shaft (Brake) Power**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 750 \times 41.202}{60}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 750 \times 41.202}{60} = \dfrac{194{,}201.6}{60} = 3236.7 \text{ W} \approx 3.237 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5: Water Power**

$$
P_{water} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 \times 8 = 4708.8 \text{ W} \approx 4.709 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6: Overall Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{o} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}} = \dfrac{3236.7}{4708.8} = 0.6874 = 68.74 \%
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{shaft} = 3.24 \text{ kW},\ P_{water} = 4.71 \text{ kW},\ \eta_{o} = 68.74\%$

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
*(a) Explain the procedure for finding the best gate opening of a Francis turbine. What are the typical characteristic curves plotted? (7 marks)*
*(b) During a best gate opening test, the following readings were taken at constant head H = 10 m and speed N = 1000 rpm. The brake drum radius is 15 cm. Calculate the efficiency at each gate opening and determine the best gate opening.*

| Gate Opening (%) | Q (L/s) | W (kg) | S (kg) |
|------------------|---------|--------|--------|
| 20 | 8  | 4  | 1 |
| 40 | 18 | 8  | 2 |
| 60 | 30 | 12 | 3 |
| 80 | 42 | 15 | 6 |
| 100 | 50 | 16 | 9 |

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Procedure for Best Gate Opening Test:**
1. Start the pump and allow water to flow at the rated head H (read from piezometer).
2. Run the turbine at rated speed N using an external motor or by adjusting the brake until synchronous speed is reached.
3. Set the guide vane opening to a small value (say 20%). Apply minimum brake load.
4. Record Q, W, S, N. Compute η₀ using $\eta_o = 2\pi N(W-S)R/60\rho g Q H$.
5. Increase the gate opening in steps of 20% up to 100%. At each step, adjust the brake so that N remains at rated speed.
6. Tabulate all observations. Compute η₀ for each opening.
7. Plot **η₀ (%) vs Gate Opening (%)**. The peak of this curve is the **best gate opening**.
8. Optionally also plot **P_shaft vs η₀** and **P_water vs η₀** for a complete performance map.

**Typical characteristic curves:**
- η₀ vs Gate Opening (%)
- P_shaft vs Gate Opening (%)
- Q vs Gate Opening (%)
- η₀ vs P_shaft (kW) — called the **load characteristic**

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks (Step-by-Step with Tabulation):**

**Given:** H = 10 m, N = 1000 rpm, R_eff = 15 cm = 0.15 m, g = 9.81 m/s².

**Formula to use for each row:**

$$
\eta_o \, (\%) = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) g R_{eff}}{60 \times \rho \, g \, Q \, H} \times 100 = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R_{eff}}{60 \times \rho \, Q \, H} \times 100
$$

Note: the **g cancels out** when (W−S) is taken in kg-force (which already contains g implicitly). To be precise, we convert W−S to Newtons: $(W-S) \times 9.81$ N.

**Step 1: Compute net load (W−S) in kg and convert to N:**

| Gate (%) | W (kg) | S (kg) | W−S (kg) | F (N) = 9.81(W−S) |
|----------|--------|--------|----------|-------------------|
| 20  | 4  | 1 | 3  | 29.43 |
| 40  | 8  | 2 | 6  | 58.86 |
| 60  | 12 | 3 | 9  | 88.29 |
| 80  | 15 | 6 | 9  | 88.29 |
| 100 | 16 | 9 | 7  | 68.67 |

**Step 2: Compute Torque T = F × R_eff (R_eff = 0.15 m):**

| Gate (%) | T (N·m) |
|----------|---------|
| 20  | 4.4145 |
| 40  | 8.8290 |
| 60  | 13.2435 |
| 80  | 13.2435 |
| 100 | 10.3005 |

**Step 3: Compute Shaft Power $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N T / 60$ (N = 1000 rpm):**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} \times T = 104.72 \times T
$$

| Gate (%) | P_shaft (W) |
|----------|-------------|
| 20  | 462.3 |
| 40  | 924.6 |
| 60  | 1386.9 |
| 80  | 1386.9 |
| 100 | 1078.7 |

**Step 4: Compute Water Power $P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 9810 \times Q \times 10$:**

| Gate (%) | Q (m³/s) | P_water (W) |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| 20  | 0.008 | 784.8 |
| 40  | 0.018 | 1765.8 |
| 60  | 0.030 | 2943.0 |
| 80  | 0.042 | 4120.2 |
| 100 | 0.050 | 4905.0 |

**Step 5: Compute Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{water}} \times 100
$$

| Gate (%) | P_shaft (W) | P_water (W) | η₀ (%) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|--------|
| 20  | 462.3  | 784.8  | **58.91** |
| 40  | 924.6  | 1765.8 | **52.36** |
| 60  | 1386.9 | 2943.0 | **47.13** |
| 80  | 1386.9 | 4120.2 | **33.66** |
| 100 | 1078.7 | 4905.0 | **22.00** |

**Interpretation:**
Looking at the trend, η₀ **peaks at 20% gate opening** (58.91%) and **decreases** as opening increases. This means the **best gate opening for this operating condition is 20%**.

(Note: in real rigs, the peak is usually in the **60–80% opening** range; the values here are pedagogical.)

**Final Answer:** Best gate opening ≈ **20%**, with η₀ ≈ **58.91%**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kg-force to Newton:** Many students write $F = W - S$ (in kg) and forget the **g = 9.81** factor. This gives **a 7-mark question zero**. Always state: "Net force in Newtons = (W − S) × 9.81".
> 2. **Wrong effective radius:** Some students use the **outer drum diameter** instead of $(D + d)/2$. The rope contacts the drum at its **outer surface** and has its own thickness, so the **mean radius is required**.
> 3. **Not plotting a smooth curve:** KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** if data points are plotted but the smooth characteristic curve is not drawn or labelled.
> 4. **Mixing up volumetric Q and manometric Q:** If the lab uses a **venturi meter**, Q comes from the manometer reading using $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh/(1-(A_2/A_1)^2)}$. Do **not** use the volumetric value if venturi is given.
> 5. **Negative spring balance reading:** This is a **practical clue** that you are **over-loading the brake** — reduce W and re-take readings. KTU expects you to mention this in viva.
> 6. **No units in final answer:** Always write **kW** for power and **%** for efficiency. Writing "0.6874" instead of "68.74%" is a 0.5-mark deduction in ESE.
> 7. **Confusing load test with constant-gate test:** Load test = vary W, keep gate constant. Best-gate test = vary gate, adjust W to keep N constant. **This is the most-asked viva question** in KTU labs.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Francis turbine** is a **reaction, mixed-flow** turbine operating at **low to medium head** (30 m–300 m typical).
- ✅ **Load test** plots **η₀ vs Load (kW)** at constant H and N.
- ✅ **Best gate opening test** plots **η₀ vs Gate Opening (%)** at constant H and N.
- ✅ **Shaft power formula:** $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N (W-S) R_{eff} / 60$ (Watts).
- ✅ **Water power formula:** $P_{water} = 9810 \, Q \, H$ (Watts) — with Q in m³/s and H in m.
- ✅ **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_o = P_{shaft} / P_{water}$, always written in **%**.
- ✅ **Specific speed range for Francis:** **60 ≤ Nₛ ≤ 400** (SI metric).
- ✅ **Efficiency curve is inverted-U** — peaks at the **best gate opening** for that head.
- ✅ **Efficiency drops at low gate opening** (friction dominates) and at high gate opening (shock losses dominate).
- ✅ **Three efficiency components:** $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m \times \eta_v$.
- ✅ **Best gate opening** is **head-dependent** — at higher heads, the optimum opening shifts.
- ✅ **For KTU 2024 lab record:** Always include (1) aim, (2) apparatus, (3) theory, (4) procedure, (5) tabulation, (6) sample calculation, (7) graphs, (8) result, (9) sources of error, (10) viva Q&A.
- ✅ **Marine hydrodynamics link:** Francis-type **bulb turbines** are studied for **tidal energy** and **ship-mounted micro-hydro** applications — KTU often asks cross-application questions in CO₂ mapping.
- ✅ **Common viva questions:** "Why does efficiency fall at part load?", "What is runaway speed?", "Why is the brake drum cooled?", "How do you measure discharge if the tank is overflowing?"
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite mark-grabber:** Always draw the **characteristic curves** with **labelled axes, units, and gridlines**. A clean graph in the exam record is worth 2–3 bonus marks in practical assessment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Kaplan Turbine (Load test and best vane angle opening)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Kaplan Turbine (Load test and best vane angle opening)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Performance Characteristic Test on a Kaplan Turbine** is a standardized experimental procedure conducted on a reaction-type axial flow turbine to determine its operational behavior under varying load conditions and to identify the **best vane angle opening (gauge opening)** that yields maximum efficiency at a given head and constant speed of operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Kaplan turbine** is a **propeller-type axial flow reaction turbine** with **adjustable guide vanes (wicket gates)** and **adjustable runner blades**. The **load test** involves progressively loading the turbine by applying a mechanical brake load on the coupled generator/rope brake dynamometer, and recording parameters like head (H), discharge (Q), speed (N), and load (W). The **best vane angle opening** is the specific guide vane opening (or runner blade angle) at which the turbine achieves its **maximum overall efficiency** for the given head and speed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Adjustable Propeller Underwater"

Imagine a **ceiling fan running in reverse, completely submerged in flowing water** at the bottom of a tank. The fan blades hit the water, get pushed by it, and rotate a shaft connected to a generator. Now, suppose the water pressure (head) is fixed, but you want to run a heavier load (more electricity demand). 

Just like you would tilt the blades of a real boat **propeller** to match the boat's speed and load, in a **Kaplan turbine**, you can **tilt (or adjust) the runner blades** AND the **guide vanes** to maximize efficiency. The "load test" is essentially like **revving up the engine in different gears** — you apply different loads and observe which gear (vane angle) gives you the **best mileage (highest efficiency)**.

- **Head (H)** = Water pressure "fuel"
- **Discharge (Q)** = Volume of water flowing per second
- **Guide Vane Opening** = The "throttle" or "gear" setting
- **Output Power** = Useful work extracted
- **Overall Efficiency (ηₒ)** = How well we convert water power to shaft power

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Load Test** = Varying the **brake load (W)** at a **constant head (H)** and **constant speed (N)**, and observing Q, N, W, etc.
> - **Constant Head Test** = Keeping **H constant** and varying the load.
> - **Best Vane Angle Opening** = The particular guide vane opening at which **ηₒ is maximum** for the given operating condition.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water | **ρ** | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | **w = ρg** | **9810 N/m³** |
| Rotational speed | **N** | **rpm (revolutions per minute)** |
| Angular velocity | **ω = 2πN/60** | **rad/s** |
| Head | **H** | **metres (m)** |
| Discharge | **Q** | **m³/s** (or L/s converted) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of Kaplan Turbine (Theoretical Foundation)

The Kaplan turbine operates on the **impulse-reaction (fully reaction) principle**, where both **pressure drop** and **velocity change** occur across the runner. The complete energy conversion chain is:

**Pressure Energy (Head) → Kinetic Energy (Velocity) at Guide Vanes → Pressure + Velocity Energy at Runner → Mechanical Energy (Shaft rotation) → Electrical Energy**

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic:

- **Step 1 — Water Entry:** Water from the penstock enters the **spiral casing**, which distributes it uniformly around the runner circumference.
- **Step 2 — Guide Vane Regulation:** Water then passes through the **adjustable guide vanes (wicket gates)**. The **angle of the guide vanes** controls the **swirl component** and the **discharge (Q)** entering the runner.
- **Step 3 — Runner Blade Action:** The water strikes the **adjustable runner blades** at the designed inlet angle, creating a **change in momentum (force)**, which produces torque on the runner hub.
- **Step 4 — Draft Tube Recovery:** Water exits the runner with residual kinetic energy. The **draft tube** converts this residual velocity head into useful **pressure head**, increasing the effective head across the runner.
- **Step 5 — Mechanical Output:** The rotating shaft drives a **rope brake dynamometer** (in the lab) or a **generator** (in actual operation). The brake load (W) applied is measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Point:**
> The **Kaplan turbine is an "axial flow" reaction turbine** with **specific speed Nₛ between 300 to 1000 (metric)**. It is used for **low head (H < 60 m)** and **high discharge (large Q)** applications — typically in **dams and tidal power plants**.

## 2.2 Concept of "Best Vane Angle Opening"

The guide vane opening (and runner blade angle in modern units) is **the most critical adjustable parameter** in a Kaplan turbine. As the vane opening changes:

- **Small opening** → Low discharge (Q ↓) → Low input power → Low output but possibly high ηₒ at low load
- **Optimum opening** → Best matching of inlet velocity triangle → **Maximum ηₒ**
- **Large opening** → High Q → High input power, but losses (eddy, friction) increase disproportionately → ηₒ drops

**Best vane angle opening** = the opening at which the **flow enters the runner blades without shock (smooth entry)**, making the **relative velocity (Vr)** tangential to the blade inlet angle (β). At this point, the **hydraulic efficiency is maximum**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | **Input Power (Water Power)** | $P_{in} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | W (or kW) | Energy supplied by water per second |
| 2 | **Output Power (Brake Power)** | $P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N (W - w_0) R}{60}$ | W (or kW) | From rope brake dynamometer |
| 3 | **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{o} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - w_0) R}{60 \cdot \rho g Q H}$ | Dimensionless | KTU's most-asked formula |
| 4 | **Net Head (H)** | $H = H_{gross} - h_f$ | m | After deducting frictional losses |
| 5 | **Discharge (Q)** | $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | Using venturi/orifice at inlet |
| 6 | **Specific Speed** | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | rpm·kW^0.5·m^-1.25 | Identifies turbine type |
| 7 | **Unit Speed** | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm/m^0.5 | For comparing turbines |
| 8 | **Unit Discharge** | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s/m^0.5 | Per unit head |
| 9 | **Unit Power** | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | W/m^1.5 | Per unit head |
| 10 | **Spring Balance Reading Difference** | $(W - w_0)$ | kgf or N | $W$ = loaded, $w_0$ = no-load reading |
| 11 | **Brake Drum Torque** | $T = (W - w_0) \cdot R \cdot g$ | N·m | For SI units (with g) |
| 12 | **Angular Velocity** | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | Used directly: $P = T \cdot \omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Conversion:**
> - **1 kgf = 9.81 N**
> - **Brake Power (SI)** = $\dfrac{2 \pi N (W - w_0) R g}{60}$ when $(W-w_0)$ is in kgf
> - **Brake Power (using ω)** = $T \cdot \omega = (W - w_0) g \cdot R \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The performance characteristics obtained from this test are used to:

- **Design selection** of turbines for specific site conditions (head, discharge, load).
- **Predicting turbine behavior** at off-design conditions using **unit quantities** and **specific speed** concepts.
- **Establishing "hill charts"** for homologous turbines (used during manufacturing).
- **Marine Engineering relevance:** Axial flow turbines (like the **Kaplan** and **propeller turbines**) are kinematically similar to **marine propellers**. The same **blade element theory** and **velocity triangles** govern both. Understanding Kaplan turbine performance helps marine engineers analyze **propeller-hull interaction**, **cavitation behavior**, and **propulsive efficiency**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Overall Efficiency Expression

The overall efficiency is the ratio of **useful mechanical power output** to the **hydraulic power input**. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** as expected in KTU valuation.

### Step A — Hydraulic Input Power (Water Power)

The water at the turbine inlet possesses potential energy due to head **H** and flows at rate **Q** (m³/s).

Mass of water flowing per second:

$$m = \rho \cdot Q \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

Potential energy per unit mass (specific energy) due to head H:

$$E_{specific} = g \cdot H \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$

Total hydraulic power input (rate of energy supply by water):

$$P_{in} = m \cdot E_{specific} = \rho \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot H \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

This is the **gross water power** available to the turbine.

### Step B — Mechanical Output Power (Brake Power)

The rope brake dynamometer applies a frictional load on the brake drum. The **net tangential force** at the drum rim is:

$$F_{net} = (W - w_0) \cdot g \quad [\text{Newtons}]$$

where:
- **W** = spring balance reading under load (kgf)
- **w₀** = spring balance reading at no-load (kgf) — accounts for **friction in the dynamometer bearings**

The torque transmitted to the brake drum:

$$T = F_{net} \cdot R = (W - w_0) \cdot g \cdot R \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

The angular velocity of the shaft (in radians per second):

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

Mechanical output (brake) power:

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = (W - w_0) \cdot g \cdot R \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

### Step C — Overall Efficiency

Dividing output by input:

$$\eta_{o} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\eta_{o} = \frac{(W - w_0) \cdot g \cdot R \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}}{\rho \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot H}$$

The **g cancels out** (since the same g appears in both numerator and denominator):

$$\eta_{o} = \frac{2\pi N (W - w_0) R}{60 \cdot \rho \cdot Q \cdot H}$$

In **percentage form**:

$$\boxed{\eta_{o} \,(\%) = \frac{2\pi N (W - w_0) R}{60 \cdot \rho \cdot Q \cdot H} \times 100}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:**
> If the problem provides **(W − w₀) in Newtons directly** (not kgf), the formula simplifies to:
> 
> $$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N \cdot (W - w_0) \cdot R}{60}$$
> 
> where $(W - w_0)$ is in Newtons and R is in metres. Always check the units given in the problem.

## 3.2 Derivation of Discharge Using Venturimeter / Orifice Meter

The discharge is measured using a **venturimeter** fitted in the supply pipe. By Bernoulli's equation and continuity, the theoretical discharge is:

$$Q_{th} = a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$$

The actual discharge incorporates a **coefficient of discharge (C_d)**:

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = C_d \cdot \frac{a_1 \cdot a_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

where:
- **a₁** = area of inlet (larger) section
- **a₂** = area of throat
- **h** = manometer reading (head difference in metres of water)
- **C_d** = coefficient of discharge (typically 0.95 to 0.98 for venturimeter)

## 3.3 Derivation of Net Head (H)

The **gross head** is the difference between the **upstream water level in the tank/reservoir** and the **tailrace level**. However, due to frictional losses in the pipe and fittings, the **net head available at the turbine inlet** is:

$$H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_f$$

where $h_f$ is the head lost due to friction in the penstock and bends. In a typical lab setup with a small supply tank, $h_f$ is often **neglected** or **measured directly** using a **piezometer at the turbine inlet**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A Kaplan turbine test rig gives the following observations at a particular vane opening: Head H = 8 m, Discharge Q = 0.05 m³/s, Speed N = 1500 rpm, Spring balance readings W = 12 kgf, w₀ = 1.5 kgf, Brake drum radius R = 0.25 m. Calculate the overall efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- H = 8 m, Q = 0.05 m³/s, N = 1500 rpm
- W = 12 kgf, w₀ = 1.5 kgf, R = 0.25 m
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Input Power:**

$$P_{in} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 8$$

$$P_{in} = 9810 \times 0.05 \times 8 = 9810 \times 0.4 = 3924 \text{ W} = 3.924 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Net Load:**

$$W - w_0 = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 \text{ kgf}$$

Converting to Newtons: $10.5 \times 9.81 = 103.005$ N

**Step 3 — Output Power:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N (W - w_0) g R}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 1500 \times 10.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.25}{60}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \times 3.14159 \times 1500 \times 10.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.25}{60}$$

Computing numerator:
- $2 \times 3.14159 = 6.2832$
- $6.2832 \times 1500 = 9424.78$
- $9424.78 \times 10.5 = 98960.2$
- $98960.2 \times 9.81 = 970,789.6$
- $970,789.6 \times 0.25 = 242,697.4$

$$P_{out} = \frac{242697.4}{60} = 4044.96 \text{ W} \approx 4.045 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{o} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{4044.96}{3924} = 1.0309$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **>100%**, which is physically impossible. This indicates either (a) measurement error, (b) the given vane opening is not realistic for the test, or (c) some constant frictional/offset values were missed. In real exams, students must comment on such anomalies and suggest a **plausible physical interpretation** (e.g., the unit quantities or the head measurement error).

## 3.5 Derivation of "Best Vane Angle" Concept (Velocity Triangle Basis)

At the **runner inlet**, the absolute velocity of water **V₁** has two components: **whirl velocity Vw₁** (tangential) and **flow velocity Vf₁** (axial). The relative velocity **Vr₁** enters the blade at angle **β₁** (blade angle).

The condition for **shockless entry** (no loss due to sudden change in direction):

$$V_{w1} = V_{r1} \cos(\beta_1) \quad \text{(velocity triangle condition)}$$

The **hydraulic efficiency** is given by Euler's equation:

$$\eta_{h} = \frac{V_{w1} \cdot u_1 - V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g \cdot H}$$

For an **axial flow turbine**, $u_1 = u_2 = u = \pi D N / 60$, and assuming $V_{w2} \approx 0$ (for a well-designed draft tube):

$$\eta_{h,max} = \frac{V_{w1,opt}}{gH/u} \cdot u$$

The **best vane angle** is the angle **β₁** at which the velocity triangle is **closed** (no shock loss). This corresponds to maximum ηₕ, and consequently, maximum overall efficiency ηₒ for a given set of operating conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kaplan Turbine Experimental Test Rig — Block Functional Architecture

Below is the **block-level functional architecture** of the lab test rig, showing the flow of energy and measurement points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supply Pump] --> B[Supply Tank with Sump]
    B --> C[Control Valve]
    C --> D[Venturimeter with Manometer]
    D --> E[Pressure Gauge at Inlet]
    E --> F[Kaplan Turbine Casing]
    F --> G[Guide Vanes - Adjustable]
    G --> H[Runner Blades - Adjustable]
    H --> I[Draft Tube]
    I --> J[Tailrace Tank]
    J --> K[Measuring Tank with Level Indicator]
    J --> L[Volumetric Discharge Q via Q = A x h / t]
    F --> M[Turbine Shaft]
    M --> N[Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    N --> O[Spring Balance W]
    N --> P[Spring Balance w0]
    M --> Q[Tachometer for N]
    F --> R[Pressure Gauge at Outlet]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Description

| Block | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| A | Supply Pump | Pumps water from reservoir to supply tank |
| B | Supply Tank | Provides stable head; water level measured by pointer gauge |
| C | Control Valve | Regulates flow to turbine |
| D | Venturimeter + Manometer | Measures discharge Q |
| E | Inlet Pressure Gauge | Measures inlet head |
| F | Turbine Casing (Spiral Casing) | Distributes water uniformly |
| G | Guide Vanes | Adjustable — control discharge and swirl |
| H | Runner Blades | Adjustable — convert hydraulic to mechanical energy |
| I | Draft Tube | Recovers kinetic energy as pressure |
| J | Tailrace Tank | Collects discharged water |
| K | Measuring Tank | Used to verify Q = A·h/t (volumetric method) |
| L | Discharge Calculation | Volumetric check: Q = Area × Rise in Level / Time |
| M | Turbine Shaft | Transmits mechanical power |
| N | Rope Brake Dynamometer | Applies variable load (W − w₀) |
| O, P | Spring Balances | Measure tight-side and slack-side tensions |
| Q | Tachometer | Measures rotational speed N (rpm) |
| R | Outlet Pressure Gauge | Measures outlet head (for net head) |

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Velocity Triangle at Runner Inlet (Kaplan Turbine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Absolute Velocity V1 at Inlet] --> B[Whirl Component Vw1 Tangential]
    A --> C[Flow Component Vf1 Axial]
    B --> D[Blade Velocity U1 = pi x D x N / 60]
    C --> E[Relative Velocity Vr1 = V1 - U1 Vectorially]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Blade Angle Beta1 - Best Vane Angle]
    F --> G[Condition for Shockless Entry Vr1 Tangent to Blade]
    G --> H[Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency etah_max]
    H --> I[Maximum Overall Efficiency etao_max]
    I --> J[Best Vane Angle Opening Identified]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Load Test Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Start Pump and Adjust to Test Head] --> B[Step 2: Set Guide Vane Opening to Desired Angle]
    B --> C[Step 3: Adjust Speed to Rated N via DC Motor Drive]
    C --> D[Step 4: Apply Zero Load - Record w0 and Q at No Load]
    D --> E[Step 5: Apply Incremental Brake Loads - Record W N Q H]
    E --> F[Step 6: Repeat for Different Vane Openings]
    F --> G[Step 7: Calculate Pin, Pout, etao for Each Load]
    G --> H[Step 8: Plot etao vs Output Power for Each Vane Opening]
    H --> I[Step 9: Identify Curve with Peak etao = Best Vane Angle Opening]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Graphical Output (Characteristic Curves to be Plotted)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load Test Data: H N Q W w0 R] --> B[Calculations: Pin Pout etao]
    B --> C[Plot 1: etao vs Output Power for Each Vane Opening]
    B --> D[Plot 2: etao vs Q Discharge]
    B --> E[Plot 3: Q vs Output Power]
    B --> F[Plot 4: N vs Output Power]
    C --> G[Best Vane Angle Opening = Curve with Maximum etao]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is a Kaplan turbine? Why is it classified as a reaction turbine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Kaplan turbine** is an **axial flow, propeller-type, adjustable-blade reaction turbine** used for low heads (typically H < 60 m) and high discharges. It is classified as a **reaction turbine** because:

1. **(1 mark)** Water flows **over the runner blades under pressure** — the pressure at the runner inlet is **greater than atmospheric** and **decreases** as water passes over the blades.
2. **(1 mark)** The water **enters the runner at a higher pressure** and **exits at a lower pressure**, with the **pressure drop occurring across the runner itself**.
3. **(1 mark)** Energy conversion is by **both impulse and reaction effects**, and the energy **remaining in the water leaving the runner** is recovered by a **draft tube** (a feature unique to reaction turbines).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "best vane angle opening" in the context of a Kaplan turbine load test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Best vane angle opening** is the specific **guide vane (and/or runner blade) opening** at which the Kaplan turbine delivers its **maximum overall efficiency** for a given **constant head (H)** and **constant speed (N)** during a load test.

1. **(1 mark)** It is identified by **plotting the overall efficiency (ηₒ) vs output power** curves for several vane openings.
2. **(1 mark)** The curve with the **highest peak value of ηₒ** corresponds to the **best vane angle opening**.
3. **(1 mark)** At this opening, the water enters the runner blades **without shock** (the velocity triangle is "closed"), giving **maximum hydraulic efficiency**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with two sub-parts typically of **7 marks each**. Module Internal Choice means the student answers **either Question A OR Question B** (both from the same module).

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**A Kaplan turbine test rig is operated at a constant head of 7 m and constant speed of 1450 rpm. The following observations were recorded at one particular guide vane opening:**

| Trial | Spring Balance W (kgf) | No-load reading w₀ (kgf) | Discharge Q (L/s) | Speed N (rpm) |
|-------|------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 2.0 | 0.5 | 28 | 1450 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 0.5 | 32 | 1450 |
| 3 | 6.0 | 0.5 | 36 | 1450 |
| 4 | 8.0 | 0.5 | 41 | 1450 |
| 5 | 10.0 | 0.5 | 47 | 1450 |

**Brake drum radius R = 20 cm. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².**

#### **(a) Calculate the input power, output power, and overall efficiency for each trial. (7 marks)**

#### **(b) Plot the characteristic curves (ηₒ vs Output Power) and identify the best vane angle opening. Comment on the trend. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Step-by-Step Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** H = 7 m, N = 1450 rpm, R = 0.20 m, w₀ = 0.5 kgf (constant), ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

**Constants to compute once:**
- $\dfrac{2\pi N R g}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times \pi \times 1450 \times 0.20 \times 9.81}{60} = \dfrac{17873.16}{60} = 297.886$ W per (kgf)

This factor multiplied by $(W - w_0)$ gives output power directly.

- $\rho g H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 7 = 68,670$ W/(m³/s)

**Trial 1:** W − w₀ = 2.0 − 0.5 = 1.5 kgf; Q = 28 L/s = 0.028 m³/s
- $P_{out} = 297.886 \times 1.5 = 446.83$ W ≈ **0.447 kW**
- $P_{in} = 68670 \times 0.028 = 1922.76$ W ≈ **1.923 kW**
- $\eta_{o} = 446.83 / 1922.76 = 0.2324$ ≈ **23.24%**

**Trial 2:** W − w₀ = 4.0 − 0.5 = 3.5 kgf; Q = 0.032 m³/s
- $P_{out} = 297.886 \times 3.5 = 1042.6$ W ≈ **1.043 kW**
- $P_{in} = 68670 \times 0.032 = 2197.44$ W ≈ **2.197 kW**
- $\eta_{o} = 1042.6 / 2197.44 = 0.4745$ ≈ **47.45%**

**Trial 3:** W − w₀ = 6.0 − 0.5 = 5.5 kgf; Q = 0.036 m³/s
- $P_{out} = 297.886 \times 5.5 = 1638.37$ W ≈ **1.638 kW**
- $P_{in} = 68670 \times 0.036 = 2472.12$ W ≈ **2.472 kW**
- $\eta_{o} = 1638.37 / 2472.12 = 0.6628$ ≈ **66.28%**

**Trial 4:** W − w₀ = 8.0 − 0.5 = 7.5 kgf; Q = 0.041 m³/s
- $P_{out} = 297.886 \times 7.5 = 2234.15$ W ≈ **2.234 kW**
- $P_{in} = 68670 \times 0.041 = 2815.47$ W ≈ **2.815 kW**
- $\eta_{o} = 2234.15 / 2815.47 = 0.7935$ ≈ **79.35%**

**Trial 5:** W − w₀ = 10.0 − 0.5 = 9.5 kgf; Q = 0.047 m³/s
- $P_{out} = 297.886 \times 9.5 = 2829.92$ W ≈ **2.830 kW**
- $P_{in} = 68670 \times 0.047 = 3227.49$ W ≈ **3.227 kW**
- $\eta_{o} = 2829.92 / 3227.49 = 0.8769$ ≈ **87.69%**

**Summary Table (Computed Values):**

| Trial | W − w₀ (kgf) | Q (m³/s) | P_in (kW) | P_out (kW) | ηₒ (%) |
|-------|---------------|-----------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 1.5 | 0.028 | 1.923 | 0.447 | 23.24 |
| 2 | 3.5 | 0.032 | 2.197 | 1.043 | 47.45 |
| 3 | 5.5 | 0.036 | 2.472 | 1.638 | 66.28 |
| 4 | 7.5 | 0.041 | 2.815 | 2.234 | 79.35 |
| 5 | 9.5 | 0.047 | 3.227 | 2.830 | 87.69 |

**Calculation Formula Used (for record):**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N (W - w_0) g R}{60}, \quad P_{in} = \rho g Q H, \quad \eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Plotting the Characteristic Curve and Identifying Best Opening (7 marks)**

**Graph to be plotted:** $\eta_o$ (Y-axis, %) vs $P_{out}$ (X-axis, kW) — single curve for the given vane opening.

**Key points to plot:**

| P_out (kW) | ηₒ (%) |
|--------------|----------|
| 0.447 | 23.24 |
| 1.043 | 47.45 |
| 1.638 | 66.28 |
| 2.234 | 79.35 |
| 2.830 | 87.69 |

**Description of the curve (for 7-mark answer):**

1. **(2 marks)** The curve is **monotonically increasing** — efficiency rises with output power for this vane opening. This indicates that this vane opening is **smaller than the best opening**, and the turbine is **under-loaded** throughout the test range.

2. **(2 marks)** To find the **best vane angle opening**, the load test must be **repeated at progressively larger vane openings**, and a family of ηₒ vs P_out curves plotted. The **envelope of peaks** of these curves gives the **best operating line**, and the **vane opening corresponding to the maximum peak** is the **best vane angle opening**.

3. **(2 marks)** **Comment on trend:** The linear-to-curvilinear increase in efficiency suggests:
   - At low loads, **shock losses at the runner inlet** dominate (vane opening too small for the flow).
   - As load increases, the **velocity triangles become more aligned** with the blade angles, reducing shock.
   - The **maximum achievable ηₒ** at this opening appears to be **above 87%** but would plateau and decline at very high loads due to **friction and eddy losses**.

4. **(1 mark)** Conclusion: For this vane opening, the turbine has not yet reached its maximum efficiency, so a **larger vane opening** must be tested in the next trial to identify the best opening.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Kaplan turbine. List the main components and state the function of the draft tube. (7 marks)**

**(b) During a load test on a Kaplan turbine, the following readings were taken at constant head H = 6 m and constant speed N = 1400 rpm. The brake drum diameter is 40 cm. Compute the unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power for the trial where the output is 2.5 kW and the discharge is 0.04 m³/s. Comment on whether the unit quantities fall within the typical range for a Kaplan turbine. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Components (7 marks)**

**Construction of Kaplan Turbine:**

A Kaplan turbine consists of the following main components:

1. **(0.5 mark) Spiral Casing:** Receives water from the penstock and distributes it uniformly around the runner.
2. **(0.5 mark) Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates):** Adjustable vanes that regulate flow and create the desired whirl component.
3. **(0.5 mark) Guide Vane Mechanism:** A servomotor-controlled ring to simultaneously rotate all guide vanes.
4. **(0.5 mark) Runner with Adjustable Blades:** Hub with 3 to 6 blades whose pitch can be adjusted while running.
5. **(0.5 mark) Runner Blade Operating Mechanism:** Hydraulic servomotor inside the hub that rotates the blades.
6. **(0.5 mark) Draft Tube:** Conical or elbow-shaped tube that gradually expands to recover kinetic energy.
7. **(0.5 mark) Shaft and Bearings:** Transmit mechanical power to the generator.

**Working Principle (1.5 marks):**

- Water from the **penstock** enters the **spiral casing**, which directs it radially inward through the **guide vanes**.
- The **guide vanes** create a **whirl component** and direct the water onto the **runner blades** at the correct angle.
- Water flows **axially** through the runner (hence "axial flow") and imparts a **change in angular momentum** to the blades, producing torque.
- The **adjustable runner blades** can be rotated to match the flow angle, ensuring **shockless entry** and maximum efficiency at all loads.
- Water then enters the **draft tube**, where its **kinetic energy is converted to pressure energy** (the draft tube creates a suction effect at the runner outlet, increasing the effective head).
- The shaft transmits mechanical power to the **rope brake dynamometer** (in lab) or **generator** (in field).

**Function of the Draft Tube (1 mark):**

- Converts the **residual kinetic energy** of water leaving the runner into **useful pressure head**.
- Creates a **suction head (vacuum)** at the runner outlet, increasing the **net head** across the runner.
- Allows the turbine to be set **above the tailrace level** (practical installation advantage).

---

##### **Part (b) — Unit Quantities Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- H = 6 m, N = 1400 rpm
- Output P = 2.5 kW = 2500 W
- Q = 0.04 m³/s
- Brake drum diameter = 40 cm → R = 0.20 m (given for reference)

**Step 1 — Unit Speed:**

$$N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{1400}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{1400}{2.4495}$$

$$\boxed{N_u = 571.55 \text{ rpm/m}^{0.5}}$$

**Step 2 — Unit Discharge:**

$$Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{0.04}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{0.04}{2.4495}$$

$$\boxed{Q_u = 0.01633 \text{ m}^3/\text{s/m}^{0.5}}$$

**Step 3 — Unit Power:**

$$P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \frac{2500}{6^{3/2}} = \frac{2500}{6 \times \sqrt{6}} = \frac{2500}{14.6969}$$

$$\boxed{P_u = 170.10 \text{ W/m}^{1.5}}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Typical Ranges (1.5 marks):**

| Unit Quantity | Computed | Typical Range for Kaplan | Within Range? |
|---------------|----------|--------------------------|----------------|
