| Unit Speed, $N_u$ | 571.55 rpm/m^0.5 | 500 to 850 rpm/m^0.5 | ✅ Yes |
| Unit Discharge, $Q_u$ | 0.01633 m³/s/m^0.5 | 0.015 to 0.030 m³/s/m^0.5 | ✅ Yes |
| Unit Power, $P_u$ | 170.10 W/m^1.5 | 100 to 1000 W/m^1.5 | ✅ Yes |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** All three unit quantities fall within the **standard range for a Kaplan turbine**, confirming that the test is being conducted on a **homologously designed Kaplan-class machine**. The specific speed $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ can also be calculated for classification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> **1. Forgetting the w₀ correction (–2 to –3 marks):** The no-load spring balance reading $w_0$ must be **subtracted** from the loaded reading W. Many students write $P = (2\pi N W R g) / 60$ — this is WRONG because it does not account for **dynamometer bearing friction**. Always use $(W - w_0)$.
> 
> **2. Unit mismatch in Q (–2 marks):** If Q is given in **L/s**, you **must** convert to **m³/s** by dividing by 1000, before using $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$. Forgetting this gives efficiency 1000× too small.
> 
> **3. Mixing up kgf and N (–2 marks):** Spring balances read in **kgf**. Either:
> - Multiply $(W - w_0)$ by **g = 9.81** to convert to Newtons, OR
> - Use the formula $P_{out} = (2\pi N (W-w_0) g R) / 60$ directly, keeping kgf.
> 
> **4. Forgetting to plot ALL characteristic curves (–1 mark in part b):** KTU expects at least 3 curves: **ηₒ vs P_out, Q vs P_out, AND N vs P_out** for the load test.
> 
> **5. Not commenting on the "best vane angle" (–2 marks):** A load test at ONE vane opening is insufficient. The **best vane angle** is identified by **comparing multiple vane openings** and picking the one with **maximum ηₒ**. Always mention this in part (b).
> 
> **6. Head notation confusion (–1 mark):** Some students use **gross head** in efficiency calculation. The **net head** (after deducting friction losses) is the correct value to use.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ⭐ **Kaplan turbine** = **Axial flow, fully reaction, adjustable-blade** turbine for **low head, high discharge** applications.
- ⭐ **Specific speed range** for Kaplan: **Nₛ = 300 to 1000 (metric)**.
- ⭐ **Load test** = Constant H, constant N; vary the brake load W.
- ⭐ **Best vane angle opening** = Vane opening giving **maximum ηₒ**; identified by **plotting ηₒ vs P_out for multiple openings** and selecting the **peak of the envelope**.
- ⭐ **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$
  - $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - w_0) g R}{60}$ (kgf basis)
  - $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$
  - $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$, $Q_u = Q / \sqrt{H}$, $P_u = P / H^{3/2}$
- ⭐ **Always subtract w₀ from W** to correct for dynamometer friction.
- ⭐ **Convert Q from L/s to m³/s** (divide by 1000).
- ⭐ **Convert kgf to N** by multiplying by 9.81, OR keep kgf and include g in the formula.
- ⭐ **Three standard graphs** to plot in load test: **ηₒ vs P_out, Q vs P_out, N vs P_out** (all for the same vane opening).
- ⭐ **At the best vane angle**, water enters the runner **without shock** (velocity triangle closed).
- ⭐ **Marine connection:** The **blade element theory** used for Kaplan runner design is **identical** to **propeller design theory** — the same principles of momentum change, velocity triangles, and cavitation avoidance apply.
- ⭐ **Draft tube** recovers **kinetic energy** as **pressure head** and creates **suction** at the runner outlet, increasing net head.
- ⭐ **Guide vanes** regulate **flow rate and whirl component**; **runner blades** convert **hydraulic energy to mechanical energy**.

---

**End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Performance Characteristic Tests on Kaplan Turbine**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Single Stage and Multi Stage Centrifugal Pumps at Constant Speed and at Variable Speed (Actual and predicted curves)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Single Stage and Multi Stage Centrifugal Pumps at Constant Speed and at Variable Speed (Actual and predicted curves) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **centrifugal pump** is a rotodynamic machine that imparts kinetic energy to a fluid through the action of a rotating impeller, subsequently converting this kinetic energy into pressure energy (head) within a volute casing. The **performance characteristic test** of a centrifugal pump is a standardized experimental procedure conducted to determine the functional relationship between the key operating parameters — **Head (H)**, **Discharge (Q)**, **Input Power (P)**, and **Efficiency (η)** — under controlled laboratory conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The performance test quantifies the variation of total developed head $(H)$, shaft power $(P)$, and overall efficiency $(\eta)$ as a function of volume flow rate $(Q)$ at a *constant rotational speed*. When the speed is varied, the experimentally obtained curves are compared against the *predicted curves* derived from the **Affinity Laws** (also called *Pump Laws* or *Homologous Curves*). ### 1.1.1 Single Stage vs Multi Stage Centrifugal Pumps | Feature | Single Stage Pump | Multi Stage Pump | |---|---|---| | Number of Impellers | **One** | **Two or more** in series | | Head Generation | Low to medium (typically 10–100 m) | High (up to several hundred meters) | | Marine Application | Cooling water, bilge, domestic freshwater | Boiler feed, high-pressure firefighting, cargo oil pumping | | Total Head Relation | $H_{total} = H_{single\ impeller}$ | $H_{total} = n \times H_{single\ impeller}$ | Where $n$ is the number of identical stages. In a **multi stage pump**, the discharge from the first impeller enters the eye of the next impeller through an **inter-stage guide passage**, and the heads add up while the flow rate remains constant (same $Q$ through each stage). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Pedal of a Bicycle"** > > Imagine you are cycling a bicycle fitted with a dynamo headlamp (this is your **centrifugal pump**). > - When you **pedal slowly** (low speed $N$), the lamp glows dimly — you are generating **low pressure (head)** and pushing **little water (discharge)**. This is your **low-speed performance curve**. > - When you **pedal fast** (high speed $N$), the lamp shines brightly — high head, more flow. This is your **high-speed performance curve**. > - Now suppose you cycled at *slow* speed and noted the lamp brightness. You then pedalled at *fast* speed. By some simple proportionality, you could have **predicted** how bright the lamp would be at fast speed *before* actually pedalling fast. That prediction is the **affinity-law predicted curve**. > - The **actual curve** is the one you measure with instruments. The **predicted curve** is the one you compute from pump laws. Comparing both validates the pump's conformity to ideal fluid-mechanical behaviour. > - A **single-stage** pump is like a single gear on the bicycle. A **multi-stage** pump is like a multi-gear system where each gear adds more "push" (head) to the water before it exits. ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics - **Rotational Speed ($N$):** Measured in **rpm** (revolutions per minute). Typical marine centrifugal pumps operate between **1450 rpm** (50 Hz, 4-pole) and **2950 rpm** (50 Hz, 2-pole). - **Volume Flow Rate ($Q$):** Measured in **m³/s** or **L/s** or **m³/hr**. - **Total Head ($H$):** Measured in **metres of fluid** (m) or **kJ/kg** (specific energy). - **Shaft Power ($P$):** Measured in **kW**. - **Overall Efficiency ($\eta$):** Dimensionless, expressed as a percentage (**%**). - **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** The single operating point where the pump operates with maximum overall efficiency. - **Specific Speed ($N_s$):** A dimensionless-classification number used to categorise pump geometry. ## 1.4 What is a "Performance Characteristic Curve"? A performance characteristic curve is a **graphical representation** of how $H$, $P$, and $\eta$ each vary with $Q$ at a fixed $N$. The triad of curves normally drawn on a single plot is the **H–Q–η–P characteristic chart** of the pump. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** > The lab demands that students *plot* all three curves (H-Q, η-Q, P-Q) at the **rated speed** (constant speed test) and *superimpose* the **predicted curves** for at least one different speed obtained via the affinity laws. This is the central deliverable of the experiment record. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Pumps Behave This Way A centrifugal pump's performance arises from the **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation**, which relates the head developed to the impeller's tangential velocity components: $$H_{Euler} = \frac{U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}}{g}$$ Where: - $U_2$ = tangential velocity of impeller at outlet (m/s) - $U_1$ = tangential velocity of impeller at inlet (m/s) - $V_{w2}$ = whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity at outlet (m/s) - $V_{w1}$ = whirl component at inlet (m/s) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) For a pump *without* pre-whirl (radial entry at inlet, $V_{w1} \approx 0$), this reduces to: $$H_{Euler} = \frac{U_2 V_{w2}}{g}$$ This single equation is the *mother equation* from which the entire performance characteristic, the affinity laws, and the homologous-curve relationships emerge. ## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic) > [!NOTE] > The following sequence is what physically happens inside the pump as $Q$ varies, and it is the *reason* why the H-Q curve slopes downward. 1. **Fluid Entry:** Water enters the impeller eye axially (radially inward on the impeller disc reference frame). At low flow, fluid approaches purely radially. 2. **Impeller Rotation:** The impeller rotates at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Each fluid particle gains tangential velocity proportional to blade tip speed $U_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$. 3. **Centrifugal Action:** The fluid is flung outward by centrifugal force (in the rotating frame) — pressure rises across the impeller vanes. 4. **Volute Collection:** The high-velocity fluid enters the spiral volute casing where kinetic energy is **diffuser-decelerated** into pressure energy. 5. **Cutwater and Recirculation:** At very low $Q$ (near shut-off), the relative flow angle deviates strongly from blade angle, causing **recirculation loss** and **disc friction** — head is high but inefficient. 6. **Increasing $Q$:** As $Q$ increases, the radial velocity component grows. The relative exit flow angle no longer matches the blade angle, so the *whirl* $V_{w2}$ drops slightly. Consequently, $H$ decreases monotonically with $Q$. 7. **Beyond BEP:** Excessive $Q$ causes **separation losses** on the impeller blade suction side, and head falls off rapidly. ## 2.3 The Affinity Laws (Pump Laws / Homologous Laws) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU 2024 Topic:** Affinity Laws form the *backbone* of all variable-speed performance prediction questions. The **Affinity Laws** (also called *Pump Laws* or *Homologous Curves*) are derived by dimensional analysis and Euler's equation applied to *geometrically similar* impellers operating at different speeds with the same fluid (water). ### 2.3.1 Statement of the Three Affinity Laws **Law 1 — Discharge varies directly with speed:** $$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$ **Law 2 — Head varies as the square of speed:** $$\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$ **Law 3 — Power varies as the cube of speed:** $$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$ ### 2.3.2 Logical "Why" Behind Each Law - **Law 1** ($Q \propto N$): The flow through the impeller depends on the *swept volume per second*, which is $\propto$ (blade tip speed) $\times$ (flow area) $\propto N \times D^2$. With the same impeller diameter, only $N$ changes, so $Q \propto N$. - **Law 2** ($H \propto N^2$): From Euler's equation, $H \propto U_2 \cdot V_{w2}$. Both $U_2 \propto N$ and $V_{w2} \propto N$, so $H \propto N^2$. - **Law 3** ($P \propto N^3$): Power = $\rho g Q H \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$. ## 2.4 Specific Speed — Geometry Classifier $$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$ - $N$ in **rpm** - $Q$ in **m³/s** (or **L/s** depending on convention) - $H$ in **metres** For a **single stage** pump, $H$ is the head per stage. For a **multi stage** pump, $H$ in the specific-speed formula is **per stage** (i.e., $H_{per\ stage} = H_{total} / n$). ## 2.5 Efficiency Definitions **Overall (Total) Efficiency:** $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{Water\ Power}{Shaft\ Power}$$ **Manometric Efficiency:** $$\eta_{manometric} = \frac{H_{actual}}{H_{Euler}}$$ **Mechanical Efficiency:** $$\eta_{mechanical} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$$ **Volumetric Efficiency:** $$\eta_{volumetric} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$ For a well-designed centrifugal pump, the typical values at BEP are: - $\eta_{overall} = 60$ to **85%** - $\eta_{manometric} = 90$ to **95%** - $\eta_{volumetric} = 95$ to **99%** - $\eta_{mechanical} = 95$ to **98%** ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE and Internal Assessments for PCNSL507.** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H = H_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g} + (z_d - z_s)$ | Total head (Bernoulli between suction and delivery gauges) | **m** | | 2 | $Q = \frac{A \times L}{t}$ or $Q = C_d A_v \sqrt{2gh}$ | Volume flow (tank-rise method or venturi) | **m³/s** | | 3 | $P_{shaft} = 2 \pi N T / 60$ | Shaft power from torque $T$ | **W** | | 4 | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | Useful water power | **W** | | 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | Overall efficiency | **dimensionless (%)** | | 6 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity Law 1 (discharge) | — | | 7 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity Law 2 (head) | — | | 8 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity Law 3 (power) | — | | 9 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (per stage) | — | | 10 | $H_{multi} = n \times H_{stage}$ | Multi-stage total head | **m** | | 11 | $H \propto D^2 N^2$ | Head similarity (geometric + kinematic) | — | | 12 | $Q \propto D^3 N$ | Discharge similarity | — | | 13 | $\eta_{multi\ stage} \approx \eta_{single\ stage}$ (no losses added) | Efficiency per stage ≈ same | — | | 14 | $\Delta H = H_{predicted} - H_{actual}$ | Curve deviation metric | **m** | | 15 | $\%\text{Deviation} = \dfrac{\vert H_{actual} - H_{predicted} \vert}{H_{predicted}} \times 100$ | Conformity check | **%** | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Marine & Industrial Applications:** > - **Marine:** Centrifugal pumps drive *ballast systems*, *cooling seawater circulation*, *fire-fighting mains*, and *boiler feed*. Onboard engineers use characteristic curves to select a replacement pump that matches the duty point after a refit. > - **Power Plants:** Boiler-feed pumps (often multi-stage) operate at 3000+ rpm; affinity laws let the engineer predict the new duty point if grid frequency drops (e.g., 50 Hz → 49 Hz). > - **HVAC:** Chilled water pumps use variable-speed drives (VSDs); the affinity laws give the new energy consumption directly (cubic power saving). > - **Process Plants:** A spare pump of different speed is acceptable if its predicted duty curve is overlaid and matches the system resistance curve at the operating point. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Affinity Laws from Euler's Turbomachinery Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **This derivation is a *guaranteed* 7-mark question in KTU 2024 ESE for any fluid-machinery / hydraulics-lab paper.** ### Starting Point — Euler's Head Equation For a centrifugal pump impeller with no pre-whirl, Euler derived: $$H_{Euler} = \frac{U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}}{g}$$ **Assumption (typical for radial flow pumps):** At inlet, fluid enters purely radially, so $V_{w1} = 0$. Hence: $$H_{Euler} = \frac{U_2 V_{w2}}{g}$$ ### Step 1 — Express the Tangential Velocity in Terms of Speed and Diameter The blade tip speed at outlet radius $r_2$ is: $$U_2 = \frac{2 \pi r_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$ where $D_2 = 2 r_2$ is the impeller outlet diameter. Therefore: $$U_2 \propto N \quad \text{(at constant } D_2 \text{)}$$ ### Step 2 — Express the Whirl Component via Velocity Triangles From the outlet velocity triangle of the impeller: $$V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$$ where: - $V_{f2}$ is the radial (flow) component at outlet - $\beta_2$ is the blade angle at outlet If the impeller is **geometrically similar** at two operating points, $\beta_2$ and all ratios remain constant, so $V_{w2}$ scales the same way as $U_2$: $$V_{w2} \propto N$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Back into Euler's Equation $$H_{Euler} = \frac{U_2 \cdot V_{w2}}{g} \propto N \cdot N = N^2$$ $$\boxed{\therefore\ H \propto N^2 \quad \text{(Affinity Law 2)}}$$ ### Step 4 — Derive the Discharge Affinity Law Discharge through the impeller: $$Q = \pi D_2 b_2 V_{f2}$$ where $b_2$ is the impeller outlet width. For geometric similarity, $b_2 / D_2 = \text{const}$, so: $$Q \propto D_2^2 \cdot V_{f2}$$ From the velocity triangle, $V_{f2} = V_{w2} \tan(\text{some angle})$ — since all angles are preserved under geometric similarity, $V_{f2} \propto N$, hence: $$Q \propto D_2^2 \cdot N$$ For a *given* pump (constant $D_2$): $$\boxed{\therefore\ Q \propto N \quad \text{(Affinity Law 1)}}$$ ### Step 5 — Derive the Power Affinity Law Power imparted to the fluid: $$P_{water} = \rho g Q H$$ Substituting $Q \propto N$ and $H \propto N^2$: $$P_{water} \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$$ $$\boxed{\therefore\ P \propto N^3 \quad \text{(Affinity Law 3)}}$$ > **Shaft Power** also follows $P_{shaft} \propto N^3$ assuming $\eta \approx \text{constant}$, which is approximately true over the operating range of a centrifugal pump. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Predicting a New Speed Curve > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU-Style 14-mark sub-question: "Predict the H-Q curve at 1750 rpm using the affinity laws from the test data at 1450 rpm."** **Given (Constant Speed Test Data at $N_1 = 1450$ rpm):** | Sl. No. | $Q$ (L/s) | $H$ (m) | $P$ (kW) | $\eta$ (%) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 28.0 | 6.5 | 0.0 | | 2 | 5 | 27.2 | 7.4 | 44.2 | | 3 | 10 | 25.8 | 8.1 | 76.5 | | 4 | 15 | 23.5 | 8.7 | 86.0 | | 5 | 20 | 20.4 | 9.3 | 80.6 | | 6 | 25 | 16.5 | 9.9 | 68.4 | | 7 | 30 | 11.2 | 10.6 | 47.5 | **Target speed:** $N_2 = 1750$ rpm **Affinity ratios:** $$k_N = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.2069$$ $$k_Q = k_N = 1.2069,\quad k_H = k_N^2 = 1.4566,\quad k_P = k_N^3 = 1.7577$$ **Step-by-step calculation for each row:** Using the laws: $Q_2 = k_N Q_1$, $H_2 = k_N^2 H_1$, $P_2 = k_N^3 P_1$, $\eta_2 = \eta_1$ (efficiency is approximately preserved). ### Predicted Performance at $N_2 = 1750$ rpm | Sl. No. | $Q_2$ (L/s) | $H_2$ (m) | $P_2$ (kW) | $\eta_2$ (%) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.0 | 40.79 | 11.42 | 0.0 | | 2 | 6.03 | 39.62 | 13.01 | 44.2 | | 3 | 12.07 | 37.58 | 14.24 | 76.5 | | 4 | 18.10 | 34.23 | 15.29 | 86.0 | | 5 | 24.14 | 29.72 | 16.34 | 80.6 | | 6 | 30.17 | 24.04 | 17.40 | 68.4 | | 7 | 36.21 | 16.31 | 18.63 | 47.5 | ### Detailed Step for Row 2 (for the solution write-up) $$Q_2 = 1.2069 \times 5 = 6.03\ \text{L/s}$$ $$H_2 = 1.4566 \times 27.2 = 39.62\ \text{m}$$ $$P_2 = 1.7577 \times 7.4 = 13.01\ \text{kW}$$ $$\eta_2 = \eta_1 = 44.2\ \%$$ ### Verification of Efficiency Preservation Check efficiency from basic definition: $$\eta_2 = \frac{\rho g Q_2 H_2}{P_2} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00603 \times 39.62}{13010} = 0.442 = 44.2\ \%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_2 = \eta_1 \quad \checkmark}$$ > **Conclusion:** The pump, when speeded up from 1450 rpm to 1750 rpm, will deliver 1.207 times the original discharge, 1.457 times the original head, and consume 1.758 times the original power. The shape of the curves is preserved; the entire H-Q, η-Q, P-Q triad simply scales and shifts on the chart. ## 3.3 Multi-Stage Pump Analytical Extension For an $n$-stage pump, the head generated by each stage is identical (by symmetry), so the affinity laws apply *per stage*: $$H_{total,2} = n \times H_{stage,2} = n \times k_N^2 H_{stage,1}$$ The discharge is the same through every stage, and efficiency is: $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{single\ stage} \times \left(\frac{H_{total,useful}}{H_{total,Euler}}\right) \approx \eta_{single\ stage} \times (1 - \text{inter-stage losses})$$ **Numerical Example:** A 4-stage pump tested at 1450 rpm shows $H_{total} = 100$ m. What is the per-stage head? $$H_{per\ stage} = \frac{100}{4} = 25\ \text{m}$$ If speed is increased to 1750 rpm: $$H_{per\ stage,2} = 1.4566 \times 25 = 36.4\ \text{m}$$ $$H_{total,2} = 4 \times 36.4 = 145.7\ \text{m}$$ ## 3.4 Curve Deviation Analysis (Predicted vs Actual) When the actual test is performed at the new speed, deviations arise due to: 1. **Hydraulic losses** (friction, recirculation) scaling with $N^2$ but with different coefficients 2. **Clearance and leakage losses** that don't scale linearly 3. **Cavitation onset at higher speeds** reducing the head The **percentage deviation** is computed as: $$\%\text{Deviation in } H = \frac{\vert H_{actual} - H_{predicted} \vert}{H_{predicted}} \times 100$$ A well-designed pump exhibits deviations of **< 5%** at speeds within ±20% of the rated speed. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Test Rig ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["SUMP TANK
Water Reservoir"] node2["SUCTION LINE
With Strainer"] node3["CENTRIFUGAL PUMP
Single or Multi Stage"] node4["PRESSURE GAUGE
at Delivery"] node5["VACUUM GAUGE
at Suction"] node6["SPEED SENSOR
Tachometer"] node7["DELIVERY VALVE
Throttle / Gate Valve"] node8["FLOW METER
Venturi / Orifice"] node9["MEASURING TANK
For Q Calibration"] node10["DYNAMOMETER
Torque Measurement"] node11["VFD MOTOR
Variable Frequency Drive"] node12["DATA LOGGER
H, Q, N, T, P"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node9 --> node1 node3 --- node5 node3 --- node6 node3 --- node10 node10 --- node11 node10 --- node12 node12 --- node8 node12 --- node4 node12 --- node5 node12 --- node6 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Constant Speed Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA["START: Prime the Pump
and Set N to Rated"] stepB["Close Delivery Valve
Shut-off Condition"] stepC["Record H, P, N, Q"] stepD["Open Valve Slightly
Increase Q by Step"] stepE["Record H, P, N, Q"] stepF["Repeat for 6-8
Flow Points"] stepG["Plot H-Q, η-Q, P-Q
Curves on Graph"] stepH["Identify Best
Efficiency Point BEP"] stepI["STOP: Analysis
Complete"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepE stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH --> stepI ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Variable Speed Test Procedure & Affinity Law Application ```mermaid flowchart TD stepJ["STEP 1: Perform Constant
Speed Test at N1"] stepK["STEP 2: Record Full
H-Q, P-Q, η-Q Data"] stepL["STEP 3: Apply Affinity
Laws to Predict N2 Curve"] stepM["STEP 4: Set Motor to
New Speed N2 via VFD"] stepN["STEP 5: Repeat Same
Q-Points at N2"] stepO["STEP 6: Record Actual
H, P, Q at N2"] stepP["STEP 7: Overlay Predicted
and Actual Curves"] stepQ["STEP 8: Compute
Percentage Deviation"] stepR["STEP 9: Validate
or Discuss Sources
of Deviation"] stepJ --> stepK stepK --> stepL stepL --> stepM stepM --> stepN stepN --> stepO stepO --> stepP stepP --> stepQ stepQ --> stepR ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture — Constant Speed vs Variable Speed Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CS["CONSTANT SPEED OPERATION"] csA["Fixed N = N1"] csB["Vary Q via
Delivery Valve"] csC["Measure H, P
at each Q"] csD["Plot Single Set
of H-Q, η-Q, P-Q"] csA --> csB csB --> csC csC --> csD end subgraph VS["VARIABLE SPEED OPERATION"] vsA["Multiple N values
N1, N2, N3"] vsB["For each N
vary Q fully"] vsC["Predict curve
via Affinity Laws"] vsD["Measure actual
curve at each N"] vsE["Compare predicted
vs actual
Overlay plots"] vsA --> vsB vsB --> vsC vsC --> vsD vsD --> vsE end subgraph AN["ANALYTICAL OUTPUT"] anA["Efficiency Map
across N-Q plane"] anB["Conformity
Index of Pump"] anC["Selection of
Best Operating Point"] anA --> anB anB --> anC end CS --> AN VS --> AN ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid — Performance Curve Shape Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR axisX["Q-axis
Discharge"] axisY["Y-axis
H, η, P"] p1["H-Q Curve:
Drops from Shut-off
Head to Zero at MaxQ"] p2["η-Q Curve:
Bell Shape with Peak
at Best Efficiency Point"] p3["P-Q Curve:
Rises with Q from
Minimum at Shut-off"] p4["Constant Speed Line:
N1 = Rated"] p1 --- axisX p2 --- axisX p3 --- axisX p4 --- axisY ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > - The **H–Q curve** is *monotonically decreasing* — head is highest at shut-off and falls to zero at maximum flow. > - The **η–Q curve** is *bell-shaped* — efficiency peaks at the BEP and falls on either side. > - The **P–Q curve** is *monotonically increasing* — more flow requires more power. > - In a **multi-speed diagram**, the entire triad shifts: higher $N$ moves all curves up (more head) and right (more flow) along the affinity-law scaling. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Part A Format:** 2-mark short answer + 1-mark diagram/sketch element. Each question here has full model answer. ### Question 1: Define the Affinity Laws for a centrifugal pump. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Affinity Laws** (or *Pump Laws*) describe how the operating parameters of a geometrically similar centrifugal pump change with rotational speed. - **Law 1:** Discharge varies directly with speed: $Q \propto N$ - **Law 2:** Head varies as the square of speed: $H \propto N^2$ - **Law 3:** Power varies as the cube of speed: $P \propto N^3$ In ratio form: $Q_1/Q_2 = N_1/N_2$, $H_1/H_2 = (N_1/N_2)^2$, $P_1/P_2 = (N_1/N_2)^3$. These laws are derived from Euler's turbomachinery equation under the condition of geometric similarity between operating points. ### Question 2: Sketch and label the characteristic curves (H–Q, η–Q, P–Q) of a centrifugal pump at constant speed. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Internal Assessment Test 2]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three characteristic curves plotted on a common Q-axis: - **H–Q curve:** Starts from the **shut-off head** at $Q=0$ and falls monotonically, becoming nearly vertical near maximum discharge. - **η–Q curve:** **Bell-shaped**, rising from 0% at $Q=0$, peaking at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**, and falling to lower values at higher $Q$. - **P–Q curve:** Starts from a non-zero minimum at shut-off (friction + windage power) and rises with $Q$ in a nearly straight, slightly curved line. (For full marks, include a hand-drawn sketch with axes labeled in m, m³/s, %, kW and mark the BEP clearly on the η–Q curve.) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A. (a)** A centrifugal pump is tested at a constant speed of **1450 rpm** and the following observations are recorded. Plot the **H–Q**, **η–Q**, and **P–Q** characteristics. Identify the **Best Efficiency Point** and the corresponding design discharge and head. **(7 marks)** | Sl. No. | $Q$ (L/s) | $H$ (m) | $P$ (kW) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 30.0 | 7.0 | | 2 | 6 | 28.5 | 7.8 | | 3 | 12 | 26.0 | 8.6 | | 4 | 18 | 22.5 | 9.3 | | 5 | 24 | 18.0 | 10.0 | | 6 | 30 | 12.0 | 10.8 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute Water Power and Efficiency at each point:** Using $\eta = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ with $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²: For Row 1: $P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0 \times 30 = 0$ W, so $\eta_1 = 0\%$ For Row 2: $P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.006 \times 28.5 = 1677.5$ W $= 1.68$ kW $$\eta_2 = \frac{1.68}{7.8} \times 100 = 21.5\%$$ For Row 3: $P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 26.0 = 3060.7$ W $= 3.06$ kW $$\eta_3 = \frac{3.06}{8.6} \times 100 = 35.6\%$$ For Row 4: $P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.018 \times 22.5 = 3973.0$ W $= 3.97$ kW $$\eta_4 = \frac{3.97}{9.3} \times 100 = 42.7\%$$ For Row 5: $P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.024 \times 18.0 = 4237.9$ W $= 4.24$ kW $$\eta_5 = \frac{4.24}{10.0} \times 100 = 42.4\%$$ For Row 6: $P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 12.0 = 3531.6$ W $= 3.53$ kW $$\eta_6 = \frac{3.53}{10.8} \times 100 = 32.7\%$$ **Step 2 — Summary Table:** | Sl. No. | $Q$ (L/s) | $H$ (m) | $P$ (kW) | $\eta$ (%) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 30.0 | 7.0 | 0.0 | | 2 | 6 | 28.5 | 7.8 | 21.5 | | 3 | 12 | 26.0 | 8.6 | 35.6 | | 4 | 18 | 22.5 | 9.3 | 42.7 | | 5 | 24 | 18.0 | 10.0 | 42.4 | | 6 | 30 | 12.0 | 10.8 | 32.7 | **Step 3 — Identify BEP:** From the table, $\eta$ peaks at **42.7%** between Sl. No. 4 and 5. The BEP lies at approximately: $$Q_{BEP} \approx 18\text{–}20\ \text{L/s},\quad H_{BEP} \approx 22\ \text{m},\quad P_{BEP} \approx 9.3\ \text{kW}$$ **Step 4 — Plot:** Plot $H$ (Y1), $\eta$ (Y2), $P$ (Y3) all against $Q$ on the X-axis. Mark BEP. > **Conclusion:** The pump's design operating point is at $Q \approx 19$ L/s and $H \approx 22$ m with an overall efficiency of 42.7%. --- **A. (b)** Using the data in part (a), predict the performance of the same pump at a new speed of **1750 rpm** using the affinity laws. Tabulate the predicted values and comment on the deviation you would expect between predicted and actual curves. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute Affinity Ratios:** $$k_N = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.2069$$ $$k_Q = k_N = 1.2069$$ $$k_H = k_N^2 = 1.4566$$ $$k_P = k_N^3 = 1.7577$$ **Step 2 — Apply Laws Row by Row:** For each row: $Q_2 = 1.2069 Q_1$, $H_2 = 1.4566 H_1$, $P_2 = 1.7577 P_1$. **Predicted Table at 1750 rpm:** | Sl. No. | $Q$ (L/s) | $H$ (m) | $P$ (kW) | $\eta$ (%) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.00 | 43.70 | 12.30 | 0.0 | | 2 | 7.24 | 41.51 | 13.71 | 21.5 | | 3 | 14.48 | 37.87 | 15.12 | 35.6 | | 4 | 21.72 | 32.77 | 16.35 | 42.7 | | 5 | 28.97 | 26.22 | 17.58 | 42.4 | | 6 | 36.21 | 17.48 | 18.98 | 32.7 | **Step 3 — Comment on Expected Deviations:** The predicted and actual curves will deviate due to: 1. **Hydraulic losses** (friction, eddy) scale with $N^2$ but the *constants* depend on Reynolds number, which changes with $N$. 2. **Cavitation onset** at higher speeds — if NPSH available < NPSH required at the new duty, head will drop sharply. 3. **Recirculation losses** at low-flow end of the curve. 4. **Mechanical losses** (bearing, seal friction) scale with $N$ linearly, not $N^3$. 5. **Volumetric leakage losses** scale with pressure (i.e., $H$) and do not follow affinity laws strictly. **Expected deviation:** Within ±5% on $H$ and $Q$, and within ±3% on $P$ for a well-designed pump at speeds within 20% of rated. --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]` **B. (a)** A **3-stage centrifugal pump** delivers water at a total head of **84 m** when running at **2900 rpm** with a discharge of **25 L/s**. Calculate the head per stage and the specific speed of the pump. If the speed is reduced to **2600 rpm**, predict the new discharge, head, and power input assuming the same efficiency. The original shaft power was **24 kW**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Head per Stage:** $$H_{per\ stage} = \frac{H_{total}}{n} = \frac{84}{3} = 28\ \text{m per stage}$$ **Step 2 — Specific Speed:** Using the per-stage head: $$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H_{per\ stage}^{3/4}} = \frac{2900 \times \sqrt{0.025}}{(28)^{3/4}}$$ Compute the denominator: $28^{0.75} = 28^{3/4}$. Using logarithms: $\log(28) = 1.4472$, so $0.75 \times 1.4472 = 1.0854$, and $10^{1.0854} = 12.18$. $$N_s = \frac{2900 \times 0.1581}{12.18} = \frac{458.5}{12.18} = 37.6\ \text{(SI units)}$$ **Step 3 — Affinity Ratios for New Speed:** $$k_N = \frac{2600}{2900} = 0.8966$$ $$k_Q = 0.8966,\quad k_H = 0.8038,\quad k_P = 0.7206$$ **Step 4 — Predict New Operating Point:** $$Q_{new} = 0.8966 \times 25 = 22.42\ \text{L/s}$$ $$H_{new} = 0.8038 \times 84 = 67.52\ \text{m}$$ $$P_{new} = 0.7206 \times 24 = 17.29\ \text{kW}$$ **Step 5 — Verify Efficiency Preservation:** Original efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 84}{24000} = \frac{20596}{24000} = 0.858 = 85.8\%$$ Check at new speed: $$\eta_{new} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02242 \times 67.52}{17290} = \frac{14847}{17290} = 0.858 = 85.8\%\ \checkmark$$ > **Answer:** $H_{per\ stage} = 28$ m, $N_s = 37.6$, $Q_{new} = 22.42$ L/s, $H_{new} = 67.52$ m, $P_{new} = 17.29$ kW at 2600 rpm with efficiency 85.8%. --- **B. (b)** Explain in detail with a neat sketch the **variable speed test procedure** for a centrifugal pump. How are the experimentally measured curves at the new speed compared with the predicted curves from the affinity laws? Discuss three practical sources of deviation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Variable Speed Test Procedure (with sketch description):** **Apparatus:** Centrifugal pump test rig, variable frequency drive (VFD) on the driving motor, pressure and vacuum gauges at delivery and suction, a calibrated flow meter (venturi or orifice), a digital tachometer for speed, and a torque-meter / dynamometer between motor and pump. **Procedure:** 1. **Calibrate all instruments** (gauges, flow meter, tachometer, torque sensor) before the test. 2. **Prime the pump** and ensure the suction line is fully filled with water; the delivery valve is closed. 3. **Set speed** $N_1$ (e.g., 1450 rpm) via the VFD and confirm stable operation. 4. **Vary discharge** by gradually opening the delivery valve in 6 to 8 steps from shut-off ($Q=0$) to maximum $Q$. 5. **At each step**, record: $H$ (from $P_d$ and $P_v$ gauges), $Q$ (flow meter), $T$ (torque-meter), $N$ (tachometer), and calculate $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N T / 60$, and $\eta = \rho g Q H / P_{shaft}$. 6. **Repeat** the entire procedure at a second speed $N_2$ (e.g., 1750 rpm) and ideally a third $N_3$ (e.g., 1200 rpm) for full coverage. 7. **Plot the three sets of characteristic curves** (H–Q, η–Q, P–Q) — one for each $N$. **Step 2 — Comparison of Actual vs Predicted:** Using the data set at $N_1$, apply the affinity laws to compute the predicted curves at $N_2$ and $N_3$ as tabulated values. On the same graph: - Plot the **predicted** H–Q curve at $N_2$ as a **dashed line**. - Plot the **actual** H–Q curve at $N_2$ as a **solid line** with measured data points. - The **deviation** is the vertical (head) or horizontal (flow) gap between the two. **Step 3 — Three Sources of Deviation:** 1. **Cavitation:** At higher speeds, NPSH available may become less than NPSH required, causing vapour bubble formation and head collapse. This makes actual $H$ fall below predicted $H$ at high flows. 2. **Reynolds Number Effects:** Affinity laws assume the flow remains dynamically similar. The Reynolds number changes with $N$, so viscous losses (friction factor) change non-proportionally, especially in the laminar / transition regime. 3. **Mechanical and Volumetric Losses:** Bearing friction scales as $N^1$ (not $N^3$), and internal leakage (volumetric loss) scales with pressure differential $H$, producing efficiency variations not captured by the affinity laws. > **Conclusion:** The variable-speed test validates the affinity-law-based performance prediction. Good conformity (deviation <5%) confirms the pump operates with dynamically similar flow patterns; significant deviation indicates the pump is operating outside its design Reynolds regime or is cavitating. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 ESE:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing L/s with m³/s without conversion. Always convert to SI (m³/s) before applying $\rho g Q H$ formula. **1-mark deduction** per wrong unit. > > 2. **Forgetting Efficiency Preservation:** When applying affinity laws, $\eta$ is *preserved* (not $\eta \propto N$). Students who write $\eta_2 = (N_2/N_1) \eta_1$ will lose 2 marks. > > 3. **Multi-Stage Head Misinterpretation:** The specific-speed formula uses **per-stage head**, not total head. Using $H_{total}$ instead of $H_{per\ stage}$ will give a $n^{3/4}$-times wrong $N_s$ value. > > 4. **Predicting Power without Efficiency:** If the question gives $Q$ and $H$ and asks for power, you must compute water power first, divide by efficiency to get shaft power. Skipping the efficiency step loses 2 marks. > > 5. **Plotting on Wrong Axes:** Forgetting to mark BEP on the η–Q curve, not labeling axes with units, or not indicating the speed value on each curve set — each costs 1 mark. > > 6. **Head Calculation Error:** Forgetting to convert the gauge readings to absolute pressure (or omitting the velocity head and datum head terms). Use $H = (P_d - P_s)/\rho g + (V_d^2 - V_s^2)/2g + (z_d - z_s)$. > > 7. **Citing Affinity Laws Without Defining Geometric Similarity:** The laws apply *only* under geometric and kinematic similarity. State this precondition explicitly for full marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for PCNSL507 — Module Experiment 2:** - [ ] **Definition:** Performance test = experiment to determine H, P, η as functions of Q at a fixed N. - [ ] **Constant Speed Test:** Vary Q via delivery valve; plot H–Q (decreasing), η–Q (bell-shaped), P–Q (increasing). - [ ] **Variable Speed Test:** Repeat at multiple N; superimpose predicted curves from affinity laws. - [ ] **Affinity Law 1:** $Q \propto N$ — derived from $Q = \pi D_2 b_2 V_{f2} \propto D^2 N$. - [ ] **Affinity Law 2:** $H \propto N^2$ — derived from Euler's $H = U_2 V_{w2} / g$. - [ ] **Affinity Law 3:** $P \propto N^3$ — derived from $P = \rho g Q H \propto N \cdot N^2$. - [ ] **Efficiency Preserved:** $\eta_{new} \approx \eta_{old}$ (assumption of affinity laws). - [ ] **Multi-Stage Head:** $H_{total} = n \times H_{per\ stage}$; $Q$ is same through every stage. - [ ] **Specific Speed:** $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H_{per\ stage}^{3/4}$ — geometric classifier (radial < 50, mixed 50–150, axial > 150). - [ ] **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Peak of η–Q curve; design duty point. - [ ] **System Curve vs Pump Curve:** Operating point is the intersection of pump H–Q and system H–Q. - [ ] **NPSH:** Net Positive Suction Head — must exceed NPSH required to prevent cavitation. - [ ] **Shut-off Head:** $H$ at $Q = 0$; equals $H_{Euler}$ for a radial-flow pump. - [ ] **Maximum Discharge:** $Q$ at $H = 0$; theoretical limit, never actually reached. - [ ] **Deviation Metrics:** $\% \text{Deviation} = \vert H_{actual} - H_{predicted} \vert / H_{predicted} \times 100$. - [ ] **Geometric Similarity:** All linear dimensions scaled by the same ratio; all angles preserved — the precondition for the affinity laws. - [ ] **Kinematic Similarity:** Velocity ratios at corresponding points preserved — the dynamic consequence. - [ ] **Marine Context:** Centrifugal pumps onboard ships run at 1450 or 2950 rpm (50 Hz supply); speed variation only via VFD or pulley swap. - [ ] **Cubic Power Law:** A 10% speed increase → 33% power increase — important for marine energy management. - [ ] **Practical Use of Curves:** Select a pump whose H–Q curve passes through the required duty point with adequate margin and high efficiency. - [ ] **Lab Procedure (KTU Expected):** Tabulate readings → compute H, P, η → plot curves → identify BEP → apply affinity laws → comment on deviation. - [ ] **Units Standard:** Use $N$ in rpm, $Q$ in m³/s (convert L/s by ÷1000), $H$ in m, $P$ in kW, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². ---

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Self-priming, Airlift and Deep Well Pumps
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Performance Characteristic Tests on Pumps

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Performance Characteristic Tests** on pumps are standardized experimental procedures conducted in a hydraulic/marine engineering laboratory to determine the operational behaviour of a pump under varying load conditions. The tests quantify the relationship between **discharge (Q)**, **head (H)**, **shaft power (P)**, and **overall efficiency (η)** for self-priming, airlift, and deep well pumps used in marine and industrial applications.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme lab course **PCNSL507 – Marine Hydrodynamics and Hydraulics Lab**, Module Experiment 2 requires the student to plot the characteristic curves of these three pump types, calculate their efficiencies, and interpret the test results in accordance with **IS 1710:1989** and **ISO 9906:2012** standards for rotodynamic pump testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A pump is deemed to have passed its performance test only when its **actual duty point** lies within the tolerance band specified by the relevant BIS/ISO standard, and its **efficiency** at the design point is within **±3%** of the rated efficiency.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sipping a thick milkshake through a long straw. If you suck gently, the liquid rises slowly (low Q, low H needed). If you suck hard, the liquid rises faster, but your mouth muscles get tired (higher power consumption, lower efficiency at extremes). A **pump does exactly the same thing**: it lifts and moves fluid, and like your mouth, it has an "operating sweet spot" where it works the most efficiently.

- A **self-priming pump** is like a straw with a built-in "burp" mechanism — it can pull air out of its body to start working even when dry.
- An **airlift pump** is like blowing bubbles at the bottom of a fish tank to push water up — no moving parts touch the water.
- A **deep well pump** is like lowering a long straw deep into a well, with the "sucking" mechanism placed near the water surface inside the well.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Definitions)

The following **four fundamental performance parameters** are measured in every pump test:

- **Discharge (Q):** The volume of water delivered per unit time, measured in **L/s** or **m³/hr**.
- **Head (H):** The energy imparted per unit weight of fluid, measured in **metres of water column (m)**.
- **Shaft Power (SP):** The mechanical power supplied to the pump shaft, measured in **kilowatts (kW)**.
- **Overall Efficiency (η):** The ratio of water power output to shaft power input, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental relationship is:
> $$\text{Water Power} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \quad [\text{Watts}]$$
> where **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** (density of water) and **g = 9.81 m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity).

### 1.4 Why These Three Pump Types?

Marine vessels and offshore platforms require pumps that can handle **air-water mixtures**, operate with **large suction lifts**, and pump from **great depths**. Self-priming, airlift, and deep well pumps are specifically engineered for these challenging marine duty conditions — making their performance testing essential to the **PCNSL507** curriculum.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Self-Priming Pump — Operational Theory

A **self-priming pump** is a centrifugal pump with a special recirculation chamber that allows it to evacuate air from the suction line and prime itself without external assistance. After initial priming, it can re-prime automatically if air enters the casing.

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Initial Condition:** The pump casing is filled with a small amount of residual water (the "priming charge").
2. **Air Evacuation Phase:** On starting, the impeller throws the residual water outward, forming a **vortex ring** that creates a partial vacuum at the eye of the impeller.
3. **Air-Water Separation:** The recirculation chamber separates air from water. Air is vented out, and water returns to the impeller eye.
4. **Steady-State Pumping:** Once all air is removed, the suction pipe fills with water and normal centrifugal pumping begins.

### Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description |
|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| Discharge | $Q = A \cdot V$ | m³/s | Measured using a collecting tank or rotameter |
| Total Head | $H = H_d + H_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | m | Difference between delivery and suction gauges + velocity head |
| Shaft Power | $SP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | From torque and speed measurement |
| Pump Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\rho g Q H}{SP} \times 100$ | % | Ratio of water power to shaft power |

### Real-World Utility

Self-priming pumps are extensively used in **bilge pumping**, **fire-fighting systems**, and **deck wash-down** on ships because they can handle intermittent air ingress without losing prime.

## 2.2 Airlift Pump — Operational Theory

An **airlift pump** is a pneumatic pump with **no moving parts** in the fluid. It uses compressed air injected at the bottom of a submerged discharge pipe to lift water through a vertical riser. It is governed by the principle of **density reduction** of the air-water mixture.

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Submergence Setup:** The lower end of the riser pipe is submerged to a depth **$L_s$** (submergence) below the water surface.
2. **Air Injection:** Compressed air is injected through a nozzle at depth **$L_s$** from the surface.
3. **Density Reduction:** The air mixes with water, forming a **two-phase mixture** whose effective density is much lower than that of water.
4. **Buoyant Lift:** Due to hydrostatic pressure imbalance, the lighter column inside the riser is pushed upward by the heavier water column outside.
5. **Discharge:** The air-water mixture exits at the top, where air escapes and water is collected.

### Key Geometric Ratio

$$\text{Submergence Ratio} = \frac{L_s}{L_s + L_d}$$

where $L_d$ is the delivery length above water surface. Typical optimum value: **0.65 to 0.75**.

### Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description |
|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| Water Delivery | $Q_w = \frac{V_{tank}}{t}$ | m³/s | Measured by tank filling method |
| Air Consumption | $Q_a = \frac{V_{air}}{t}$ | m³/s | Measured by air rotameter or gas meter |
| Air-Water Ratio | $R = \frac{Q_a}{Q_w}$ | dimensionless | Efficiency indicator (lower is better) |
| Pumping Head | $H = L_s + L_d$ | m | Total vertical lift |
| Compressor Power | $P_c = p_a \cdot Q_a \cdot \ln\left(\frac{p_{in}}{p_{atm}}\right)$ | W | Isothermal compression power |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\rho g Q_w H}{P_c} \times 100$ | % | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The airlift pump efficiency is generally very low (**15–30%**), but it is preferred in marine applications for handling **corrosive fluids**, **slurries**, and **sewage** because it has no impeller to wear out.

## 2.3 Deep Well Pump — Operational Theory

A **deep well pump** is a multistage centrifugal pump designed with a **narrow-bore impeller stack** that can be lowered into a borehole of small diameter (typically **100 mm to 300 mm**). The motor is either submerged (submersible type) or placed at the top of the well with a long drive shaft (vertical turbine type).

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Installation:** The bowl assembly (impellers + diffusers) is lowered into the well below the static water level.
2. **Multi-Stage Pressure Build-up:** As water passes through successive impellers, pressure increases incrementally — each stage adds a fixed head (**stage head**).
3. **Discharge Through Column:** Water is pushed up through the vertical column pipe to the surface.
4. **Foot Valve Operation:** A foot valve at the suction strainer prevents back-flow, maintaining prime between starts.

### Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description |
|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| Total Head | $H = H_d + H_{static} + H_{friction}$ | m | Delivery head + static lift + pipe friction |
| Stage Head | $H_{stage} = \frac{H_{total}}{n_{stages}}$ | m | Per-stage contribution |
| Specific Speed | $N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | Used for pump classification |
| Pump Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\rho g Q H}{SP} \times 100$ | % | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** For the lab test, the deep well pump is typically tested in a **sump tank arrangement** where the suction lift is varied by adjusting the water level in the tank. The performance is plotted as **H vs Q** and **η vs Q** curves.

## 2.4 Complete KTU Formula Sheet

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Applicable To |
|---------|----------|---------|------|---------------|
| 1 | Discharge (volumetric) | $Q = \frac{V}{t}$ | m³/s | All pumps |
| 2 | Discharge (rotameter) | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | Self-priming |
| 3 | Total Manometric Head | $H_m = H_d + H_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | m | Self-priming, Deep well |
| 4 | Shaft Power (dynamometer) | $SP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | All pumps |
| 5 | Shaft Power (electrical) | $SP = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_m$ | W | All pumps |
| 6 | Water Power (Output) | $W_P = \rho g Q H$ | W | All pumps |
| 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \frac{W_P}{SP} \times 100$ | % | All pumps |
| 8 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \frac{H_m}{H_t}$ | % | Centrifugal types |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | % | All pumps |
| 10 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{W_P}{SP_{shaft - bearing}}$ | % | All pumps |
| 11 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | rpm·m units | All rotodynamic |
| 12 | Air-Water Ratio | $R = \frac{Q_{air}}{Q_{water}}$ | — | Airlift only |
| 13 | Submergence Ratio | $\sigma = \frac{L_s}{L_s + L_d}$ | — | Airlift only |
| 14 | Airlift Theoretical Head | $H_{th} = \frac{L_s (\rho_w - \rho_m)}{\rho_m}$ | m | Airlift only |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Manometric Head of a Self-Priming Pump

### Statement

Derive the expression for the **total manometric head** delivered by a centrifugal self-priming pump fitted with a suction and delivery gauge.

### Given

- Suction gauge reading: $P_s$ (N/m², vacuum gauge if below atmospheric)
- Delivery gauge reading: $P_d$ (N/m², pressure gauge)
- Height of suction gauge above pump centreline: $h_s$ (m)
- Height of delivery gauge above pump centreline: $h_d$ (m)
- Velocity at delivery nozzle: $V_d$ (m/s)
- Velocity at suction nozzle: $V_s$ (m/s)
- Density of fluid: $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

### Derivation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the **suction gauge tapping (S)** and the **delivery gauge tapping (D)**, taking the pump centreline as the datum.

$$
\frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + z_s + H_m = \frac{P_d}{\rho g} + \frac{V_d^2}{2g} + z_d
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This equation states that the total energy at the suction gauge plus the energy added by the pump ($H_m$) equals the total energy at the delivery gauge. We are equating energy per unit weight (head) on both sides.

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $H_m$:

$$
H_m = \left(\frac{P_d}{\rho g} - \frac{P_s}{\rho g}\right) + \left(\frac{V_d^2}{2g} - \frac{V_s^2}{2g}\right) + (z_d - z_s)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The total head is the sum of the **pressure head difference**, the **kinetic head difference**, and the **datum head difference** between the two gauge points.

**Step 3:** Substitute $z_d = h_d$ and $z_s = h_s$ (heights above the pump centreline):

$$
H_m = \left(\frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g}\right) + \left(\frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}\right) + (h_d - h_s)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This is the final working formula. In practice, when the suction gauge reads vacuum, $P_s$ is taken as a negative value, which automatically adds to the head value.

**Step 4:** If the suction and delivery pipe diameters are the **same**, then $V_d = V_s$ and the velocity head terms cancel, simplifying to:

$$
H_m = \left(\frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g}\right) + (h_d - h_s)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This simplification is used in most KTU lab setups where uniform pipe diameters are used between the pump and the gauges.

### Result

The final, exam-ready formula is:

$$
\boxed{H_m = \left(\frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g}\right) + (h_d - h_s) + \left(\frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}\right) \quad [\text{metres of fluid}]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Head of an Airlift Pump

### Statement

Derive the theoretical maximum head an airlift pump can deliver based on density reduction of the water column.

### Given

- Submergence depth of air injection: $L_s$ (m)
- Delivery height above water surface: $L_d$ (m)
- Density of water: $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m³
- Density of air-water mixture inside riser: $\rho_m$ (kg/m³)

### Derivation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Consider the hydrostatic pressure balance at the **bottom of the riser** (where air is injected).

The pressure at this point from **outside** the riser (pure water column):
$$
P_{outside} = P_{atm} + \rho_w g L_s
$$

The pressure at this point from **inside** the riser (air-water mixture):
$$
P_{inside} = P_{atm} + \rho_m g (L_s + L_d)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** At the injection point, the external water column of depth $L_s$ exerts a hydrostatic pressure, while the internal column of the lighter two-phase mixture of total height $(L_s + L_d)$ exerts a much smaller pressure.

**Step 2:** For the mixture to be lifted, the **external pressure must exceed the internal pressure** by an amount sufficient to overcome friction and acceleration:

$$
P_{outside} > P_{inside}
$$

**Step 3:** At the threshold of lifting (no flow condition):

$$
\rho_w g L_s = \rho_m g (L_s + L_d)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This is the static equilibrium condition where the upward buoyant force (from the heavier external water) is exactly balanced by the weight of the lighter internal column.

**Step 4:** Solve for the theoretical delivery head $L_d$:

$$
L_d = L_s \left(\frac{\rho_w - \rho_m}{\rho_m}\right)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The maximum possible delivery head is directly proportional to the submergence depth and the fractional reduction in mixture density. This explains why **greater submergence** yields **higher delivery**.

**Step 5:** The **actual head** is always less than this theoretical value due to friction, slippage of air bubbles, and acceleration losses:

$$
H_{actual} = L_d \cdot k
$$

where $k$ is a velocity coefficient typically between **0.6 and 0.85**.

### Result

$$
\boxed{H_{theoretical} = L_s \left(\frac{\rho_w - \rho_m}{\rho_m}\right) \quad [\text{metres of water}]}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

### Problem

A self-priming centrifugal pump delivers water under the following test conditions:

- Delivery gauge reading: $P_d = 1.6$ bar (gauge)
- Suction gauge reading: $P_s = 0.3$ bar (vacuum)
- Height difference between gauges: $h_d - h_s = 0.4$ m
- Delivery pipe diameter: 50 mm; Suction pipe diameter: 75 mm
- Measured discharge: $Q = 12$ L/s
- Brake power (input to shaft): 4.5 kW
- Pump speed: 1450 rpm

**Find:** (a) Total manometric head, (b) Water power, (c) Overall efficiency.

### Solution

**Given Data (in SI units):**
- $P_d = 1.6 \times 10^5$ N/m²
- $P_s = -0.3 \times 10^5$ N/m² (vacuum = negative gauge)
- $h_d - h_s = 0.4$ m
- $D_d = 0.050$ m; $D_s = 0.075$ m
- $Q = 12 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $SP = 4500$ W

**Step 1: Calculate velocities**

Discharge area at delivery:
$$
A_d = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Velocity at delivery:
$$
V_d = \frac{Q}{A_d} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.11 \text{ m/s}
$$

Discharge area at suction:
$$
A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Velocity at suction:
$$
V_s = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.72 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Manometric Head**

Using the derived formula:

$$
H_m = \left(\frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g}\right) + (h_d - h_s) + \left(\frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}\right)
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
H_m = \left(\frac{1.6 \times 10^5 - (-0.3 \times 10^5)}{1000 \times 9.81}\right) + 0.4 + \left(\frac{(6.11)^2 - (2.72)^2}{2 \times 9.81}\right)
$$

$$
H_m = \left(\frac{1.9 \times 10^5}{9810}\right) + 0.4 + \left(\frac{37.33 - 7.40}{19.62}\right)
$$

$$
H_m = 19.37 + 0.4 + 1.525
$$

$$
\boxed{H_m = 21.30 \text{ m of water}}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Water Power**

$$
W_P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_m
$$

$$
W_P = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 10^{-3} \times 21.30
$$

$$
\boxed{W_P = 2507 \text{ W} \approx 2.51 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Overall Efficiency**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{W_P}{SP} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_o = \frac{2507}{4500} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_o = 55.7\%}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pump Comparison Summary

| Feature | Self-Priming Pump | Airlift Pump | Deep Well Pump |
|---------|------------------|--------------|----------------|
| Moving Parts in Fluid | Yes (impeller) | **No (none)** | Yes (multi-impeller) |
| Max Suction Lift | Up to **8 m** | Limited by submergence | **> 50 m** |
| Typical Efficiency | **50–70%** | **15–30%** | **65–80%** |
| Best Suited For | Bilge, fire-fighting | Corrosive, slurry | Borehole, deep sea |
| Priming Required | **Self** (recirculation) | Not required | Foot valve |
| Characteristic Curve Type | H vs Q (falling) | H vs Q (rising plateau) | H vs Q (steep falling) |
| Marine Application | Deck wash, bilge | Ballast, sewage | Cooling water intake |
| Maintenance Need | High (mechanical seal) | **Very low** | Moderate (long shaft) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Self-Priming Pump
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**Q:** Define a self-priming pump. List **two** advantages of using it in marine applications.

**Model Answer:**

A **self-priming pump** is a centrifugal pump equipped with a recirculation chamber that enables it to evacuate air from the suction line and prime itself automatically without the need for external priming.

**Two marine advantages:**
1. Can re-prime itself if air enters the casing (important in **bilge** and **fire-fighting** systems where air pockets are common).
2. Eliminates the need for a separate **foot valve** and **priming tank**, saving space and reducing maintenance in ship engine rooms.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for each advantage)*

---

### Question 2: Airlift Pump Principle
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q:** State the working principle of an airlift pump. What is meant by **submergence ratio**?

**Model Answer:**

An **airlift pump** operates on the principle of **density reduction of a fluid column**. Compressed air injected at the bottom of a submerged riser mixes with water, creating a lighter air-water mixture. The hydrostatic pressure imbalance between the heavier water column outside and the lighter mixture inside pushes the mixture upward, delivering water at the top.

**Submergence Ratio** is defined as the ratio of the depth of air injection below water surface ($L_s$) to the total vertical length of the riser ($L_s + L_d$):
$$
\sigma = \frac{L_s}{L_s + L_d}
$$

An optimum submergence ratio of **0.65 to 0.75** gives maximum delivery.

*(3 marks: 2 for principle, 1 for submergence ratio)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**Q:** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **self-priming centrifugal pump**. Discuss its characteristic curves.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction and Working

**Construction:**

A self-priming pump consists of:
1. **Casing** — Volute-type with an enlarged recirculation chamber.
2. **Impeller** — Semi-open or enclosed type, mounted on the shaft.
3. **Recirculation Chamber** — A side chamber that traps water and separates air.
4. **Air Vent Valve** — Releases air from the top of the chamber to atmosphere.
5. **Suction and Delivery Nozzles** — With isolation valves and gauge tappings.
6. **Shaft and Bearings** — Supported in oil-bath bearings.
7. **Mechanical Seal** — Prevents leakage along the shaft.

**Working:**

- On the **first start**, a priming charge of water is poured into the casing.
- As the impeller rotates, the water is thrown outward by centrifugal force, creating a **vortex** that entraps air from the suction line.
- The air-water mixture enters the recirculation chamber, where water (heavier) settles back and air (lighter) rises to the vent and is expelled.
- The water returning to the impeller eye continues to remove air until the suction pipe is fully primed.
- Once primed, the pump operates as a normal centrifugal pump delivering water to the delivery pipe.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Characteristic Curves

The three standard characteristic curves plotted from test data are:

1. **H vs Q Curve (Head-Delivery Curve):** Head decreases as discharge increases. The curve is **parabolic with a falling trend**.

2. **SP vs Q Curve (Power-Delivery Curve):** Shaft power increases as discharge increases. The curve is **rising, often nearly linear**.

3. **η vs Q Curve (Efficiency-Delivery Curve):** Efficiency rises to a maximum (Best Efficiency Point, BEP) and then falls. The curve is **parabolic with a peak**.

**Significance:** The BEP represents the most economical operating point. KTU evaluation expects the student to identify the BEP from the curves and comment on whether it matches the rated duty point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q:** A self-priming pump test gave the following readings at a constant speed of 1450 rpm:

| Trial | Delivery Gauge (bar) | Suction Gauge (bar) | Discharge (L/s) | Shaft Power (W) |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | 1.8 | 0.20 (vac) | 0.0 | 2400 |
| 2 | 1.7 | 0.18 (vac) | 4.0 | 3050 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 0.15 (vac) | 8.0 | 3700 |
| 4 | 1.2 | 0.10 (vac) | 12.0 | 4400 |
| 5 | 0.8 | 0.05 (vac) | 15.0 | 5050 |

The height difference between gauges is 0.5 m. Delivery pipe diameter = 50 mm; Suction pipe diameter = 75 mm. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

Calculate for **each trial**:
#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Total Manometric Head $H_m$ and Discharge $Q$

**Velocity Calculations (constant for all trials):**

Delivery area:
$$
A_d = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Suction area:
$$
A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Velocity head difference (constant term):
$$
\Delta h_v = \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}
$$

For trial 1 (Q = 0): $V_d = V_s = 0$, so $\Delta h_v = 0$.

For Q = 4 L/s = 0.004 m³/s:
$$
V_d = \frac{0.004}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.04 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
V_s = \frac{0.004}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.905 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\Delta h_v = \frac{(2.04)^2 - (0.905)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{4.16 - 0.819}{19.62} = 0.170 \text{ m}
$$

**Sample Calculation for Trial 2 (Q = 4 L/s):**

Pressure head difference:
$$
\frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g} = \frac{(1.7 - (-0.18)) \times 10^5}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{1.88 \times 10^5}{9810} = 19.16 \text{ m}
$$

Total head:
$$
H_m = 19.16 + 0.5 + 0.17 = 19.83 \text{ m}
$$

**Complete Tabulated Results:**

| Trial | Q (L/s) | $H_m$ (m) | $\Delta h_v$ (m) |
|-------|---------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | 0.0 | 22.96 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 19.83 | 0.17 |
| 3 | 8.0 | 16.55 | 0.54 |
| 4 | 12.0 | 13.33 | 1.21 |
| 5 | 15.0 | 8.85 | 2.07 |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Pump Efficiency η for Each Trial

Efficiency formula:
$$
\eta = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{SP} \times 100
$$

**Trial 1:** Q = 0, so $W_P = 0$, $\eta_1 = 0\%$.

**Trial 2:** 
$$
\eta_2 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.004 \times 19.83}{3050} \times 100 = 25.5\%
$$

**Trial 3:** 
$$
\eta_3 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.008 \times 16.55}{3700} \times 100 = 35.1\%
$$

**Trial 4:** 
$$
\eta_4 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 13.33}{4400} \times 100 = 35.6\%
$$

**Trial 5:** 
$$
\eta_5 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 8.85}{5050} \times 100 = 25.8\%
$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Trial | Q (L/s) | $H_m$ (m) | SP (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|-------|---------|-----------|--------|------------|
| 1 | 0.0 | 22.96 | 2400 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 19.83 | 3050 | 25.5 |
| 3 | 8.0 | 16.55 | 3700 | 35.1 |
| 4 | 12.0 | 13.33 | 4400 | **35.6 (BEP)** |
| 5 | 15.0 | 8.85 | 5050 | 25.8 |

**Plotting:** On graph paper, plot:
- $H_m$ (y-axis) vs Q (x-axis) → **Falling parabolic curve**
- SP (y-axis) vs Q (x-axis) → **Rising linear curve**
- η (y-axis) vs Q (x-axis) → **Inverted parabolic curve with peak at Q = 12 L/s**

**Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Q ≈ 12 L/s, H ≈ 13.3 m, η ≈ 35.6%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the **velocity head correction term** $(V_d^2 - V_s^2)/2g$ when pipe diameters differ. Marks lost: 1–2 per trial.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using **gauge pressure** (not absolute) for the suction side when it's a vacuum gauge. Always assign a **negative sign** to vacuum readings. Marks lost: 1.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Computing $W_P$ in **W** but $SP$ in **kW** without unit conversion. Marks lost: 1.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to label axes, units, and BEP on the characteristic curves. Marks lost: 1–2.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Reporting Q in **L/s** in the table but plotting it as **m³/s** on the graph. Always be consistent. Marks lost: 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### Self-Priming Pump Key Points
- Recirculation chamber is the **defining feature** — it differentiates it from a standard centrifugal pump.
- Has a **priming charge** requirement only on first start; **re-primes automatically**.
- Ideal for **intermittent duty** and **air-handling** marine applications.
- Characteristic: H vs Q is **falling**; SP vs Q is **rising**; η vs Q peaks at BEP.

### Airlift Pump Key Points
- **No moving parts** in contact with the fluid → ideal for **corrosive** and **abrasive** fluids.
- Performance depends on **submergence ratio** ($\sigma$): optimum = **0.65 to 0.75**.
- **Air-water ratio** $R$ is the key efficiency indicator (lower = better).
- Theoretical head: $H_{th} = L_s (\rho_w - \rho_m)/\rho_m$.
- Efficiency is inherently low (**15–30%**), but **maintenance cost is also low**.

### Deep Well Pump Key Points
- **Multi-stage** construction — each stage adds a fixed head.
- **Specific speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ is used to classify the impeller.
- Suitable for **high head, low discharge** applications.
- **Submersible** or **vertical turbine** types — both need careful alignment.

### Universal Test Formulas (MUST memorize)
- Discharge: $Q = V/t$ (volumetric method) or $Q = AV$ (velocity method)
- Total Head: $H_m = (P_d - P_s)/(\rho g) + (h_d - h_s) + (V_d^2 - V_s^2)/(2g)$
- Shaft Power: $SP = 2\pi NT/60$ (mechanical) or $SP = \sqrt{3} VI \cos\phi \eta_m$ (electrical)
- Water Power: $W_P = \rho g Q H_m$
- Overall Efficiency: $\eta_o = (W_P/SP) \times 100$

### Characteristic Curve Plotting Rules
- Always plot **Q on x-axis** (independent variable) and **H, SP, η on y-axis**.
- Use the **same Q scale** for all three curves.
- Smooth the curve — do not connect points with straight zig-zag lines.
- Clearly mark the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** on the η vs Q curve.
- Mention the **rated duty point** and compare with BEP for completeness.

### KTU Practical Record Submission Checklist
- Title page with **course code, experiment number, student name, date**.
- **Aim** and **apparatus used** with specifications.
- **Theoretical background** (with the derivation of $H_m$).
- **Procedure** in numbered steps.
- **Observation table** with units in headers.
- **Sample calculation** for one trial (showing all steps).
- **Graph sheets** with characteristic curves.
- **Result** section with BEP, rated efficiency, and conclusion.
- **Sources of error** and **precautions** taken.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCNSL507 Marine Hydrodynamics and Hydraulics Lab**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Hydraulic Ram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Hydraulic Ram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — PERFORMANCE CHARACTERISTIC TESTS ON HYDRAULIC RAM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Ram (Hydram):** A self-actuating, reciprocating, displacement-type hydraulic machine that utilizes the **kinetic energy** of a large quantity of water descending from a modest height (supply head) to lift a smaller fraction of that water to a substantially greater height (delivery head). It operates without any external prime mover — the motive fluid itself is the power source.

A hydraulic ram belongs to the class of **fluid-power conversion devices** and is governed by the principles of **momentum transfer**, **water hammer**, and **inertial energy storage**. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, the lab experiment is designed to determine the **performance characteristics** by varying the delivery head at a constant supply head, and conversely.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Notation)

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $H_s$ | Static supply head (height of supply reservoir above ram) | m |
| $H_d$ | Delivery head (vertical lift to delivery tank) | m |
| $h$ | Height of waste valve above supply level | m |
| $Q_s$ | Supply discharge (input flow rate) | m³/s or L/s |
| $Q_d$ | Delivery discharge (useful flow lifted) | m³/s or L/s |
| $Q_w$ | Waste discharge (flow over waste valve) | m³/s or L/s |
| $N$ | Number of beats per minute (operating frequency) | beats/min |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Hydraulic Ram like this:** Imagine you have a small hill with a stream flowing over it. You cannot pump that water uphill to your house because there's no electricity nearby. The hydraulic ram is a clever "automatic water elevator" that uses the **momentum** of the descending water itself as the energy source. As the falling water gains speed, it suddenly slams a valve shut — this creates a pressure surge (called **water hammer**) that pushes a small portion of water up a much taller pipe. The rest spills out as waste. It's like a kid on a swing: a small push from a steady rhythm (waste water) keeps the swing (delivery) going higher than the starting point.

The phenomenon of pressure surge that drives the ram is essentially the **water-hammer effect** described by the Joukowsky equation. This is why hydrams are deployed in remote, off-grid locations — particularly relevant in marine coastal villages, hill stations, and remote harbours where the KTU 2024 scheme places strong engineering focus.

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Coastal village water supply** — Kerala backwater regions.
- **Marine craft drinking water** — small boats using ram pumps from shore streams.
- **Hill irrigation schemes** — Western Ghats plantations.
- **Off-grid farm water delivery** — sustainable development (SDG 6 alignment).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Cycle (Phases)

The hydraulic ram operates through a self-sustaining **cyclic process**:

1. **Acceleration Phase:** The waste (impulse) valve is open. Supply water $Q_s$ accelerates under gravity through the drive pipe, gaining kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$.
2. **Closure Phase:** When the velocity exceeds a critical threshold, the waste valve snaps shut due to back-pressure / inertial drag.
3. **Pressure Surge Phase:** Sudden closure produces a **water-hammer pressure rise** $\Delta p = \rho \, a \, \Delta v$, where $a$ is the celerity of the pressure wave. This forces the **delivery (check) valve** open.
4. **Delivery Phase:** A fraction of water $Q_d$ is pushed into the delivery pipe and up to the delivery tank against head $H_d$.
5. **Recoil & Re-Opening:** Pressure decays, the delivery valve closes (check valve is unidirectional), and the waste valve re-opens due to its own weight. The cycle restarts.

> [!NOTE]
> The **beat frequency** $N$ (typically 30–90 beats/min for small hydrams) depends on the supply head and the inertial mass of the waste valve mechanism.

## 2.2 Continuity (Conservation of Mass)

$$Q_s = Q_d + Q_w$$

This is the fundamental mass balance applied at the ram body. Both $Q_d$ and $Q_w$ can be measured volumetrically (measuring tank + stopwatch), and $Q_s$ is verified as their sum.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all** formulas that examiners expect in calculations. Memorise the boxed relations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Physical Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mass continuity | $Q_s = Q_d + Q_w$ | Supply splits into useful + waste |
| 2 | D'Aubuisson's efficiency | $\eta_D = \dfrac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{Q_s \cdot H_s}$ | Overall hydraulic efficiency (output power ÷ input power) |
| 3 | Rankine's efficiency | $\eta_R = \dfrac{H_d}{H_s} \cdot \dfrac{1 - (Q_d/Q_s)}{1 + (Q_d/Q_s)}$ | Internal efficiency accounting for waste losses |
| 4 | Hydraulic power input | $P_{in} = \rho \, g \, Q_s \, H_s$ | Power available from supply head |
| 5 | Hydraulic power output | $P_{out} = \rho \, g \, Q_d \, H_d$ | Useful power lifted |
| 6 | Waste power | $P_w = \rho \, g \, Q_w \cdot h$ | Power lost through waste valve (where $h$ is waste head) |
| 7 | Water-hammer pressure rise | $\Delta p = \rho \, a \, \Delta v$ | Joukowsky surge pressure |
| 8 | Pressure-wave celerity | $a = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + (K/E)(D/t)}}$ | For elastic pipe (Mohan's equation) |
| 9 | Flow ratio | $r = Q_d / Q_s$ | Dimensionless delivery fraction |
| 10 | Head ratio | $R_H = H_d / H_s$ | Dimensionless lift ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU BOARD TIP:** The constant $\rho g$ cancels out in efficiency ratios. Therefore, **you do NOT need to use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ or $g = 9.81$ m/s² in efficiency calculations** — examiners will not award extra marks for including them, but forgetting them is harmless. Just work in consistent head units (m) and discharge units (L/s or m³/s).

## 2.4 Typical Performance Trend (What the Curves Should Look Like)

When plotting experimental results, KTU expects the following qualitative behaviour:

- As $H_d$ increases at constant $H_s$ → $Q_d$ **decreases** monotonically.
- As $H_d$ increases → $\eta_D$ first **increases**, attains a **maximum**, then **decreases**.
- The **maximum efficiency point** is the **design operating point** of the ram.
- Rankine's efficiency $\eta_R$ is always numerically **less than** D'Aubuisson's efficiency for a real machine.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

In marine and coastal engineering contexts, hydraulic rams are deployed where:
- **No external power** is available (offshore platforms, remote lighthouses).
- The supply water source is **renewable** (spring, stream).
- The required **lift ratio $H_d/H_s > 5$** (most pumps cannot match this economically).

The performance test quantifies the trade-off between **delivery lift** and **delivery quantity**, allowing optimum sizing of the ram for a given site.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of D'Aubuisson's Efficiency

**Starting premise:** Hydraulic efficiency is the ratio of useful power output to total hydraulic power input.

**Step 1 — Express input power.**
The supply water at discharge $Q_s$ falls from height $H_s$, releasing power per unit time:

$$P_{in} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_s \cdot H_s$$

Here, $\rho$ is the mass density of water (kg/m³), $g$ is gravitational acceleration (m/s²), $Q_s$ is volumetric flow (m³/s), and $H_s$ is the supply head (m). The product $\rho g Q H$ has SI units of Watts.

**Step 2 — Express output (useful) power.**
A smaller discharge $Q_d$ is lifted to a greater head $H_d$:

$$P_{out} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_d \cdot H_d$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**
By definition, D'Aubuisson's efficiency is:

$$\eta_D = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\rho \, g \, Q_d \, H_d}{\rho \, g \, Q_s \, H_s}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel common factors.**

$$\boxed{\eta_D = \frac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{Q_s \cdot H_s}}$$

The constants $\rho$ and $g$ cancel identically, giving a dimensionless efficiency. This is the form used in **all** KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of Rankine's Efficiency

**Starting premise:** Rankine recognized that a portion of the input power is *wasted* as kinetic energy in the jet escaping through the waste valve. The effective efficiency must subtract this loss.

**Step 1 — Write the waste-flow energy per cycle.**
The waste water leaves the ram with the supply head $H_s$ effectively still available (it has not been converted to delivery head). So wasted power per unit time is:

$$P_{waste} = \rho \, g \, Q_w \cdot H_s$$

**Step 2 — Net effective input power.**
The truly "useful" input (excluding the fraction that inevitably becomes waste) is:

$$P_{net} = P_{in} - P_{waste} = \rho \, g \, (Q_s - Q_w) \cdot H_s$$

**Step 3 — Substitute continuity $Q_w = Q_s - Q_d$.**

$$P_{net} = \rho \, g \, (Q_s - Q_s + Q_d) \cdot H_s = \rho \, g \, Q_d \cdot H_s$$

**Step 4 — Form Rankine's ratio.**

$$\eta_R = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{net}} = \frac{\rho \, g \, Q_d \, H_d}{\rho \, g \, Q_d \, H_s} = \frac{H_d}{H_s}$$

But this is **not** the complete Rankine formula — the standard form introduces the flow ratio correction:

$$\boxed{\eta_R = \frac{H_d}{H_s} \cdot \frac{1 - (Q_d/Q_s)}{1 + (Q_d/Q_s)}}$$

The factor $\dfrac{1 - r}{1 + r}$ (where $r = Q_d/Q_s$) corrects for the fact that during each cycle, both supply **and** waste streams experience acceleration losses — the denominator $(1 + Q_d/Q_s)$ accounts for this symmetric dissipation. The student should memorise this boxed form as the KTU board answer.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical 14-Mark Question Style):**
> During a hydraulic ram test, the following readings are recorded at steady state:
> - Supply head $H_s = 4.0$ m
> - Delivery head $H_d = 24.0$ m
> - Delivery collected in 60 s = 36 L
> - Waste collected in 60 s = 144 L
>
> Determine: (a) D'Aubuisson's efficiency (b) Rankine's efficiency (c) Whether the ram is operating at near-optimum conditions.

### Solution

**Part (a): Discharge computations.**

$$Q_d = \frac{36 \text{ L}}{60 \text{ s}} = 0.6 \text{ L/s} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_w = \frac{144 \text{ L}}{60 \text{ s}} = 2.4 \text{ L/s} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_s = Q_d + Q_w = 0.6 + 2.4 = 3.0 \text{ L/s}$$
