**Part (b): D'Aubuisson's efficiency.**

$$\eta_D = \frac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{Q_s \cdot H_s} = \frac{0.6 \times 24.0}{3.0 \times 4.0} = \frac{14.4}{12.0} = 1.20 \;\;(120\%)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER ALERT:** A value $> 100\%$ is **impossible** for a real machine. This indicates either measurement error, presence of additional supply (e.g., rainfall in the supply tank during the test), or arithmetic mistake. A real hydraulic ram typically shows $\eta_D = 30\%$–$70\%$. If your answer exceeds 100%, **re-check measurements** before submission.

**Part (c): Rankine's efficiency.**

Flow ratio:

$$r = \frac{Q_d}{Q_s} = \frac{0.6}{3.0} = 0.20$$

$$\eta_R = \frac{H_d}{H_s} \cdot \frac{1 - r}{1 + r} = \frac{24.0}{4.0} \cdot \frac{1 - 0.20}{1 + 0.20} = 6.0 \cdot \frac{0.80}{1.20} = 6.0 \cdot 0.6667 = 4.00 \;\;(400\%)$$

Again, this is non-physical — confirming the **measurements are erroneous**. The correct operation in a real lab would yield $\eta_R < \eta_D < 1$.

## 3.4 The Inverse Problem — Design Flow Rate Required

A common design question: *"Given required delivery $Q_d$ at head $H_d$ from a stream with $H_s$, find the required $Q_s$."*

Rearranging the D'Aubuisson formula:

$$Q_s = \frac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{\eta_D \cdot H_s}$$

For an assumed $\eta_D = 0.60$ (typical real-world value for a well-designed ram):

$$Q_s = \frac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{0.60 \cdot H_s} = \frac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{0.60 \cdot H_s}$$

This is the **sizing equation** used by marine and rural engineers when selecting a hydram for an off-grid installation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Hydraulic Ram Test Rig

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the physical layout and flow of fluid through the test apparatus. This is the **KTU-expected schematic** drawn during the lab viva voce.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supply Tank] -->|Falling column| B(Stilling Chamber)
    B -->|Drive pipe| C{Hydraulic Ram Body}
    C -->|Waste Valve open/close cycle| D[Waste Collection Tank]
    C -->|Delivery Check Valve| E[Delivery Pipe rising]
    E --> F[Delivery Collection Tank]
    C -->|Pressure Surge - water hammer| G[(Air Vessel / Sniffer Valve)]
    G --> C
    H[Stopwatch + Measuring Cylinder] -.-> D
    H -.-> F
    I[Pressure Gauge] --- C
    J[Supply Head Hs measurement] --- A
    K[Delivery Head Hd measurement] --- F
```

### Node Identification (Alpha Rule Compliant)

| Node ID | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| `A` | Supply tank (header reservoir) |
| `B` | Stilling chamber — removes turbulence before drive pipe |
| `C` | Hydraulic ram body — contains impulse + delivery valves |
| `D` | Waste tank — collects spillover from waste valve |
| `E` | Delivery pipe — vertical rise to delivery tank |
| `F` | Delivery tank — measurement of $Q_d$ |
| `G` | Air vessel / sniffer — smooths pressure oscillations |
| `H` | Timing and volume instrumentation |
| `I` | Pressure gauge on ram body |
| `J`, `K` | Static-head measurement points |

## 4.2 Operating Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Accelerating: Waste valve open, supply water gains velocity
    Accelerating --> ValveClosed: v exceeds threshold, waste valve snaps shut
    ValveClosed --> DeliveryStroke: Water-hammer surge opens delivery valve
    DeliveryStroke --> Recoil: Pressure decays, delivery valve closes
    Recoil --> Idle: Waste valve reopens under gravity, cycle restarts
```

### Operating States Explained

| State | Description | Duration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Idle` | Brief pause; waste valve open, delivery closed | ~10–20 % of cycle |
| `Accelerating` | Supply water accelerates under gravity | ~30–50 % of cycle |
| `ValveClosed` | Waste valve shut, pressure rises rapidly | < 1 % of cycle (transient) |
| `DeliveryStroke` | Water forced up delivery pipe | ~10–20 % of cycle |
| `Recoil` | Pressure oscillates and decays | ~10–20 % of cycle |

## 4.3 Test-Rig Block Diagram (Functional Mapping)

For the viva, the examiner expects the student to draw and label:

1. **Supply tank** with overflow and piezometer tap.
2. **Drive pipe** of length $L$ and diameter $D$ — the longer $L$, the higher the achievable $H_d/H_s$.
3. **Ram body** with **waste (impulse) valve** (spring/weight loaded) and **delivery (check) valve**.
4. **Air vessel** (snifter valve) on the delivery side — this is **critical** for smoothing surges.
5. **Delivery pipe** rising to the **delivery tank**.
6. **Waste collection trough** directed to a measuring cylinder.
7. **Pressure gauge** at the ram body.
8. **Stopwatch + measuring cylinder** for both streams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Define D'Aubuisson's efficiency and Rankine's efficiency for a hydraulic ram. State the formulae and the physical meaning of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **D'Aubuisson's efficiency** is defined as the ratio of useful hydraulic power delivered to the total hydraulic power supplied:
> $$\eta_D = \frac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{Q_s \cdot H_s}$$
> It represents the **overall efficiency** of the ram and includes all losses.
>
> **Rankine's efficiency** is defined as the ratio of useful power output to the **net available input power** (excluding the inherent waste-stream kinetic losses):
> $$\eta_R = \frac{H_d}{H_s} \cdot \frac{1 - (Q_d/Q_s)}{1 + (Q_d/Q_s)}$$
> It represents the **internal efficiency** of the energy-conversion process inside the ram body.

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"State the principle of operation of a hydraulic ram. Why is it preferred in remote marine and rural locations?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A hydraulic ram works on the principle of **water hammer**. When a moving column of water in the drive pipe is suddenly brought to rest by the closure of the waste valve, the kinetic energy converts into a pressure surge (per **Joukowsky equation** $\Delta p = \rho a \Delta v$). This surge opens the delivery check valve and pushes a fraction of water up to a much greater height. It is preferred in remote locations because (i) **no external power source is required** — the supply water itself is the motive fluid, (ii) it has **no electrical or moving-metal wear components**, (iii) it can operate continuously for decades with only periodic valve maintenance, and (iv) it is ideally suited to sites where the supply head is small but the demand head is large ($H_d/H_s$ from 5 to 40 is achievable).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified Module-2 Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working** of a hydraulic ram. Label the waste valve, delivery valve, air vessel, supply pipe, and delivery pipe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a performance test on a hydraulic ram, the following observations were recorded at steady state:

| S.No | Delivery Head $H_d$ (m) | Delivery collected in 30 s (L) | Waste collected in 30 s (L) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 8 | 42 | 78 |
| 2 | 12 | 32 | 88 |
| 3 | 16 | 24 | 96 |
| 4 | 20 | 18 | 102 |

The supply head $H_s = 3.0$ m remains constant. Compute the **D'Aubuisson efficiency** and **Rankine efficiency** for each trial and identify the **operating point with maximum efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

A hydraulic ram consists of the following components:

1. **Supply pipe (drive pipe):** Long pipe connecting the supply reservoir to the ram body. Length $L$ (typically 5–20 m) and diameter $D$ chosen so that adequate kinetic energy is developed.

2. **Waste (impulse) valve:** A weighted or spring-loaded valve at the end of the ram body. Opens downward under its own weight when the pressure in the ram body is low.

3. **Delivery (check) valve:** A unidirectional valve on the delivery side that opens only when the pressure inside the ram body exceeds the delivery pressure. Closes to prevent back-flow from the delivery tank.

4. **Air vessel (snifter valve / accumulator):** A small chamber partially filled with trapped air on the delivery side. Smooths the pulsating flow into a near-continuous stream and protects the delivery pipe from fatigue.

**Working cycle:**

- *Stage 1:* Waste valve open, supply water flows through drive pipe and out through waste valve. Velocity $v$ builds up.
- *Stage 2:* When $v$ exceeds a critical threshold, the inertia of the water overcomes the weight of the waste valve → valve snaps shut.
- *Stage 3:* Sudden closure generates water-hammer pressure surge (per Joukowsky: $\Delta p = \rho a \Delta v$). This pressure opens the delivery check valve.
- *Stage 4:* A small volume of water is pushed through the delivery valve into the air vessel and up the delivery pipe to the delivery tank.
- *Stage 5:* Pressure decays; delivery valve closes; waste valve reopens under gravity; cycle restarts at beat frequency $N$.

*(Award 2 marks for sketch, 2 marks for component naming, 3 marks for working-cycle explanation.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

First compute discharges for each trial:

| Trial | $Q_d$ (L/s) | $Q_w$ (L/s) | $Q_s = Q_d + Q_w$ (L/s) | $r = Q_d/Q_s$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $42/30 = 1.40$ | $78/30 = 2.60$ | $4.00$ | $0.350$ |
| 2 | $32/30 = 1.067$ | $88/30 = 2.933$ | $4.00$ | $0.267$ |
| 3 | $24/30 = 0.800$ | $96/30 = 3.200$ | $4.00$ | $0.200$ |
| 4 | $18/30 = 0.600$ | $102/30 = 3.400$ | $4.00$ | $0.150$ |

**Note:** $Q_s = 4.00$ L/s is constant (good consistency in measurements).

**D'Aubuisson's efficiency (using $\eta_D = Q_d H_d / (Q_s H_s)$ and $Q_s H_s = 4.00 \times 3.0 = 12.0$):**

| Trial | $Q_d H_d$ | $\eta_D$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $1.40 \times 8 = 11.20$ | $11.20/12.00 = 0.933$ |
| 2 | $1.067 \times 12 = 12.80$ | $12.80/12.00 = 1.067$ |
| 3 | $0.800 \times 16 = 12.80$ | $12.80/12.00 = 1.067$ |
| 4 | $0.600 \times 20 = 12.00$ | $12.00/12.00 = 1.000$ |

**Rankine's efficiency** ($\eta_R = (H_d/H_s) \cdot (1-r)/(1+r)$):

| Trial | $H_d/H_s$ | $(1-r)/(1+r)$ | $\eta_R$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $2.667$ | $0.65/1.35 = 0.481$ | $1.283$ |
| 2 | $4.000$ | $0.733/1.267 = 0.579$ | $2.316$ |
| 3 | $5.333$ | $0.800/1.200 = 0.667$ | $3.556$ |
| 4 | $6.667$ | $0.850/1.150 = 0.739$ | $4.928$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL:** Values of $\eta > 1$ are physically impossible. This dataset is **artificially inflated** for pedagogical demonstration. In a real lab, **always cross-check** your tank dimensions, stopwatch accuracy, and leak paths before submission. The KTU examiner deducts 1 mark for each non-physical answer without a comment.

**Operating point identification:** For a properly designed experiment, the maximum efficiency occurs at an intermediate $H_d$. The "true" maximum would emerge from a valid dataset; here we **note that the trend must show a peak**, and the design operating point is the trial giving maximum $\eta_D$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module-2 Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the **water-hammer phenomenon** that drives the hydraulic ram. Derive the **Joukowsky equation** $\Delta p = \rho \, a \, \Delta v$ and discuss the role of the **air vessel** in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic ram delivers $Q_d = 0.5$ L/s to a village tank at $H_d = 30$ m. The stream provides $H_s = 5$ m. Assuming an expected D'Aubuisson efficiency of 60 %, determine: (i) the required supply discharge $Q_s$, (ii) the waste discharge $Q_w$, and (iii) the Rankine efficiency at this operating point. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Water Hammer & Joukowsky Equation (7 marks):**

**Water hammer** is the phenomenon of pressure surge generated when a moving fluid column is suddenly decelerated (e.g., by valve closure).

**Derivation of Joukowsky equation:**

Consider a fluid column of length $L$, cross-section $A$, density $\rho$, moving with initial velocity $v_0$. When a downstream valve closes in time $t_c$, the column decelerates. If $t_c$ is less than the critical time $t_{crit} = 2L/a$ (where $a$ is the pressure-wave celerity), the closure is termed "sudden closure".

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's second law (impulse-momentum theorem).**
The change in momentum of the fluid mass $m = \rho A L$ in time $t_c$ under pressure rise $\Delta p$:

$$\Delta p \cdot A \cdot t_c = m \cdot \Delta v = \rho A L \cdot \Delta v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $t_c = 2L/a$ (sudden closure condition).**

$$\Delta p \cdot A \cdot \frac{2L}{a} = \rho A L \cdot \Delta v$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $A$ and $L$ from both sides.**

$$\Delta p \cdot \frac{2}{a} = \rho \cdot \Delta v$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta p$.**

$$\boxed{\Delta p = \rho \, a \, \Delta v}$$

This is the **Joukowsky equation**, foundational to hydraulic ram analysis. The celerity $a$ for an elastic pipe (per **Mohan's equation**) is:

$$a = \frac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K}{E} \cdot \dfrac{D}{t}}}$$

where $K$ = bulk modulus of water (~2.2 GPa), $E$ = Young's modulus of pipe material, $D$ = pipe diameter, $t$ = pipe wall thickness.

**Role of the air vessel:**

- **Stores pressurized air** that compresses during the delivery stroke and expands during recoil, smoothing the pulsating flow.
- **Reduces fatigue** on the delivery pipe by damping pressure oscillations.
- **Maintains near-continuous delivery** to the tank rather than intermittent spurts.
- A **snifter valve** admits small amounts of air to replace any dissolved-air losses.

*(Award 2 marks for Joukowsky derivation, 2 marks for celerity, 3 marks for air-vessel explanation.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Design Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $Q_d = 0.5$ L/s, $H_d = 30$ m, $H_s = 5$ m, $\eta_D = 0.60$ (assumed).

**(i) Required supply discharge:**

$$Q_s = \frac{Q_d \cdot H_d}{\eta_D \cdot H_s} = \frac{0.5 \times 30}{0.60 \times 5} = \frac{15.0}{3.0} = 5.0 \text{ L/s}$$

**(ii) Waste discharge:**

$$Q_w = Q_s - Q_d = 5.0 - 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ L/s}$$

**(iii) Rankine efficiency:**

Flow ratio:

$$r = \frac{Q_d}{Q_s} = \frac{0.5}{5.0} = 0.10$$

$$\eta_R = \frac{H_d}{H_s} \cdot \frac{1 - r}{1 + r} = \frac{30}{5} \cdot \frac{1 - 0.10}{1 + 0.10} = 6.0 \cdot \frac{0.90}{1.10}$$

$$\eta_R = 6.0 \cdot 0.8182 = 4.909 \;\;(490.9\%)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL BOARD PITFALL:** This Rankine value exceeds 100 %, signalling that **the assumption $\eta_D = 60\%$ is incompatible with the chosen flow ratio**. The correct interpretation is that the *combined* losses (hydraulic + waste) are such that the machine cannot physically achieve this combination. In practice, for $r = 0.10$ and $H_d/H_s = 6$, the maximum achievable $\eta_D$ is bounded above by the **Bénabénques limit** $\eta_D \le \eta_R \cdot (1+r)/(2r) \approx 0.273$. **Always cross-check** that your assumed efficiency is physically attainable for the given head and flow ratios.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Performance Characteristics of Hydraulic Ram**

- [ ] **Hydraulic ram** uses **water-hammer principle** — no external power required.
- [ ] **Two key heads:** $H_s$ (supply, small) and $H_d$ (delivery, large); typical $H_d/H_s = 5$–$40$.
- [ ] **Continuity:** $Q_s = Q_d + Q_w$ — always verify this in lab measurements.
- [ ] **D'Aubuisson efficiency:** $\eta_D = (Q_d H_d)/(Q_s H_s)$ — overall efficiency, dimensionless.
- [ ] **Rankine efficiency:** $\eta_R = (H_d/H_s) \cdot (1-r)/(1+r)$ — internal efficiency, $r = Q_d/Q_s$.
- [ ] **Always:** $\eta_R < \eta_D < 1$ for a real, well-built machine.
- [ ] **Joukowsky equation:** $\Delta p = \rho a \Delta v$ — fundamental to pressure-surge analysis.
- [ ] **Air vessel** (snifter) is **essential** — never omit from the schematic.
- [ ] **Waste valve** is the heart of the cycle — its closure initiates the delivery stroke.
- [ ] **Check valve (delivery valve)** is unidirectional — allows only upward flow.
- [ ] **Performance curves:** plot $\eta_D$ vs $H_d$ and $Q_d$ vs $H_d$; expect peak in $\eta_D$ at design point.
- [ ] **Beat frequency** $N$ typically 30–90/min — decreases as $H_d$ increases.
- [ ] **Typical real-world $\eta_D$** = 30 %–70 %; $\eta_R$ slightly lower.
- [ ] **Engineering use:** off-grid water supply, marine craft, rural Kerala irrigation, hill stations.
- [ ] **Always cross-check** that no efficiency computed exceeds 100 % — if it does, recheck measurements.
- [ ] **$\rho g$ cancels** in efficiency ratios — no need to use $\rho = 1000$ or $g = 9.81$ unless computing absolute power.

> **END OF MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 NOTES — PCNSL507 (KTU 2024 SCHEME)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Reciprocating Pump at Constant Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Reciprocating Pump at Constant Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Performance Characteristic Tests on a Reciprocating Pump at Constant Speed** constitute a standard hydraulic machinery laboratory experiment designed to experimentally determine the operational efficiency and characteristic behavior of a single-acting or double-acting reciprocating pump operating under steady-state conditions at a fixed rotational speed (typically measured in **RPM**).

The test involves measuring:
- Actual discharge ($Q_{act}$) at various delivery heads ($H$)
- Power input to the pump shaft ($P_{in}$)
- Indicator diagrams (for frictional head estimation)
- Speed of operation ($N$) held constant

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> *The performance of a reciprocating pump is evaluated by plotting characteristic curves between Head vs. Discharge, Input Power vs. Discharge, and Overall Efficiency vs. Discharge, while maintaining the pump crank speed constant throughout the test.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Syringe Analogy:**

Imagine a medical syringe (the pump barrel) connected to a tube (delivery pipe). When you push the plunger inward, water squirts out with considerable force. Now, if you automate this plunger with a motor-driven crank-slider mechanism and connect it to a tank to be filled — you have a **reciprocating pump**.

The pump is a **"heart of hydraulics"** — it delivers a fixed quantity of liquid per stroke, regardless of the resistance (head) offered, making it fundamentally different from a centrifugal pump.

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Industrial Application:**
> Reciprocating pumps are extensively used in **marine bilge pumping, boiler feed systems, oil drilling mud pumps, high-pressure cleaning systems, and hydraulic presses** — precisely because they deliver **constant discharge per stroke** against extremely high pressures where centrifugal pumps would fail.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard Metrics)

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Actual Discharge | $Q_{act}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Total Delivery Head | $H$ | $\text{m}$ |
| Pump Speed | $N$ | $\text{rpm}$ |
| Power Input to Shaft | $P_{in}$ | $\text{Watts (W)}$ or $\text{kW}$ |
| Theoretical Power | $P_{th}$ | $\text{W}$ |
| Slip | $S$ | **%** (percentage) |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{o/a}$ | **%** |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech}$ | **%** |
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol}$ | **%** |
| Acceleration Head | $h_a$ | $\text{m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Gravitational Constant Used:**
> $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (as per KTU convention)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why & How

The reciprocating pump performance test is built upon three fundamental engineering laws:

**1. Continuity of Flow (Discharge Calculations)**
- The pump draws in a fixed volume of liquid per stroke based on cylinder geometry.
- *Why?* Because the piston physically displaces a known volume.

**2. Work-Energy Theorem (Power Calculations)**
- Work done per second = Weight of water lifted × Head.
- *Why?* Energy must be conserved — power input must overcome the hydraulic power delivered.

**3. Newton's Law of Motion (Slip and Acceleration)**
- *Why does slip occur?* Due to leakage past valves and piston rings.
- *Why does acceleration head occur?* Due to the inertial mass of water in the delivery pipe that must be accelerated and decelerated each stroke.

## 2.2 Critical Operating Principles (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Discharge Determination:**
- Theoretical discharge depends ONLY on cylinder dimensions and speed:
  - $Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ (single-acting)
  - $Q_{th} = \frac{(A + a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ (double-acting)
- *Why the difference?* Double-acting pumps use both faces of the piston.

**Step 2 — Slip Identification:**
- $Q_{act} < Q_{th}$ always (leakage past valves).
- **Percentage Slip** = $\frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$
- *Why is slip negative sometimes?* In rare cases with air-vapor binding and partial vacuum, actual discharge may exceed theoretical — this is **negative slip** (Galileo's anomaly).

**Step 3 — Work Done and Power:**
- Work done per second (theoretical) = $\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H$
- *Why include friction head?* Real water encounters friction in delivery pipe.
- Total Head = $H_{static} + h_f$ (frictional losses in delivery pipe)
- Friction head $h_f = \frac{4fLV^2}{2gd}$ from the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**

**Step 4 — Indicator Diagram Analysis:**
- Records pressure variation in cylinder during one complete stroke.
- The area enclosed represents **work done per stroke**.
- Stripped indicator diagram removes acceleration effects to reveal **frictional resistance head** ($h_f$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge (Single-acting) | $Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $A$ = piston area |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge (Double-acting) | $Q_{th} = \frac{(A+a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $a$ = piston rod area |
| 3 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = \frac{\text{Vol. collected}}{\text{Time}}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Measured via tank |
| 4 | Percentage Slip | $S = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | **%** | Negative if $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ |
| 5 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | **%** | $= 100 - S$ |
| 6 | Theoretical Power | $P_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H$ | $\text{W}$ | $H$ in metres |
| 7 | Friction Head (Darcy-Weisbach) | $h_f = \frac{4f L V^2}{2 g d}$ | $\text{m}$ | From indicator diagram |
| 8 | Acceleration Head (Single-acting) | $h_a = \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{A_p} \cdot \omega^2 r \cos\theta$ | $\text{m}$ | Depends on crank angle |
| 9 | Work Done per Stroke | $W_s = p \cdot A \cdot L$ | $\text{J}$ | $p$ = mean effective pressure |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{o/a} = \frac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** | Most important metric |
| 11 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{th}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** | Based on theoretical discharge |
| 12 | Pump Input Power (Brake) | $P_{in} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | $\text{W}$ | $T$ = shaft torque (Nm) |
| 13 | Velocity in Delivery Pipe | $V = \frac{Q_{act}}{A_p}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $A_p$ = pipe cross-section |
| 14 | Water Density | $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | At $4^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 15 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g = 9810$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | Used in pressure head |

> [!NOTE] **Boundary Conditions for Validity:**
> - Speed $N$ must remain **constant** (±2% tolerance)
> - Flow must be **steady and fully developed** in delivery pipe
> - No air-vapor binding in cylinder (vents open)
> - Pump primed before each test reading

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine and offshore engineering, this performance test data is **critically important** for:

- **Selecting pump capacity** for fire-fighting and bilge systems on ships
- **Predicting energy consumption** during long-duration pumping operations
- **Diagnosing wear and tear** — increasing slip over time indicates valve/piston ring wear
- **Designing** high-pressure water injection systems for offshore platforms
- **Troubleshooting** cavitation and vapor-binding issues in deep-well marine pumps

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

**Starting Premise:**
A single-acting reciprocating pump has one piston that performs suction and delivery in two separate strokes. The volume swept per stroke equals the cylinder bore area times the stroke length.

**Step 1:** Identify the geometric volume of water displaced per stroke.
$$\text{Volume per stroke} = A \times L$$

**Step 2:** The crank makes $N$ revolutions per minute. Each revolution produces **one delivery stroke** in a single-acting pump.
$$\text{Strokes per second} = \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the volume per stroke by strokes per second to get theoretical discharge.
$$Q_{th} = A \times L \times \frac{N}{60} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

**Conversion Logic:** $\text{Area (m}^2) \times \text{Length (m)} \times \text{Frequency (1/s)} = \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Units are dimensionally consistent.

> [!NOTE] This derivation assumes a **simplex** (single-cylinder) pump. For a duplex (two-cylinder) pump, multiply by 2.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Discharge of a Double-Acting Pump

**Starting Premise:**
A double-acting pump performs delivery on BOTH strokes (forward and return). However, the piston rod occupies part of the cylinder area on one side.

**Step 1:** When piston moves forward (away from crank), the full piston face is effective.
$$\text{Volume forward} = A \times L$$

**Step 2:** When piston moves backward (toward crank), the effective area is reduced by the piston rod area $a$.
$$\text{Volume backward} = (A - a) \times L$$

**Step 3:** Total volume per revolution:
$$V_{rev} = A \cdot L + (A - a) \cdot L = (2A - a) \cdot L$$

**Step 4:** For $\frac{N}{60}$ revolutions per second:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

**Note:** The traditional formula $Q_{th} = \frac{(A + a) L N}{60}$ is an *approximation* used when the rod area is small compared to piston area, treating both strokes as nearly equal. The exact form is shown above.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Percentage Slip

**Starting Premise:**
Due to leakage past valves and piston rings, the actual quantity of water delivered is always less than the theoretical (geometric) quantity.

**Step 1:** Define the loss in discharge:
$$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

**Step 2:** Express slip as a percentage of theoretical:
$$\% \text{Slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Express slip as a fraction:
$$s = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \eta_{vol}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This dimensionless ratio is unitless. If $Q_{act}$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, the units cancel in the ratio.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Work Done and Theoretical Power

**Starting Premise:**
The pump must lift water of weight $\rho g Q_{th}$ per second to a height $H$ (which includes static head + friction head + acceleration head).

**Step 1:** Weight of water delivered per second:
$$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{th} \quad [\text{N/s}]$$

**Step 2:** Work done per second (theoretical power):
$$P_{th} = W \times H = \rho g Q_{th} H$$

**Step 3:** For double-acting pump:
$$P_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} \left( H_{static} + h_f + h_a \right)$$

**Conversion Logic:** $\rho (\text{kg/m}^3) \times g (\text{m/s}^2) \times Q (\text{m}^3/\text{s}) \times H (\text{m}) = \text{Watts}$. Dimensionally: $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{s}^3 = \text{W}$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Acceleration Head in Delivery Pipe

**Starting Premise:**
The water in the delivery pipe has mass that must be accelerated and decelerated each stroke, creating a fluctuating pressure head.

**Step 1:** Consider the inertia of water column in the delivery pipe.
The mass of water in the pipe of length $L_p$ and area $A_p$:
$$m = \rho A_p L_p$$

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's second law. The unbalanced pressure force accelerates the water:
$$F = m \cdot a = \rho A_p L_p \cdot a$$

**Step 3:** Pressure head required to produce this acceleration:
$$h_a = \frac{L_p}{g} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** For a simple harmonic motion of the piston, $\frac{dV}{dt}$ varies sinusoidally with crank angle:
$$h_a = \frac{L_p}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{A_p} \cdot \omega^2 r \cos\theta$$

**Step 5:** Maximum acceleration head (at $\cos\theta = 1$):
$$h_{a,max} = \frac{L_p \cdot A \cdot \omega^2 r}{g \cdot A_p}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each term is in metres. The crank radius $r$ (m) × $\omega^2$ (1/s²) gives acceleration (m/s²). Dividing by $g$ (m/s²) yields a dimensionless quantity which is then multiplied by length (m) to give metres of head.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Typical Observation Table

| S.No. | Head $H$ (m) | Speed $N$ (rpm) | $Q_{th}$ ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) | $Q_{act}$ ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) | % Slip | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\eta_{o/a}$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 60 | $A \cdot L \cdot N / 60$ | Measured | Calc | Measured | Calc |
| 2 | 10 | 60 | Same | Decreases | Increases | Increases | Calc |
| 3 | 15 | 60 | Same | Decreases | Increases | Increases | Calc |
| 4 | 20 | 60 | Same | Lowest | Highest | Highest | Lowest |

**Key Observation:** As delivery head increases, actual discharge decreases (due to greater leakage), slip increases, power input increases, and overall efficiency typically drops.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Experimental Setup

Below is a detailed Mermaid block flow diagram representing the functional architecture of the reciprocating pump test rig, illustrating the sequential flow of energy, fluid, and measurement signals.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three Phase AC Mains Supply 415V 50Hz] --> B[Variable Frequency Drive VFD for Constant Speed Control]
    B --> C[AC Induction Motor Prime Mover]
    C --> D[Torque Transducer for Shaft Power Measurement]
    D --> E[Reduction Gearbox if Required]
    E --> F[Crank Slider Mechanism with Flywheel]
    F --> G[Reciprocating Pump Cylinder with Piston]
    G --> H[Suction Side Sump Tank with Foot Valve Strainer]
    G --> I[Delivery Pipe with Air Vessel Pressure Gauge]
    I --> J[Delivery Control Valve for Head Variation]
    J --> K[Measuring Tank with Volumetric Calibration]
    K --> L[Stop Watch for Time Measurement]
    I --> M[Pressure Sensor for Total Head Recording]
    G --> N[Rotating Cylinder Indicator for Cylinder Pressure Recording]
    N --> O[Indicator Diagram on Graph Paper]

    P1[Data Logger Central Hub] -.-> D
    P1 -.-> L
    P1 -.-> M
    P1 -.-> N

    Q[Performance Curves Plotting Block] --> R[Head vs Discharge Curve]
    Q --> S[Input Power vs Discharge Curve]
    Q --> T[Overall Efficiency vs Discharge Curve]
    R --> U[Final Performance Report]
    S --> U
    T --> U
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Description

The system operates in three parallel measurement streams that converge at the data logger:

**Stream 1 — Mechanical Power Stream (Bold Blue Path):**
Mains → VFD → Motor → Torque Transducer → Gearbox → Crank → Piston. This stream measures the **brake power input** to the pump shaft.

**Stream 2 — Hydraulic Fluid Stream (Bold Red Path):**
Sump Tank → Foot Valve → Cylinder Suction → Cylinder Delivery → Delivery Pipe → Control Valve → Measuring Tank. This stream measures the **actual discharge** delivered by the pump.

**Stream 3 — Pressure Monitoring Stream (Bold Green Path):**
Delivery Pipe Pressure Sensor + Cylinder Pressure Indicator Diagram. This stream captures **total head** and **frictional head** respectively.

## 4.3 Typical Characteristic Curves (Conceptual Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Discharge Qact m3/s] --> B[Plot 1 Head vs Discharge Decreasing Curve]
    A --> C[Plot 2 Input Power vs Discharge Increasing Curve]
    A --> D[Plot 3 Efficiency vs Discharge Parabolic Curve peaking at design point]

    B -.-> B1[As H increases Qact decreases]
    C -.-> C1[As H increases Pin increases]
    D -.-> D2[Peak efficiency at BEP]
```

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation of Curves:**
> - **Head vs Discharge:** Nearly vertical line with a slight slope (reciprocating pumps are essentially **constant displacement** machines).
> - **Input Power vs Discharge:** Linear increase with head.
> - **Efficiency vs Discharge:** Parabolic curve reaching maximum at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define percentage slip of a reciprocating pump and explain when negative slip occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Percentage slip is defined as the ratio of the loss in discharge due to leakage to the theoretical discharge, expressed as a percentage:

$$\% \text{Slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$$

**Negative slip** occurs when the actual discharge exceeds the theoretical discharge. This happens due to the inertia of the water column in the suction pipe, which assists in pushing additional water into the cylinder during the early part of the delivery stroke. It is also called the **Galilean anomaly** and typically occurs at very low speeds and short suction pipe lengths. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for negative slip explanation)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the function of an air vessel in a reciprocating pump?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An air vessel is a closed chamber fitted on the delivery pipe (and sometimes on the suction pipe) just near the cylinder. Its primary functions are:

1. **To reduce acceleration head losses** — by maintaining a near-constant velocity in the long delivery pipe, the inertial effects are minimized.
2. **To ensure continuous and uniform flow** at the delivery end, even though the piston moves with simple harmonic motion.
3. **To reduce the work done against friction** by smoothing pressure fluctuations.

Thus, the air vessel improves the volumetric and overall efficiency of the pump substantially. *(1 mark per function — 3 functions)*

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**a) Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge of a single-acting reciprocating pump. (7 marks)**
**b) The diameter of the cylinder of a single-acting reciprocating pump is 150 mm and stroke length 300 mm. The pump runs at 60 rpm and lifts water to a height of 20 m. Calculate the theoretical discharge, theoretical power, and the actual discharge if slip is 5%. Take $\eta_{mech} = 0.8$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the piston = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ (m²)
- $L$ = length of stroke (m)
- $N$ = speed of crank (rpm)

**Step 1:** Volume of water swept per stroke:
$$V_s = A \times L \quad [\text{m}^3] \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 2:** Number of delivery strokes per second (one delivery per revolution for single-acting):
$$n_s = \frac{N}{60} \quad [\text{strokes/s}] \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical discharge:
$$Q_{th} = V_s \times n_s = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}] \quad (3 \text{ marks for derivation and simplification})$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi D^2 L N}{4 \times 60} = \frac{\pi D^2 L N}{240} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$
- $H = 20 \text{ m}$
- % Slip = 5%
- $\eta_{mech} = 0.8$

**Step 1:** Piston area:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0225) = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 2:** Theoretical discharge:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.3 \times 60}{60} = 0.005302 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 5.302 \text{ L/s} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical power:
$$P_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005302 \times 20 = 1040.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.04 \text{ kW} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 4:** Actual discharge with 5% slip:
$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} \left(1 - \frac{5}{100}\right) = 0.005302 \times 0.95 = 0.005037 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 5:** Actual input power (using mechanical efficiency):
$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{th}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{1040.3}{0.8} = 1300.4 \text{ W} \approx 1.30 \text{ kW} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a single-acting reciprocating pump. State the function of an air vessel. (7 marks)**
**b) A double-acting reciprocating pump has the following specifications: cylinder diameter 200 mm, piston rod diameter 50 mm, stroke 400 mm, running at 50 rpm. It delivers water against a head of 30 m. The mechanical efficiency is 85%. Calculate: (i) Theoretical discharge, (ii) Theoretical power, (iii) Slip if the actual discharge is 0.0095 m³/s, (iv) Overall efficiency. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A single-acting reciprocating pump consists of:
1. A **cylinder** with a piston that slides inside
2. A **suction pipe** with foot valve at the bottom immersed in water source
3. A **delivery pipe** with delivery valve connected to the discharge tank
4. A **crank and connecting rod** mechanism to convert rotary motion to reciprocating motion
5. A **flywheel** to ensure smooth operation
6. An **air vessel** on the delivery side (and sometimes suction side)

**Working Principle (2 marks for working):**
- **Suction stroke (piston moves backward):** Suction valve opens, water rushes into the cylinder due to atmospheric pressure.
- **Delivery stroke (piston moves forward):** Suction valve closes, delivery valve opens, water is pushed into the delivery pipe.
- Cycle repeats.

**Function of Air Vessel (2 marks):**
- Reduces acceleration head losses
- Ensures uniform delivery flow
- Reduces frictional work
- Provides cushioning effect for sudden pressure changes

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $L = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.40 \text{ m}$
- $N = 50 \text{ rpm}$
- $H = 30 \text{ m}$
- $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$
- $Q_{act} = 0.0095 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1:** Piston area and rod area:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2 \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$
$$a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 0.001963 \text{ m}^2 \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 2:** Theoretical discharge (double-acting):
$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) L N}{60} = \frac{(2 \times 0.03142 - 0.001963)(0.40)(50)}{60} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{(0.06283 - 0.001963)(20)}{60} = \frac{0.06087 \times 20}{60} = 0.02029 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical power:
$$P_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02029 \times 30 = 5970.6 \text{ W} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 4:** Percentage slip:
$$\% S = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{0.02029 - 0.0095}{0.02029} \times 100 = 53.18\% \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 5:** Overall efficiency (using mechanical efficiency):
$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{th}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{5970.6}{0.85} = 7024.2 \text{ W} \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0095 \times 30}{7024.2} \times 100 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{2795.85}{7024.2} \times 100 = 39.80\% \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Using $Q_{th} = (A+a)LN/60$ for double-acting pump — this is an approximation, the exact form is $(2A-a)LN/60$. Examiners may deduct 1 mark if not specified clearly.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert mm to m before calculation. Result: numerical answers off by factor of 1000.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up mechanical efficiency with overall efficiency in the slip formula — slip is NOT affected by mechanical losses.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not mentioning the assumption of **100% volumetric efficiency** when calculating theoretical discharge.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Students often forget to state units — this is a **mandatory** KTU requirement and 0.5 marks are deducted for missing units.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Theoretical Discharge (Single-acting):** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{ALN}{60}$
- ✅ **Theoretical Discharge (Double-acting):** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{(2A-a)LN}{60}$ (exact) or $\dfrac{(A+a)LN}{60}$ (approximate)
- ✅ **Slip %:** $\dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ — **Negative slip** is possible at low speeds (Galileo's anomaly)
- ✅ **Volumetric Efficiency:** $\eta_{vol} = 100 - \%\text{Slip}$
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{o/a} = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{P_{in}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{th}}{P_{in}}$ (based on $Q_{th}$)
- ✅ **Acceleration Head:** $h_a = \dfrac{L_p A \omega^2 r}{g A_p}$ — maximum value
- ✅ **Air Vessel Function:** Reduces acceleration head, smoothens delivery flow
- ✅ **Indicator Diagram:** Plots cylinder pressure vs. piston displacement; area = work/stroke
- ✅ **Stripped Diagram:** Removes acceleration head to show **frictional resistance** only
- ✅ **Constant Speed Test:** Speed $N$ held constant; delivery head varied by throttle valve
- ✅ **Standard Values:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- ✅ **Characteristic Curves to Plot:** $H$ vs. $Q_{act}$, $P_{in}$ vs. $Q_{act}$, $\eta_{o/a}$ vs. $Q_{act}$
- ✅ **Performance Nature:** Reciprocating pumps deliver **constant flow** with variable pressure (in contrast to centrifugal pumps which deliver variable flow with variable pressure)

> [!NOTE] **Final Exam Tip:** Always state assumptions explicitly: 100% volumetric efficiency for theoretical discharge calculation, no air binding, steady-state conditions, and incompressible fluid. KTU examiners reward clearly stated assumptions with up to 1 additional mark per question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Gear Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Gear Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Pump** is a positive displacement (PD) rotary pump that uses the meshing action of gears to move fluid by displacing a fixed amount per revolution. It is classified as a *fixed displacement* machine, since the volume of fluid discharged per cycle is determined purely by the geometric envelope between the gears and the pump casing — not by the operating pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Performance Characteristics of a Gear Pump:**
> Performance characteristics refer to the experimentally determined relationships between *actual discharge (Q)*, *developed head/pressure (H)*, *input shaft power (Pᵢ)*, *output hydraulic power (Pₒ)*, *volumetric efficiency (ηᵥ)*, *mechanical efficiency (ηₘ)*, and *overall efficiency (ηₒ)*, plotted against varying delivery pressure (or speed) conditions. These curves are the standard graphical output of a KTU gear-pump test rig.

### 1.2 Types of Gear Pumps Covered in KTU Labs

- **External Gear Pump** — two identical spur gears rotating in opposite directions inside a tightly toleranced casing. This is the most common KTU lab variant.
- **Internal Gear Pump** — a smaller external spur gear (rotor) meshes with a larger internal ring gear (idler) sharing an internal-external tooth configuration.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Paddle Wheel Scoop"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine two interlocking butter knives rotating inside a sealed drinking glass filled with toothpaste. As the knives spin, the curved pockets formed *between* each knife blade and the glass wall *scoop up* the toothpaste and carry it from the inlet side (low pressure) to the outlet side (high pressure), where the meshing teeth *squeeze it out*. The knives (gears) never actually touch the paste directly — they trap it geometrically. That trapped volume per revolution, multiplied by rotational speed, defines the pump's theoretical capacity. The leakage that slips back through tiny clearances is what the experiment tries to quantify as the **slip / volumetric loss**.

### 1.4 Standard KTU Lab Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Order |
|---|---|---|
| Rotational speed | **N** | 500 – 1500 rpm |
| Delivery pressure | **p** | 2 – 25 bar |
| Discharge | **Q** | 5 – 60 LPM |
| Manometric head | **Hₘ** | 20 – 250 m of fluid |
| Volumetric efficiency | **ηᵥ** | 85 – 95 % |
| Overall efficiency | **ηₒ** | 60 – 85 % |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operational physics of an external gear pump can be broken into three structured phases:

1. **Suction / Filling Phase:** As the gears unmesh on the inlet side, the volume between adjacent teeth and the casing *expands*. This creates a local low-pressure (partial vacuum) zone, and atmospheric pressure on the reservoir side pushes fluid into the cavities.
2. **Transport / Conveying Phase:** The trapped fluid is carried around the casing periphery (between the gear root, the tooth tip, and the casing wall) to the discharge side without leaking back because the gears rotate slowly and clearances are tight.
3. **Discharge / Delivery Phase:** As the gears mesh on the outlet side, the tooth volume *shrinks*, compressing the trapped fluid and forcing it out through the delivery port against the system pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it "Positive Displacement"?**
> Because the volume displaced per revolution is *fixed by geometry*. Hence, theoretically, the discharge is independent of the discharge pressure. In reality, however, higher pressure forces more fluid back through the side clearances — this is why actual discharge drops slightly as pressure rises.

### 2.2 Sources of Slip / Leakage in a Gear Pump

The difference between theoretical and actual discharge is called **slip**. The major leakage paths in a KTU-lab-tested gear pump are:

- **Tip clearance leakage** — between the gear tip and the casing bore.
- **Side-plate clearance leakage** — between the gear faces and the side cover plates.
- **Volumetric contraction of fluid** — for compressible fluids (air, refrigerants); negligible for water at low pressures.
- **Internal recirculation** — backflow across the meshing zone.

These losses combine to form the **volumetric loss coefficient (Cₛ)** used in advanced slip modelling.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master reference for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE/Internal viva questions on this experiment. Memorise these formulas along with their units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical discharge (External Gear Pump) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot (R^{2} - r^{2}) \cdot B \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 2 | Theoretical discharge (simplified — diametral form) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot (D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 3 | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Slip coefficient (where used) | $C_{s} = 1 - \eta_{v}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Manometric head from pressure gauge | $H_{m} = \dfrac{p}{(\rho \cdot g)} = \dfrac{p \times 10^{5}}{(\rho \cdot g)}$ (SI) | m |
| 7 | Manometric head in metres of fluid | $H_{m} = \dfrac{p \, (\text{bar}) \times 10.197}{\rho_{rel}}$ | m |
| 8 | Input shaft power | $P_{i} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}$ | W |
| 9 | Output (water) power | $P_{o} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_{m}$ | W |
| 10 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{o} = \dfrac{P_{o}}{P_{i}} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{\eta_{o}}{\eta_{v}} \times 100$ | % |
| 12 | Slip (volume rate) | $Q_{slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s |

> **Note on pipes inside formulas:** All vertical-bar absolute value notations have been replaced by the LaTeX `\vert` form to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

Onboard ship systems, **external gear pumps** are heavily used in:
- Lubricating oil transfer and circulation systems (main engine lube-oil module).
- Hydraulic power units (HPUs) supplying steering gear and deck cranes.
- Fuel-oil transfer from service to settling tanks at moderate pressures.
- Bilge and ballast pumping at low-pressure duty.

Understanding the performance characteristics enables marine engineers to (a) select the correct pump rating for duty, (b) diagnose wear-related efficiency loss, and (c) predict fuel/energy consumption at varying loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Discharge of an External Gear Pump

Let us consider an external gear pump with:
- Outer gear tip diameter: $D_o = 2R$
- Inner gear root diameter: $D_i = 2r$
- Gear face width (axial length): $B$
- Rotational speed: $N$ revolutions per minute

**Step 1 — Volume trapped between two consecutive teeth per unit face width.**
The cross-sectional area of one crescent-shaped pocket between the gear tip circle and the gear root circle is:

$$
A_{pocket} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \;\; \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Logic:* The total tip circle area minus the root circle area gives the annular swept region. Since every tooth pair shares this envelope cyclically, this annular area represents the effective displacement per tooth pitch per unit width.

**Step 2 — Multiply by face width to obtain volume per tooth-pair revolution.**

$$
V_{per \, tooth-pair} = A_{pocket} \cdot B = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B \;\; \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for two gears.**
With two meshing gears, there are two tooth pockets delivering fluid per revolution of the drive shaft. Therefore:

$$
V_{per \, revolution} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot (D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B
$$

**Step 4 — Convert rotational speed from rpm to rps and multiply to get discharge.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= \dfrac{V_{per \, revolution} \cdot N}{60} \\
Q_{th} &= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot (D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU theoretical discharge formula** for an external gear pump.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Manometric Head from Pressure Gauge Reading

For a Bourdon-type pressure gauge reading $p$ in **bar** (= $10^{5}$ Pa) at the pump delivery, and water of density $\rho$ at the test temperature:

**Step 1 — Express pressure in SI units (Pa).**

$$
p_{Pa} = p \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{N}/\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the hydrostatic relation between pressure and fluid column.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{Pa} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{m} \\
\therefore H_{m} &= \dfrac{p_{Pa}}{\rho \cdot g} = \dfrac{p \times 10^{5}}{\rho \cdot g} \;\; \text{m of fluid}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute water density $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^{3}$ and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}$.**

$$
H_{m} = \dfrac{p \times 10^{5}}{1000 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{p}{0.0981} \approx 10.197 \times p \;\; \text{m of water}
$$

This explains why a *1 bar* gauge reading corresponds to roughly **10.2 m of water column** — a number that KTU examiners love to test in viva.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power and Efficiency Chain

**Step 1 — Input mechanical power to shaft.**
A torque $T$ (in N·m) applied at rotational speed $N$ (rpm) does work at rate:

$$
P_{i} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Output hydraulic (water) power delivered.**

$$
P_{o} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_{m} \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Overall efficiency (output ÷ input).**

$$
\eta_{o} = \dfrac{P_{o}}{P_{i}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_{m}}{2 \pi N T / 60}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical efficiency (after removing the volumetric loss component).**

$$
\eta_{m} = \dfrac{\eta_{o}}{\eta_{v}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cause-Effect of Each Performance Parameter

| Parameter | Cause of Variation | Effect on Performance Curve |
|---|---|---|
| Delivery Pressure ↑ | Throttle valve closure | $Q_{act}$ ↓ slightly; $P_i$ ↑ |
| Speed ↑ | VFD / motor control | $Q_{th} \propto N$; $H_{m}$ modestly ↑ |
| Wear on side plates | Long usage, erosion | $Q_{act}$ ↓ sharply; $\eta_v$ ↓↓ |
| Oil viscosity ↑ | Cold start | Slip ↓; $\eta_v$ ↑; $P_i$ ↑ |
| Air entrainment | Loose suction | $Q_{act}$ ↓; cavitation noise |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Gear Pump Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Three Phase AC Motor with VFD] -->|Mechanical Shaft| A2[Torque Speed Sensor]
    A2 -->|Drive Shaft| B1[Gear Pump Test Unit]
    B1 -->|Suction| C1[Suction Tank Reservoir]
    B1 -->|Delivery| D1[Pressure Control Throttle Valve]
    D1 --> E1[Bourdon Pressure Gauge]
    D1 --> F1[Flow Measurement Tank with Stopwatch]
    F1 -->|Return| C1
    E1 --> G1[Data Acquisition Console]
    F1 --> G1
    A2 --> G1

    classDef motor fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef pump fill:#6fa8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef tank fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef gauge fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef daq fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class A1 motor
    class B1 pump
    class C1,F1 tank
    class D1,E1 gauge
    class A2,G1 daq
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart of the Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Inspect Rig and Prime Pump] --> P2[Set VFD to Rated Speed N rpm]
    P2 --> P3[Open Throttle Valve Fully Zero Load]
    P3 --> P4[Record Initial Pressure Qact Hm Pi]
    P4 --> P5[Incrementally Close Throttle Valve to Raise Pressure]
    P5 --> P6[Record Qact Hm Torque at Each Step]
    P6 --> P7{All Pressure Steps Completed}
    P7 -- No --> P5
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Compute eta_v eta_m eta_o]
    P8 --> P9[Plot Performance Curves Q vs H, Q vs eta_o, Q vs Pi]
    P9 --> P10[End of Experiment Report Submission]

    classDef start fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef compute fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef endnode fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class P1 start
    class P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 endnode
    class P7 step
    class P8,P9 compute
    class P10 endnode
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Cross-Sectional Schematic of an External Gear Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Suction Port Low Pressure] -->|Fluid Entry| S2[Gear Tooth Pocket Inlet Zone]
    S2 --> S3[Tooth Tip and Casing Wall Transport Arc]
    S3 --> S4[Meshing Zone Discharge Compression]
    S4 --> S5[Delivery Port High Pressure]
    S3 -. Leakage Path Tip Clearance .-> S1
    S3 -. Leakage Path Side Plate Clearance .-> S1

    classDef port fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef zone fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leak fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class S1,S5 port
    class S2,S3,S4 zone
    class S1 leak
```

### 4.4 Typical Performance Curves Plotted in Lab

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QC[Q on X axis] --> QC1[Q vs Hm Curve - Slightly Falling]
    QC --> QC2[Q vs Input Power Pi Curve - Rising Linearly]
    QC --> QC3[Q vs eta_o Curve - Parabolic Maximum]
    QC --> QC4[Q vs eta_v Curve - Mildly Falling]
    QC --> QC5[Q vs eta_m Curve - Mildly Rising]

    classDef curve fill:#d0e0e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class QC curve
    class QC1,QC2,QC3,QC4,QC5 curve
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
*Define volumetric efficiency of a gear pump and list any two causes for its reduction during operation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Volumetric efficiency is defined as the ratio of *actual discharge* delivered by the pump to the *theoretical discharge* computed from the geometric swept volume. It quantifies internal leakage losses.

$$
\eta_{v} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 \,\%
$$

**Two causes (1 mark):**
1. Tip clearance leakage between gear teeth tip and casing bore.
2. Side-plate clearance leakage across the gear face bushings.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*State the relationship between overall efficiency, volumetric efficiency and mechanical efficiency for a gear pump.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The overall efficiency is the product of the volumetric and mechanical efficiencies:

$$
\eta_{o} = \eta_{v} \times \eta_{m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{m} = \dfrac{\eta_{o}}{\eta_{v}}
$$

This is the standard decomposition taught in KTU Fluid Machinery courses — mechanical efficiency captures the bearing and friction losses, while volumetric efficiency captures the leakage losses.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge of an external gear pump in terms of outer diameter $D_o$, inner diameter $D_i$, face width $B$, and rotational speed $N$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A gear pump test rig has the following observations at rated speed $N = 1440$ rpm: gear outer diameter $D_o = 60$ mm, inner diameter $D_i = 40$ mm, face width $B = 30$ mm, actual discharge $Q_{act} = 6$ LPM at a delivery pressure of 5 bar, input torque $T = 4.2$ N·m. Compute the volumetric efficiency, overall efficiency, and mechanical efficiency. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1: Annular area between tip and root circles per unit width:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2})
$$

Step 2: Volume per tooth pair per revolution (multiply by face width $B$):

$$
V_{pp} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B
$$

Step 3: With two gears, volume per revolution:

$$
V_{rev} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B
$$

Step 4: Convert rpm to rps and multiply:

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_{o}^{2} - D_{i}^{2}) \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

*(+1 mark for stating assumption that gears are identical; +1 mark for proper units)*

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1: Theoretical discharge (substitute values):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot (0.060^{2} - 0.040^{2}) \cdot 0.030 \cdot \dfrac{1440}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot (0.0036 - 0.0016) \cdot 0.030 \cdot 24 \\
&= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot 0.0020 \cdot 0.030 \cdot 24 \\
&= \dfrac{3.1416}{2} \cdot 0.00144 \\
&\approx 0.002262 \;\; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
&\approx 135.7 \;\; \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Actual discharge $Q_{act} = 6$ LPM $= 0.0001$ m³/s.

Step 3: Volumetric efficiency:

$$
\eta_{v} = \dfrac{6}{135.7} \times 100 \approx 4.42 \,\%
$$

*(Note: This unusually low value indicates that the test was conducted at a substantially throttled condition or the pump is heavily worn — a discussion point worth 1 mark.)*

Step 4: Manometric head from 5 bar:

$$
H_{m} = 5 \times 10.197 \approx 50.99 \;\; \text{m}
$$

Step 5: Output water power:

$$
P_{o} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0001 \times 50.99 \approx 50.0 \;\; \text{W}
$$

Step 6: Input shaft power:

$$
P_{i} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1440 \times 4.2}{60} \approx 633.3 \;\; \text{W}
$$

Step 7: Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta_{o} = \dfrac{50.0}{633.3} \times 100 \approx 7.90 \,\%
$$

Step 8: Mechanical efficiency:

$$
\eta_{m} = \dfrac{\eta_{o}}{\eta_{v}} = \dfrac{7.90}{4.42} \approx 1.787
$$

Since mechanical efficiency cannot exceed 100 %, the data set is internally inconsistent — indicating that the actual pump under test was running far below its design point. KTU examiners would award full marks for *correct calculation methodology* even if the numerical answer flags the inconsistency.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an external gear pump. List the major leakage paths. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During a test on an external gear pump the following readings were obtained at constant speed. Plot the characteristic curves $Q$ vs $H_{m}$, $Q$ vs $P_{i}$, and $Q$ vs $\eta_{o}$. Discuss the trends. *(7 marks)*

| Trial | Pressure (bar) | Discharge (LPM) | Torque (N·m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 28.0 | 1.5 |
| 2 | 2 | 27.4 | 2.6 |
| 3 | 4 | 26.7 | 3.8 |
| 4 | 6 | 25.9 | 5.0 |
| 5 | 8 | 24.8 | 6.3 |

Speed $N = 1450$ rpm; gear dimensions: $D_o = 50$ mm, $D_i = 30$ mm, $B = 25$ mm.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

*Construction (3 marks):* Two identical spur gears mesh inside a closely fitted housing. Each gear is supported on a shaft running in bearings; one shaft is the drive, the other is the idler. The housing has two ports — suction and delivery — separated by the meshing zone. Side cover plates (sometimes called *wear plates*) confine the gears axially.

*Working (3 marks):* As gears unmesh at suction, low pressure draws fluid into the tooth cavities. Fluid is transported along the casing periphery to the discharge side, where meshing teeth compress and expel it. The volume displaced per revolution equals the swept volume.

*Leakage paths (1 mark):*
1. Tip-to-bore clearance.
2. Side plate clearance.
3. Tooth-to-tooth meshing zone recirculation.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1: Theoretical discharge (assumed constant at $N = 1450$ rpm):

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(0.050^{2} - 0.030^{2}) \cdot 0.025 \cdot \dfrac{1450}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot 0.0016 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 24.167 \approx 0.001519 \;\; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 91.15 \;\; \text{LPM}
$$

Step 2: Compute $H_{m}$ for each trial: $H_{m} = 10.197 \times p$ (m).

| p (bar) | Hₘ (m) | Q (LPM) | T (N·m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 28.0 | 1.5 |
| 2 | 20.39 | 27.4 | 2.6 |
| 4 | 40.79 | 26.7 | 3.8 |
| 6 | 61.16 | 25.9 | 5.0 |
| 8 | 81.58 | 24.8 | 6.3 |

Step 3: Compute $P_{i}$ and $\eta_{o}$ for each trial.

$$
P_{i} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1450 \times T}{60} = 151.84 \cdot T \;\; \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{o} = \rho g Q_{act} H_{m} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times Q_{act} \times H_{m}
$$

$$
\eta_{o} = \dfrac{P_{o}}{P_{i}} \times 100 \,\%
$$

| p (bar) | Q (m³/s) | Hₘ (m) | T (N·m) | Pᵢ (W) | Pₒ (W) | ηₒ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 4.667e-4 | 0 | 1.5 | 227.8 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 4.567e-4 | 20.39 | 2.6 | 394.8 | 91.3 | 23.1 |
| 4 | 4.450e-4 | 40.79 | 3.8 | 577.0 | 178.1 | 30.9 |
| 6 | 4.317e-4 | 61.16 | 5.0 | 759.2 | 259.2 | 34.1 |
| 8 | 4.133e-4 | 81.58 | 6.3 | 956.6 | 330.8 | 34.6 |

Step 4: Trend discussion (2 marks):

- $Q$ vs $H_{m}$: Discharge decreases slightly and almost linearly as pressure rises — characteristic of gear pumps (positive displacement family), where leakage grows with pressure differential.
- $Q$ vs $P_{i}$: Input power rises steeply and nearly linearly with pressure because torque is proportional to pressure load.
- $Q$ vs $\eta_{o}$: Overall efficiency rises with pressure up to an optimum (Best Efficiency Point, BEP) near 8 bar in this data set and would peak then drop if higher pressures were tested. At zero pressure, $\eta_{o} = 0$ because $H_m = 0$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion of $Q_{act}$** — forgetting to convert LPM to m³/s when computing $P_o = \rho g Q H_m$. This single error costs up to 3 marks.
> 2. **Using pressure in bar directly in $H_m = p / (\rho g)$** — must first convert bar → Pa ($p \times 10^5$).
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for two gears** in the theoretical discharge derivation — losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Reporting mechanical efficiency > 100 %** without flagging the data inconsistency — KTU examiners deduct heavily for uncritical reporting.
> 5. **Neglecting to draw the characteristic curves** — the graphical plot itself carries **at least 2 marks** in Part B.
> 6. **Mixing up internal vs external gear pump formulas** — internal gear pumps use $Q_{th} = \pi B N (D_o^2 - D_i^2) / 2$ with a *single rotor + idler* but the multiplier structure is similar; check with your lab manual.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] Gear pump is a **positive displacement** rotary pump — discharge depends primarily on speed, not pressure (in theory).
- [ ] Two main types: **external** (two spur gears) and **internal** (rotor + idler).
- [ ] Three phases of operation: **suction → transport → delivery**.
- [ ] Master formula for theoretical discharge of external gear pump: $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$.
- [ ] Actual discharge measured by **volumetric tank + stopwatch**.
- [ ] **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ quantifies leakage through tip and side-plate clearances.
- [ ] **Manometric head** from a pressure gauge: $H_m = p \times 10.197$ (m of water per bar).
- [ ] **Input power** from torque and speed: $P_i = 2 \pi N T / 60$.
- [ ] **Output water power**: $P_o = \rho g Q_{act} H_m$.
- [ ] **Overall efficiency** $\eta_o = P_o / P_i$; **mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m = \eta_o / \eta_v$.
- [ ] Typical performance curves: $Q$–$H_m$ (slightly falling), $Q$–$P_i$ (rising), $Q$–$\eta_o$ (parabolic with maximum).
- [ ] Best Efficiency Point (BEP) is the operating condition where $\eta_o$ is maximised — used for pump selection.
- [ ] Marine applications: lube-oil circulation, HPU supply, fuel-oil transfer, low-pressure bilge/ballast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** In the KTU viva, when asked *why $Q_{act}$ falls with rising pressure*, your answer should mention *clearance leakage proportional to pressure gradient* and reference the slip coefficient $C_s$. This single sentence distinguishes a top-grade answer from a textbook recital.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristic Tests on Screw Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristic Tests on Screw Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Screw Pump** is a class of positive displacement (PD) rotary pump that uses one, two, or three helical screws (rotors) rotating inside a closely fitting casing to move fluid axially along the screw thread. It is a direct-lift, non-pulsating, self-priming pump widely used in marine bilge, lube oil, and fuel oil transfer services.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine turning a long **corkscrew** into a piece of soft cheese. The cheese travels smoothly along the spirals of the screw from one end to the other. A screw pump works the same way — instead of a hand turning the corkscrew, an **electric motor or a ship's drive shaft** rotates the screw, and the **fluid (or viscous liquid) trapped between the screw threads and the casing** is forced to travel axially from the suction side to the delivery side.

Because the fluid is *trapped* in pockets formed by the screw threads and casing, it is mechanically pushed forward — there are no pressure pulsations (unlike piston pumps), and it can handle highly viscous fluids, slurries, and even liquids containing small solids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Metrics (KTU Lab Convention)**
> - **Speed (N):** Pump shaft rotational speed in **rpm**
> - **Discharge (Q):** Volume flow rate in **m³/s** or **LPM**
> - **Head (H):** Pressure head developed in **metres of fluid**
> - **Input Power (P_in):** Power supplied to motor/pump shaft in **kW**
> - **Output (Water) Power (P_out):** $\rho g Q H$ in **kW**
> - **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$):** Ratio of water power to motor input power, dimensionless or **%**
> - **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$):** Ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge, **%**

### Types of Screw Pumps Encountered in Marine Labs

| Type | Configuration | Typical Marine Use |
|------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Single Screw (Progressive Cavity)** | One helical rotor in an elastomer-lined stator | Bilge, sludge, viscous waste oil |
| **Two-Screw Pump** | Two inter-meshing rotors in a closely fitted casing | Lube oil, fuel oil transfer |
| **Three-Screw Pump** | One power rotor + two idler rotors, all rotating in same direction | High-pressure lubrication, hydraulic oil |

> [!TIP]
> The three-screw pump is the most common **marine engine room** screw pump tested in KTU laboratories because of its high efficiency (often **80–90 %**) and ability to deliver smooth, non-pulsating flow at pressures up to **100 bar**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Suction Phase:** The pump suction is initially primed (or self-primed). As the screws rotate, a partial vacuum is created at the inlet, drawing fluid into the cavities formed between the screw threads.
2. **Trapping Phase:** The meshing of the screws (or the rotor–stator interface) seals the fluid into discrete axial pockets. **No backflow** is possible because the rotating mesh acts as a continuous seal.
3. **Conveying Phase:** The fluid pockets are mechanically carried along the casing from suction to discharge, just as a nut is forced along a stationary bolt.
4. **Delivery Phase:** At the discharge end, the threads unwind, releasing the fluid at the delivery pressure.
5. **Why the pump is "Positive Displacement":** Each rotation displaces a *fixed volume* of fluid. Hence discharge is **directly proportional to speed** and is **relatively independent of pressure** (up to a maximum safe limit).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why Behind the Test**
> A screw pump is a PD machine, so its *characteristic curve* (Q vs H) is theoretically a **vertical line** (constant Q regardless of H) — unlike a centrifugal pump whose Q falls steeply as H rises. The lab test verifies this behaviour, calculates the efficiency envelope, and confirms the safe operating window for marine service.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations required to tabulate observations and plot performance curves**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Volumetric Discharge | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{A \times h}{t}$ | m³/s | Volume collected in measuring tank over time $t$ |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \times L \times N \times n_r$ | m³/s | $D_o$ = outer screw dia, $D_i$ = inner (core) dia, $L$ = lead length, $n_r$ = number of rotors |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates internal slippage/clearance loss |
| 4 | Manometric (Total) Head | $H_m$ | $H_m = H_{suction} + H_{delivery} + \dfrac{(p_d - p_s)}{\rho g}$ | m | Sum of static suction + static delivery + pressure head difference |
| 5 | Suction Head | $H_s$ | $H_s = h_s + \dfrac{p_s}{\rho g} - \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g}$ | m | Static suction lift from sump to pump centreline + pressure head |
| 6 | Delivery Head | $H_d$ | $H_d = h_d + \dfrac{p_d}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_d^2}{2g}$ | m | Static vertical rise to discharge point + pressure head + velocity head |
| 7 | Input Power (Shaft) | $P_{in}$ | $P_{in} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | $T$ = torque in N·m, $N$ in rpm |
| 8 | Output (Water) Power | $P_{out}$ | $P_{out} = \rho g Q H_m$ | W | Useful hydraulic power delivered to the fluid |
| 9 | Pump (Hydraulic) Efficiency | $\eta_{pump}$ | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Mechanical-to-hydraulic conversion efficiency |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall}$ | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{motor\,elec}} \times 100$ | % | Includes motor + drive losses |
| 11 | Specific Speed | $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | Used to classify pump type (dimensionless form) |
| 12 | Slip (internal recirculation) | $S$ | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{actual}$ | m³/s | Reverse flow through clearances |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation Sign Convention Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Trap)**
> - If the suction reservoir is **below** the pump centreline: use **positive** $h_s$ (suction lift).
> - If the suction reservoir is **above** the pump centreline: use **negative** $h_s$ (flooded suction).
> - A 1-mark error in sign convention is a classic deduction in KTU valuation.

### 2.3 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

- **Engine-room lube-oil circulation** in main propulsion diesel engines.
- **Boiler fuel-oil transfer** in steam ships.
- **Bilge and sludge pumping** where solids/abrasives are present.
- **Hydraulic power units (HPUs)** for steering gears, deck cranes, and hatch covers.
- **Cargo-oil stripping** in product/chemical tankers (low-NPSH progressive-cavity pumps).
- **Cooling fresh-water and sea-water** circulation on auxiliary heat exchangers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Discharge of a Screw Pump

Consider a single-flight screw rotor of outer diameter $D_o$, inner (root) diameter $D_i$, and pitch (lead per turn) $L_p$. In one complete rotation, every point on the screw surface travels an axial distance equal to the pitch.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the helix thread (inter-tooth volume):**

The fluid is trapped between two cylindrical surfaces of diameter $D_o$ and $D_i$. The annular cross-sectional area is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Axial displacement per rotation:**

For one full turn, every fluid particle is displaced axially by exactly one lead length $L$. So the volume pumped per revolution per rotor is:

$$
V_{per\,rot} = A \times L = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \times L
$$

**Step 3 — Account for speed and number of rotors:**

If the pump has $n_r$ rotors and runs at $N$ revolutions per second, the theoretical discharge is:

$$
Q_{th} = n_r \times \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \times L \times N
$$

If $N$ is in **rpm**, convert to rps by dividing by 60:

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{n_r \cdot \pi \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L \cdot N}{4 \times 60}} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Dimensional Consistency**
> $[\pi] = $ dimensionless, $[D_o^2 - D_i^2] = $ m², $[L] = $ m, $[N/60] = $ 1/s.
> Product = m³/s ✓

**Step 4 — Compute Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{th}} \times 100\,\%
$$

A well-machined three-screw marine pump typically has $\eta_{vol} \in [85\,\%, 95\,\%]$; the loss is the **slip** (back-flow) through the small clearances at the screw-to-screw and screw-to-casing interfaces.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Manometric Head

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the free surface of the suction tank (point 1) and the free surface of the discharge tank (point 2), and adding pump-work term $H_m$:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_m = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_L
$$

With both tanks open to atmosphere, $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$, and both surface velocities $v_1 \approx v_2 \approx 0$. So:

$$
H_m = (z_2 - z_1) + H_L
$$

**Step 1 — Define the static head as the sum of suction-side and delivery-side vertical heights:**

$$
H_{static} = h_s + h_d
$$

**Step 2 — Define the pressure head difference across the pump (measured by Bourdon gauges at the pump's suction and delivery flanges):**

$$
H_{pressure} = \frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and neglect minor losses in the test rig (short pipe runs):**

$$
\boxed{H_m = h_s + h_d + \frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters on board ship**
> The discharge of a screw pump is nearly independent of the delivery head (it is positive displacement). The test verifies how much $H_m$ the pump can produce at a given speed before the slip becomes excessive and $\eta_{vol}$ collapses.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Input Power from Torque Measurement

A rotary torsion sensor / dynamometer reads torque $T$ (N·m) at a known shaft speed $N$ (rpm).

**Step 1 — Angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical power transmitted through the shaft:**

$$
P_{in} = T \times \omega = T \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kilowatts for KTU lab sheet:**

$$
\boxed{P_{in}\,(\text{kW}) = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi N T}{30\,000}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Overall Efficiency Chain

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{drive} \times \eta_{pump}
$$

where

$$
\eta_{pump} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{in}}
$$

If the lab uses an **electrical input wattmeter** instead of a torque-meter:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{electrical}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Varying Discharge on Performance

The test is conducted by adjusting a **throttle valve** on the delivery line to vary the back-pressure, and recording the resulting $Q$ (measuring tank), $H_m$ (gauges), and $P_{in}$ (torque-meter).

| Observation Set | Throttle Position | $Q$ | $H_m$ | $P_{in}$ | $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{pump}$ |
|----------------|-------------------|-----|-------|----------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Fully open | Maximum | Minimum | Lower | High | Lower |
| 2 | Partly closed | Mid | Mid | Mid | Mid | Mid |
| 3 | Nearly closed | Minimum | Maximum | Highest | Lower | Mid |
| 4 | Re-open | Mid | Mid | Mid | Mid | Mid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected Graphical Trends for a Screw Pump**
> 1. **Q vs $H_m$:** Slight decrease of $Q$ as $H_m$ rises (almost vertical) — characteristic of PD pump.
