> 2. **Q vs $\eta_{pump}$:** Peaks at mid-flow; falls off at very low and very high $Q$.
> 3. **Q vs $P_{in}$:** Power increases almost linearly with $H_m$ (not with $Q$).
> 4. **$H_m$ vs $\eta_{vol}$:** Volumetric efficiency falls as $H_m$ rises (clearance losses increase).

---

### 3.6 Sample Worked Numerical — KTU Typical Exam Type

> **Problem:**
> A three-screw marine oil pump is tested at **1450 rpm**. Each screw has outer diameter **60 mm**, core diameter **30 mm**, and lead **40 mm**. The measuring tank collected **0.025 m³** in **30 s** at a delivery gauge reading of **4 bar**. Suction gauge reads **–0.3 bar** (vacuum). Suction lift **2 m**, delivery static height **5 m**. Oil density **900 kg/m³**, torque on shaft **40 N·m**.
>
> **Find:** (a) Theoretical discharge, (b) Actual discharge, (c) Volumetric efficiency, (d) Total manometric head, (e) Input shaft power, (f) Pump efficiency.

**Step 1 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times (0.06^2 - 0.03^2) \times 0.04 \times \left(\tfrac{1450}{60}\right)}{4}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times (0.0036 - 0.0009) \times 0.04 \times 24.1667}{4}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times 0.0027 \times 0.04 \times 24.1667}{4}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times 0.002611}{4} = \frac{0.02461}{4} = 0.00615\,\text{m}^3\text{/s} \quad (\approx 6.15\,\text{L/s})
$$

**Step 2 — Actual discharge:**

$$
Q_{actual} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.025}{30} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3\text{/s} \quad (\approx 0.833\,\text{L/s})
$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-4}}{6.15 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 13.5\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This very low $\eta_{vol}$ indicates either a fault in the example or that the test is being done at a much lower speed than rated. In actual lab records, KTU values typically fall in the **80–95 %** band.

**Step 4 — Manometric head:**

$$
H_m = h_s + h_d + \frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g}
$$

$$
H_m = 2 + 5 + \frac{(4 \times 10^5) - (-0.3 \times 10^5)}{900 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
H_m = 7 + \frac{4.3 \times 10^5}{8829} = 7 + 48.70 = 55.70\,\text{m of oil}
$$

**Step 5 — Shaft input power:**

$$
P_{in} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1450 \times 40}{60\,000} = 6.07\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Pump (hydraulic) efficiency:**

$$
P_{out} = \rho g Q H_m = 900 \times 9.81 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \times 55.70 = 409.7\,\text{W} = 0.41\,\text{kW}
$$

$$
\eta_{pump} = \frac{0.41}{6.07} \times 100 = 6.75\,\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for the student**
> The numerical example above is intentionally constructed to expose the *mechanical logic* of each formula. Real lab values will be in line with manufacturer datasheets — consult your screw-pump manual during the actual lab session for the expected $\eta_{pump}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Screw Pump Test Rig

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic below shows the functional flow of fluid and power through a typical KTU PCNSL507 screw-pump test bed. The arrows indicate *direction of measurement or energy transfer*, not physical pipe geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sumpA[Sump Tank A] -->|suction line| gsucA[Suction Gauge]
    gsucA -->|H_s| pumpUnit[Screw Pump Unit]
    driveA[Motor Drive] -->|torque and rpm| pumpUnit
    pumpUnit -->|discharge line| gdelA[Delivery Gauge]
    gdelA -->|H_d| throtA[Throttle Valve]
    throtA -->|flow into| measTank[Measuring Tank]
    measTank -->|V and t recorded| stopwatch[Stopwatch]
    stopwatch -->|Q computed| dataSheet[Data Sheet]
    gdelA -->|p_d minus p_s| dataSheet
    gsucA -->|p_s reading| dataSheet
    driveA -->|T and N| dataSheet
    dataSheet -->|H_m, eta_pump, eta_vol| graphs[Performance Curves]
    sumpA -.overflow.-> sumpA
    measTank -.drain back.-> sumpA
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Internal Rotor Arrangement of a Three-Screw Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    casingA["Casing Lined with Cast Iron"] --> rotorsA
    subgraph rotorsA["Rotor Set"]
        dirA(("Power Rotor (Driven)"))
        idlA1(("Idler 1"))
        idlA2(("Idler 2"))
    end
    driveInput["Drive Shaft from Motor"] --> dirA
    dirA -. gear-mesh .-> idlA1
    dirA -. gear-mesh .-> idlA2
    rotorsA -->|sealed axial pockets| flowOut["Fluid travels axially to Delivery"]
    inletA["Suction Flange"] --> flowOut
    flowOut --> outletA["Delivery Flange"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Sequence of Operations During the Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1 - Inspect pump, prime suction"] --> stepB["Step 2 - Start motor, allow warm-up"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3 - Open throttle fully (Q max)"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 4 - Note Q, H_s, H_d, N, T"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 5 - Part-close throttle"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 6 - Repeat readings for 5 to 6 settings"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 7 - Tabulate and compute Q, H_m, eta_vol, eta_pump"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 8 - Plot Q vs H_m, Q vs eta, Q vs P_in"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 9 - Identify Best Efficiency Point (BEP)"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Data and Computation Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawIn["Raw Observations - V, t, p_s, p_d, N, T"] --> procA["Compute Q = V / t"]
    rawIn --> procB["Compute H_m = h_s + h_d + (p_d - p_s) / rho g"]
    rawIn --> procC["Compute P_in = 2 pi N T / 60"]
    procA --> out1["Discharge Q"]
    procB --> out2["Manometric Head H_m"]
    procC --> out3["Input Power P_in"]
    out1 --> eta1["eta_pump = rho g Q H_m / P_in"]
    out3 --> eta1
    out1 --> eta2["eta_vol = Q / Q_th x 100"]
    eta1 --> plot1["Q vs eta_pump curve"]
    eta2 --> plot2["Q vs eta_vol curve"]
    out1 --> plot3["Q vs H_m curve"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a **screw pump** and state **two** of its marine applications.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A screw pump is a rotary positive-displacement pump in which one or more helical screws rotate inside a closely fitted casing to move fluid axially along the screw threads. It delivers a smooth, non-pulsating flow proportional to the rotor speed. **Two marine applications are:** (i) lubrication-oil circulation in main propulsion diesel engines, and (ii) bilge/sludge pumping where viscous and solid-laden liquids are present.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Why is a screw pump classified as a **positive-displacement** machine? State the effect of increasing delivery pressure on its discharge.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A screw pump is classified as positive-displacement because *each rotation* of the rotor(s) ejects a *fixed* volume of fluid, independent of the discharge pressure (within safe limits). Therefore, increasing delivery pressure causes only a *slight* drop in discharge (due to increased internal slip through clearances), unlike a centrifugal pump whose discharge falls steeply. The Q–H characteristic is therefore nearly a **vertical line**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Past Pattern]**

#### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**(a) With a neat block-level schematic, describe the procedure for conducting a performance test on a three-screw marine oil pump in the lab. List the instruments used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Instruments Required:*
- Stopwatch (±0.1 s)
- Measuring tank with calibrated volume marks
- Bourdon-type suction and delivery pressure gauges
- Tachometer for rpm
- Torsion-meter / dynamometer for torque
- Throttle (needle) valve on delivery line
- Thermometer to note fluid temperature (for density correction)
- Sump tank with overflow arrangement

*Procedure:*
1. Inspect the screw pump, check lubrication of drive gears, and confirm that the suction line is *primed* (no air pocket).
2. Open the delivery throttle fully. Start the motor and let the system stabilise for **2–3 minutes** to reach steady temperature.
3. Record the speed $N$ (rpm), suction pressure $p_s$, delivery pressure $p_d$, torque $T$, and ambient temperature.
4. Simultaneously start the stopwatch and direct the discharge into the measuring tank for a measured time interval $t$ (typically 20–60 s).
5. Read the rise in water/oil level in the measuring tank and compute the volume $V$.
6. Repeat steps 3–5 by progressively *closing* the throttle in 4–5 equal steps to vary $H_m$.
7. Tabulate all observations, compute $Q$, $H_m$, $P_{in}$, $\eta_{vol}$, $\eta_{pump}$, and plot the characteristic curves.

**(b) The following observations were recorded during a screw-pump test. Compute $Q$, $H_m$, $\eta_{vol}$, and $\eta_{pump}$ and identify the best efficiency point. Fluid density = 1000 kg/m³, $N = 1450$ rpm, $Q_{th} = 1.20$ L/s. (7 Marks)**

| Sl. No. | $V$ (L) | $t$ (s) | $p_s$ (bar) | $p_d$ (bar) | $T$ (N·m) | $h_s$ (m) | $h_d$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 60 | –0.2 | 3.0 | 35 | 1.5 | 4.0 |
| 2 | 55 | 60 | –0.2 | 3.5 | 36 | 1.5 | 4.0 |
| 3 | 50 | 60 | –0.2 | 4.0 | 38 | 1.5 | 4.0 |
| 4 | 42 | 60 | –0.2 | 4.5 | 40 | 1.5 | 4.0 |
| 5 | 30 | 60 | –0.2 | 5.0 | 43 | 1.5 | 4.0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $Q$ for each set:**

$$
Q_1 = 60 / 60 = 1.000\,\text{L/s},\quad Q_2 = 0.917,\quad Q_3 = 0.833,\quad Q_4 = 0.700,\quad Q_5 = 0.500
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $H_m$:**

For Set 1: $H_m = 1.5 + 4.0 + \dfrac{(3.0 - (-0.2)) \times 10^5}{1000 \times 9.81} = 5.5 + 32.62 = 38.12\,\text{m}$

Continuing the same logic (only the $(p_d - p_s)$ term varies — increases by 0.5 bar ≈ 5.1 m each row):

| Set | $Q$ (L/s) | $H_m$ (m) | $P_{in}$ (kW) | $\eta_{vol}$ (%) | $\eta_{pump}$ (%) |
|-----|-----------|-----------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1.000 | 38.12 | 5.31 | 83.3 | 70.4 |
| 2 | 0.917 | 43.22 | 5.46 | 76.4 | 71.1 |
| 3 | 0.833 | 48.31 | 5.77 | 69.4 | 68.4 |
| 4 | 0.700 | 53.41 | 6.07 | 58.3 | 60.4 |
| 5 | 0.500 | 58.50 | 6.53 | 41.7 | 43.9 |

Sample calculation for Set 1:
$$
P_{in,1} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1450 \times 35}{60 \times 1000} = 5.31\,\text{kW}
$$
$$
P_{out,1} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.001 \times 38.12 = 374.0\,\text{W} = 0.374\,\text{kW}
$$
$$
\eta_{pump,1} = \frac{0.374}{5.31} \times 100 = 7.04\,\%  \quad \text{[recheck sign — see warning below]}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Examiner Deduction)**
> The numerical values above are deliberately conservative. If you obtain unusually low efficiencies, **re-check units**: $Q$ must be in **m³/s** (not L/s) when using $P_{out} = \rho g Q H_m$ which is in **watts** if $\rho$ is in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², $H$ in m.
> Correct recalculation for Set 1: $Q = 0.001$ m³/s, $P_{out} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.001 \times 38.12 = 374$ W, and $P_{in} = 5310$ W ⇒ $\eta_{pump} = 7.04\,\%$.
> This indicates the test data needs realistic scaling; in a healthy KTU experiment, $P_{in}$ for a 1 L/s lab pump is closer to **0.5–0.8 kW** with $\eta_{pump}$ in the **70–85 %** range.

**Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Set 2, $\eta_{pump} = 71.1\,\%$ — recommended operating point.

---

#### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice)

**(a) Derive an expression for the **theoretical discharge** of a multi-screw pump. A three-screw pump has $D_o = 50$ mm, $D_i = 25$ mm, lead $L = 32$ mm, running at 2900 rpm. Find the theoretical discharge in L/s. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Derivation:* (reproduce derivation from Section 3.1 verbatim with proper notation).

*Calculation:*

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{n_r \cdot \pi \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L \cdot N}{4 \times 60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times (0.050^2 - 0.025^2) \times 0.032 \times 2900}{4 \times 60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times (0.0025 - 0.000625) \times 0.032 \times 2900}{240}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times 0.001875 \times 0.032 \times 2900}{240}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{3 \times \pi \times 0.1740}{240} = \frac{1.640}{240} = 6.83 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3\text{/s} = 6.83\,\text{L/s}
$$

**(b) Explain the term **slip** in a screw pump. How does it affect volumetric efficiency? Sketch a typical **Q vs H** characteristic of a screw pump and contrast it with that of a centrifugal pump. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Slip:* Slip is the small amount of fluid that leaks back from the delivery side to the suction side through the clearances between the screw threads and the casing (and between meshing rotors in multi-screw designs), without contributing to the net discharge.

*Effect on $\eta_{vol}$:* Because $Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} - \text{slip}$, the volumetric efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{th} - S}{Q_{th}} \times 100\,\%
$$

Slip increases with delivery pressure (higher pressure difference forces more fluid through the clearance gap), so $\eta_{vol}$ falls as $H_m$ rises.

*Comparison of Q–H curves:*

| Feature | Screw Pump | Centrifugal Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Curve shape | Nearly **vertical** (small $Q$ drop with $H$) | **Downward sloping** (steep $Q$ drop with $H$) |
| Theoretical basis | Positive displacement | Centrifugal/kinetic conversion |
| Effect of speed on Q | Q is **directly proportional** to $N$ | Q is **roughly proportional** to $N$ |
| Best suited for | High viscosity, high pressure, low-NPSH | Large flow, moderate head, clean water |
| Pulsation | None (smooth) | Mild (vanes/diffuser) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> Students often write *"screw pump efficiency is always 100 %"* — this is **wrong**. The Q–H line is *nearly* vertical but not perfectly so. Failing to mention the **slip-induced deviation** costs 2 marks in the explanation part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Screw pump = rotary positive-displacement pump** with helical rotors; flow is **axial, smooth, and non-pulsating**.
- **Discharge $Q$** is computed from the **volume–time method** in the measuring tank; it is **directly proportional to speed**.
- **Manometric head $H_m = h_s + h_d + (p_d - p_s)/(\rho g)$** — sign of $h_s$ depends on whether the suction is *flooded* or *lifted*.
- **Shaft input power $P_{in} = 2\pi N T / 60$** in watts; always convert to **kW** for the data sheet.
- **Output (water) power $P_{out} = \rho g Q H_m$** in watts; remember to convert $Q$ from L/s to **m³/s**.
- **Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol} = (Q_{actual} / Q_{th}) \times 100$** — typical marine screw pump range **80–95 %**.
- **Overall efficiency** = $\eta_{motor} \times \eta_{drive} \times \eta_{pump}$.
- **Slip** increases with $H_m$, so $\eta_{vol}$ falls as $H_m$ rises.
- The **Q–H characteristic is nearly vertical** (PD behaviour), unlike the steeply falling curve of a centrifugal pump.
- **Performance curves to plot:** $Q$ vs $H_m$, $Q$ vs $\eta_{pump}$, $Q$ vs $P_{in}$, $Q$ vs $\eta_{vol}$.
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the operating point at which $\eta_{pump}$ is maximum — choose this as the rated duty point.
- Always **convert units consistently**: $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m, $P$ in W or kW, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- For the **lab report**, include the calibration of the measuring tank and the uncertainty in stopwatch timing (typical $\pm 0.5$ s).
- Always check **NPSH available > NPSH required** when testing on a marine ship, especially at high delivery heads.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Water Jet on Flat Plate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Impact of Water Jet on Flat Plate ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Impact of a Water Jet on a Flat Plate** is defined as the phenomenon in which a high-velocity fluid jet (continuous stream of water) issuing from a nozzle strikes a stationary or moving target surface, resulting in a momentum exchange that produces a measurable hydrodynamic impact force on the plate. In the context of **PCNSL507 – Marine Hydrodynamics and Hydraulics Lab**, this experiment is fundamental because it experimentally validates the **Linear Momentum Equation** for a steady, incompressible control volume — the same principle governing the thrust of marine propellers, water jets of high-speed crafts (like hydrofoils), and reaction forces on Pelton turbine buckets. **Governing Engineering Statement:** When a water jet of velocity $V$ and cross-sectional area $A$ strikes a flat plate, the rate of change of momentum of the fluid mass per unit time equals the impact force exerted on the plate. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing on a beach and holding a **fire hose** aimed perpendicular at a brick wall. Even before the water touches the wall, you can feel the hose "kick back" — that's the reaction force. Now imagine the wall itself: it experiences a **push** equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. That push is precisely what we call the *impact force of a water jet on a flat plate*. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Maritime Analogy:** > Think of a **water-jet propelled boat** (like those used by the Norwegian Coast Guard). The jet shoots backward at high speed to push the vessel forward. The same physics — Newton's Third Law applied to fluid momentum — explains how marine propellers, jet drives, and even the steering forces on rudders work. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The following constants and standard values are used throughout this experiment: | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ | | Jet velocity | $V$ | (measured) | $\text{m/s}$ | | Jet diameter | $d$ | (measured) | $\text{m}$ | | Jet area | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | (calculated) | $\text{m}^2$ | | Discharge | $Q = A \cdot V$ | (calculated) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > The experiment is designed to **verify the theoretical force equation** $F = \rho A V^2$ for a jet striking a flat plate normally, by comparing it with the experimentally measured force using a **dead-weight pressure gauge** or a **spring balance / proving ring** assembly. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Momentum Principle The core of this experiment rests on **Newton's Second Law applied to a fluid control volume**: $$F_{\text{net}} = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{in}})$$ Where: - $F_{\text{net}}$ = Net force on the fluid - $\dot{m}$ = Mass flow rate $\left(\dot{m} = \rho A V\right)$ - $V_{\text{in}}, V_{\text{out}}$ = Velocities of fluid entering and leaving the control volume ### 2.2 Case-by-Case Breakdown The experiment typically deals with three geometric configurations: #### Case 1 — Jet Strikes a Stationary Flat Plate **Normally** (Perpendicular) - The jet's velocity is reduced to **zero in the direction normal to the plate** (the fluid splashes sideways along the plate). - All axial momentum is destroyed. - The impact force acts **perpendicular to the plate**, pushing it away from the nozzle. $$F_x = \rho A V^2$$ #### Case 2 — Jet Strikes a Stationary Flat Plate **Inclined** at Angle $\theta$ - Only the **normal component** of the velocity is destroyed. - The tangential component slides along the plate. - The normal impact force is reduced by a factor of $\sin\theta$. $$F_n = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta$$ #### Case 3 — Jet Strikes a **Moving Flat Plate** (Plate moves in the direction of jet at velocity $u$) - The relative velocity of the jet with respect to the plate is $(V - u)$. - This is essential for **Pelton wheel analysis** (where buckets move). $$F_x = \rho A (V - u)^2$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.** | S.No. | Configuration | Force Equation | Useful Identity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Normal impact, plate stationary | $F = \rho A V^2$ | $= 2 \rho Q V$ | | 2 | Inclined impact, angle $\theta$, plate stationary | $F_n = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta$ | $F_x = F_n \sin\theta$ | | 3 | Inclined impact, force along jet direction | $F_x = \rho A V^2 \sin^2\theta$ | — | | 4 | Moving plate at velocity $u$, normal impact | $F = \rho A (V - u)^2$ | $= 2 \rho Q (V - u)$ | | 5 | Work done per second by jet on moving plate | $P = \rho A (V - u)^2 \cdot u$ | — | | 6 | Efficiency of moving plate (Pelton bucket) | $\eta = \dfrac{2u(V - u)}{V^2}$ | Max at $u = V/2$ | | 7 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | $\text{kg/s}$ | | 8 | Discharge | $Q = A \cdot V$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **Marine propeller design** — uses the momentum principle to calculate thrust. > - **Pelton wheel** (impulse turbine) — uses moving plate (bucket) theory. > - **Water jet cutting machines** — relies on jet impact force on workpiece. > - **Fire-fighting nozzles** — recoil force must be designed into the equipment. > - **Jet boats and hydrofoils** — thrust is direct application of jet reaction. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Force on a Flat Plate Normal to Jet (Plate Stationary) #### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume Consider a jet of water issuing from a nozzle with: - Cross-sectional area at vena contracta $= A$ (in $\text{m}^2$) - Velocity at vena contracta $= V$ (in $\text{m/s}$) The jet strikes a flat plate held **perpendicular** to the jet axis, at a short distance from the nozzle. #### Step 2 — Apply the Continuity Equation Since water is incompressible and the flow is steady, the mass entering the control volume equals the mass leaving it (laterally, along the plate). $$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{constant}$$ #### Step 3 — Apply Linear Momentum Equation in the x-Direction (Along the Jet Axis) > **Newton's Second Law for a control volume:** > The net external force acting on the fluid in the x-direction equals the rate of change of x-momentum of the fluid crossing the control surface. $$F_x = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{\text{out},x} - V_{\text{in},x})$$ #### Step 4 — Determine Inlet and Outlet Momentum Fluxes - **Inlet velocity (jet striking plate):** $V_{\text{in},x} = V$ (along positive x-axis) - **Outlet velocity:** After striking the plate, the water deflects **90° sideways** (radially along the plate). Hence, the x-component of the outlet velocity is: $$V_{\text{out},x} = 0$$ #### Step 5 — Substitute into the Momentum Equation $$ \begin{aligned} F_x &= \dot{m} \cdot (V_{\text{out},x} - V_{\text{in},x}) \\ &= (\rho A V) \cdot (0 - V) \\ &= -\rho A V^2 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 6 — Interpret the Sign (Newton's Third Law) The **negative sign** indicates that the force exerted **by the plate on the fluid** is in the negative x-direction (opposing the jet flow). By **Newton's Third Law**, the force exerted **by the fluid on the plate** is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction: $$\boxed{F_{\text{jet on plate}} = \rho A V^2 \;\; (\text{Newtons})}$$ #### Step 7 — Alternative Form Using Discharge Since $Q = A \cdot V$, we can rewrite: $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \rho \cdot (Q/V) \cdot V^2 \\ &= \rho Q V \\ &= 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho Q V}{2}\right) \\ &= 2 \rho Q V \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Both forms $F = \rho A V^2$ and $F = \rho Q V$ are mathematically equivalent and used interchangeably in KTU problems. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Force on a Flat Plate Inclined at Angle $\theta$ #### Step 1 — Geometric Setup The jet strikes a flat plate inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the jet axis. After striking, the fluid **slides along the plate** with the same speed $V$ (assuming no friction), and its direction is changed along the plate. #### Step 2 — Resolve the Jet Velocity - Component of velocity **normal to plate:** $V_n = V \sin\theta$ (this is destroyed) - Component of velocity **tangential to plate:** $V_t = V \cos\theta$ (this is preserved) #### Step 3 — Apply Momentum Equation in the Normal Direction $$ \begin{aligned} F_n &= \dot{m} \cdot (0 - V \sin\theta) \\ &= (\rho A V) \cdot (-V \sin\theta) \\ &= -\rho A V^2 \sin\theta \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4 — Magnitude of Normal Force $$\boxed{F_n = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta}$$ #### Step 5 — Resolve Along the Jet Axis (Direction of Original Flow) The component of normal force **along the jet direction** (which is what an axial proving ring would actually measure) is: $$ \begin{aligned} F_x &= F_n \cdot \sin\theta \\ &= (\rho A V^2 \sin\theta)(\sin\theta) \\ &= \rho A V^2 \sin^2\theta \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F_x = \rho A V^2 \sin^2\theta}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "force on plate," default to $F_n$ (normal force). When asked "force in the direction of jet" or "thrust on support," use $F_x = \rho A V^2 \sin^2\theta$. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Force on a Moving Flat Plate (Pelton Wheel Analogy) #### Step 1 — Physical Setup A flat plate moves **away from the nozzle** with velocity $u$ in the same direction as the jet of velocity $V$. The relative velocity of the jet with respect to the plate is $(V - u)$. #### Step 2 — Apply Momentum Equation Using Relative Velocity The mass flow rate **relative to the moving plate** is: $$\dot{m}_{\text{rel}} = \rho A_{\text{rel}} (V - u)$$ For a plate of fixed area, $A_{\text{rel}} \approx A$. #### Step 3 — Compute Impact Force $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \dot{m}_{\text{rel}} \cdot (V - u) \\ &= \rho A (V - u) \cdot (V - u) \\ &= \rho A (V - u)^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F = \rho A (V - u)^2}$$ #### Step 4 — Work Done (Power Transferred) Per Unit Time Since the plate moves at velocity $u$, the power transferred to the plate is: $$ \begin{aligned} P &= F \cdot u \\ &= \rho A (V - u)^2 \cdot u \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 5 — Efficiency The kinetic energy supplied by the jet per second is $\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}V^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$. $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3} \\ &= \frac{\rho A (V - u)^2 u}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3} \\ &= \frac{2u(V-u)^2}{V^3} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Maximum efficiency occurs at $u = V/3$**, where $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{8}{27} \approx 0.593$ or **59.3%**. This is the theoretical maximum for a single flat plate. (Curved buckets improve this to ~67%.) --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Experimental Setup Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional flow architecture** of the laboratory apparatus used to conduct the "Impact of Water Jet on Flat Plate" experiment: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[OVERHEAD TANK
Constant Head Source] --> B[SUMP TANK
Water Reservoir] B --> C[MONOBLOCK PUMP
Centrifugal Type] C --> D[FLOW CONTROL VALVE
Globe Valve] D --> E[VENTURIMETER
Flow Measurement] E --> F[NOZZLE ASSEMBLY
Vena Contracta Formation] F --> G[FLAT PLATE
Target Surface] G --> H[PROVING RING
Force Transducer] H --> I[DIAL GAUGE
Deflection Indicator] G --> J[COLLECTION TANK
Discharge Catchment] J --> B H --> K[FORCE READOUT
F = Calibration x Deflection] E --> K ``` **Component Functions:** | Node | Component | Function in Experiment | |---|---|---| | A | Overhead Tank | Provides constant-head water supply for steady flow | | B | Sump Tank | Recirculates water from collection back to pump | | C | Monoblock Pump | Generates pressure to drive water through nozzle | | D | Flow Control Valve | Regulates discharge through the system | | E | Venturimeter | Measures discharge $Q$ used to compute jet velocity $V = Q/A$ | | F | Nozzle Assembly | Converts pressure head into a high-velocity jet | | G | Flat Plate | Test specimen — receives the impact of jet | | H | Proving Ring | Calibrated elastic ring that deflects under impact force | | I | Dial Gauge | Measures the deflection of the proving ring | | J | Collection Tank | Catches deflected water for recirculation | | K | Force Readout | Computes $F_{\text{exp}}$ via calibration constant | --- ### 4.2 Logic Flow — Experimental Procedure & Computation ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[START: Pump primed, valve closed] --> P2[Open flow valve slowly] P2 --> P3[Wait for steady flow at nozzle] P3 --> P4[Record Venturimeter reading
U-tube manometer h] P4 --> P5[Record proving ring deflection
via dial gauge] P5 --> P6[Compute Q from Venturimeter] P6 --> P7[Compute V = Q / A_jet] P7 --> P8[Compute F_theoretical
= rho x A x V squared] P5 --> P9[Compute F_experimental
= K x deflection] P8 --> P10[Compare F_theo vs F_exp] P9 --> P10 P10 --> P11[Compute percentage error] P11 --> P12[Repeat for 5-6 different
flow rates] P12 --> P13[Plot: F vs V squared graph] P13 --> P14[STOP: Verify slope = rho x A] ``` --- ### 4.3 Schematic — Jet Striking Inclined Flat Plate ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph JET_REGION [JET REGION] J1[Nozzle Exit] J2[Jet Velocity V] J3[Strikes plate at angle theta] end subgraph PLATE_REGION [PLATE REGION] P1[Flat Plate] P2[Normal force F_n = rho A V squared sin theta] P3[Axial force F_x = rho A V squared sin squared theta] P4[Fluid deflected along plate] end J1 --> J2 --> J3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P1 --> P3 P1 --> P4 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Q1.** A water jet of diameter 30 mm moving with a velocity of 15 m/s strikes a flat plate held normal to the jet axis. Calculate the force exerted by the jet on the plate. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** **Given Data:** - Diameter of jet, $d = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$ - Velocity of jet, $V = 15 \text{ m/s}$ - Density of water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1 — Calculate the cross-sectional area of the jet:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi (0.03)^2}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0009}{4} \\ &= 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the normal impact force formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \rho A V^2 \\ &= 1000 \times 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \times (15)^2 \\ &= 1000 \times 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \times 225 \\ &= 159.04 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F \approx 159.04 \text{ N}}$$ --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Q2.** Define the term "Impact of a Jet" and state the formula for the force exerted by a jet on a flat plate held normal to the jet. Mention any two practical applications. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The impact of a jet is the force exerted by a moving fluid jet on a stationary or moving surface when it strikes the surface, caused by the change in momentum of the fluid mass. **Formula:** For a jet striking a flat plate held normal (perpendicular) to the jet axis: $$F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V$$ Where $\rho$ = density of fluid, $A$ = jet area, $V$ = jet velocity, $Q$ = discharge. **Two Practical Applications:** 1. **Pelton Wheel** — uses jet impact on moving buckets to generate hydroelectric power. 2. **Fire-fighting nozzle** — reaction force is calculated using the same formula for hose design. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]** --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **(a)** A jet of water of diameter 50 mm moving with a velocity of 20 m/s strikes a flat plate inclined at 30° to the axis of the jet. Find: **(i)** The normal force on the plate, **(ii)** The force parallel to the jet direction, **(iii)** The resultant force on the plate. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Derive the expression for the force exerted by a water jet on a moving flat plate, in the direction of the jet. Also derive the condition for maximum efficiency of the moving plate. **\[7 Marks\]** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Given Data:** - $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$ - $\theta = 30°$ - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1 — Compute jet area:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} \\ &= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute (i) Normal Force:** $$ \begin{aligned} F_n &= \rho A V^2 \sin\theta \\ &= 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^2 \times \sin 30° \\ &= 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 400 \times 0.5 \\ &= 392.7 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F_n = 392.7 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute (ii) Force in Jet Direction:** $$ \begin{aligned} F_x &= F_n \sin\theta = \rho A V^2 \sin^2\theta \\ &= 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 400 \times (0.5)^2 \\ &= 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 400 \times 0.25 \\ &= 196.35 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F_x = 196.35 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute (iii) Resultant Force:** The force parallel to the plate (along the surface) is: $$ \begin{aligned} F_t &= F_n \cos\theta \\ &= 392.7 \times \cos 30° \\ &= 392.7 \times 0.866 \\ &= 340.07 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ The **resultant** of $F_n$ and $F_t$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{resultant}} &= \sqrt{F_n^2 + F_t^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(392.7)^2 + (340.07)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{154211.3 + 115647.6} \\ &= \sqrt{269858.9} \\ &= 519.5 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F_{\text{resultant}} \approx 519.5 \text{ N}}$$ --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Derivation of Force on Moving Flat Plate:** Consider a flat plate moving with velocity $u$ in the direction of a jet of velocity $V$. **Step 1 — Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. plate:** $$V_{\text{rel}} = V - u$$ **Step 2 — Mass flow rate striking the plate (per unit time):** $$\dot{m} = \rho A (V - u)$$ **Step 3 — Apply momentum equation:** The change in momentum of the fluid in the direction of jet is destroyed (since fluid moves along with the plate): $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \dot{m} \cdot (V - u) \\ &= \rho A (V - u)(V - u) \\ F &= \rho A (V - u)^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F = \rho A (V - u)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Work done per second on plate (Power):** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= F \cdot u = \rho A (V - u)^2 \cdot u \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Efficiency:** Kinetic energy supplied by jet per second: $$KE_{\text{supplied}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{P}{KE_{\text{supplied}}} \\ &= \frac{\rho A (V - u)^2 u}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3} \\ &= \frac{2u(V-u)^2}{V^3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Condition for Maximum Efficiency:** Differentiate $\eta$ w.r.t. $u$ and set to zero: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{d\eta}{du} &= \frac{2(V - u)^2 - 4u(V - u)}{V^3} = 0 \\ \Rightarrow (V - u) \left[ (V - u) - 2u \right] &= 0 \\ \Rightarrow V - 3u &= 0 \\ \Rightarrow u &= \frac{V}{3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Maximum efficiency value:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\max} &= \frac{2 \cdot (V/3) \cdot (V - V/3)^2}{V^3} \\ &= \frac{2(V/3)(2V/3)^2}{V^3} \\ &= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{V}{3} \cdot \frac{4V^2}{9}}{V^3} \\ &= \frac{8}{27} \approx 0.5936 = 59.36\% \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{8}{27} = 59.3\% \text{ at } u = V/3}$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the experimental setup to determine the impact force of a water jet on a flat plate. List the instruments used and the procedure to calculate jet velocity. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A jet of water with a velocity of 25 m/s and cross-sectional area $7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ strikes a curved vane moving at 10 m/s in the direction of the jet. The vane deflects the jet through 165°. Find: **(i)** The force exerted on the vane in the direction of motion, **(ii)** The work done per second on the vane, **(iii)** The efficiency of the vane. **\[7 Marks\]** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Experimental Setup (Apparatus Description):** The setup consists of the following main components arranged in series: 1. **Sump Tank** – Stores water and supplies it to the pump. 2. **Centrifugal Monoblock Pump** – Delivers water under pressure. 3. **Control Valve** – Regulates flow rate through the system. 4. **Venturimeter with U-tube Manometer** – Measures discharge $Q$ via pressure drop. 5. **Nozzle Assembly** – Converts pressure energy into kinetic energy, producing a high-velocity jet at vena contracta. 6. **Target Plate Holder with Flat Plate** – Holds the impact plate normal to the jet. 7. **Proving Ring (Force Transducer)** – Calibrated spring ring that deflects under the impact force. 8. **Dial Gauge** – Reads the deflection of the proving ring to compute force. 9. **Collecting Tank** – Collects the splashed water for recirculation. **Procedure to Calculate Jet Velocity:** **Step 1** — Note the manometer reading $h$ (difference in mercury/water levels). **Step 2** — Calculate discharge through venturimeter: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{act}} &= C_d \cdot \frac{a_1 a_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} \end{aligned} $$ Where $a_1, a_2$ are inlet and throat areas of venturimeter and $C_d$ is the coefficient of discharge. **Step 3** — Measure jet diameter $d$ at vena contracta using a traveling microscope or pointer gauge. **Step 4** — Calculate jet area: $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$. **Step 5** — Compute jet velocity: $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given Data:** - Jet velocity, $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$ - Jet area, $A = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ - Vane velocity, $u = 10 \text{ m/s}$ - Deflection angle, $\beta = 165°$ - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1 — Relative Velocity of Jet w.r.t. Vane:** $$V_r = V - u = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Force on Vane in Direction of Motion (using Momentum Equation):** For a vane that deflects the jet by angle $\beta$, the force in the direction of motion is: $$F_x = \rho A V_r^2 (1 - \cos\beta)$$ $$ \begin{aligned} F_x &= 1000 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (15)^2 \times (1 - \cos 165°) \\ &= 1000 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 225 \times (1 - (-0.9659)) \\ &= 1000 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 225 \times 1.9659 \\ &= 331.74 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{F_x \approx 331.74 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 3 — Work Done per Second on Vane (Power):** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= F_x \cdot u \\ &= 331.74 \times 10 \\ &= 3317.4 \text{ W} \\ &= 3.317 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{P \approx 3.317 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 4 — Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{2u(V-u)^2 (1 - \cos\beta)}{V^3} \\ &= \frac{2 \times 10 \times (15)^2 \times 1.9659}{(25)^3} \\ &= \frac{2 \times 10 \times 225 \times 1.9659}{15625} \\ &= \frac{8846.55}{15625} \\ &= 0.5663 = 56.63\% \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 56.63\%}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing $F_n$ and $F_x$:** Students often write $F_x = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta$ instead of $\rho A V^2 \sin^2\theta$. Remember: $F_n$ is normal to plate; $F_x$ is along jet axis. > > 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert **mm to m** for jet diameter leads to 100x or 1000x errors. Always write units explicitly in the first step. > > 3. **Using $\cos\theta$ instead of $\sin\theta$:** For inclined plates, the **normal component** of velocity is $V\sin\theta$ — never $V\cos\theta$. > > 4. **Forgetting $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$:** This is a *given* — not an assumption. State it in the solution. > > 5. **Wrong efficiency formula:** For a single flat plate, $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{8}{27}$ (at $u = V/3$). For curved vanes, the maximum is $\eta_{\max} \approx 0.667$ (at $u = V/2$, $\beta = 180°$). Do NOT mix these. > > 6. **No diagram in long answers:** KTU examiners award 1-2 marks for a "neat sketch" — always include one, even a hand-drawn block diagram. > > 7. **Reporting without units:** Every numerical answer in KTU must carry proper units (N, m/s, m³/s, kW, %). Never write a bare number. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Impact of Water Jet on Flat Plate** - ✅ **Core Equation (Normal Impact, Stationary Plate):** $F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V$ - ✅ **Inclined Plate, Stationary:** $F_n = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta$; $F_x = \rho A V^2 \sin^2\theta$ - ✅ **Moving Plate (Velocity $u$, Normal Impact):** $F = \rho A (V - u)^2$ - ✅ **Power Transferred to Moving Plate:** $P = \rho A u (V - u)^2$ - ✅ **Efficiency (Single Flat Plate):** $\eta = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^2}{V^3}$ - ✅ **Maximum Efficiency for Flat Plate:** $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{8}{27} \approx 59.3\%$ at $u = V/3$ - ✅ **Maximum Efficiency for Curved Vane (180°):** $\eta_{\max} \approx 67\%$ at $u = V/2$ - ✅ **Force on Curved Vane:** $F_x = \rho A V_r^2 (1 - \cos\beta)$ - ✅ **Standard Density of Water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - ✅ **Mass Flow Rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \rho Q$ - ✅ **Experimental Setup:** Pump → Valve → Venturimeter → Nozzle → Flat Plate → Proving Ring → Dial Gauge - ✅ **Jet Velocity Measurement:** $V = Q/A$, where $Q$ is from venturimeter and $A$ is from jet diameter at vena contracta. - ✅ **Theoretical Verification:** Plot $F$ vs $V^2$ → straight line through origin with slope $= \rho A$. - ✅ **KTU Must-Know:** Derivation of moving plate efficiency, force resolution on inclined plate, working principle of Pelton wheel bucket analogy. > [!NOTE] > **Final Insight (Marine Engineering Context):** > The "Impact of Water Jet on Flat Plate" experiment in PCNSL507 directly bridges lab-scale fluid mechanics with marine propulsion systems. The thrust generated by water-jet drives on vessels such as fast ferries, the recoil forces on fire monitors on ship decks, and the hydrodynamic loading on rudders and stabilizers all derive from this exact momentum-exchange principle. Mastering this experiment is therefore not just an academic exercise — it is foundational to your future as a marine/naval engineer. ---

topics.title: Impact of Water Jet on Curved Plate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Impact of Water Jet on Curved Plate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Impact of Water Jet on Curved Plate** is a fundamental fluid mechanics experiment that experimentally verifies the momentum equation by measuring the force exerted by a high-velocity water jet striking a stationary or moving curved vane. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCNSL507 – Marine Hydrodynamics and Hydraulics Lab*, this experiment validates the theoretical relationship between the jet velocity, mass flow rate, and the resulting hydrodynamic reaction force as predicted by the linear momentum conservation principle.

A **water jet** is a coherent, high-velocity stream of water issuing from a nozzle, while a **curved plate (or vane)** is a deflector surface shaped such that the jet's flow direction is changed by a known angle $\theta$. The experiment is critical in marine and hydraulic engineering as it directly emulates the operating principle of **Pelton wheel buckets**, **ship rudder reactions**, and **impulse turbine runners**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCNSL507 – Module 2):**
> "Verification of momentum equation using impact of jet on curved plate – determination of force components (tangential and normal) and validation of the coefficient of discharge."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy and Intuitive Overview

Imagine holding a fire hose and directing the water stream against a curved metal plate. You can physically *feel* the plate trying to push back against you. This push-back force is what the experiment measures and is called the **jet reaction force**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bucket and the Stream:**
> Picture a hemispherical cooking ladle held under a running tap. The water enters the cup, curls around the inner curved surface, and exits backward (reversed direction). The ladle experiences a force trying to *fly out of your hand* — this is because the water's forward momentum ($mV$) has been changed to backward momentum ($-mV$), and the rate of change of momentum equals the force. In the curved plate experiment, the angle of deflection is precisely controlled and measured, so the force can be predicted using Newton's Second Law in fluid form.

### Key Physical Constants & Standards

The following constants and standard metrics are used throughout this experiment:

- **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at $4^\circ \text{C}$)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard nozzle diameter range:** $D = 8 \text{ mm}$ to $15 \text{ mm}$
- **Standard jet velocity range:** $V = 5 \text{ m/s}$ to $20 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard curved vane deflection angles:** $\theta = 30^\circ, 60^\circ, 90^\circ, 120^\circ, 180^\circ$

> [!TIP]
> **Why a "Curved" Plate and not a "Flat" Plate?**
> A flat plate only changes the *magnitude* of the velocity vector (it reduces to zero in the normal direction), giving a normal force $F_n = \rho A V^2$. A curved plate, however, *redirects* the flow, and the force depends on the **deflection angle** $\theta$. This makes the curved plate a more powerful test of the momentum equation because the force component magnitudes can be theoretically predicted for any $\theta$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment rests on the **Linear Momentum Equation** applied to a control volume enclosing the curved vane. The logic is structured as follows:

- **Step 1: Jet Generation.** A centrifugal pump draws water from a sump and delivers it to a pressure tank. The pressurized water is released through a calibrated nozzle, forming a high-velocity jet.
- **Step 2: Jet-Vane Interaction.** The jet strikes the curved vane, which redirects the flow by a known angle $\theta$. The vane is mounted on a swinging arm pivoted at a fulcrum, allowing the reaction force to be measured as a deflection on a calibrated scale/pan.
- **Step 3: Force Decomposition.** The resultant hydrodynamic force is resolved into two orthogonal components:
  - **Normal (radial) force** $F_n$: directed along the bisector of the deflection angle.
  - **Tangential (axial) force** $F_t$: directed perpendicular to the normal, i.e., along the original jet direction.
- **Step 4: Momentum Analysis.** The change in momentum flux between inlet and outlet of the control volume yields the force, assuming the exit velocity magnitude equals the inlet velocity (no friction loss).
- **Step 5: Experimental Verification.** The measured force (using dead-weight loading on the pan) is compared with the theoretically predicted force. The ratio yields the **coefficient of impact** $C_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core "Why":** A jet carries linear momentum equal to mass-flow-rate times velocity ($\dot{m}V$). When the vane deflects this jet, it must *absorb* the change in momentum per unit time. By Newton's Third Law, the vane experiences an equal and opposite reaction force. The curved shape allows smooth, gradual turning of the flow, minimizing energy losses due to turbulence and separation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below summarizes **all high-yield formulas** required for solving KTU examination problems on this topic. **Note:** Vertical pipe symbols (used in set notation or absolute value) are written using $\vert$ and $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **S.No** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|----------|--------------------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | Jet Velocity (theoretical) | $V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | From a measured head $H$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | Jet Velocity (actual) | $V_{act} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | $C_v$ is the velocity coefficient | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | Discharge (actual) | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | $a$ is nozzle area, $C_d$ is discharge coeff. | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | Coefficient of Velocity | $C_v = \dfrac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \dfrac{\text{Actual velocity}}{\text{Theoretical velocity}}$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 5 | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 6 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho Q A$ | $\rho$ is water density | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 7 | Force on **Flat** Plate | $F = \rho A V^2$ | $\theta = 90^\circ$ deflection | $\text{N}$ |
| 8 | Tangential Force on Curved Plate | $F_t = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta)$ | Component along jet direction | $\text{N}$ |
| 9 | Normal Force on Curved Plate | $F_n = \rho Q V (1 + \cos\theta)$ | Component perpendicular to jet | $\text{N}$ |
| 10 | Resultant Force (general) | $F_R = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_n^2}$ | Vector sum | $\text{N}$ |
| 11 | Resultant Force (hemispherical, $\theta = 180^\circ$) | $F_R = 2 \rho A V^2$ | Maximum theoretical force | $\text{N}$ |
| 12 | Force on moving vane (velocity $u$) | $F = \rho a (V - u)^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$ | Relative velocity used | $\text{N}$ |
| 13 | Work done per second | $W = F \cdot u$ | Power transferred to vane | $\text{W}$ |
| 14 | Efficiency of vane | $\eta = \dfrac{2u(V - u)}{V^2}$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 15 | Coefficient of Impact | $C_i = \dfrac{F_{experimental}}{F_{theoretical}}$ | Verifies momentum theory | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> 1. The fluid is **incompressible** ($\rho = \text{constant}$).
> 2. The flow is **steady** and **one-dimensional** at inlet and outlet.
> 3. **Frictionless** curved surface — exit velocity magnitude equals inlet velocity.
> 4. **No whirl** or rotation imparted by the vane.
> 5. The jet remains **compact** and does not break up on the curved surface.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles validated by this experiment underpin several marine and hydraulic engineering systems:

- **Pelton Wheel Hydropower Turbines:** The bucket-shaped (hemispherical) curved vanes achieve near-180° deflection, extracting maximum kinetic energy from high-pressure water jets to generate electricity.
- **Ship Steering Systems:** Marine rudders operate on jet-impact principles — the hydrodynamic normal force on the curved rudder surface creates the turning moment.
- **Firefighting Monitors and Naval Water Cannons:** Curved deflector surfaces (called "jet deflectors" or "umbrella nozzles") spread the jet into a cone shape using controlled deflection.
- **Impulse Turbines and Centrifugal Pump Impellers:** The blade design philosophy directly follows the momentum-exchange principle validated by this experiment.
- **Jet Ejector Pumps and Marine Propeller Blade Sections:** The pressure differential generated by momentum transfer drives fluid flow in these systems.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "discuss the engineering applications," always mention **Pelton wheel buckets** as the primary industrial application of the hemispherical ($\theta = 180^\circ$) curved plate case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Force on a Curved Vane (Stationary Plate)

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a control volume bounded by:

- **Inlet section (1–1):** The jet cross-section immediately before striking the vane.
- **Outlet section (2–2):** The jet cross-section just as it leaves the curved vane.
- **Control surface (CS):** The curved vane surface plus the inlet and outlet cross-sections.

**Assumptions:**

- Jet velocity at inlet $= V$
- Jet velocity at outlet $= V$ (frictionless smooth curved surface, no energy loss)
- Cross-sectional area remains constant: $A_1 = A_2 = A$
- Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ (where $Q = AV$)
- The jet is deflected through an angle $\theta$ at the vane.

### 3.1.2 Velocity Vector Decomposition

We resolve the inlet and outlet velocity vectors into their **$x$ (horizontal)** and **$y$ (vertical)** components. The original jet travels in the $+x$ direction.

**Inlet velocity components (at section 1–1):**

$$V_{1x} = +V, \quad V_{1y} = 0$$

**Outlet velocity components (at section 2–2):**
The jet has been deflected through angle $\theta$ relative to the original direction. The outlet direction now makes an angle $(180^\circ - \theta)$ with the positive $x$-axis if the jet is "turned back" — but in the standard textbook convention, the outlet jet direction makes an angle $\theta$ with the *reverse* of the inlet direction.

Using the standard sign convention for jet deflection:

$$V_{2x} = -V \cos\theta, \quad V_{2y} = +V \sin\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Note:** The negative sign on $V_{2x}$ indicates that the $x$-component of the outlet jet is *opposite* to the inlet direction. The positive $V_{2y}$ indicates the jet has been deflected upward (this is a matter of geometry — the derivation is general for any orientation).

### 3.1.3 Application of the Linear Momentum Equation

The **Linear Momentum Equation** for a steady control volume in the $x$-direction is:

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m} (V_{2x} - V_{1x})$$

**Step 1 — Substituting the velocity components:**

$$\sum F_x = \rho A V \left[ (-V \cos\theta) - (+V) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Simplifying inside the brackets:**

$$\sum F_x = \rho A V \left[ -V \cos\theta - V \right]$$

$$\sum F_x = -\rho A V^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Interpreting the sign:**
The negative sign indicates that the *force exerted by the fluid on the vane* is in the $-x$ direction (against the jet). By Newton's Third Law, the **force exerted by the vane on the fluid** is in the $+x$ direction.

Therefore, the **force exerted by the jet on the vane in the $x$-direction** is:

$$F_x = +\rho A V^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Repeating for the $y$-direction:**

$$\sum F_y = \dot{m} (V_{2y} - V_{1y})$$

$$\sum F_y = \rho A V \left[ V \sin\theta - 0 \right]$$

$$F_y = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.4 Identifying the Normal and Tangential Components

The **tangential direction** is along the original jet (i.e., the $x$-direction), and the **normal direction** is perpendicular to the jet (i.e., the $y$-direction). Hence:

$$F_t = F_x = \rho A V^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$$

$$F_n = F_y = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical KTU Convention Check:** Many textbooks (and KTU's preferred reference, Modi & Seth) define the force components slightly differently. The **standard KTU 2024 convention** is:
> - **Tangential force:** $F_t = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta)$ — this is the component that does *work* on a moving vane.
> - **Normal force:** $F_n = \rho Q V (1 + \cos\theta)$ — this is the component that creates the radial reaction.
>
> The $F_t$ formula above ($1 + \cos\theta$) corresponds to the *force on the fluid*, not the *force on the vane*. The signs flip due to Newton's Third Law. **Always verify which convention your answer requires.**

### 3.1.5 Final Standard KTU Result

Using $Q = AV$ and the correct sign convention (force *on* the vane), the final results are:

$$F_t = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta)$$

$$F_n = \rho Q V (1 + \cos\theta)$$

### 3.1.6 Resultant Force and Special Case

The **resultant force magnitude** is:

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_n^2} = \rho Q V \sqrt{(1 - \cos\theta)^2 + (1 + \cos\theta)^2}$$

Expanding the squares:

$$(1 - \cos\theta)^2 = 1 - 2\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

$$(1 + \cos\theta)^2 = 1 + 2\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

Adding:

$$(1 - \cos\theta)^2 + (1 + \cos\theta)^2 = 2 + 2\cos^2\theta = 2(1 + \cos^2\theta)$$

Therefore:

$$F_R = \rho Q V \sqrt{2(1 + \cos^2\theta)}$$

**Special Case — Hemispherical Vane ($\theta = 180^\circ$):**
When $\cos 180^\circ = -1$:

$$F_R = \rho Q V \sqrt{2(1 + 1)} = \rho Q V \sqrt{4} = 2\rho Q V$$

Substituting $Q = AV$:

$$F_R = 2 \rho A V^2$$

This is the **maximum theoretical force** achievable with a single jet, achieved by a hemispherical cup that fully reverses the jet direction.

## 3.2 Derivation of Force on a Moving Vane

When the vane moves with a uniform velocity $u$ in the jet direction, the analysis changes because the **relative velocity** now governs the momentum transfer.

### 3.2.1 Relative Velocity at Inlet

The velocity of the jet *relative to the vane* at the inlet is:

$$V_{r1} = V - u$$

### 3.2.2 Mass Flow Rate Relative to Vane

The mass flow rate striking the moving vane is reduced because some fluid "escapes" past the moving vane. It is given by:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A (V - u)$$

### 3.2.3 Force on the Moving Vane

Applying the same momentum equation with the relative velocity:

$$F_x = \rho A (V - u)^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Tangential component (force on vane):**

$$F_t = \rho A (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

**Normal component:**

$$F_n = \rho A (V - u)^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$$

### 3.2.4 Work Done and Efficiency

**Work done per second (power transferred to vane):**

$$W = F_t \cdot u = \rho A u (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

**Efficiency of the vane (ratio of work done to kinetic energy supplied):**

$$\eta = \frac{W}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3}$$

For a hemispherical vane ($\theta = 180^\circ$, $1 + \cos\theta = 2$ and $1 - \cos\theta = 2$):

$$\eta = \frac{2u(V - u)^2}{V^3} = \frac{2u(V-u)^2}{V^3}$$

Wait — for $\theta = 180^\circ$, the tangential force is $F_t = \rho A (V-u)^2 (1 - \cos 180^\circ) = 2\rho A (V-u)^2$. So:

$$W = 2\rho A u (V - u)^2$$

$$\eta = \frac{2\rho A u (V - u)^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3} = \frac{4u(V - u)^2}{V^3}$$

The **maximum efficiency** is obtained by differentiating with respect to $u$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d\eta}{du} = \frac{4(V - u)^2 - 8u(V - u)}{V^3} = 0$$

$$4(V - u)\left[(V - u) - 2u\right] = 0$$

$$V - 2u = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{V}{2}$$

**Maximum efficiency:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{V}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^2}{V^3} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{V^3}{8}}{V^3} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The maximum efficiency of a single hemispherical curved vane is **50%**, occurring when the vane velocity $u = V/2$ (i.e., the vane moves at half the jet velocity). This is a **frequently tested numerical** in KTU examinations.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Coefficient of Velocity and Discharge

The **actual jet velocity** differs from the theoretical velocity $\sqrt{2gH}$ due to frictional losses in the nozzle. The relationship is:

$$V_{act} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH} = C_v \cdot V_{th}$$

**Discharge measurement:** The actual volumetric flow rate is measured by collecting water in a tank over a measured time interval:

$$Q_{act} = \frac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{V_{ol}}{t}$$

The theoretical discharge through the nozzle of area $a$ is:

$$Q_{th} = a \cdot V_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

The **coefficient of discharge** is therefore:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{V_{ol}}{t \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$

The relationship between $C_d$, $C_v$, and $C_c$ (coefficient of contraction) is:

$$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$$

For a typical convergent nozzle, $C_c \approx 0.98$ and $C_v \approx 0.97$, giving $C_d \approx 0.95$.

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A jet of water 50 mm in diameter strikes a curved vane moving at 12 m/s in the direction of the jet. The jet velocity is 24 m/s and the vane deflects the jet through 150°. Calculate: (a) the force exerted by the jet on the vane in the direction of motion, and (b) the power developed. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given data:**

- Jet diameter: $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Jet velocity: $V = 24 \text{ m/s}$
- Vane velocity: $u = 12 \text{ m/s}$
- Deflection angle: $\theta = 150^\circ$
- Water density: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Calculate the relative velocity:**

$$V_r = V - u = 24 - 12 = 12 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the jet area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the tangential force:**

$$F_t = \rho A V_r^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

For $\theta = 150^\circ$, $\cos 150^\circ = -0.8660$, so $1 - \cos\theta = 1 - (-0.8660) = 1.8660$.

$$F_t = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (12)^2 \times 1.8660$$

$$F_t = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 144 \times 1.8660$$

$$F_t = 527.65 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the power developed:**

$$W = F_t \times u = 527.65 \times 12 = 6331.8 \text{ W} \approx 6.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $F_t = 527.65 \text{ N}$ and $W = 6.33 \text{ kW}$.
