# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern for 3-mark short-answer questions. They test direct recall of definitions, formulas, and conceptual clarity at the **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]

**Define the coefficient of impact. How is it experimentally determined in the curved plate experiment?**

**Model Answer:**

The **coefficient of impact** ($C_i$) is defined as the ratio of the **experimental (actual) force** exerted by the water jet on the curved vane to the **theoretically predicted force** calculated using the momentum equation:

$$C_i = \frac{F_{experimental}}{F_{theoretical}}$$

**Experimental determination procedure:**

1. Set the pressure head $H$ in the supply tank using a piezometer or gauge.
2. Allow the jet to strike the curved vane and record the **dead-weight** required on the pan to bring the pointer to its original null position.
3. Measure the **time** $t$ required to collect a known **volume** $V_{ol}$ of water in a measuring cylinder.
4. Calculate the actual jet velocity as $V_{act} = \dfrac{V_{ol}}{a \cdot t}$ (where $a$ is the nozzle area).
5. Compute $F_{theoretical} = \rho a V_{act}^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$ for a hemispherical vane.
6. The ratio $F_{exp} / F_{th}$ gives the coefficient of impact, typically $C_i \approx 0.92$ to $0.98$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]

**State the assumptions made in deriving the force exerted by a jet on a curved plate.**

**Model Answer:**

The following assumptions are made in the derivation:

1. The fluid (water) is **incompressible** and has a constant density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
2. The flow is **steady, uniform, and one-dimensional** at the inlet and outlet sections.
3. The jet is **compact** and does not break up or disperse on striking the vane.
4. The friction between the jet and the curved surface is **negligible**, so the **outlet velocity magnitude equals the inlet velocity** ($V_1 = V_2 = V$).
5. The pressure at the inlet and outlet of the control volume is **atmospheric** (gauge pressure = 0).
6. The curved vane is **smooth and symmetric**, ensuring that the entire jet is deflected by the design angle $\theta$ with no loss of fluid.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Examination Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with an internal choice. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided below. Each is divided into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**A jet of water having a velocity of 20 m/s strikes a curved vane which is moving with a velocity of 8 m/s in the direction of the jet. The jet diameter is 60 mm. The vane deflects the jet through an angle of 165°. Determine:**

**(a)** The force exerted by the jet on the vane in the direction of motion.  
**(b)** The power developed by the vane and the efficiency of the system.

**Model Solution:**

**Given data:**

- Jet velocity: $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$
- Vane velocity: $u = 8 \text{ m/s}$
- Jet diameter: $D = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- Deflection angle: $\theta = 165^\circ$
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Calculate the jet area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.06)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the relative velocity:**

$$V_r = V - u = 20 - 8 = 12 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Find the trigonometric value:**

$$\cos 165^\circ = -0.9659$$

**Sub-part (a) — Force in the direction of motion:**

The tangential (axial) force is:

$$F_t = \rho A V_r^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

$$F_t = 1000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times (12)^2 \times (1 - (-0.9659))$$

$$F_t = 1000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 144 \times 1.9659$$

$$F_t = 800.0 \text{ N}$$

**Sub-part (b) — Power and Efficiency:**

**Power developed:**

$$W = F_t \times u = 800.0 \times 8 = 6400 \text{ W} = 6.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Kinetic energy supplied per second (input power):**

$$KE_{input} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^3$$

$$KE_{input} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 8000 = 11{,}308 \text{ W}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{W}{KE_{input}} = \frac{6400}{11{,}308} = 0.5660 = 56.60\%$$

**Final Answers:**

- (a) $F_t = 800 \text{ N}$
- (b) Power = $6.4 \text{ kW}$, Efficiency = $56.6\%$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**A stationary curved vane deflects a water jet through an angle of 120°. The jet diameter is 40 mm and the velocity of the jet is 18 m/s. Calculate:**

**(a)** The components of the resultant force (tangential and normal) exerted by the jet on the vane.  
**(b)** The magnitude and direction of the resultant force. Also find the work done per unit weight of water striking the vane.

**Model Solution:**

**Given data:**

- Deflection angle: $\theta = 120^\circ$
- Jet diameter: $D = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- Jet velocity: $V = 18 \text{ m/s}$
- Density: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Jet area and discharge:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.04)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = AV = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 18 = 0.02262 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Trigonometric values:**

$$\cos 120^\circ = -0.5, \quad \sin 120^\circ = 0.8660$$

**Sub-part (a) — Force components:**

**Tangential force:**

$$F_t = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta) = 1000 \times 0.02262 \times 18 \times (1 - (-0.5))$$

$$F_t = 1000 \times 0.02262 \times 18 \times 1.5 = 610.74 \text{ N}$$

**Normal force:**

$$F_n = \rho Q V (1 + \cos\theta) = 1000 \times 0.02262 \times 18 \times (1 + (-0.5))$$

$$F_n = 1000 \times 0.02262 \times 18 \times 0.5 = 203.58 \text{ N}$$

**Sub-part (b) — Resultant magnitude, direction, and work:**

**Resultant force magnitude:**

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_n^2} = \sqrt{(610.74)^2 + (203.58)^2}$$

$$F_R = \sqrt{373{,}003 + 41{,}445} = \sqrt{414{,}448} = 643.78 \text{ N}$$

**Direction (angle with tangential):**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{F_n}{F_t} = \frac{203.58}{610.74} = 0.3333$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.3333) = 18.43^\circ$$

**Work done per unit weight of water striking the vane:**

Since the vane is **stationary**, no work is done *on the vane* ($W = F_t \times u = F_t \times 0 = 0$). However, the **change in kinetic energy per unit weight** represents the energy loss in the vane (which should be zero in the ideal frictionless case).

The velocity of water at exit has the same magnitude $V = 18 \text{ m/s}$ but its direction is changed by $120^\circ$. The component of exit velocity in the original jet direction is:

$$V_{2x} = V \cos(180^\circ - 120^\circ) = V \cos 60^\circ = 18 \times 0.5 = 9 \text{ m/s}$$

The work done per unit weight (using $W = \Delta KE / \text{weight}$, but in ideal case $\Delta KE = 0$):

$$W_{per\ unit\ weight} = \frac{V^2 - V_{2x}^2}{2g} = \frac{(18)^2 - (9)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{324 - 81}{19.62} = 12.39 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This $12.39 \text{ m}$ represents the *effective* head loss per unit weight of water due to the directional change. In an ideal (frictionless) vane, this energy is *recovered* in the form of the reaction force — it is the energy "given back" to the vane. In a real vane, some of this is dissipated as turbulence.

**Final Answers:**

- (a) $F_t = 610.74 \text{ N}$, $F_n = 203.58 \text{ N}$
- (b) $F_R = 643.78 \text{ N}$ at $\alpha = 18.43^\circ$ from the jet axis. Work per unit weight $= 12.39 \text{ m}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in the tangential force formula:** Students frequently write $F_t = \rho Q V (1 + \cos\theta)$ instead of the correct $F_t = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta)$. This single sign error costs **3 to 4 marks** out of 14.
> 2. **Using jet velocity $V$ instead of relative velocity $(V - u)$ for moving vanes:** When the vane is in motion, the *momentum transfer* depends on the **relative** velocity, not the absolute jet velocity. Using $V$ directly is a fundamental conceptual error.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** The jet diameter is typically given in mm (e.g., 50 mm), but all formulas require SI units (m). A factor-of-1000 error in the area calculation cascades through the entire solution.
> 4. **Confusing $C_d$ and $C_v$:** $C_d$ relates to discharge measurement, $C_v$ relates to velocity measurement. They are not interchangeable. $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$.
> 5. **Neglecting the coefficient of impact in efficiency calculations:** In a real experiment, $C_i < 1$ due to friction. The KTU examiner expects students to either state this assumption explicitly or incorporate it in the calculation.
> 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Always write N (Newtons) for force, W or kW for power, m for head — examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units.
> 7. **Incorrect trigonometric values for angles like 165° or 150°:** These are obtuse angles where $\cos\theta$ is **negative**. A positive sign error here propagates everywhere.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before Your Lab Exam:**

- **Core Principle:** The experiment validates the **Linear Momentum Equation** for steady, incompressible flow.
- **Standard Formulae to Memorize:**
  - Tangential force: $F_t = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta)$
  - Normal force: $F_n = \rho Q V (1 + \cos\theta)$
  - Hemispherical case ($\theta = 180^\circ$): $F = 2 \rho A V^2$
  - Force on moving vane: $F = \rho A (V - u)^2 (1 \pm \cos\theta)$
  - Maximum vane efficiency: $\eta_{max} = 50\%$ at $u = V/2$ for hemispherical case.
- **Key Definitions:**
  - Coefficient of velocity: $C_v = V_{actual} / V_{theoretical}$
  - Coefficient of discharge: $C_d = Q_{actual} / Q_{theoretical}$
  - Coefficient of impact: $C_i = F_{experimental} / F_{theoretical}$
- **Apparatus Components (Know All of These):** Sump tank → Pump → Pressure tank with overflow → Nozzle → Curved vane (mounted on swinging arm) → Pan and dead weights → Measuring cylinder and stopwatch.
- **Procedure Outline (5 Steps):** (1) Set head, (2) Measure discharge, (3) Read pan weight, (4) Calculate theoretical force, (5) Compute $C_i$.
- **Tabulation Columns:** Head $H$ | Time $t$ | Volume $V_{ol}$ | Discharge $Q$ | Jet velocity $V$ | Theoretical force $F_{th}$ | Experimental force $F_{exp}$ | Coefficient of impact $C_i$.
- **Precautions (Frequently Asked in Viva):**
  1. Ensure no air bubbles in the flow.
  2. The pointer must return to the *exact* null position before reading.
  3. Maintain a *constant* head throughout the trial (use the overflow pipe).
  4. The jet must strike the vane at its designed *centerline* (no off-center impact).
  5. Run the pump for a few minutes to stabilize the flow before recording data.
- **Graph to Plot (Mandatory in Lab Report):** $F_{theoretical}$ on the $y$-axis vs $F_{experimental}$ on the $x$-axis. A straight line with slope = $C_i$ passing through the origin verifies the momentum theory.
- **Industrial Applications to Mention:**
  - Pelton wheel turbine buckets (hemispherical vanes).
  - Marine rudder design (curved foil sections).
  - Jet deflectors in firefighting monitors.
  - Impulse turbine blade design.
  - Jet propulsion thrust vectoring.
- **Common Error Traps:** Sign of $\cos\theta$ for obtuse angles, mm-to-m conversion, using $V$ instead of $(V-u)$ for moving vanes.
- **Numericals to Practice:** Force calculation for $\theta = 90^\circ, 120^\circ, 165^\circ, 180^\circ$ on both stationary and moving vanes. Power and efficiency for $u = V/3, V/2, 2V/3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prediction of Ship Hull Resistance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Prediction of Ship Hull Resistance

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Hull Resistance (R_T):** The total force opposing the forward motion of a vessel through still water at a given speed, expressed in **Newtons (N)** or **kiloNewtons (kN)**. Mathematically, it is the component of the hydrodynamic force acting on the wetted hull surface in the direction opposite to the ship's velocity vector.

The resistance of a ship hull is a fundamental parameter in naval architecture because it directly determines the **Effective Power (P_E)** required from the propulsion plant. For a vessel advancing at speed **V** through calm water, the effective power is:

$$P_E = R_T \cdot V$$

In KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory practice, this resistance is measured on a **scaled geometric model** towed in a towing tank, and the results are extrapolated to the full-scale ship using the **ITTC 1978 Performance Prediction Method** (the international standard accepted by the International Towing Tank Conference).

## 1.2 Components of Total Resistance

The total resistance is the linear superposition of physically distinct hydrodynamic phenomena:

| Component | Symbol | Physical Origin |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|
| **Frictional Resistance** | $R_F$ | Viscous shear in the turbulent boundary layer along the wetted hull |
| **Wave-Making Resistance** | $R_W$ | Energy radiated as surface gravity waves by the hull pressure field |
| **Eddy / Viscous Pressure Resistance** | $R_{VP}$ | Normal pressure forces due to flow separation (form drag) |
| **Appendage Resistance** | $R_{APP}$ | Resistance of rudders, bilge keels, propeller bossings, shaft brackets |
| **Air Resistance** | $R_{AA}$ | Wind drag on the above-water hull and superstructure |

Therefore:

$$R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{VP} + R_{APP} + R_{AA}$$

By convention, the first two components are bundled into the **Residuary Resistance** $R_R$ (wave-making + viscous pressure + all non-frictional parts), while $R_F$ is treated separately because it scales purely with Reynolds number.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine sliding a flat wooden plank through a swimming pool versus a streamlined canoe at the same speed. The plank requires far more "push." That extra push is **resistance**.

Think of a ship as a giant "intruder" pushing water out of its way. Three things happen simultaneously:

1. **The hull drags a thin layer of water along with it** (viscous friction — like rubbing your hand across a table).
2. **The hull squeezes the water surface upward**, generating a **bow wave** and a **stern wave** — energy you can actually *see* leaving the ship.
3. **Water tries to curl around sharp corners** on the hull (stern, bilge, brackets), creating eddies and low-pressure wakes — this is *form drag*.

The lab's job is to **measure** the combined push needed on a small model, then **predict** what the full-scale ship will need. Because water and air are **not the same fluid**, scaling must be done carefully — this is where **Reynolds number** and **Froude number** become essential.

## 1.4 Why Model Testing Cannot Be Skipped

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Lab Principle:** Full-scale resistance cannot be computed analytically for a complex ship hull — no closed-form solution exists for the **Navier–Stokes equations** around a 3D ship geometry. Therefore, scaled physical model testing in a towing tank is the **industry-standard, ITTC-recommended** method for predicting full-scale resistance.

The fundamental scaling parameters are:
- **Froude Number** (Fr): similarity of **wave patterns** (gravity vs inertia)
- **Reynolds Number** (Re): similarity of **boundary-layer turbulence** (inertia vs viscosity)

Because Fr and Re cannot be matched simultaneously in a single water test (different scales imply different fluid speeds), the **Froude scaling** is preserved while the **frictional part** is computed separately using empirical friction lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dimensional Analysis Foundation

The resistance of a ship depends on:

$$R_T = f(\rho, \nu, g, V, L, S, \nabla)$$

where:
- $\rho$ = mass density of water (kg/m³)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of water (m²/s)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $V$ = ship speed (m/s)
- $L$ = characteristic length (m) — usually **Length Between Perpendiculars (L_PP)**
- $S$ = wetted surface area (m²)
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³)

By Buckingham's Pi theorem, three dimensionless groups govern the problem:

$$\frac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S} = f\!\left(\frac{VL}{\nu},\; \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}},\; \frac{\nabla^{2/3}}{S}\right)$$

The three non-dimensional groups are:
1. **Resistance Coefficient** $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$
2. **Reynolds Number** $Re = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ (viscous similarity)
3. **Froude Number** $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ (wave similarity)
4. **Form Parameter** $\dfrac{\nabla^{2/3}}{S}$ (geometric similarity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a model test, the **geometric similarity** is fixed by the model scale ratio $\lambda = L_s / L_m$, and **Froude similarity** is enforced by setting the model speed according to $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$. However, **Reynolds similarity is intentionally broken**, and the friction part is corrected analytically.

## 2.2 The Form Factor (1 + k) and Its Physical Meaning

When the model is towed, the measured total resistance coefficient $C_{TM}$ contains a "viscous pressure" component that does **not** scale like pure friction. The **form factor (1+k)** accounts for the excess viscous drag caused by the **three-dimensional shape** of the hull, beyond what a flat plate would experience.

$$R_V = (1 + k) \cdot R_F$$

where:
- $R_V$ = **viscous resistance** (friction + viscous-pressure form drag)
- $R_F$ = **frictional resistance** of an equivalent flat plate
- $k$ = **form factor** (typically 0.1 to 0.5; slender cargo ships ~0.15, blunt forms ~0.4)

The **residuary resistance** $R_R$ is then defined as:

$$R_R = R_T - (1+k)R_F$$

This decomposition is the heart of the ITTC method because $R_R$ scales only with Froude number (geometrically and wave-wise), while $(1+k)R_F$ scales only with Reynolds number.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $R_T = R_F + R_R$ | Total resistance partition | N |
| 2 | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Total resistance coefficient | — |
| 3 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log Re - 2)^2}$ | **ITTC-57 friction line** | — |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | — |
| 5 | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number | — |
| 6 | $C_R = \dfrac{R_R}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Residuary resistance coefficient | — |
| 7 | $C_{TS} = \dfrac{C_{TM} - C_{FM} + (1+k)C_{FS}}{(1+k)}$ | (Legacy form, see §3) | — |
| 8 | $V_m = V_s\sqrt{\lambda}$ | Froude-scaled model speed | m/s |
| 9 | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | Effective power | W |
| 10 | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | Effective horsepower (metric) | HP |
| 11 | $C_A = 0.0004$ | Correlation allowance (ITTC-78) | — |
| 12 | $\Delta C_F = \dfrac{0.046}{Re^{0.2}} - \dfrac{0.075}{(\log Re - 2)^2}$ | Friction-line correction | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** Resistance in N, speed in m/s, power in W (SI). For EHP, divide by 745.7 to get **imperial HP** or by 75 to get **metric HP (PS)**. KTU problems may use either; always check the problem statement.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The prediction of ship hull resistance is **not an academic exercise** — it is a contractual requirement. A 10% over-estimation of resistance on a bulk carrier translates to:
- A larger (more expensive) engine
- Higher bunker consumption
- Greater emissions
- A heavier displacement (which further increases resistance — a vicious cycle)

Conversely, an under-estimation means the ship cannot meet its **contracted service speed**, leading to penalty clauses worth millions of dollars.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ITTC-1978 Performance Prediction Method

### Step 1 — Measure Total Resistance on the Model

The model is towed at a series of pre-selected Froude numbers $Fr_m$. For each speed, the dynamometer reads the **towing force** $R_{TM}$ (model total resistance). The wetted surface area $S_m$ of the model is known from offset table integration.

$$C_{TM} = \frac{R_{TM}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_m V_m^2 S_m}$$

The model Reynolds number is $Re_m = V_m L_m / \nu_m$, where $L_m$ is the model length (typically 2–6 m in the KTU towing tank).

### Step 2 — Compute the Model Friction Coefficient

Using the **ITTC-1957 friction line** (the only line fully consistent with the ITTC-1978 method), evaluated at the **model Reynolds number**:

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_m - 2)^2}$$

The model friction resistance is:

$$R_{FM} = C_{FM} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_m V_m^2 S_m$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Model Residuary Resistance Coefficient

The residuary resistance coefficient at model scale is defined as the **non-frictional** part of the total, **not** corrected by the form factor at this stage:

$$C_{RM} = C_{TM} - C_{FM}$$

**Important:** At the model scale, the form factor is **already implicitly present** in $C_{TM}$ because the model has the same shape as the ship. The model residuary contains the wave-making + the model's viscous-pressure form drag, both of which are functions of Froude number (because the model is geometrically similar).

### Step 4 — Froude-Scale the Residuary Resistance to Full Scale

Because Froude similarity is preserved between model and ship, the **residuary resistance coefficient** is the **same** at corresponding Froude numbers:

$$C_{RS} = C_{RM}$$

This is the *core* of the ITTC-1978 method. We assume the wave-making and viscous-pressure components — which depend on Froude number and geometry but not on Reynolds number (to first order) — are identical for model and ship.

The full-scale residuary resistance is then:

$$R_{RS} = C_{RM} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Ship Friction Coefficient

At the corresponding full-scale Reynolds number $Re_s = V_s L_s / \nu_s$:

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re_s - 2)^2}$$

The full-scale friction resistance (as if the ship were a flat plate):

$$R_{FS} = C_{FS} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s$$

### Step 6 — Add the Correlation Allowance

The ITTC-1978 method introduces a small empirical **correlation allowance** $C_A$ to correct for the systematic scale-effect error:

$$R_{A} = C_{A} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_s V_s^2 S_s$$

The recommended value of $C_A$ is **0.0004**, which is essentially a constant 4×10⁻⁴ added to the resistance coefficient for ships with a length between 70 m and 300 m.

### Step 7 — Compute the Predicted Total Ship Resistance

Combining the full-scale friction, the Froude-scaled residuary, and the correlation allowance:

$$\boxed{R_{TS} = R_{FS} + R_{RS} + R_A}$$

Equivalently, in coefficient form:

$$C_{TS} = C_{FS} + C_{RM} + C_A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Form Factor (1+k):** The ITTC-1978 method described above **does not** use the (1+k) form-factor correction. That correction belongs to the **older ITTC-1963 / Hughes methods**. The ITTC-1978 procedure is the **current KTU syllabus standard** and is fully consistent with modern CFD benchmark validations.

## 3.2 Full Worked Example (Sample Lab Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical KTU lab-viva question. Work through every line.

**Given (Model data, salt water, 15 °C):**
- Model length: $L_m = 3.0$ m
- Wetted surface area of model: $S_m = 1.20$ m²
- Towing speed: $V_m = 2.40$ m/s
- Measured model resistance: $R_{TM} = 9.20$ N
- $\rho_m = 999$ kg/m³, $\nu_m = 1.139 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- Geometric scale ratio: $\lambda = L_s / L_m = 20$
- Corresponding ship speed (Froude-scaled): $V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda} = 2.40 \times \sqrt{20} = 10.733$ m/s ≈ **21.0 knots**
- Ship wetted area (from similarity): $S_s = S_m \cdot \lambda^2 = 1.20 \times 400 = 480$ m²
- Ship Reynolds number water (15 °C sea): $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu_s = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**Step 1 — Model Resistance Coefficient:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{9.20}{0.5 \times 999 \times 2.40^2 \times 1.20} = \frac{9.20}{3452.5} = 0.002665$$

**Step 2 — Model Reynolds Number and Friction Coefficient:**

$$Re_m = \frac{2.40 \times 3.0}{1.139 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.32 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(6.32 \times 10^6) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.801 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.801)^2} = \frac{0.075}{23.05} = 0.003254$$

**Step 3 — Model Residuary Coefficient:**

$$C_{RM} = C_{TM} - C_{FM} = 0.002665 - 0.003254 = -0.000589$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap!** Students often panic when $C_{RM}$ comes out **negative**. This is normal at very low Froude numbers for slender forms — it just means the **3D form-drag and wave drag** are smaller than the friction-line over-prediction. **Never** "correct" a negative $C_{RM}$ to zero unless instructed; report the true value.

**Step 4 — Apply Froude Similarity to Ship:**

$$C_{RS} = C_{RM} = -0.000589$$

**Step 5 — Ship Reynolds Number and Friction Coefficient:**

$$Re_s = \frac{10.733 \times 60.0}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.42 \times 10^{8}$$

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(5.42 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(8.734 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.734)^2} = \frac{0.075}{45.35} = 0.001654$$

**Step 6 — Correlation Allowance Force:**

$$R_A = 0.0004 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 10.733^2 \times 480 = 0.0004 \times 28,331,000 = 11{,}332 \text{ N} \approx 11.33 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Ship Friction Force and Residuary Force:**

$$R_{FS} = 0.001654 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 10.733^2 \times 480 = 46{,}852 \text{ N}$$

$$R_{RS} = -0.000589 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 10.733^2 \times 480 = -16{,}685 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Total Ship Resistance:**

$$R_{TS} = 46{,}852 + (-16{,}685) + 11{,}332 = 41{,}499 \text{ N} \approx 41.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 9 — Effective Power:**

$$P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_s = 41{,}499 \times 10.733 = 445{,}400 \text{ W} \approx 445.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10 — Effective Horsepower (metric):**

$$EHP = \frac{P_E}{735.5} = \frac{445{,}400}{735.5} \approx 605.6 \text{ PS}$$

(Using $1 \text{ PS} = 735.5 \text{ W}$.)

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Conversion Logic Summary

| Quantity | From → To | Logic |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| Speed | knots → m/s | multiply by 0.5144 |
| Speed | m/s → knots | multiply by 1.944 |
| Power | kW → metric HP | divide by 0.7355 |
| Power | kW → imperial HP | divide by 0.7457 |
| Length | m → ft | multiply by 3.281 |
| Area | m² → ft² | multiply by 10.764 |
| Resistance | kN → lbf | multiply by 224.81 |
| Viscosity (fresh 20 °C) | — | $1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| Viscosity (salt 15 °C) | — | $1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Compliance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams below follow the strict Mermaid node-identifier rules: every node ID is **alphanumeric, prefixed with a letter**, and **never** uses reserved keywords as standalone names. Node labels contain **no markdown formatting** (no asterisks, no bold, no italics).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Towing-Tank Resistance Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Setup: Model Hull on Towing Carriage] --> B[Calibrate Dynamometer]
    B --> C[Set Target Model Speed Vm]
    C --> D[Tow Model Through Calm Water]
    D --> E[Record Steady-State Tow Force RTM]
    E --> F[Repeat for Multiple Froude Numbers]
    F --> G[Compute Resistance Curve C_TM vs Fr]
    G --> H[Apply Froude Scaling to Ship Speed Vs]
    H --> I[Compute Ship Friction C_FS using ITTC-57]
    I --> J[Residuary C_RS equals C_RM at model]
    J --> K[Add Correlation Allowance C_A equals 0.0004]
    K --> L[Sum: C_TS equals C_FS plus C_RM plus C_A]
    L --> M[Convert to Total Ship Resistance R_TS]
    M --> N[Compute Effective Power P_E equals R_TS times Vs]
    N --> O[Report: Resistance Curve, EHP Curve, EHP at Contract Speed]
```

## 4.3 Resistance Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Resistance R_T] --> B[Frictional R_F]
    A --> C[Wave-Making R_W]
    A --> D[Viscous Pressure R_VP]
    A --> E[Appendage R_APP]
    A --> F[Air R_AA]
    B --> G[Scales with Re]
    C --> H[Scales with Fr]
    D --> H
    E --> I[Geometric Similarity Only]
    F --> J[Wind Tunnel Correction]
    G --> K[ITTC-57 Friction Line]
    H --> L[ITTC-1978 Froude Scaling]
    K --> M[Total Ship Resistance]
    L --> M
    I --> M
    J --> M
```

## 4.4 Experimental Towing-Tank Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TANK[TOWING TANK]
        T1[Rails and Towing Carriage]
        T2[Dynamometer Load Cell]
        T3[Model Hull Attached to Strut]
        T4[Wave Absorber Beach]
        T5[Depth Markers and Scale]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL ROOM]
        C1[PC with DAQ Card]
        C2[Carriage Drive Controller]
        C3[Speed Encoder]
    end
    T1 <--> C2
    T2 --> C1
    C3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

## 4.5 Resistance Coefficient Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Froude Number] --> B[CT dominated by Friction: Linear Rise]
    B --> C[Interference Region: Hummock and Hollow]
    C --> D[High Froude Number: Steep Wave-Making Rise]
    D --> E[Planing or Broaching Regime Beyond Design Speed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Hint:** When you plot $C_T$ vs $Fr$ from the towing-tank data, you will almost always see a characteristic "interference hump" between $Fr \approx 0.20$ and $Fr \approx 0.35$. This is the **transom-stern wave** interfering with the bow wave, and it is the physical origin of the **hull resistance barrier** — the reason supertankers are limited to ~16 knots even though their engines could push faster.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign errors on $C_R$:** If $C_{RM} < C_{FM}$, the residual is negative — **do not** force it to zero. Just report.
> 2. **Wrong friction line:** Students often use the **Schönherr** or **Grigson** line. KTU accepts only the **ITTC-57** line in ITTC-1978 problems, unless the question states otherwise.
> 3. **Unit confusion in EHP:** $1 \text{ metric HP (PS)} = 735.5$ W, **not** 746 W. Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing the wrong constant.
> 4. **Forgetting the correlation allowance:** $C_A = 0.0004$ is mandatory in ITTC-1978. Omitting it loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Using model $\nu$ for the ship:** Always use the **ship's** water temperature to compute $\nu_s$ and $Re_s$. A common slip-up is reusing $\nu_m$.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** 
*Define the term "residuary resistance" of a ship hull. Why is it treated separately from frictional resistance in the ITTC-1978 prediction method?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Residuary resistance $R_R$ is defined as the **difference between the total resistance and the frictional resistance** of a ship:

$$R_R = R_T - R_F$$

It physically represents the **non-frictional** components: wave-making resistance, viscous-pressure (form) resistance, and any other pressure-derived forces.

It is treated separately in the ITTC-1978 method because it scales predominantly with the **Froude number** (i.e., wave-making is governed by gravity/inertia, not by viscosity). By separating $R_R$ from $R_F$ (which scales with Reynolds number), the method exploits Froude similarity to transfer the wave-making part from the model to the ship, while computing the frictional part analytically at the ship's Reynolds number. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*What is the correlation allowance in the ITTC-1978 method, and what is its standard numerical value? Why is it required?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The correlation allowance $C_A$ is a **small empirical constant** added to the predicted ship resistance coefficient to compensate for **scale effects** that are not captured by Froude scaling and the ITTC-57 friction line.

The recommended value (ITTC-1978) is:

$$C_A = 0.0004$$

It is required because the ITTC-57 friction line, while the best available general-purpose estimate, has a small systematic bias compared to full-scale trial data. The allowance brings the prediction into statistical agreement with real-ship measurement scatter. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **procedure** for conducting a **resistance test** on a ship model in a towing tank. List the major instruments used and the data recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A model of scale ratio $\lambda = 25$ is towed at $V_m = 2.0$ m/s in fresh water at 20 °C. The measured total resistance is $R_{TM} = 6.50$ N. The model wetted surface area is $S_m = 1.05$ m² and model length is $L_m = 2.4$ m. The corresponding ship will operate in salt water at 15 °C with $L_s = 60$ m. Use the ITTC-1978 method with $C_A = 0.0004$ to:
1. Compute the model resistance coefficient $C_{TM}$ and the model friction coefficient $C_{FM}$.
2. Compute the ship speed $V_s$, the ship Reynolds number $Re_s$, and the ship friction coefficient $C_{FS}$.
3. Determine the predicted total ship resistance $R_{TS}$ in kN and the effective power $P_E$ in kW. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Resistance Test Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Preparation:** The ship model (typically fibre-glass or wood, scale 1:20 to 1:50) is fitted with bilge keels, rudder, and propeller bossing if the resistance-with-appendage test is required. For bare-hull resistance, appendages are removed and their drag added later by estimation.

2. **Mounting:** The model is attached to a **vertical strut** connected to the **towing carriage** via a **dynamometer** (load cell). The strut's angle of attack is adjustable to ensure zero pitch moment.

3. **Towing speeds:** A series of 6–10 model speeds is selected so that the **Froude number** $Fr_m = V_m / \sqrt{g L_m}$ covers the design range (e.g., 0.10 to 0.40).

4. **For each speed:** The carriage is accelerated to the target speed, held steady, and the **average towing force** is recorded after the wave system is fully developed (blockage correction is small for slender models in deep tanks).

5. **Data recorded:** carriage speed $V_m$, towing force $R_{TM}$, water temperature, carriage position vs. time.

6. **Post-processing:** Compute $C_{TM}$ and plot the **resistance curve** $C_T$ vs. $Fr$. **(7 marks)**

**Instruments used:** Towing carriage with controlled drive, dynamometer/load cell, wave probes (optional), PC-based data acquisition, water-temperature probe.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 25$, $V_m = 2.0$ m/s, $R_{TM} = 6.50$ N
- $S_m = 1.05$ m², $L_m = 2.4$ m
- Fresh water 20 °C: $\rho_m = 998$ kg/m³, $\nu_m = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- Salt water 15 °C: $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu_s = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $L_s = \lambda L_m = 25 \times 2.4 = 60$ m, $S_s = \lambda^2 S_m = 625 \times 1.05 = 656.25$ m²
- $V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda} = 2.0 \times 5 = 10.0$ m/s

**Part 1 — Model Coefficients (3 of 7 marks):**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{6.50}{0.5 \times 998 \times 2.0^2 \times 1.05} = \frac{6.50}{2095.8} = 0.003101$$

$$Re_m = \frac{2.0 \times 2.4}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.78 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(4.78 \times 10^6) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.680 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.680)^2} = \frac{0.075}{21.90} = 0.003425$$

$$C_{RM} = 0.003101 - 0.003425 = -0.000324 \text{ (acceptable, low Fr)}$$

**Part 2 — Ship Coefficients (2 of 7 marks):**

$$Re_s = \frac{10.0 \times 60}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.05 \times 10^{8}$$

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(5.05 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(8.703 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.703)^2} = \frac{0.075}{44.93} = 0.001670$$

**Part 3 — Total Ship Resistance and Power (2 of 7 marks):**

$$R_{FS} = 0.001670 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 10.0^2 \times 656.25 = 56{,}203 \text{ N}$$

$$R_{RS} = -0.000324 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 10.0^2 \times 656.25 = -10{,}905 \text{ N}$$

$$R_A = 0.0004 \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 10.0^2 \times 656.25 = 13{,}463 \text{ N}$$

$$R_{TS} = 56{,}203 - 10{,}905 + 13{,}463 = 58{,}761 \text{ N} \approx 58.76 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_s = 58{,}761 \times 10.0 = 587{,}610 \text{ W} \approx 587.6 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{R_{TS} \approx 58.76 \text{ kN}, \quad P_E \approx 587.6 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the **Froude's hypothesis of extrapolation** and state its fundamental limitations. How did the ITTC-1978 procedure overcome these limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A geometrically similar model of a fishing trawler is tested in a towing tank. The following data are obtained at one speed:

| Quantity | Model | Ship |
|----------|-------|------|
| Length $L$ | 2.5 m | 50 m |
| Speed $V$ | 1.80 m/s | (to be found) |
| Resistance $R_T$ | 4.20 N | (to be found) |
| Wetted area $S$ | 1.10 m² | $\lambda^2 S_m$ |
| Fluid | Fresh, 20 °C | Salt, 15 °C |
| $\rho$ | 998 kg/m³ | 1025 kg/m³ |
| $\nu$ | $1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s | $1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |

Using the ITTC-1978 method with $C_A = 0.0004$, compute: (i) the ship speed in m/s, (ii) the ship wetted area, (iii) the predicted total ship resistance, and (iv) the effective horsepower in metric HP. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Froude's Hypothesis and its Limitations (7 marks):**

Froude's hypothesis (1868) proposed that the total ship resistance can be **split into two additive parts**:
- A **frictional** part depending only on Reynolds number (treated via plank experiments)
- A **residuary** part depending only on Froude number (transferred from model by Froude scaling)

The two parts are then summed to get the full-scale total resistance.

**Limitations of Froude's Original Method:**

1. **Inadequate frictional estimate:** Froude used plank-friction experiments on **rough** wooden planks, which overestimated the smooth-hull friction of modern ships.
2. **No form factor:** Froude did not separate the **viscous-pressure** component (form drag) from the wave-making component, leading to errors for full-form ships.
3. **No correlation allowance:** Systematic scale effects in wave-making (e.g., viscosity damping of waves) were unaccounted for, causing scatter of 5–15% against full-scale trials.
4. **No roughness allowance:** Modern ships have **rough hull paint** adding significant drag — Froude's smooth planks ignored this.

**How ITTC-1978 Overcame These:**

1. Adopted the **ITTC-1957 smooth-plate friction line** (analytically extrapolated to ship scale) instead of plank data.
2. Removed the explicit form factor (1+k) by including a constant **correlation allowance** $C_A = 0.0004$ that absorbs the residual bias.
3. Used **standardized test procedures** (blockage, end effects, submergence corrections) to minimize experimental scatter.
4. Validated against a large database of full-scale trials to statistically tune $C_A$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 50 / 2.5 = 20$
- $V_s = V_m \sqrt{\lambda} = 1.80 \times \sqrt{20} = 1.80 \times 4.4721 = 8.05$ m/s
- $S_s = \lambda^2 S_m = 400 \times 1.10 = 440.0$ m²
- $L_s = 50$ m, $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu_s = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**Model coefficients:**

$$C_{TM} = \frac{4.20}{0.5 \times 998 \times 1.80^2 \times 1.10} = \frac{4.20}{1778.0} = 0.002362$$

$$Re_m = \frac{1.80 \times 2.5}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.48 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{FM} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(4.48 \times 10^6) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.651 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.651)^2} = \frac{0.075}{21.63} = 0.003468$$

$$C_{RM} = 0.002362 - 0.003468 = -0.001106 \text{ (low-Fr, slender hull)}$$

**Ship coefficients:**

$$Re_s = \frac{8.05 \times 50}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.39 \times 10^{8}$$

$$C_{FS} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(3.39 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(8.530 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.530)^2} = \frac{0.075}{42.64} = 0.001759$$

**Total ship resistance:**

$$R_{TS} = (C_{FS} + C_{RM} + C_A) \times 0.5 \rho_s V_s^2 S_s$$

$$R_{TS} = (0.001759 - 0.001106 + 0.0004) \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 8.05^2 \times 440$$

$$R_{TS} = 0.001053 \times 14{,}929{,}800 = 15{,}721 \text{ N} \approx 15.72 \text{ kN}$$

**Effective Power and EHP:**

$$P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_s = 15{,}721 \times 8.05 = 126{,}554 \text{ W} \approx 126.6 \text{ kW}$$

$$EHP = \frac{P_E}{735.5} = \frac{126{,}554}{735.5} \approx 172.1 \text{ PS}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 8.05 \text{ m/s},\; S_s = 440 \text{ m}^2,\; R_{TS} \approx 15.72 \text{ kN},\; EHP \approx 172.1 \text{ PS}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module Experiment 2 — Prediction of Ship Hull Resistance**

- **Total Resistance:** $R_T = R_F + R_R$ (friction + residuary), or expanded as $R_F + R_W + R_{VP} + R_{APP} + R_{AA}$.
- **Resistance Coefficient:** $C_T = R_T / (0.5 \rho V^2 S)$ — non-dimensionalises the force.
- **Froude Number:** $Fr = V / \sqrt{gL}$ — controls wave pattern; Froude-scaled: $V_m = V_s \sqrt{\lambda}$.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = VL / \nu$ — controls boundary-layer turbulence.
- **ITTC-57 Friction Line:** $C_F = 0.075 / (\log Re - 2)^2$ — the only friction line for ITTC-1978.
- **ITTC-1978 Procedure:** $C_{TS} = C_{FS} + C_{RM} + C_A$ (no form factor needed).
- **Correlation Allowance:** $C_A = 0.0004$ (constant for $L = 70\text{–}300$ m).
- **Froude's Hypothesis:** $R_T = f(Re) + g(Fr)$ — additive split.
- **Scale Effect:** arises because $C_F$ changes with $Re$ (viscous) but $C_R$ is assumed constant (geometric/Froude-similar).
- **Effective Power:** $P_E = R_{TS} \cdot V_s$ (W).
- **EHP:** divide by 735.5 (metric PS) or 745.7 (imperial HP).
- **Negative $C_{RM}$:** normal at low Fr or slender forms — report, do not zero.
- **Appendages:** not removed; $R_{APP}$ is added to bare-hull $R_T$ in real-ship predictions.
- **Blockage Correction:** < 0.5% for slender hulls in deep tanks; usually ignored at KTU level.
- **IACC Yacht Formula** (mention only — not in KTU syllabus): $TRI = T + 0.5 A + 0.75 B + C + 0.5 D$.
- **Towing-Tank Instruments:** carriage, dynamometer, PC-DAQ, wave probes.
- **Standard Test Speeds:** 6–10 $Fr$ values, ranging $Fr = 0.10$ to $0.45$.
- **Wetted Area $S$:** computed from offsets using the **Simpson's rule** integration, or approximated by Mumford's formula for preliminary design: $S = C_s \sqrt{\nabla L}$, $C_s \approx 2.0$–$2.4$ depending on form.
- **Always** check: (a) fluid properties for the ship environment, (b) sign of $C_{RM}$, (c) the constant in the EHP formula.
- **One-sentence exam mantra:** *"Friction is analytical at ship Re; everything else is Froude-scaled from the model; add 0.0004 for correlation."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prediction of Propeller Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Prediction of Propeller Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛳️ SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Prediction of Propeller Performance** is the systematic procedure of estimating the full-scale (ship) operating parameters — namely the rotational speed (N), delivered thrust (T), delivered torque (Q), delivered power (P_D), and the open-water efficiency (η₀) — of a marine propeller by processing the non-dimensional open-water characteristics (Kt, Kq vs J) of a geometrically similar **model propeller** tested in a cavitation tunnel or towing tank, combined with the in-service hull data such as ship speed, resistance, wake fraction, and thrust-deduction fraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (must memorise verbatim for 3-mark questions):**
> *"Prediction of Propeller Performance is the determination of the full-scale operating point (N, T, Q, P) of a ship's propeller by matching the non-dimensional open-water coefficients of a geometrically similar model propeller with the service condition of the ship, using one of the three identity methods — Thrust, Torque, or Combined."*

The three classical identity methods defined by the **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC)** are:

1. **Method I — Thrust Identity:** Kt at model = Kt at ship (at corresponding J)
2. **Method II — Torque Identity:** Kq at model = Kq at ship (at corresponding J)
3. **Method III — Combined / Power Identity:** (Kt^(3/2)/Kq) at model = (Kt^(3/2)/Kq) at ship

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wind Tunnel for Ships"

Imagine you have built a brand-new aeroplane, but you are *not allowed* to fly the full-scale aircraft into a wind tunnel — it is too large, too expensive, and you might crash it. So, what engineers do is build a **scaled-down model** of the wing and propeller, place it in a wind tunnel, and measure the lift, drag, and torque at various wind speeds and RPMs.

Marine propeller prediction works on the **exact same principle**. We cannot test a 4-metre ship propeller in open sea at full load, so:

- A **scaled model propeller** (typically 0.25 m to 0.4 m diameter) is manufactured to be *geometrically similar* to the ship propeller.
- This model is run in a **cavitation tunnel** (a closed-loop water tunnel) where it is possible to vary the inflow velocity V_A and the rotational speed n independently.
- The thrust and torque are measured for many combinations, and the results are expressed in **non-dimensional form** (Kt, Kq, η₀ vs J), so they apply to *any* size of geometrically similar propeller.
- The full-scale operating point is then "back-calculated" using the ship's actual service condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Why Non-Dimensional Coefficients?):**
> A 0.3 m model and a 4 m ship propeller behave **identically in non-dimensional space**. If you plot Kt against J, both propellers fall on the *same curve*, provided they are geometrically similar and Reynolds-number effects are corrected. This is the single most powerful idea behind propeller prediction.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of sea water | ρ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Density of fresh water | ρ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water | ν | **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** (approx) |
| 1 knot | — | **0.5144 m/s** |
| Wageningen B-Series Propellers | — | **Most common model propeller family in KTU labs** |
| Standard KTU lab P/D ratio | — | **0.5 to 1.0** |
| Standard KTU lab blade count | Z | **3, 4, or 5 blades** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Non-Dimensional Coefficients

The behaviour of a marine propeller in open water is governed by **three non-dimensional coefficients** that strip away the size and speed, leaving only the *shape* of the performance curve.

### 2.1.1 Advance Coefficient (J)

$$J = \frac{V_A}{n \cdot D}$$

where:
- V_A = **advance velocity** (m/s) — the velocity of water entering the propeller disc
- n = **rotational speed** (revolutions per second, rps)
- D = **propeller diameter** (m)

> **Plain English:** J tells you "how fast the water is rushing past the propeller compared to how fast the propeller blade is slicing through the water."

### 2.1.2 Thrust Coefficient (Kt)

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4}$$

where T is the thrust produced (N) and ρ is the water density (kg/m³).

> **Plain English:** Kt is the *non-dimensional thrust*. Two propellers of different size running at different speeds will have the same Kt if they are geometrically similar and operating at the same J.

### 2.1.3 Torque Coefficient (Kq)

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5}$$

where Q is the torque (N·m).

### 2.1.4 Open-Water Efficiency (η₀)

$$\eta_0 = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi \cdot n \cdot Q} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$

> **Plain English:** η₀ is the *fraction of the mechanical power delivered to the propeller shaft* that is converted into useful *thrust work* pushing the ship forward. The rest is lost to friction, cavitation, and rotational wake.

---

## 2.2 The Three Identity Methods — Conceptual Logic

The **core problem** of propeller prediction is this:
- The *ship* is fixed in its service condition: it must travel at speed V, overcome resistance R, and use a propeller of diameter D_S.
- The *model* is what we actually test. It is geometrically similar to the ship propeller, so its open-water curve (Kt, Kq vs J) is identical to the ship's.
- We need to find the **single operating point** (one value of J, one value of n_S) that satisfies the service condition.

The three ITTC methods differ in **how the model operating point is matched to the ship operating point.**

### 2.2.1 Method I — Thrust Identity

**Assumption:** The thrust coefficient at the model equals the thrust coefficient at the ship at the corresponding advance coefficient.

$$K_{T,M} = K_{T,S} \quad \text{at the same } J$$

This means:
- The model is run at the *same J* as the ship.
- The model's RPM is set so that V_A,M = J × n_M × D_M equals the *ship's* advance velocity.
- The model's measured thrust T_M is then scaled up to ship thrust using geometric similarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "thrust identity"?** Because we are equating the *thrust coefficient*. The model is essentially used as a *thrust transducer* — we measure the thrust on the model, and from the non-dimensional coefficient we compute the full-scale thrust.

### 2.2.2 Method II — Torque Identity

**Assumption:** The torque coefficient at the model equals the torque coefficient at the ship.

$$K_{Q,M} = K_{Q,S} \quad \text{at the same } J$$

The procedure mirrors Method I, but the matching variable is the *torque coefficient* instead of the thrust coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "torque identity"?** This method is more directly useful for *power* prediction because P ∝ n·Q, so the torque measurement on the model translates directly to full-scale torque when scaled geometrically.

### 2.2.3 Method III — Combined (Power) Identity

**Assumption:** A combined non-dimensional group involving both Kt and Kq is preserved between model and ship.

$$\left(\frac{K_T^{3/2}}{K_Q}\right)_M = \left(\frac{K_T^{3/2}}{K_Q}\right)_S$$

This is essentially a **geometric mean** of Methods I and II and is theoretically the most balanced approach. It is sometimes called the **Power Identity Method** because:

$$\frac{K_T^{3/2}}{K_Q} \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{Q} \cdot \frac{D^2}{n} \propto \frac{T^{3/2} \cdot D^2}{P / (2\pi n)} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \propto \frac{T^{3/2} \cdot D^2 \cdot (2\pi n)^2}{P \cdot n}$$

which captures both thrust and power scaling simultaneously.

---

## 2.3 Hull–Propeller Interaction — Service Condition

The ship propeller does **not** operate in the same flow that the model propeller sees in the cavitation tunnel. The hull creates:

- **Wake fraction (w):** The hull moves through still water, but the propeller disc sees a slower inflow because the hull "drags" water with it. The effective velocity at the propeller disc is V_A = V(1 − w).
- **Thrust deduction fraction (t):** The propeller sucks water forward, lowering the pressure on the hull stern. This creates additional resistance, so the propeller must produce *more* thrust than the bare-hull resistance: T = R / (1 − t).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Condition Equations (HIGH-YIELD — must memorise):**
>
> $$T = \frac{R}{1 - t}$$
>
> $$V_A = V (1 - w)$$

## 2.4 The Three Efficiencies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question: "Define and explain the three efficiencies in ship propulsion."**

### 2.4.1 Hull Efficiency (η_H)

$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w}$$

> **Plain English:** Compares the *useful power delivered to the propeller (T·V_A)* with the *effective power required by the hull (R·V)*. If the hull is "clean" (low w, low t), η_H ≈ 1. A typical value is 1.0 to 1.2.

### 2.4.2 Rotative Efficiency (η_R)

$$\eta_R = \frac{\eta_0}{\eta_{0,\text{behind}}}$$

> **Plain English:** Accounts for the fact that the propeller operates *behind* the ship in a non-uniform wake, which causes extra losses. Measured in model–ship correlation trials. A typical value is 0.95 to 1.05 (slightly above 1.0 is possible for heavily loaded propellers).

### 2.4.3 Propulsive Efficiency (η_D) — *The Big One*

$$\eta_D = \frac{P_E}{P_D} = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0$$

> **Plain English:** The *overall* efficiency of the entire propulsion plant. η_D is typically 0.55 to 0.70 for a well-designed single-screw ship. It is what determines the fuel bill.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = V_A / (n \cdot D)$ | Advance coefficient | Given any 3, find the 4th |
| 2 | $K_T = T / (\rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4)$ | Thrust coefficient | Open-water data interpretation |
| 3 | $K_Q = Q / (\rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5)$ | Torque coefficient | Open-water data interpretation |
| 4 | $\eta_0 = (J / 2\pi) \cdot (K_T / K_Q)$ | Open-water efficiency | Efficiency calculation |
| 5 | $T = R / (1 - t)$ | Service thrust | Service condition |
| 6 | $V_A = V \cdot (1 - w)$ | Service advance velocity | Service condition |
| 7 | $\eta_H = (1 - t) / (1 - w)$ | Hull efficiency | Efficiency chain |
| 8 | $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0$ | Propulsive efficiency | Final answer |
| 9 | $P_D = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot Q$ | Delivered power | Power calculation |
| 10 | $P_E = R \cdot V$ | Effective power | Power chain |
| 11 | $K_{T,M} = K_{T,S}$ | Thrust identity | Method I |
| 12 | $K_{Q,M} = K_{Q,S}$ | Torque identity | Method II |
| 13 | $(K_T^{3/2}/K_Q)_M = (K_T^{3/2}/K_Q)_S$ | Combined identity | Method III |
| 14 | $n_S = n_M \cdot (D_M / D_S)$ | RPM scaling (geom. sim.) | Model → Ship |
| 15 | $T_S = T_M \cdot (D_S / D_M)^2$ | Thrust scaling | Model → Ship |
| 16 | $Q_S = Q_M \cdot (D_S / D_M)^3$ | Torque scaling | Model → Ship |
| 17 | $P_S = P_M \cdot (D_S / D_M)^3 \cdot (n_S/n_M) = P_M \cdot (D_S / D_M)^2$ | Power scaling | Model → Ship |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in every ship design office worldwide — by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) for powering predictions, by shipyards for engine selection, and by navies for range estimation. The same methodology extends to submarines (with appendage corrections), AUVs, and even tidal turbines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Thrust Identity Scaling Laws

We will now derive **every line** of the scaling law that converts a model test result into a full-scale ship prediction under Method I (Thrust Identity).

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

For a geometrically similar propeller, the open-water curve Kt = f(J) is the same for model and ship (in inviscid flow). The "Thrust Identity" therefore states:

$$K_{T,M}(J_M) = K_{T,S}(J_S) \quad \text{with} \quad J_M = J_S = J$$

(The same advance coefficient applies to both.)

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Express Kt for both model and ship

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{T,M} &= \frac{T_M}{\rho_M \cdot n_M^2 \cdot D_M^4} \\[4pt]
K_{T,S} &= \frac{T_S}{\rho_S \cdot n_S^2 \cdot D_S^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Set them equal (Thrust Identity)

$$
\frac{T_M}{\rho_M \cdot n_M^2 \cdot D_M^4} = \frac{T_S}{\rho_S \cdot n_S^2 \cdot D_S^4}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Use the J-constraint to relate n_S and n_M

From J_M = J_S:

$$\frac{V_{A,M}}{n_M \cdot D_M} = \frac{V_{A,S}}{n_S \cdot D_S}$$

In Method I, the model is conventionally tested at the *same advance velocity* as the ship advance velocity (V_A,M = V_A,S = V_A):

$$V_A = n_M \cdot D_M \cdot J = n_S \cdot D_S \cdot J$$

Solving for n_S:

$$n_S = n_M \cdot \frac{D_M}{D_S}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Since the model propeller is *smaller*, it must spin *faster* to produce the same J at the same V_A. The ship propeller, being larger, spins slower.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Substitute n_S into the identity equation

With n_S = n_M · D_M / D_S, the ratio n_S² · D_S⁴ becomes:

$$n_S^2 \cdot D_S^4 = n_M^2 \cdot \frac{D_M^2}{D_S^2} \cdot D_S^4 = n_M^2 \cdot D_M^2 \cdot D_S^2$$

Substituting back into the identity:

$$\frac{T_M}{\rho_M \cdot n_M^2 \cdot D_M^4} = \frac{T_S}{\rho_S \cdot n_M^2 \cdot D_M^2 \cdot D_S^2}$$

Rearranging for T_S:

$$T_S = T_M \cdot \frac{\rho_S}{\rho_M} \cdot \frac{D_S^2}{D_M^2}$$

For the standard case where model and ship are in the *same water* (ρ_S = ρ_M = ρ):

$$\boxed{T_S = T_M \cdot \left(\frac{D_S}{D_M}\right)^2}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Derive the torque and power scaling

Using K_Q,M = K_Q,S (because both are functions of J, and J is the same):

$$\frac{Q_M}{\rho \cdot n_M^2 \cdot D_M^5} = \frac{Q_S}{\rho \cdot n_S^2 \cdot D_S^5}$$

Substitute n_S = n_M · D_M / D_S:

$$Q_S = Q_M \cdot \left(\frac{D_S}{D_M}\right)^3$$

For delivered power P_D = 2π · n · Q:

$$P_S = 2\pi \cdot n_S \cdot Q_S = 2\pi \cdot n_M \cdot \frac{D_M}{D_S} \cdot Q_M \cdot \left(\frac{D_S}{D_M}\right)^3$$

$$\boxed{P_S = P_M \cdot \left(\frac{D_S}{D_M}\right)^2}$$

> **Plain English Summary of Scaling Laws:** *Thrust scales with the square of the diameter ratio, torque scales with the cube, RPM scales with the inverse, and power scales with the square (because RPM goes down while torque goes up).*

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example (Model → Ship, Method I)

> This is the *exact* style of numerical problem that appears in KTU exams. Solve this completely, and you are prepared for any variant.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A **Wageningen B-Series 4-bladed model propeller** with diameter D_M = **0.30 m**, pitch ratio P/D = **0.70**, and blade-area ratio EAR = **0.80** is tested in a cavitation tunnel in fresh water (ρ_M = 1000 kg/m³). The open-water data are tabulated below.

| J | K_T | K_Q | η₀ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.30 | 0.270 | 0.0400 | 0.323 |
| 0.40 | 0.240 | 0.0360 | 0.265 |
| 0.50 | 0.200 | 0.0320 | 0.249 |
| 0.60 | 0.160 | 0.0270 | 0.226 |
| 0.70 | 0.120 | 0.0220 | 0.194 |
| 0.80 | 0.080 | 0.0170 | 0.149 |

The full-scale ship has:
- Service speed V_S = **15 knots** = 15 × 0.5144 = **7.716 m/s**
- Effective resistance R = **600 kN** at this speed
- Wake fraction w = **0.30**
- Thrust deduction t = **0.18**
- Rotative efficiency η_R = **1.00**
- Ship propeller diameter D_S = **3.00 m** (geometrically similar to model)
- Sea water density ρ_S = **1025 kg/m³**

**Required:** Predict the full-scale propeller performance using the **Thrust Identity Method (Method I)**.

### 3.2.2 Solution — Step 1: Service Condition

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_S &= \frac{R}{1 - t} = \frac{600{,}000}{1 - 0.18} = \frac{600{,}000}{0.82} = 731{,}707 \, \text{N} \approx 731.7 \, \text{kN} \\[4pt]
V_A &= V_S \cdot (1 - w) = 7.716 \times (1 - 0.30) = 7.716 \times 0.70 = 5.401 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Solution — Step 2: Compute the Required Model Thrust

Using the scaling law derived in §3.1.5:

$$T_M = T_S \cdot \left(\frac{D_M}{D_S}\right)^2 = 731{,}707 \times \left(\frac{0.30}{3.00}\right)^2 = 731{,}707 \times 0.01 = 7{,}317.07 \, \text{N}$$

### 3.2.4 Solution — Step 3: Determine the Operating J on the Open-Water Curve

For each row of the open-water data, compute the *required* n_M to achieve V_A = 5.401 m/s, and then compute T_M using Kt:

$$n_M = \frac{V_A}{J \cdot D_M} = \frac{5.401}{J \times 0.30} = \frac{18.00}{J} \quad \text{rps}$$

$$T_M = K_T \cdot \rho_M \cdot n_M^2 \cdot D_M^4 = K_T \times 1000 \times n_M^2 \times (0.30)^4 = K_T \times 1000 \times n_M^2 \times 0.0081 = 8.1 \cdot K_T \cdot n_M^2$$

We need T_M = 7317 N, so let us tabulate T_M as a function of J:

| J | K_T | n_M (rps) | n_M² | T_M (N) = 8.1 · K_T · n_M² |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.30 | 0.270 | 60.00 | 3600.0 | 0.270 × 8.1 × 3600 = **7873** |
| 0.40 | 0.240 | 45.00 | 2025.0 | 0.240 × 8.1 × 2025 = **3937** |
| 0.50 | 0.200 | 36.00 | 1296.0 | 0.200 × 8.1 × 1296 = **2099** |
| 0.60 | 0.160 | 30.00 | 900.0 | 0.160 × 8.1 × 900 = **1166** |
| 0.70 | 0.120 | 25.71 | 661.2 | 0.120 × 8.1 × 661.2 = **643** |

> **Observation:** T_M is a monotonically decreasing function of J. The required T_M = 7317 N falls between J = 0.30 (T_M = 7873 N) and J = 0.40 (T_M = 3937 N).

### 3.2.5 Solution — Step 4: Linear Interpolation for the Operating J

$$J = 0.30 + (0.40 - 0.30) \cdot \frac{7873 - 7317}{7873 - 3937} = 0.30 + 0.10 \times \frac{556}{3936} = 0.30 + 0.0141 = 0.3141$$

At this J, the Kq value (interpolated):

$$K_Q = 0.0400 + (0.0360 - 0.0400) \times 0.141 = 0.0400 - 0.000565 = 0.03944$$

The open-water efficiency:

$$\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.3141}{6.2832} \times \frac{0.2665}{0.03944} = 0.04999 \times 6.756 = 0.3377$$

### 3.2.6 Solution — Step 5: Scale Model RPM to Ship RPM

$$n_S = n_M \cdot \frac{D_M}{D_S} = \left(\frac{18.00}{0.3141}\right) \times \frac{0.30}{3.00} = 57.31 \times 0.10 = 5.731 \, \text{rps}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_S = n_S \times 60 = 5.731 \times 60 = 343.9 \, \text{RPM}$$

### 3.2.7 Solution — Step 6: Compute Full-Scale Thrust, Torque, and Power

**Verification of Thrust (sanity check):**

$$T_S = K_{T,S} \cdot \rho_S \cdot n_S^2 \cdot D_S^4 = 0.2665 \times 1025 \times 5.731^2 \times 3.00^4 = 0.2665 \times 1025 \times 32.84 \times 81.0 = 726{,}983 \, \text{N} \approx 727.0 \, \text{kN}$$

(Acceptable: matches the 731.7 kN target within interpolation error.)

**Torque:**

$$Q_S = K_{Q,S} \cdot \rho_S \cdot n_S^2 \cdot D_S^5 = 0.03944 \times 1025 \times 32.84 \times 3.00^5 = 0.03944 \times 1025 \times 32.84 \times 243.0$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_S &= 0.03944 \times 1025 \times 32.84 \times 243.0 \\
&= 0.03944 \times 1025 \times 7981.0 \\
&= 0.03944 \times 8{,}180{,}525 \\
&= 322{,}640 \, \text{N·m} \approx 322.6 \, \text{kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Delivered Power:**

$$P_D = 2\pi \cdot n_S \cdot Q_S = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 5.731 \times 322{,}640 = 11{,}613{,}500 \, \text{W} \approx 11.61 \, \text{MW}$$

### 3.2.8 Solution — Step 7: Compute the Three Efficiencies

**Hull Efficiency:**

$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{0.82}{0.70} = 1.171$$

**Propulsive Efficiency:**

$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_0 = 1.171 \times 1.00 \times 0.3377 = 0.3955$$

**Effective Power (for cross-check):**

$$P_E = R \cdot V_S = 600{,}000 \times 7.716 = 4{,}629{,}600 \, \text{W} = 4.63 \, \text{MW}$$

**Verification of P_E / P_D:**

$$\frac{P_E}{P_D} = \frac{4.63}{11.61} = 0.399 \approx \eta_D \quad \checkmark$$

> **Match within 1%** confirms the entire calculation is self-consistent. This is the KTU "valuation key" trick — always verify η_D using P_E/P_D as a check.

### 3.2.9 Final Answer Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating advance coefficient | J | **0.314** | — |
| Operating thrust coefficient | K_T | **0.267** | — |
| Operating torque coefficient | K_Q | **0.0394** | — |
| Open-water efficiency | η₀ | **0.338** | — |
| Hull efficiency | η_H | **1.171** | — |
| Propulsive efficiency | η_D | **0.396** | — |
| Full-scale RPM | N_S | **344** | RPM |
| Full-scale thrust | T_S | **727** | kN |
| Full-scale torque | Q_S | **323** | kN·m |
| Full-scale delivered power | P_D | **11.6** | MW |
| Effective power | P_E | **4.63** | MW |
| Brake power (assuming η_G = 0.98) | P_B | **11.84** | MW |

# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Three-Stage Prediction Pipeline

The propeller performance prediction procedure can be modelled as a four-stage pipeline, with each stage consuming data from the previous stage and producing the inputs for the next.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1 SHIP SERVICE CONDITION] --> A1[V_S Ship Speed m per s]
    A --> A2[R Total Resistance kN]
    A --> A3[w Wake Fraction]
    A --> A4[t Thrust Deduction Fraction]
    A1 --> B1[Compute V_A = V x 1-w]
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B2[Compute T_S = R divided by 1-t]
    B1 --> C[STAGE 2 MODEL OPEN WATER DATA]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[J vs K_T curve]
    C --> C2[J vs K_Q curve]
    C --> C3[J vs eta_0 curve]
    C1 --> D[STAGE 3 IDENTITY MATCHING]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Method I Thrust Identity K_T equal K_T]
    D --> D2[Method II Torque Identity K_Q equal K_Q]
    D --> D3[Method III Combined Identity K_T^3 by 2 over K_Q equal]
    D1 --> E[STAGE 4 FULL SCALE PREDICTION]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[N_S = n_M x D_M over D_S RPM]
    E --> E2[Q_S = Q_M x D_S over D_M cubed kN m]
    E --> E3[P_D = 2 pi n_S Q_S MW]
    E --> E4[eta_D = eta_H eta_R eta_0]
    E1 --> F[FINAL OUTPUTS]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1[Selected Engine Sizing]
    F --> F2[Fuel Consumption Estimate]
    F --> F3[Voyage Range Calculation]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#ffe1f5
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
    style F fill:#f5e1ff
```

## 4.2 Functional Decomposition: Inputs → Methods → Outputs

The following block diagram shows the *decision flow* that an engineer follows when selecting which identity method to use for a given problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Ship Data V R w t] --> PROC1[Compute T and V_A]
    IN2[Model Open Water Chart] --> PROC2[Read K_T and K_Q at J]
    PROC1 --> SEL{Which Identity?}
    PROC2 --> SEL
    SEL -->|Method I| M1[K_T model = K_T ship]
    SEL -->|Method II| M2[K_Q model = K_Q ship]
    SEL -->|Method III| M3[K_T 3/2 over K_Q model = K_T 3/2 over K_Q ship]
    M1 --> OUT1[Find J then n_M then n_S]
    M2 --> OUT2[Find J then n_M then n_S]
    M3 --> OUT3[Find J then n_M then n_S]
    OUT1 --> FINAL[Final Ship N T Q P_D eta_D]
    OUT2 --> FINAL
    OUT3 --> FINAL
    style SEL fill:#ffe1e1
    style FINAL fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.3 Comparative Matrix: When to Use Each Method

| Engineering Situation | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| We need the *exact* ship thrust for engine selection | **Method I — Thrust** | Direct measurement of thrust on the model is most reliable |
| We need the *most accurate* delivered power prediction | **Method II — Torque** | Torque meter on model is more accurate than thrust meter |
| We need a *balanced* estimate for preliminary design | **Method III — Combined** | Averages the biases of Methods I and II |
| Reynolds number effect is significant (very large ship) | Apply **ITTC 1978 correction** to Kt after Method I or II | Corrects for scale effect on viscous losses |
| Cavitation is suspected at ship loading | Use Method I result to check **sigma cavitation parameter** | Avoids cavitation-induced erosion |
| The model test was in **salt water** but ship is in **fresh water** | Multiply T by ρ_S/ρ_F | Density correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Worth Memorising):** The three methods give *slightly different* full-scale RPMs, and the **true answer lies between the Method I and Method II predictions**. Method III is the best single-point estimate, but experienced naval architects often use the average of Methods I and II for final acceptance trials.
