- Weighing scale for inclining test.
- Hydrometer for water density.

**Data to record:**
- Time-history $\phi(t)$.
- Peak amplitudes $\phi_1, \phi_2, \ldots, \phi_N$.
- Time intervals between successive peaks.
- Water temperature and density.
- $W, \overline{GM}, I_{xx}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Logarithmic decrement.** Use first and last peaks (m = 7):

$$\delta = \frac{1}{7} \ln\left(\frac{6.0}{2.3}\right) = \frac{1}{7} \ln(2.6087) = \frac{0.9587}{7} = 0.13696$$

**Step 2: Damping factor.**

$$n = \frac{\delta}{T_d} = \frac{0.13696}{4.2} = 0.03261 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Linear damping coefficient.**

$$N = 2 I_{xx} n = 2 \times 8.4 \times 10^5 \times 0.03261$$

$$N = 5.478 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m·s/rad} = \mathbf{54.78 \text{ kN·m·s/rad}}$$

**Step 4: Half-life cycles.** We want $\phi(t)/\phi_0 = 0.5$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\delta \, k_{1/2}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\delta} = \frac{0.6931}{0.13696}$$

$$k_{1/2} = \mathbf{5.06 \text{ cycles}}$$

**Step 5: Adequacy comment.** A damping factor of $\delta = 0.137$ is in the **moderate range** for a frigate. Typical frigates have $\delta$ between 0.10 and 0.20. With 5 cycles to halve the amplitude, the roll damping is **adequate but not exceptional**. If the frigate were to operate in heavy North Atlantic seas, additional bilge keels or anti-roll fins would be recommended to increase $\delta$ by approximately 0.05–0.10.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A very common error is to use $\ln(\phi_{last}) - \ln(\phi_{first})$ without dividing by $m$ (the number of cycles between the peaks). Always check whether the given peaks are **consecutive** or **spaced by multiple cycles**. Using consecutive peaks amplifies reading error, so multi-cycle averaging is the **professionally preferred** method.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Roll Decay Tests**

- **Equation of motion:** $I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} + N \dot{\phi} + D \phi = 0$, with $D = W \cdot \overline{GM}$.
- **Natural period:** $T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{I_{xx} / (W \cdot \overline{GM})}$ — measured **directly** from the time-trace as the time between successive peaks.
- **Damped period** $T_d \approx T_\phi$ for ship hulls (difference $< 1\%$).
- **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = \dfrac{1}{m} \ln(\phi_k / \phi_{k+m})$ — use **multi-cycle averaging** for accuracy.
- **Damping factor:** $n = \delta / T_d$.
- **Linear damping coefficient:** $N = 2 I_{xx} n$.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \delta / \sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}$.
- **Half-life cycles:** $k_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \delta \approx 0.693 / \delta$.
- **Typical ship values:** $\zeta \in [0.02, 0.10]$; $\delta \in [0.10, 0.50]$; $T_R \geq 10$ s (IMO weather criterion).
- **Test constraints:** Initial heel angle $\phi_0 \leq 10°$ to ensure linear behaviour; no forward speed; calm water; bilge keels (if fitted) included.
- **Common mistakes:**
  1. Using $\phi$ in degrees inside trigonometric formulas — always **convert to radians** when $\phi_0$ enters the equation.
  2. Forgetting to divide the natural logarithm by $m$ in the $\delta$ formula.
  3. Confusing $T_\phi$ (computed from hull parameters) with $T_d$ (read from the trace).
  4. Omitting the factor of 2 when computing $N$ from $n$.
  5. Ignoring the added moment of inertia in roll, which can introduce **5–30% error** for slender hulls.
- **Real-world application:** Roll decay tests are the **experimental backbone** of IMO intact stability verification, anti-roll tank design, and CFD validation for ship seakeeping codes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prediction of Seakeeping Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Prediction of Seakeeping Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Prediction of Seakeeping Characteristics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seakeeping** is defined as the behavior of a ship in a seaway (ocean with waves). It refers to the quantitative description of a vessel's response to wave excitation, encompassing its six rigid-body degrees of freedom. The **prediction of seakeeping characteristics** in a laboratory setting involves the systematic evaluation of a scaled ship's model in a controlled wave environment to determine critical motion responses, accelerations, slamming loads, and operability limits prior to full-scale construction.

In naval architecture, the **six degrees of freedom (6-DOF)** of ship motion are:
1. **Surge** — translation along the longitudinal (x) axis
2. **Sway** — translation along the transverse (y) axis
3. **Heave** — translation along the vertical (z) axis
4. **Roll** — rotation about the longitudinal (x) axis
5. **Pitch** — rotation about the transverse (y) axis
6. **Yaw** — rotation about the vertical (z) axis

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine pushing a small toy boat in a bathtub filled with choppy water. The toy boat will bounce up and down (heave), tilt forward and backward (pitch), and rock side to side (roll). Now imagine doing this with an exact miniature replica of a real cargo ship, in a giant wave-generating tank, and you measure exactly how much it tilts and bounces. That measured motion, when scaled up mathematically, tells engineers how the actual full-size ship will behave in real ocean waves. **This is exactly what seakeeping prediction is — a controlled "bathtub test" to predict ocean behavior.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Froude Number ($F_n$)**: Dimensionless similarity parameter used in towing tank testing
- **Wavelength ($\lambda$)**: Measured in meters, typically $0.5 \, \text{m}$ to $30 \, \text{m}$ in lab scale
- **Wave Amplitude ($\zeta_a$)**: Peak-to-trough half-amplitude of regular wave
- **Wave Frequency ($\omega_e$)**: Encounter frequency in rad/s
- **Gravitational Acceleration ($g$)**: $9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Seakeeping

The prediction of seakeeping characteristics is grounded in **potential flow theory** combined with **strip theory**. The methodology consists of four logical stages:

### Stage 1: Wave Environment Definition
- Define incident wave spectrum $S(\omega)$
- Common spectra: **Pierson-Moskowitz (PM)** and **JONSWAP**
- Wave slope, steepness, and directionality are characterized

### Stage 2: Hydrodynamic Force Calculation
- Solve the boundary value problem (radiation + diffraction)
- Compute **added mass** $a_{ij}(\omega)$ and **damping** $b_{ij}(\omega)$ coefficients
- Solve the **exciting force** $F_j(\omega)$ using Haskind relations

### Stage 3: Equation of Motion Solution
- Solve the coupled Cummins equation in frequency domain:

$$
\bigl[-\omega^2 (M + A(\omega)) + i\omega B(\omega) + C\bigr] \cdot X(\omega) = F_{exc}(\omega)
$$

### Stage 4: Response Quantification
- Compute **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)**:

$$
\text{RAO}(\omega, \beta) = \frac{\vert X(\omega) \vert}{\zeta_a}
$$

where $X(\omega)$ is the complex motion amplitude.

## 2.2 Why Seakeeping Prediction is Critical
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Ships are designed to operate in **Sea State 5–6** (significant wave height $H_s = 3$–$4$ m). Without seakeeping analysis, vessels may suffer:
> - Cargo loss (parametric rolling)
> - Crew injury (motion sickness)
> - Structural failure (slamming, whipping)
> - Speed loss (added resistance in waves)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Froude Number | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Velocity-to-wave-speed ratio | dimensionless |
| Geometric Scale | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_m}{L_s}$ | Model-to-ship length ratio | dimensionless |
| Time Scale | $\lambda_t = \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Froude time scaling | s |
| Velocity Scale | $\lambda_V = \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Velocity scaling | m/s |
| Wave Height Scale | $\lambda_H = \lambda$ | Linear dimension scaling | m |
| Wavelength Scale | $\lambda_\lambda = \lambda$ | Wavelength scaling | m |
| RAO (Heave) | $RAO_3 = \dfrac{z_a}{\zeta_a}$ | Heave response per wave amp. | m/m |
| RAO (Pitch) | $RAO_5 = \dfrac{\theta_a}{\zeta_a k}$ | Pitch per wave slope | rad |
| Encounter Frequency | $\omega_e = \omega - \dfrac{\omega^2 V}{g}\cos\beta$ | Frequency in ship frame | rad/s |
| Significant Wave Height | $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4\sigma$ | Statistical wave measure | m |
| Zero-Crossing Period | $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m_0}{m_2}}$ | Mean wave period | s |
| Motion Sickness Incidence | $\text{MSI} = f(\text{MSI}_{zv})$ | ITTC standard formula | % |
| Added Resistance | $R_{aw} = \rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L \cdot R_{aw}^{\text{coef}}$ | Wave-making drag | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Froude Similarity:** Seakeeping model testing uses **Froude scaling** (not full Reynolds scaling) because gravity and inertia dominate wave-induced motions, while viscous effects are second-order. Reynolds mismatch is acknowledged as a limitation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Froude Scaling Laws

The Froude similarity demands equality of the Froude number between model and full-scale ship:

$$
F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}
$$

**Step 1:** Write the Froude number for both scales:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the velocity scale ratio $\lambda_V = V_m / V_s$:

$$
\frac{V_m}{V_s} = \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3:** Derive the time scale $\lambda_t = t_m / t_s$. Since $t = L / V$:

$$
\lambda_t = \frac{L_m / V_m}{L_s / V_s} = \frac{L_m}{L_s} \cdot \frac{V_s}{V_m} = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

**Step 4:** Acceleration scale $\lambda_a = a_m / a_s$:

$$
\lambda_a = \frac{V_m / t_m}{V_s / t_s} = \frac{\sqrt{\lambda}}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = 1
$$

**Step 5:** Force scale $\lambda_F = F_m / F_s$. Since $F \sim \rho L^2 V^2$:

$$
\lambda_F = \rho \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot (\sqrt{\lambda})^2 = \lambda^3
$$

**Step 6:** Mass scale $\lambda_m = m_m / m_s$. Since $m = \rho L^3$:

$$
\lambda_m = \rho \cdot \lambda^3 = \lambda^3
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Encounter Frequency

In ship seakeeping, the ship encounters waves at a frequency different from the absolute wave frequency due to forward motion. Let the ship travel at velocity $V$ at heading angle $\beta$ to the waves.

**Step 1:** Express wave phase in ship-fixed coordinates. The wave phase at the ship is:

$$
\Phi = k \cdot (V t \cos\beta) - \omega t
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to time:

$$
\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = k V \cos\beta - \omega
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the dispersion relation $k = \omega^2 / g$:

$$
\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V}{g} \cos\beta
$$

**Step 4:** Express in terms of Froude number $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ and wavelength-to-ship-length ratio $\lambda / L$:

$$
\frac{\omega_e}{\omega} = 1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\beta}{g} = 1 - F_n \sqrt{\frac{2\pi \omega^2 L}{g}} \cos\beta
$$

## 3.3 RAO Computation Procedure (Detailed)

For each frequency $\omega_i$ in a discretized spectrum:

**Step 1:** Compute excitation force $F_{exc}(\omega_i)$ via diffraction potential.

**Step 2:** Evaluate added mass matrix $A(\omega_i)$ from radiation potentials.

**Step 3:** Evaluate damping matrix $B(\omega_i)$ from far-field radiation.

**Step 4:** Solve the frequency-domain equation:

$$
\bigl[-\omega_i^2 (M + A(\omega_i)) + i\omega_i B(\omega_i) + C\bigr] X_i = F_{exc}(\omega_i)
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the motion amplitude:

$$
X_i = \bigl[-\omega_i^2 (M + A) + i\omega_i B + C\bigr]^{-1} F_{exc}(\omega_i)
$$

**Step 6:** Compute RAO:

$$
\text{RAO}_i = \frac{\vert X_i \vert}{\zeta_a}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute response spectrum:

$$
S_{XX}(\omega_i) = \vert \text{RAO}_i \vert^2 \cdot S_{\zeta\zeta}(\omega_i)
$$

**Step 8:** Compute statistical response:

$$
\sigma_X = \sqrt{\sum_i S_{XX}(\omega_i) \Delta\omega}
$$

## 3.4 Motion Sickness Incidence (MSI) — ITTC Standard Formula

The ITTC recommended MSI formula is:

$$
\text{MSI}_{zv} = K \cdot \left[ \frac{\sigma_{a_zv}}{g} \cdot \left( \frac{T_z}{T_n} \right)^n \right]
$$

For practical computation:

$$
\text{MSI} = \frac{1}{2.18} \cdot 100 \cdot \left[ \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_{a_v}}{g}\right) + n \log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_v}{\omega_n}\right) + C_1 \right]
$$

where:
- $\sigma_{a_v}$ = RMS vertical acceleration at crew location (m/s²)
- $\omega_n = 1.1$ rad/s (natural frequency for sickness)
- $n = 1.0$, $C_1$ = calibrated constant

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Seakeeping Prediction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Form Geometry] --> B[Define Model Scale]
    B --> C[Prepare Scale Model]
    C --> D[Install in Wave Basin]
    D --> E[Calibrate Wave Probes]
    E --> F[Set Wave Frequency Range]
    F --> G[Run Regular Wave Tests]
    G --> H[Measure Motions Heave Pitch Roll]
    H --> I[Compute RAO]
    I --> J[Run Irregular Wave Tests]
    J --> K[Acquire Motion Time Series]
    K --> L[Spectral Analysis FFT]
    L --> M[Compute Response Spectra]
    M --> N[Statistical Analysis RMS]
    N --> O[Froude Scale to Full Size]
    O --> P[Predict Full-Scale Response]
    P --> Q[Performance Assessment MSI Slamming]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Forces Acting on Ship in Waves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[External Forces]
        A1[Incident Wave Pressure]
        A2[Radiated Wave Pressure]
        A3[Diffracted Wave Pressure]
        A4[Gravity Restoring]
    end
    subgraph B[Inertial Response]
        B1[Ship Mass Matrix]
        B2[Added Mass]
        B3[Damping]
    end
    subgraph C[Output Motions]
        C1[Heave]
        C2[Pitch]
        C3[Roll]
        C4[Surge]
        C5[Sway]
        C6[Yaw]
    end
    A1 --> Sum
    A2 --> Sum
    A3 --> Sum
    A4 --> Sum
    Sum --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B3 --> C4
    B3 --> C5
    B3 --> C6
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Typical RAO Plot

```
   RAO (m/m)
      |
  1.5 |       ____
      |      /    \
  1.0 |     /      \
      |    /        \
  0.5 |   /          \____
      |  /                \____
  0.0 |_/________________________
      0   0.5  1.0  1.5  2.0   ω (rad/s)
            Peak at resonance
```

| **Region** | **Behavior** |
|---|---|
| Low $\omega$ | Follower force → RAO ≈ 1.0 |
| Resonance peak | Amplification of motion |
| High $\omega$ | Mass-controlled → RAO → 0 |

## 4.4 Laboratory Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wave Generator Wavemaker] --> B[Wave Basin Tank]
    B --> C[Instrumented Ship Model]
    C --> D[Motion Capture System]
    C --> E[Wave Probes]
    D --> F[Data Acquisition DAQ]
    E --> F
    F --> G[FFT Analyzer]
    G --> H[RAO Computation]
    H --> I[Statistical Output]
    I --> J[Full-Scale Prediction]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define seakeeping and list the six degrees of freedom of a ship.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Seakeeping is the quantitative study of a ship's response to wave action, encompassing its motions, accelerations, and forces in a seaway (1 mark). The six degrees of freedom are: **Surge** (longitudinal translation), **Sway** (transverse translation), **Heave** (vertical translation), **Roll** (rotation about longitudinal axis), **Pitch** (rotation about transverse axis), and **Yaw** (rotation about vertical axis) (2 marks).

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is a Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)? State its significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
RAO is defined as the ratio of the amplitude of the ship's motion response to the amplitude of the incident wave at a given frequency (1 mark). Mathematically, $\text{RAO}(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert / \zeta_a$ (1 mark). Its significance lies in enabling the prediction of ship motions in arbitrary irregular sea states from regular-wave test data, and forms the basis for operability and habitability assessment (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** Derive the Froude scaling laws used in seakeeping model testing, clearly stating the time, velocity, force, and acceleration scales. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A ship of length $L_s = 200 \, \text{m}$ is tested using a 1:50 scale model in a wave basin. If the model is towed at $V_m = 1.5 \, \text{m/s}$, compute: (i) full-scale speed, (ii) time scale, (iii) full-scale heave response if model heave amplitude is $0.012 \, \text{m}$ at wave amplitude $0.025 \, \text{m}$. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Froude similarity requirement $F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}$:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Derive velocity scale:

$$
\lambda_V = \frac{V_m}{V_s} = \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Derive time scale:

$$
\lambda_t = \frac{t_m}{t_s} = \sqrt{\lambda}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Derive acceleration scale:

$$
\lambda_a = \frac{a_m}{a_s} = \frac{\lambda_V}{\lambda_t} = \frac{\sqrt{\lambda}}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = 1
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Derive force scale:

$$
\lambda_F = \lambda^3
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Derive mass scale:

$$
\lambda_m = \lambda^3
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude with table summary of scales.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda = L_m / L_s = 1/50 = 0.02$, $V_m = 1.5 \, \text{m/s}$, $z_{a,m} = 0.012 \, \text{m}$, $\zeta_{a,m} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$.

**(i) Full-scale speed (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda_V = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{0.02} = 0.1414
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{V_m}{\lambda_V} = \frac{1.5}{0.1414} = 10.61 \, \text{m/s} \approx 20.62 \, \text{knots}
$$

**(ii) Time scale (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda_t = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{0.02} = 0.1414
$$

So model time runs $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$ times faster than full scale.

**(iii) Full-scale heave (3 marks):**

The geometric length scale applies to displacements:

$$
\lambda_z = \lambda = 0.02
$$

$$
z_{a,s} = \frac{z_{a,m}}{\lambda_z} = \frac{0.012}{0.02} = 0.6 \, \text{m}
$$

Verify RAO equality (model and full-scale RAO are preserved in linear theory):

$$
\text{RAO}_m = \frac{0.012}{0.025} = 0.48
$$

The full-scale RAO equals the model RAO by Froude similarity (in linear seakeeping). Hence:

$$
\text{Full-scale heave} = 0.48 \times \zeta_{a,s}
$$

If full-scale wave amplitude is the same $\zeta_{a,s} = 0.025 \times 50 = 1.25 \, \text{m}$, then full-scale heave = $0.48 \times 1.25 = 0.6 \, \text{m}$, which matches the geometric scaling result.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme Alternative]**
> **Question B:**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of encounter frequency. Derive its expression for a ship moving at velocity $V$ in head seas ($\beta = 180°$). (7 marks)
> **(b)** A ship of length $L = 150 \, \text{m}$ moves at $V = 15 \, \text{knots}$ ($7.72 \, \text{m/s}$) in head seas with wave period $T = 8 \, \text{s}$ and amplitude $\zeta_a = 1.5 \, \text{m}$. Compute: (i) wave frequency, (ii) encounter frequency, (iii) wave number, (iv) wavelength. The measured heave RAO at this frequency is 0.6. Predict the full-scale heave amplitude. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define encounter frequency. The frequency at which the ship meets successive wave crests is different from the absolute wave frequency because of ship forward motion.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Set up phase equation. Let $\beta$ be the angle between ship heading and wave direction. Wave phase at ship's reference point:

$$
\Phi = k V t \cos\beta - \omega t
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Differentiate and set to zero for encounter:

$$
\omega_e = \omega - k V \cos\beta = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V}{g}\cos\beta
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Specialize to head seas ($\beta = 180°$, $\cos\beta = -1$):

$$
\omega_e = \omega + \frac{\omega^2 V}{g}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude: In head seas, encounter frequency is **higher** than wave frequency; in following seas ($\beta = 0$), encounter frequency is **lower**.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $L = 150 \, \text{m}$, $V = 7.72 \, \text{m/s}$, $T = 8 \, \text{s}$, $\zeta_a = 1.5 \, \text{m}$, $\text{RAO} = 0.6$.

**(i) Wave frequency (1 mark):**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{8} = 0.785 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Encounter frequency (2 marks):** For head seas:

$$
\omega_e = \omega + \frac{\omega^2 V}{g} = 0.785 + \frac{(0.785)^2 (7.72)}{9.81}
$$

$$
\omega_e = 0.785 + \frac{0.616 \times 7.72}{9.81} = 0.785 + 0.485 = 1.270 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

**(iii) Wave number (2 marks):** Using dispersion relation $k = \omega^2 / g$:

$$
k = \frac{(0.785)^2}{9.81} = \frac{0.616}{9.81} = 0.0628 \, \text{rad/m}
$$

**(iv) Wavelength (1 mark):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k} = \frac{2\pi}{0.0628} = 100.0 \, \text{m}
$$

**(v) Heave amplitude prediction (1 mark):**

$$
z_a = \text{RAO} \times \zeta_a = 0.6 \times 1.5 = 0.9 \, \text{m}
$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Reynolds and Froude scaling** — Seakeeping uses **Froude scaling** (gravity-inertial), not Reynolds (viscous). Confusing these loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\cos\beta$ term in encounter frequency** — Many students write $\omega_e = \omega + kV$ without the angle, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong RAO definition** — RAO is **dimensionless for heave**, but **has units of rad for pitch** (pitch RAO is normalized by wave slope $k\zeta_a$). Mixing these definitions loses marks.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — Linear potential theory, Froude scaling validity, and deep-water dispersion are essential assumptions to mention.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversions** — Knots to m/s (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s) is a frequent conversion error.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist
- ✅ Seakeeping = study of ship behavior in waves; involves 6-DOF motions
- ✅ Model testing uses **Froude scaling** ($\lambda_t = \sqrt{\lambda}$); viscous effects neglected
- ✅ Velocity scale $\sqrt{\lambda}$, force scale $\lambda^3$, mass scale $\lambda^3$, acceleration scale 1
- ✅ **RAO** = $|X(\omega)| / \zeta_a$; preserved between model and full scale in linear theory
- ✅ **Encounter frequency** $\omega_e = \omega - (\omega^2 V/g)\cos\beta$
- ✅ In **head seas** ($\beta = 180°$): $\omega_e > \omega$ (ship meets waves faster)
- ✅ In **following seas** ($\beta = 0°$): $\omega_e < \omega$
- ✅ **Dispersion relation** (deep water): $k = \omega^2/g$, so $\lambda = 2\pi g / \omega^2$
- ✅ Common spectra: Pierson-Moskowitz (fully developed), JONSWAP (fetch-limited)
- ✅ **MSI** quantifies crew habitability; ITTC standard formula uses vertical acceleration
- ✅ **Added resistance** $R_{aw}$ causes speed loss in waves — critical for fuel estimation
- ✅ **Strip theory** assumption: 2D cross-sectional analysis extended along ship length
- ✅ Peak RAO occurs near **resonance** (heave natural period $\sim$ wave period)
- ✅ Linear superposition valid for irregular seas: $S_{XX}(\omega) = |\text{RAO}|^2 \cdot S_{\zeta\zeta}(\omega)$
- ✅ Significant wave height $H_s \approx 4\sigma$, where $\sigma$ is RMS surface elevation

### Key Equations to Memorize

$$
F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}, \quad \lambda_t = \sqrt{\lambda}, \quad \omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V}{g}\cos\beta
$$

$$
\text{RAO} = \frac{|X|}{\zeta_a}, \quad k = \frac{\omega^2}{g}, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{\int S_{XX}(\omega)\, d\omega}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on Open Water Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Open Water Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Water Test (OWT):** A standardised hydrodynamic experiment performed on a **geometrically similar model propeller** in an undisturbed (free-stream) flow field, typically inside a **towing tank** or a **cavitation tunnel**, to obtain the non-dimensional hydrodynamic characteristics of the propeller in the absence of any ship-hull interaction.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) curriculum for **PCNSL507 – Marine Hydrodynamics and Hydraulics Lab**, the Open Water Test is the **second core experiment** of the syllabus and is the experimental basis for the well-known **KT, KQ–J curves** (propeller open-water characteristic diagrams).

The test yields the following three primary non-dimensional coefficients plotted as functions of the **Advance Coefficient $J$**:

| Symbol | Name | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $K_T$ | Thrust Coefficient | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ |
| $K_Q$ | Torque Coefficient | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ |
| $\eta_0$ | Open Water Efficiency | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot K_Q}$ |

where $T$ is thrust, $Q$ is torque, $n$ is rotational speed in rev/s, $D$ is propeller diameter, and $\rho$ is the **mass density of water (≈ 998 kg/m³ for fresh, ≈ 1025 kg/m³ for sea water)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kite in Steady Wind"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine holding a kite in a steady, uniform wind tunnel. If the kite spins at a fixed rate and you slowly walk it *into* the wind, the pull on the string (thrust) and the resistance to spinning (torque) keep changing. By recording these forces at various wind speeds and spin rates, you build a "performance fingerprint" of that kite.
>
> A marine propeller in an open water test behaves **exactly like this kite**: the towing carriage plays the role of the steady wind, the rotating shaft represents the kite's spin, and the load cell captures the forces. The "fingerprint" obtained is universal — it characterises the propeller's *shape* alone, with no hull interference.

### 1.3 Why "Open Water"?

The phrase **"open water"** specifically excludes any **hull wake**, **hull-propeller interaction**, or **boundary-layer effects** from the ship. The model propeller is therefore placed in a **quasi-uniform inflow** (typically at the geometric centre of the test-section cross-section, well ahead of the carriage-mounted support strut wake).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Hook:** Examiners frequently ask, *"Why is the test called 'open water' and not simply 'propeller test'?"* — the answer is precisely to distinguish it from the **behind-hull condition**, where the propeller operates in the non-uniform, accelerated wake of the ship.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Principle — Dimensional Homogeneity

The Open Water Test is grounded in the **Buckingham Π Theorem** of dimensional analysis. The thrust $T$ generated by a propeller rotating in a fluid depends on **seven physical variables**:

$$T = f(\rho, n, D, V_A, \mu, g, \text{geometry})$$

Reducing these using dimensional analysis yields **three non-dimensional groups** that completely govern the propeller's hydrodynamic behaviour:

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \phi\!\left(\frac{V_A}{nD},\; \frac{\rho n D^2}{\mu},\; \frac{n^2 D}{g}\right)}$$

| Group | Name | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | **Advance Coefficient** | $J$ | Ratio of translational to rotational speed |
| $\dfrac{\rho n D^2}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds Number** | $Re$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |
| $\dfrac{n^2 D}{g}$ | **Froude Number (rotational)** | $Fn$ | Ratio of inertial to gravitational forces |

For a fully submerged propeller (no free-surface influence), the **Froude number effect is negligible**, and at model-scale test Reynolds numbers of the order **$10^5$ to $10^6$**, the viscous Reynolds number dependence is also weak. Hence the test result collapses to a clean **2-parameter function**:

$$K_T = f_T(J), \qquad K_Q = f_Q(J), \qquad \eta_0 = \frac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot K_Q}$$

### 2.2 The Five Logical Steps of an Open Water Test

1. **Geometric Scaling** — The model propeller is a **scaled replica** (scale ratio $\lambda = D_m / D_s$) of the full-scale (ship) propeller, with identical **pitch ratio ($P/D$)**, **blade area ratio ($A_E/A_0$)**, **skew**, and **rake**.
2. **Test Medium Selection** — Tests are run in **fresh water** (cavitation tunnels) or in a **towing tank** filled with fresh water at controlled temperature. **Sea water** is reserved for cavitation-validation runs.
3. **Independent Variation of $J$** — The investigator either **fixes $n$ and varies $V_A$** (preferred in towing tanks) or **fixes $V_A$ and varies $n$** (typical in cavitation tunnels). The advance coefficient $J$ is swept across the design range, typically **0.0 ≤ $J$ ≤ 1.4**.
4. **Force Measurement** — A **dynamometer** (mechanical, hydraulic, or strain-gauge based) measures $T$ and $Q$ at each step. Shaft speed $n$ is read from a **tachometer** or optical encoder.
5. **Non-Dimensionalisation & Plotting** — Raw forces are converted to $K_T$, $K_Q$, and $\eta_0$, and plotted against $J$. The resulting **$K_T$–$J$, $K_Q$–$J$, and $\eta_0$–$J$ curves** form the propeller's *open-water characteristic*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-dimensionalise?** A propeller of diameter 0.25 m tested in a lab and a full-scale propeller of diameter 5.0 m on a ship obey the **same $K_T(J)$ curve** if geometrically similar and operating at matched Reynolds numbers. This **dynamic similarity** is the entire power of the test.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units (SI) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | Advance Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 2 | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Thrust Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Torque Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot K_Q}$ | Open Water Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho n D^2}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (RPS form) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{Th} = \dfrac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2 A_0}$ | Thrust Loading Coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_D = 2\pi n Q$ | Delivered Power to Propeller | W (watts) |
| 8 | $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ | Thrust Power (Useful Power) | W (watts) |
| 9 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{P_T}{P_D} = \dfrac{T V_A}{2\pi n Q}$ | Efficiency (Power Ratio) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_A = V (1 - w)$ | Advance Speed (with wake fraction $w$) | m/s |

where $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$ is the propeller disc area, $V$ is the ship speed, and $w$ is the **Taylor wake fraction**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Propeller Design Validation:** Newly designed propellers (e.g., Wageningen B-Series, Gawn, KCD, KP-Series) are routinely validated by OWT against predicted $K_T$/$K_Q$ curves.
- **Cavitation Margin Assessment:** OWT at varied cavitation numbers $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2$ determines the **cavitation bucket** of the propeller.
- **Computational Benchmarking:** CFD codes (RANS, panel methods like BEM) are calibrated against OWT data.
- **Full-Scale Performance Prediction:** Combined with the **self-propulsion test**, OWT data drives the **effective power → delivered power → brake power** chain used in the ship's power-plant sizing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Thrust Coefficient $K_T$

Starting from the dimensional form of thrust:

$$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

where $C_T$ is a dimensionless thrust constant. We want to extract the form of $K_T$ that the OWT delivers. The **measured thrust** $T_{\text{meas}}$ is the **net thrust** after subtracting the frictional drag of the bare shaft (with the propeller removed) at the corresponding advance speed:

$$T_{\text{net}} = T_{\text{meas, with prop}} - T_{\text{meas, shaft alone}}$$

The non-dimensional thrust coefficient is defined as the ratio of the thrust to the **dynamic pressure of the rotating disc**:

$$K_T = \frac{T_{\text{net}}}{\rho \, n^2 \, D^4}$$

**Derivation Steps:**

1. **Start with dimensional thrust:**
   $$T = \text{[Force]} = M L T^{-2}$$

2. **Identify governing variables for thrust:** $T = f(\rho, n, D, V_A)$.
   - $[\rho] = M L^{-3}$
   - $[n] = T^{-1}$
   - $[D] = L$
   - $[V_A] = L T^{-1}$

3. **Form a dimensionless group for thrust using $\rho$, $n$, $D$:**
   $$\pi_1 = \frac{T}{\rho^a n^b D^c}$$

4. **Match dimensions:**
   $$M L T^{-2} = (M L^{-3})^a (T^{-1})^b (L)^c = M^a L^{-3a+c} T^{-b}$$

5. **Equate exponents of $M$, $L$, $T$:**
   - $M$: $1 = a$  →  $a = 1$
   - $T$: $-2 = -b$  →  $b = 2$
   - $L$: $1 = -3a + c = -3 + c$  →  $c = 4$

6. **Therefore:**
   $$\boxed{K_T = \frac{T}{\rho \, n^2 \, D^4}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Coefficient $K_Q$

Following identical dimensional analysis for torque $Q = [\text{Force} \times \text{Length}] = M L^2 T^{-2}$:

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho^a n^b D^c}$$

$$M L^2 T^{-2} = M^a L^{-3a + c} T^{-b}$$

Solving:
- $M$: $a = 1$
- $T$: $-b = -2$  →  $b = 2$
- $L$: $2 = -3(1) + c$  →  $c = 5$

$$\boxed{K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho \, n^2 \, D^5}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Open Water Efficiency $\eta_0$

The **useful (thrust) power** developed by the propeller in open water is:

$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

The **delivered power** absorbed by the rotating shaft is:

$$P_D = 2\pi n Q$$

The open water efficiency is the ratio:

$$\eta_0 = \frac{P_T}{P_D} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n Q}$$

Now substitute $T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4$ and $Q = K_Q \rho n^2 D^5$:

$$\eta_0 = \frac{(K_T \rho n^2 D^4) \cdot V_A}{2\pi n \cdot (K_Q \rho n^2 D^5)}$$

$$\eta_0 = \frac{K_T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n \cdot D \cdot K_Q}$$

Recognising that $V_A / (nD) = J$:

$$\boxed{\eta_0 = \frac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot K_Q}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Sheet)

**Given data** for a model propeller OWT:

| Run | $V_A$ (m/s) | $n$ (rev/s) | $T$ (N) | $Q$ (N·m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1.50 | 8.0 | 62.40 | 1.260 |
| 2 | 1.80 | 8.0 | 48.00 | 1.080 |
| 3 | 2.10 | 8.0 | 33.60 | 0.900 |
| 4 | 2.40 | 8.0 | 19.20 | 0.720 |

Model diameter $D = 0.25$ m, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³.

**Solution for Run 1:**

$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{1.50}{8.0 \times 0.25} = \frac{1.50}{2.00} = 0.750$$

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{62.40}{998 \times (8.0)^2 \times (0.25)^4} = \frac{62.40}{998 \times 64 \times 0.00390625}$$

$$K_T = \frac{62.40}{998 \times 0.2500} = \frac{62.40}{249.5} = 0.2501 \approx 0.250$$

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} = \frac{1.260}{998 \times 64 \times (0.25)^5} = \frac{1.260}{998 \times 64 \times 0.0009766}$$

$$K_Q = \frac{1.260}{62.375} = 0.02020$$

$$\eta_0 = \frac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot K_Q} = \frac{0.750 \times 0.2501}{2\pi \times 0.02020} = \frac{0.1876}{0.1269} = 1.478$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check:** An efficiency of **1.478 > 1** is **physically impossible**! This signals either (a) a data-entry error in the lab sheet, (b) an incorrect unit for $n$ (e.g., RPM used where rev/s is required), or (c) shaft-friction correction **not subtracted** from $Q$. Always check $\eta_0 \le 1$ first.

**Re-evaluating with corrected raw data** (after subtracting shaft friction $Q_{\text{shaft}} = 0.20$ N·m, and noting $Q_{\text{actual}} = Q_{\text{meas}} - Q_{\text{shaft}} = 1.06$ N·m):

$$K_Q^{\text{corrected}} = \frac{1.06}{62.375} = 0.01699$$

$$\eta_0 = \frac{0.750 \times 0.2501}{2\pi \times 0.01699} = \frac{0.1876}{0.1068} = 1.757$$

Still > 1 — indicates further correction needed. The **canonical KTU expected answer set** for this template yields a curve where $\eta_0$ peaks around **0.6 – 0.7** at $J \approx 0.7$–$0.8$.

### 3.5 Reynolds-Number Scaling Correction

Model-to-full-scale extrapolation uses the **ITTC 1978 correlation line**:

$$C_{T,\text{full}} = C_{T,\text{model}} + 0.0004 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{Re_{\text{model}}}{Re_{\text{full}}}\right)^{0.5} \right]$$

where the Reynolds number is based on the **0.7R radius chord** and the **local relative velocity** $V_R = \sqrt{V_A^2 + (0.7\pi n D)^2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **For OWT, Reynolds number scaling is applied to $K_T$ and $10 K_Q$** (the latter is multiplied by 10 because the torque coefficient is roughly an order of magnitude smaller than the thrust coefficient). This is a standard KTU viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Open Water Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[TOWING CARRIAGE] --> B[INSTRUMENTED STRUT ASSEMBLY]
    B --> C[SHAFT FAIRING]
    C --> D[MODEL PROPELLER HUB]
    D --> E[BLADES D FIXED PITCH]
    B --> F[DYNAMOMETER STRAIN GAUGE BRIDGE]
    F --> G[SIGNAL CONDITIONER AND AMPLIFIER]
    G --> H[DATA ACQUISITION DAQ SYSTEM]
    I[SERVO MOTOR DRIVE] --> J[TORQUE TRANSMITTER]
    J --> K[SHAFT]
    K --> D
    H --> L[COMPUTER WITH LABVIEW SOFTWARE]
    L --> M[REAL TIME KT KQ ETA0 VS J PLOT]
    N[TACHOMETER OPTICAL ENCODER] --> K
    N --> H
    O[TEMPERATURE PROBE] --> H
    P[CARRIAGE SPEED ENCODER] --> A
    P --> H
    H --> Q[REPORT GENERATION KT KQ ETA0 TABLES]
    M --> Q
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Data Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[RAW VA T Q N DATA] --> R2[UNIT CONSISTENCY CHECK]
    R2 --> R3[SHAFT FRICTION CORRECTION]
    R3 --> R4[COMPUTE J]
    R4 --> R5[COMPUTE KT]
    R4 --> R6[COMPUTE KQ]
    R5 --> R7[COMPUTE ETA0]
    R6 --> R7
    R7 --> R8[PLOT KT J CURVE]
    R7 --> R9[PLOT KQ J CURVE]
    R7 --> R10[PLOT ETA0 J CURVE]
    R8 --> R11[FIT POLYNOMIAL KT A0 A1 J A2 J2]
    R9 --> R12[FIT POLYNOMIAL KQ B0 B1 J B2 J2]
    R11 --> R13[COMPARISON WITH WAGENINGEN B SERIES]
    R12 --> R13
    R13 --> R14[DESIGN POINT INTERPOLATION]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Propeller Disc and Force Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[SHIP HULL NOT PRESENT IN OWT] --> X[OPEN WATER UNIFORM INFLOW VA]
    X --> PD[PROPELLER DISC OF AREA A0]
    PD --> SF[SLIPSTREAM CONTRACTION]
    PD --> TV[THRUST FORCE T ALONG SHAFT AXIS]
    PD --> TR[TORQUE Q ABOUT SHAFT AXIS]
    TV --> UP[USEFUL THRUST POWER PT T TIMES VA]
    TR --> DP[DELIVERED POWER PD 2 PI N Q]
    UP --> EFF[OPEN WATER EFFICIENCY ETA0 PT BY PD]
    DP --> EFF
    PD --> CP[CAVITATION NUMBER SIGMA IF RELEVANT]
```

### 4.4 Key Instrumentation Reference

| Block ID | Hardware | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| F | Strain-gauge dynamometer | Measures $T$ and $Q$ |
| N | Optical/laser tachometer | Measures $n$ (rev/s) |
| P | Carriage encoder | Measures $V_A$ |
| O | Thermistor | Records water temperature → $\rho$, $\mu$ |
| H, L | DAQ + PC | Logs data, computes $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$ |
| I, J | Servo motor + torque transmitter | Drives propeller at set $n$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Favourite:** *"Which instrument has the highest uncertainty — the dynamometer, the tachometer, or the carriage encoder?"* Answer: The **dynamometer** (typically ±0.5% of full scale), because it is the most mechanically complex and the strain-gauge temperature-drift compensation is non-trivial. The tachometer and encoder typically have ±0.1% or better.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Advance Coefficient $J$ of a propeller. Why is it a non-dimensional parameter, and what is its typical design range for a marine propeller?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Advance Coefficient** is defined as:
$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD}$$
where $V_A$ is the advance speed (m/s), $n$ is the rotational speed in rev/s, and $D$ is the propeller diameter (m). It is non-dimensional because both numerator and denominator have dimensions of $[L T^{-1}]$, so all units cancel. Physically, $J$ represents the **ratio of translational to rotational (peripheral) speed** of the propeller. For a marine propeller, the typical design range is **0.5 ≤ $J$ ≤ 0.9** at the design condition. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for non-dimensionality, 1 for range.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by "open water" condition in a propeller test? How does it differ from the "behind-hull" condition?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **"open water" condition** refers to the propeller being tested in a **uniform, undisturbed inflow** — i.e., without any ship hull present. The test is conducted either in a towing tank with the model propeller mounted ahead of the support strut's wake, or in a cavitation tunnel with a uniform flow.
In contrast, the **"behind-hull" condition** is the actual operating environment of the propeller when fitted to a ship, where the inflow is **non-uniform, has a rotational component, and is accelerated by the hull boundary layer** — characterised by the **Taylor wake fraction $w$** and the **wake distribution**. Open water data serves as the *baseline*; behind-hull performance is obtained by **superposing the wake** onto the open-water characteristic. *(3 marks — 1 for open water, 1 for behind-hull, 1 for distinction/relation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations for ESE (End Semester Evaluation) of Lab Courses, each Part-B question carries 14 marks and typically has a 7+7 sub-part structure.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** A model propeller of diameter $D = 0.30$ m is tested in a towing tank at $n = 12$ rev/s. The measured thrust is $T = 85$ N and torque is $Q = 1.95$ N·m at an advance speed $V_A = 2.40$ m/s. Take $\rho = 998$ kg/m³. Calculate the advance coefficient $J$, the thrust coefficient $K_T$, the torque coefficient $K_Q$, and the open water efficiency $\eta_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **functional block architecture** of an open water test setup in a towing tank. List the **three non-dimensional coefficients** obtained and state the **range of $J$** typically covered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{2.40}{12 \times 0.30} = \frac{2.40}{3.60} = 0.667$$

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{85}{998 \times (12)^2 \times (0.30)^4}$$

Computing denominator: $(12)^2 = 144$, $(0.30)^4 = 0.0081$, $144 \times 0.0081 = 1.1664$, $998 \times 1.1664 = 1164.07$.

$$K_T = \frac{85}{1164.07} = 0.0730$$

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} = \frac{1.95}{998 \times 144 \times (0.30)^5}$$

$(0.30)^5 = 0.00243$, $144 \times 0.00243 = 0.34992$, $998 \times 0.34992 = 349.28$.

$$K_Q = \frac{1.95}{349.28} = 0.00558$$

$$\eta_0 = \frac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot K_Q} = \frac{0.667 \times 0.0730}{2\pi \times 0.00558} = \frac{0.04869}{0.03506} = 1.389$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The efficiency exceeding 1.0 again signals a **data inconsistency** in the question (as it often does in real lab data before shaft-friction correction). For a KTU exam, **state the assumption** — e.g., *"Assuming the given data is the corrected (post-friction) data; if not, subtract $Q_{\text{shaft}} \approx 0.15$–$0.30$ N·m first."* Awarded marks: 2 for $J$, 2 for $K_T$, 2 for $K_Q$, 1 for $\eta_0$ formula and substitution. **Loss of 1 mark** is typical for not commenting on the unrealistic $\eta_0$.

**Model Solution (b):**
Refer to the **Section 4 Mermaid block diagrams** for the test setup architecture. Key elements to include in the sketch:

1. **Towing tank** (length 100–300 m, width 3–6 m, depth 2–5 m) filled with fresh water.
2. **Towing carriage** running on rails with controllable speed (typically 0.1–6 m/s).
3. **Instrumented strut** mounted on the carriage, holding the **dynamometer**.
4. **Shaft with model propeller**, driven by a **servo motor** above the waterline.
5. **Dynamometer** (mechanical or strain-gauge) measuring $T$ and $Q$ via Wheatstone bridge.
6. **Tachometer** (optical encoder) measuring $n$ on the shaft.
7. **DAQ + PC** for real-time computation and plotting.

The three non-dimensional coefficients are $K_T$, $K_Q$, and $\eta_0$, and the typical $J$ range is **0.0 to 1.4** (covering bollard pull condition to free-wheeling condition). *(7 marks — 3 for sketch, 2 for coefficient list, 1 each for instrument description and $J$ range.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **Thrust Coefficient $K_T$** and **Torque Coefficient $K_Q$** of a propeller using the **Buckingham Π Theorem**, taking the governing variables as $T$ (or $Q$), $\rho$, $n$, $D$, and $V_A$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained from an open water test on a model propeller ($D = 0.25$ m) at $n = 10$ rev/s, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³:

| $V_A$ (m/s) | $T$ (N) | $Q$ (N·m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.50 | 95.0 | 1.60 |
| 1.00 | 70.0 | 1.35 |
| 1.50 | 45.0 | 1.10 |
| 2.00 | 20.0 | 0.85 |

Compute $K_T$, $K_Q$, and $\eta_0$ for each run, and identify the run corresponding to **maximum open water efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

For **Thrust**, choose repeating variables $\rho, n, D$ (since they collectively span $M, L, T$):

- $[\rho] = M L^{-3}$
- $[n] = T^{-1}$
- $[D] = L$

Form the $\pi$-group for thrust:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{T}{\rho^a n^b D^c}$$

Matching $[M L T^{-2}] = [M^a L^{-3a+c} T^{-b}]$:

- $M$: $a = 1$
- $T$: $b = 2$
- $L$: $1 = -3(1) + c$  →  $c = 4$

$$\pi_1 = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = K_T$$

Similarly, the $\pi$-group for $V_A$:

$$\pi_2 = \frac{V_A}{nD} = J$$

The functional relationship is $K_T = \phi(J)$, which is the **$K_T$–$J$ open water characteristic**.

For **Torque**, $[Q] = M L^2 T^{-2}$:

- $M$: $a = 1$
- $T$: $b = 2$
- $L$: $2 = -3 + c$  →  $c = 5$

$$\pi_3 = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} = K_Q = \psi(J)$$

*(7 marks — 2 for variable list, 3 for dimensional matching, 1 each for the final expressions of $K_T$ and $K_Q$.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

Common denominator calculations (using $n=10$ rev/s, $D=0.25$ m, $\rho=998$ kg/m³):

$\rho n^2 D^4 = 998 \times 100 \times 0.00390625 = 389.84$ N (per unit $K_T$)
$\rho n^2 D^5 = 998 \times 100 \times 0.0009766 = 97.46$ N·m (per unit $K_Q$)

**Run 1:** $V_A = 0.50$ m/s
- $J = 0.50 / (10 \times 0.25) = 0.200$
- $K_T = 95.0 / 389.84 = 0.2437$
- $K_Q = 1.60 / 97.46 = 0.01642$
- $\eta_0 = (0.200 \times 0.2437) / (2\pi \times 0.01642) = 0.04874 / 0.10317 = 0.4724$

**Run 2:** $V_A = 1.00$ m/s
- $J = 1.00 / 2.50 = 0.400$
- $K_T = 70.0 / 389.84 = 0.1796$
- $K_Q = 1.35 / 97.46 = 0.01385$
- $\eta_0 = (0.400 \times 0.1796) / (2\pi \times 0.01385) = 0.07184 / 0.08702 = 0.8255$

**Run 3:** $V_A = 1.50$ m/s
- $J = 1.50 / 2.50 = 0.600$
- $K_T = 45.0 / 389.84 = 0.1154$
- $K_Q = 1.10 / 97.46 = 0.01129$
- $\eta_0 = (0.600 \times 0.1154) / (2\pi \times 0.01129) = 0.06924 / 0.07093 = 0.9762$

**Run 4:** $V_A = 2.00$ m/s
- $J = 2.00 / 2.50 = 0.800$
- $K_T = 20.0 / 389.84 = 0.0513$
- $K_Q = 0.85 / 97.46 = 0.008722$
- $\eta_0 = (0.800 \times 0.0513) / (2\pi \times 0.008722) = 0.04104 / 0.05480 = 0.7490$

| Run | $J$ | $K_T$ | $K_Q$ | $\eta_0$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.200 | 0.2437 | 0.01642 | 0.472 |
| 2 | 0.400 | 0.1796 | 0.01385 | 0.826 |
| 3 | 0.600 | 0.1154 | 0.01129 | **0.976** |
| 4 | 0.800 | 0.0513 | 0.00872 | 0.749 |

**Maximum open water efficiency is at Run 3, $J = 0.600$, $\eta_0 = 0.976$.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion of $n$:** Some students use RPM directly instead of rev/s. Since $J$ and $K_T$, $K_Q$ contain $n^2$, using RPM inflates $K_T$ and $K_Q$ by a factor of **3600** — an instant **0/7** mark scenario.
> 2. **Mixing SI with CGS:** Density in g/cm³ or torque in kgf·cm without conversion is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Skipping the table:** A KTU lab exam demands a **tabular presentation** of all four runs. A scattered paragraph solution loses at least **2 marks** on presentation.
> 4. **Not commenting on shaft friction correction:** Always add the line, *"The given $Q$ values are assumed to be the net (post-correction) torque."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2: Open Water Tests**

- ✅ **Open Water Test (OWT)** = Propeller tested in **uniform inflow**, **no hull**, **no free-surface effects**.
- ✅ **Two test facilities:** Towing tank (preferred for performance) and cavitation tunnel (for cavitation bucket).
- ✅ **Three primary results:** $K_T(J)$, $K_Q(J)$, $\eta_0(J)$.
- ✅ **Master Formulas:**
  - $J = V_A / (nD)$ — use $n$ in **rev/s** (not RPM).
  - $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$
  - $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$
  - $\eta_0 = J K_T / (2\pi K_Q)$
- ✅ **Range of $J$:** **0.0 (bollard pull) to 1.4 (free-wheeling)**, design point typically **0.5–0.9**.
- ✅ **Always check $\eta_0 \le 1$**; values > 1 indicate a data or unit error.
- ✅ **Shaft-friction correction:** Net $T$ and $Q$ are obtained by subtracting the **bare-shaft** drag at each $V_A$.
- ✅ **Reynolds Number scaling** uses the ITTC 1978 line: $C_T^{\text{full}} = C_T^{\text{model}} + 0.0004[1 - (Re_m/Re_f)^{0.5}]$.
- ✅ **Comparison standard:** Wageningen B-Series charts (for $K_T, 10K_Q$ vs $J$, $P/D$, $A_E/A_0$).
- ✅ **KTU Viva favourites:**
  - Why is the test called "open water"?
  - What is the difference between $J$ and $\lambda$ (advance ratio)?
  - Why is $\eta_0 > 0.7$ rarely exceeded for single-screw propellers?
  - Explain Froude and Reynolds scaling as applied to OWT.
  - How is cavitation number $\sigma$ measured in a cavitation tunnel?

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When answering in the lab record book, **always draw the $K_T, K_Q, \eta_0$ vs $J$ curves** — they carry **at least 2 marks** in the ESE viva-voce and Continuous Evaluation (CE) component. Use **graph paper with proper axis labels and units** (even though the coefficients are dimensionless, label them clearly).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on Manoeuvring Performance of Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL507 - MARINE HYDRODYNAMICS AND HYDRAULICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Manoeuvring Performance of Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Manoeuvring Performance of Ships** is defined as the quantitative assessment of a vessel's ability to change course and speed in response to helm (rudder) commands, as governed by the **hydrodynamic forces and moments** generated by the hull, propeller, and rudder when operating at low (non-zero) drift angles in a forward-speed condition.

In marine hydrodynamics, manoeuvring is the **non-linear, large-amplitude, free-surface** condition where the ship operates at a **finite drift angle (β ≠ 0)** and a **non-zero yaw rate (r ≠ 0)**. This is distinct from resistance and propulsion tests (straight-line motion, β = 0, r = 0).

The performance is benchmarked against the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Standardized Ship Manoeuvrability Standards** codified under **Resolution MSC.137(76)** and the earlier **A.601(15)** standards.

## 1.2 Fundamental Manoeuvres (IMO Standardized Tests)

The IMO Resolution **MSC.137(76)** prescribes the following mandatory sea trials for ship manoeuvrability evaluation:

| Manoeuvre | Purpose | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Turning Circle Test (35° rudder)** | Course-changing ability | Tactical Diameter, Advance, Transfer, Reach |
| **10°/10° Zig-Zag Test** | Course-keeping & initial response | First overshoot angle, Time to check yaw |
| **20°/20° Zig-Zag Test** | Course-keeping at higher demand | Second overshoot, Time to reach opposite heading |
| **Pull-Out (Spiral) Test** | Directional stability | Stable / unstable steering regions |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle on a Wet Road

Imagine a heavily loaded bicycle travelling on a wet road at speed:

- The bicycle is the **ship** (forward speed = **U**)
- The handlebar is the **rudder** (deflection = **δ**)
- When you twist the handlebar, the bicycle tilts (leans inward) before turning sharply — this is the **drift angle (β)** the hull makes with its trajectory.
- The bicycle's reaction (how sharply it turns) depends on **speed**, **handlebar angle**, and **road friction (hull form & load)**.
- A slower speed means wider turns; a faster speed means tighter but more dangerous turns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed of advance (U), rudder angle (δ), drift angle (β), and yaw rate (r)** are the four governing variables in any manoeuvring analysis. Every IMO standard trial is essentially a controlled variation of these four parameters.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used throughout manoeuvring analysis:

- **Length Between Perpendiculars (L_pp)** ≈ **0.96 × L_OA** (Length Overall)
- **1 nautical mile (nm)** = **1852 m** = **6080 ft**
- **Standard Trial Speed (V_t)** ≈ **0.6 V_max to 0.8 V_max** (typically **15–20 knots** for cargo vessels)
- **Typical Tactical Diameter** for a modern cargo ship ≈ **3L to 5L_pp**
- **Typical Advance** ≈ **1.0L to 1.5L_pp**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU lab viva, always quote dimensions in terms of **L_pp (Length Between Perpendiculars)** rather than L_OA. This is the IMO-standard reference length.

---

## 1.5 The Three Degrees of Freedom (3-DOF) in Manoeuvring

Unlike 6-DOF seakeeping, manoeuvring analysis is restricted to **horizontal-plane motion**:

$$\text{Manoeuvring DOF} = \underbrace{\text{Surge (u)}}_{\text{forward speed}} + \underbrace{\text{Sway (v)}}_{\text{lateral drift}} + \underbrace{\text{Yaw (r)}}_{\text{rotation about vertical axis}}$$

Heave, roll, and pitch are **decoupled and neglected** because their time-scales are an order of magnitude shorter than the manoeuvring time-scale.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Manoeuvring Triangle — Three Critical Distances

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every KTU exam question on turning circles revolves around the four geometric distances measured from the point where the rudder is first deflected to the point where the ship reaches the new steady heading of 90°.**

### Step-by-Step Logic of Turning Geometry

1. **Initial Position (Point O)**: Rudder deflected to 35° (or any preset angle). Ship heading = initial heading. Ship starts to swing.
2. **90° Heading Change Reached**: After time **T_90**, the ship's heading has changed by 90°.
3. **Advance (A)**: Distance along the **original heading direction** from O to the point where the heading has changed by 90°.
4. **Transfer (T)**: Distance **perpendicular to the original heading** from O to the point where the heading has changed by 90°.
5. **Reach (R)**: Distance travelled **along the perpendicular to the original heading** when heading has changed by 90°.
6. **Tactical Diameter (D_t)**: Distance **perpendicular to the original heading** between the original and final (steady-state) tracks once the ship completes a 180° turn.
7. **Final Diameter (D_f)**: Diameter of the steady turning circle at 360° heading change.

## 2.2 Reference Frames — Two Coordinate Systems

In manoeuvring analysis, two frames are used:

| Frame | Symbol | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earth-fixed (inertial)** | O-XYZ | Fixed to the seabed; X points North | Measures Advance, Transfer, Tactical Diameter |
| **Body-fixed (ship-fixed)** | o-xyz | Origin at midship; x along longitudinal axis | Measures u, v, r, β, δ |

The **drift angle** is the angle between the velocity vector and the body-fixed x-axis:

$$\beta = \arctan\left(\frac{-v}{u}\right) \approx -\frac{v}{U}$$

For small drift angles (typical for manoeuvring, **|β| < 10°**), this linearization is valid.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drift angle** | $\beta = -v/U$ | radians (or deg) | Small-angle approximation |
| 2 | **Non-dimensional yaw rate** | $r' = rL/U$ | dimensionless | Prime denotes non-dim |
| 3 | **Non-dimensional sway velocity** | $v' = v/U$ | dimensionless | |
| 4 | **Non-dimensional rudder angle** | $\delta' = \delta$ (deg) or $\delta \cdot L/U^2$ | varies | Convention dependent |
| 5 | **Advance** | $A = f(L_{pp}, \delta, U)$ | metres | Empirical ≈ 1.0–1.5 L_pp |
| 6 | **Transfer** | $T = f(L_{pp}, \delta, U)$ | metres | Empirical ≈ 3.5–4.5 L_pp |
| 7 | **Tactical Diameter** | $D_t = f(L_{pp}, \delta, U)$ | metres | Empirical ≈ 3–5 L_pp |
| 8 | **Reach** | $R = f(L_{pp}, \delta, U)$ | metres | Empirical ≈ 1.5–2.0 L_pp |
| 9 | **Turning Rate (steady)** | $\omega_{ss} = U/R_{turn}$ | rad/s | R_turn = turning radius |
| 10 | **Steering indices IMO** | See § 2.4 | dimensionless | Pass/Fail criteria |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard rule-of-thumb ratios** (useful when only L_pp is given):
> - **D_t / L_pp ≈ 3 to 5** (cargo/tanker)
> - **A / L_pp ≈ 1.0 to 1.5**
> - **T / L_pp ≈ 3.5 to 4.5**
> - **D_f / D_t ≈ 1.05 to 1.10**

## 2.4 IMO MSC.137(76) Pass/Fail Criteria

The IMO Resolution **MSC.137(76)** defines six mandatory criteria. A ship fails if **any** criterion is violated:

| # | Criterion | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Advance at 90° with 35° rudder | $A \leq 4.5\,L_{pp}$ |
| 2 | Tactical diameter with 35° rudder | $D_t \leq 5.0\,L_{pp}$ |
| 3 | 10°/10° first overshoot | $\theta_{1,os} \leq 10°$ (if L_pp ≥ 100 m) |
| 4 | 10°/10° time to check yaw | $t_{check} \leq 10\,s$ |
| 5 | 20°/20° second overshoot | $\theta_{2,os} \leq 25°$ |
| 6 | 20°/20° time to reach opposite heading | $t_{opp} \leq 50\,s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note**: When asked about "manoeuvring standards", always mention **MSC.137(76)** along with its 6 numerical criteria. The earlier **A.601(15)** is now superseded; do not mix the two in answers.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design Phase**: Manoeuvring simulations (CFD + MMG model) predict these metrics before launch, saving costly sea-trial re-runs.
- **Port Approach Design**: Channel width and turning basin diameter are sized using **Tactical Diameter + safety margin (typically 1.5× to 2× D_t)**.
- **Pilot Training**: Simulators use these standardized manoeuvres to train harbour pilots.
- **Accident Investigation**: Post-incident reconstruction (e.g., collision, grounding) requires the same trial data to validate the actual manoeuvre that occurred.

---

## 2.6 Body-Fixed Equations of Motion (3-DOF Manoeuvring)

These equations form the basis of any computer simulation (MMG or Abkowitz model):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\dot{u} - v r + x_G \dot{r}) &= X_H + X_P + X_R \\
m(\dot{v} + u r + x_G \dot{r}) &= Y_H + Y_P + Y_R \\
I_{zz}\dot{r} + m x_G(\dot{v} + u r) &= N_H + N_P + N_R
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:

- **m** = mass of ship (kg)
- **I_zz** = moment of inertia about vertical axis (kg·m²)
- **x_G** = longitudinal centre of gravity from midship (m)
- **X, Y, N** = surge force, sway force, yaw moment
- Subscripts: **H** = hull, **P** = propeller, **R** = rudder
- **u, v, r** = surge velocity, sway velocity, yaw rate

> [!NOTE]
> The **Abkowitz model** treats the hull forces (X_H, Y_H, N_H) as Taylor series in (v, r, δ) using hydrodynamic derivatives. The **MMG (Mathematical Modelling Group) model** splits them into hull, propeller, and rudder sub-systems — the modern KTU-expected approach.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Drift Angle, Yaw Rate, and Turning Radius

### Given Data
A ship is in steady turning at constant forward speed **U** and constant yaw rate **r = dψ/dt**.

### Step 1 — Coordinate relationship

In body-fixed coordinates, the velocity vector has components:
- Surge: $u = U \cos(\beta)$
- Sway: $v = -U \sin(\beta)$

### Step 2 — Drift angle linearization

For small drift angles (β << 1 rad, which holds for steady turning):
$$\cos(\beta) \approx 1 \implies u \approx U$$
$$\sin(\beta) \approx \beta \implies v \approx -U\beta$$

Rearranging:
$$\beta \approx -\frac{v}{U}$$

### Step 3 — Geometric turning radius

In the earth-fixed frame, the ship traces a circular path of radius **R_turn**. The instantaneous centre of rotation is perpendicular to the velocity vector. The angular velocity about this centre equals **r**:

$$r = \frac{U}{R_{turn}}$$

### Step 4 — Solving for the turning radius

Rearranging Step 3:

$$R_{turn} = \frac{U}{r}$$

### Step 5 — Connection to tactical diameter

The tactical diameter is the diameter of the circle the ship ultimately traces (after the transient settles):

$$D_t = 2 R_{turn,ss} = \frac{2U}{r_{ss}}$$

where $r_{ss}$ is the **steady-state yaw rate**.

### Step 6 — Expressing in non-dimensional form

Using the **non-dimensional yaw rate** $r' = rL/U$:

$$R_{turn} = \frac{U}{r} = \frac{U}{(r' \cdot U / L)} = \frac{L}{r'}$$

Thus:

$$\frac{D_t}{L_{pp}} = \frac{2}{r'_{ss}}$$

**This is a critical KTU result.** A typical cargo ship has $r'_{ss} \approx 0.4$ to 0.6, giving $D_t/L \approx 3.3$ to 5.0 — exactly the empirical range quoted in Section 2.3.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Tactical Diameter Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a KTU-style numerical. Practice the methodology.

### Problem
A tanker of length **L_pp = 200 m** executes a steady turning circle at **U = 14 knots** with **35° rudder**. Sea-trial data gives a steady-state yaw rate **r_ss = 0.034 rad/s**.

### Step 1 — Convert speed to SI

$$U = 14 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 \text{ m/s per knot}$$
$$U = 7.20 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute turning radius

$$R_{turn} = \frac{U}{r_{ss}} = \frac{7.20}{0.034}$$

$$R_{turn} = 211.76 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Compute tactical diameter

$$D_t = 2 R_{turn} = 2 \times 211.76$$
$$D_t = 423.53 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Non-dimensionalize

$$\frac{D_t}{L_{pp}} = \frac{423.53}{200} = 2.12$$

### Step 5 — Compare with IMO standard

The IMO criterion for a 35° rudder is $D_t \leq 5.0\,L_{pp}$:
$$5.0 \times L_{pp} = 5.0 \times 200 = 1000 \text{ m}$$

Since $D_t = 423.53 \text{ m} \ll 1000 \text{ m}$:

**Result**: The ship **passes the IMO criterion** for tactical diameter, with significant margin (only **42.4%** of the limit used).

### Step 6 — Comment on realism

A ratio of $D_t/L = 2.12$ is **smaller than the typical cargo-ship range of 3–5**. This suggests either:
- A very fine hull form (e.g., warship, fast ferry)
- An extremely large rudder (high aspect ratio)
- An error in the trial data

A more realistic value would be $D_t \approx 3.5 \times 200 = 700$ m.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Zig-Zag (10°/10°) Test Interpretation

### Problem
A bulk carrier executes a **10°/10° zig-zag test**. The recorded first overshoot angle is **θ_1,os = 7.2°** and the time to check the yaw is **t_check = 8.5 s**. Ship length **L_pp = 175 m**.

### Step 1 — Compare with IMO criterion 3

$$\theta_{1,os} \leq 10°$$

$$7.2° \leq 10° \quad \checkmark \text{ (PASS)}$$

### Step 2 — Compare with IMO criterion 4

$$t_{check} \leq 10 \text{ s}$$

$$8.5 \text{ s} \leq 10 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark \text{ (PASS)}$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the physical meaning

- The **first overshoot** measures how far the ship "oversteers" past the prescribed 10° heading change before the helmsman can counter-steer.
- A **smaller overshoot** (7.2° vs 10° limit) indicates **good course-keeping** and effective directional stability.
- A **shorter check time** (8.5 s vs 10 s limit) indicates the ship is **responsive** and the rudder has adequate hydrodynamic authority.

### Step 4 — Pass/Fail verdict

The bulk carrier **passes both IMO criteria** for the 10°/10° zig-zag test.

---

## 3.4 Compact Reference Matrix — Manoeuvre → Measured Quantity → IMO Criterion

| Manoeuvre | Measured Quantity | Symbol | IMO Limit | Failure Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Turning Circle | Advance at 90° | A | $\leq 4.5\,L$ | Poor course-changing ability |
| Turning Circle | Tactical Diameter | D_t | $\leq 5.0\,L$ | Excessive turning space needed |
| 10°/10° Zig-Zag | 1st overshoot | $\theta_1$ | $\leq 10°$ | Poor course-keeping |
| 10°/10° Zig-Zag | Time to check yaw | $t_{check}$ | $\leq 10\,s$ | Sluggish yaw damping |
| 20°/20° Zig-Zag | 2nd overshoot | $\theta_2$ | $\leq 25°$ | Severe instability at large rudder |
| 20°/20° Zig-Zag | Time to opposite | $t_{opp}$ | $\leq 50\,s$ | Too slow to recover |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Manoeuvring Trial Workflow

> The figure below maps the **end-to-end procedure** of conducting a ship manoeuvring performance analysis, from initial sea-trial preparation through IMO compliance check. It replaces a physical drawing of the turning-circle geometry, which would be impractical to render in a Mermaid diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: IMO Manoeuvrability Sea Trial]) --> prepV[Step 1: Verify Vessel Displacement and Trim]
    prepV --> prepEnv[Step 2: Confirm Calm Sea State — Sea State 3 or Below]
    prepEnv --> prepSpd[Step 3: Establish Trial Speed Vt at 0.7 Vmax]
    prepSpd --> tc[Step 4: Conduct Turning Circle Test with 35 deg Rudder]
    tc --> tcMeas[Step 5: Measure Advance A, Transfer T, Reach R, Tactical Diameter Dt]
    tcMeas --> zz1[Step 6: Conduct 10 deg by 10 deg Zig Zag Test]
    zz1 --> zz1Meas[Step 7: Measure First Overshoot theta1 and Check Time tcheck]
    zz1Meas --> zz2[Step 8: Conduct 20 deg by 20 deg Zig Zag Test]
    zz2 --> zz2Meas[Step 9: Measure Second Overshoot theta2 and Time to Opposite Heading topp]
    zz2Meas --> po[Step 10: Conduct Pull Out Spiral Test]
    po --> poMeas[Step 11: Record Yaw Rate versus Rudder Angle Hysteresis Loop]
    poMeas --> judge[Step 12: Compare All Six Metrics with MSC137 Criteria]
    judge --> pass{Pass All Six IMO Limits?}
    pass -- Yes --> compliant[Vessel Certified Manoeuvrable]
    pass -- No --> fail[Fail — Identify Limiting Manoeuvre and Recommend Hull or Rudder Modification]
    fail --> compliant
    compliant([End: Certificate Issued])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Turning Circle Geometry (Schematic Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pointO[Point O: Rudder Deflected to 35 deg] --> adv[Advance A: Distance Along Original Heading]
    pointO --> tra[Transfer T: Distance Perpendicular to Original Heading]
    pointO --> reach[Reach R: Distance Along Final Heading Perpendicular]
    pointO --> tactDt[Tactical Diameter Dt: Diameter of Final Steady Circle]
    adv --> headingChg[At 90 deg Heading Change]
    tra --> headingChg
    reach --> headingChg
    tactDt --> headingChg
    headingChg --> review[IMO MSC137 Criterion Check]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Manoeuvring Force Components on the Hull

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputs[Inputs: u, v, r, delta] --> hullF[Hull Hydrodynamic Forces XH, YH, NH]
    inputs --> propF[Propeller Forces XP, YP]
    inputs --> rudF[Rudder Forces XR, YR, NR]
    hullF --> sumX[Total Surge Force X = XH plus XP plus XR]
    propF --> sumX
    rudF --> sumX
    hullF --> sumY[Total Sway Force Y = YH plus YP plus YR]
    propF --> sumY
    rudF --> sumY
    hullF --> sumN[Total Yaw Moment N = NH plus NP plus NR]
    propF --> sumN
    rudF --> sumN
    sumX --> eom[Equations of Motion 3DOF]
    sumY --> eom
    sumN --> eom
    eom --> solve[Numerical Integration — 4th Order Runge Kutta]
    solve --> out[Outputs: Path Trajectory, Heading Time History, Drift Angle]
```

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — Turning Circle (Text-Based Description)

For lab notebook reference, the turning circle has four landmark points:

- **Point O** → Position where 35° rudder is applied
- **Point P** → Position where heading has changed by 90° (measures A and T)
- **Point Q** → Position where heading has changed by 180° (measures D_t / 2)
- **Point R** → Position where heading has changed by 360° (steady turning, measures D_f / 2)

```
                 (Final steady circle, diameter D_f)
                      ╭─────────────╮
                    ╱                 ╲
                  ╱     D_t / 2        ╲
                ╱         ↑              ╲
              ╱           │  Point Q      ╲
            ╱             │                ╲
   ──────●─────────────────┼───────────●─── (Original heading line)
         O                 │           P
                           │
                  Transfer T  ↘ Advance A
                            ↓
```

> [!TIP]
> When drawing the turning circle in your lab record, **always label Point O, Point P, and the Tactical Diameter line** clearly. Examiners reward clearly labelled diagrams.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Define "Tactical Diameter" of a ship and state its significance in port design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

> The **Tactical Diameter (D_t)** of a ship is defined as the distance, measured perpendicular to the original course, between the ship's initial track and the position where the ship has completed a 180° turn and is on the opposite heading, executing a steady-state turning circle.
>
> Mathematically: $D_t = 2 R_{turn,ss} = 2U/r_{ss}$ **(1 mark)**
>
> For a typical cargo vessel, $D_t \approx 3\,L_{pp}$ to $5\,L_{pp}$ **(1 mark)**
>
> **Significance in port design**: The turning basin diameter in a harbour must be at least **1.5 × D_t** (typically 2× D_t) to allow safe completion of a turn by the largest vessel calling at the port. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 — Definition (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: List the four standard IMO manoeuvres used for assessing ship manoeuvrability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

1. **Turning Circle Test** with 35° rudder (port and starboard) **(0.75 mark)**
2. **10°/10° Zig-Zag Test** **(0.75 mark)**
3. **20°/20° Zig-Zag Test** **(0.75 mark)**
4. **Pull-Out (Spiral) Test** to determine directional stability **(0.75 mark)**

These are codified under **IMO Resolution MSC.137(76)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### Question A — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: A container ship of length L_pp = 280 m performs a turning circle test at 18 knots with 35° rudder. The steady-state yaw rate recorded is r = 0.025 rad/s. Determine:**
**(a)** The steady turning radius
**(b)** The tactical diameter and verify compliance with IMO MSC.137(76).

### Model Solution

**Given**:
- $L_{pp} = 280$ m
- $U = 18$ knots = $18 \times 0.5144 = 9.26$ m/s
- $r_{ss} = 0.025$ rad/s
- Rudder angle = 35° (standard IMO)

#### Part (a) — Steady Turning Radius (3 Marks)

Using the geometric relation:

$$R_{turn} = \frac{U}{r_{ss}}$$

$$R_{turn} = \frac{9.26}{0.025}$$

$$R_{turn} = 370.4 \text{ m}$$

**Result**: The ship traces a circle of radius **370.4 m** in steady turning. **(3 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Tactical Diameter & IMO Compliance (4 Marks)

$$D_t = 2 R_{turn} = 2 \times 370.4 = 740.8 \text{ m}$$

Non-dimensional ratio:

$$\frac{D_t}{L_{pp}} = \frac{740.8}{280} = 2.65$$

IMO MSC.137(76) Criterion:

$$D_t \leq 5.0 \times L_{pp} = 5.0 \times 280 = 1400 \text{ m}$$

Comparison:

$$740.8 \text{ m} \leq 1400 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark \quad \text{(PASS)}$$

The ship uses only **52.9%** of the IMO limit, indicating **ample compliance margin**.

**Result**: The container ship **passes** the IMO tactical diameter criterion. **(4 Marks)**

---

### Question A — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the aid of a sketch, describe the Turning Circle Test and define the parameters Advance, Transfer, Tactical Diameter, and Reach.**

### Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Test Description (3 Marks)**:

The Turning Circle Test is conducted by:
1. The ship is brought to a steady trial speed on a straight course.
2. The rudder is deflected to a fixed angle (typically 35°) to one side (e.g., starboard).
3. The rudder angle is maintained throughout the test.
4. The ship's trajectory is plotted relative to its initial heading.
5. The IMO standard requires that the **sea state** is **3 or below** (significant wave height $\leq 1.25$ m) and **wind** is **≤ Beaufort Force 4** to ensure repeatable results.

**Parameter Definitions (4 Marks — 1 mark each)**:

| Parameter | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Advance (A)** | Distance along the original heading from the point of rudder deflection to the point where the heading has changed by 90°. |
| **Transfer (T)** | Distance perpendicular to the original heading from the point of rudder deflection to the point where the heading has changed by 90°. |
| **Tactical Diameter (D_t)** | Distance perpendicular to the original heading between the original and final tracks once the ship has turned through 180° (steady turning circle diameter). |
| **Reach (R)** | Distance travelled along the perpendicular to the original heading when the ship has changed heading by 90°. |

**(Sketch should show Points O and P, with arrows for A, T, and R, plus the final steady circle showing D_t.)**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### Question B — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Explain the 10°/10° Zig-Zag Test. A bulk carrier performs this test and records a first overshoot of 8.5° and a check time of 9.2 s for L_pp = 220 m. Assess IMO compliance.**

### Model Solution

**Test Explanation (4 Marks)**:

The **10°/10° Zig-Zag Test** is conducted as follows:
1. The ship steers a steady heading at trial speed.
2. The rudder is deflected to **10° to one side** (say starboard).
3. When the ship's heading has changed by **10°**, the rudder is reversed to **10° to the opposite side** (port).
4. When the heading changes by **10°** in the opposite direction, the rudder is again reversed.
5. The test continues for at least **five full cycles**.

The recorded parameters are:
- **First Overshoot Angle ($\theta_{1,os}$)**: The maximum heading angle beyond the commanded 10° before the helmsman reverses the rudder.
- **Time to Check Yaw ($t_{check}$)**: The time from the initial rudder reversal to the moment when the yaw rate first becomes zero (heading momentarily stops swinging).
- **Second Overshoot ($\theta_{2,os}$)**: Recorded in the 20°/20° version.

**IMO Compliance Check (3 Marks)**:

| Criterion | IMO Limit | Measured | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| First overshoot | $\leq 10°$ | $8.5°$ | **PASS** ✓ |
| Check time | $\leq 10$ s | $9.2$ s | **PASS** ✓ |

**Verdict**: The bulk carrier **passes both IMO criteria** for the 10°/10° zig-zag test. The ship exhibits good directional stability and responsive yaw damping.

---

### Question B — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Derive the relationship between drift angle, sway velocity, and forward speed for a ship in steady turning. State the small-angle approximation used.**

### Model Solution

**Setup (2 Marks)**:

Consider a ship moving with velocity **V** at an angle **β** to its longitudinal (body-fixed) x-axis. The velocity components in the body-fixed frame are:

- Surge component (along x): $u = V \cos(\beta)$
- Sway component (along y): $v = -V \sin(\beta)$

The negative sign arises because the **sway velocity is defined positive to port**, while drift angle is measured from the velocity vector to the body x-axis.

**Small-Angle Approximation (2 Marks)**:

For typical manoeuvring conditions, the drift angle is small ($\beta < 10° \approx 0.175$ rad). Using Taylor series:

$$\cos(\beta) \approx 1 - \frac{\beta^2}{2} + ... \approx 1 \text{ for } |\beta| \ll 1$$

$$\sin(\beta) \approx \beta - \frac{\beta^3}{6} + ... \approx \beta \text{ for } |\beta| \ll 1$$

Substituting and using **V ≈ U** (forward speed) since surge velocity dominates:

$$u \approx U$$
$$v \approx -U\beta$$

**Final Result (3 Marks)**:

$$\beta \approx -\frac{v}{U}$$

This is the **fundamental linearized relationship** used throughout manoeuvring analysis. It allows the non-linear hydrodynamic derivatives to be linearized in (v, r) for the 3-DOF equations of motion.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** in KTU Manoeuvring Questions:
>
> 1. **Wrong reference length**: Using L_OA instead of L_pp — costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing IMO standards**: Citing A.601(15) instead of MSC.137(76) — costs **0.5–1 mark**.
> 3. **Units in knots**: Forgetting to convert to SI (m/s) before using formulas — costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Sign of drift angle**: Forgetting the negative sign in $\beta = -v/U$ — costs **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting non-dimensional form**: Writing D_t in metres without comparing to IMO limit (which is in terms of L_pp) — examiner will deduct.
> 6. **No sketch in descriptive questions**: A clear labelled diagram of the turning circle earns **1–2 bonus marks** even when not explicitly asked.
> 7. **Confusing Advance with Reach**: Advance is along original heading; Reach is perpendicular — common error, costs full marks.

## 5.5 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and review 24 hours before the lab viva / university exam.**

### Key Concepts
- [ ] Manoeuvring is **3-DOF horizontal motion** (surge, sway, yaw) at finite β and r.
- [ ] **Drift angle β ≈ -v/U** is the linearized body-to-earth kinematic relation.
- [ ] **Turning radius** $R_{turn} = U/r$.
- [ ] **Tactical diameter** $D_t = 2R_{turn} = 2U/r_{ss}$.
- [ ] **Non-dimensional yaw rate** $r' = rL/U$ — used in MMG models.

### Key Tests
- [ ] **Turning Circle Test** → measures A, T, R, D_t (IMO limits: A ≤ 4.5L, D_t ≤ 5.0L).
- [ ] **10°/10° Zig-Zag** → measures θ₁ and t_check (IMO limits: 10° and 10 s).
- [ ] **20°/20° Zig-Zag** → measures θ₂ and t_opp (IMO limits: 25° and 50 s).
- [ ] **Pull-Out (Spiral) Test** → checks directional stability.

### Key Standards
- [ ] **IMO Resolution MSC.137(76)** — current standard (supersedes A.601(15)).
- [ ] Six mandatory numerical criteria.
- [ ] Sea state ≤ 3, wind ≤ Beaufort 4 for valid trials.

### Empirical Ranges (Memorize)
- [ ] D_t / L_pp ≈ **3 to 5**
- [ ] A / L_pp ≈ **1.0 to 1.5**
- [ ] T / L_pp ≈ **3.5 to 4.5**
- [ ] D_f / D_t ≈ **1.05 to 1.10**

### Common Conversions
- [ ] 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s**
- [ ] L_pp ≈ **0.96 × L_OA**
- [ ] Degrees to radians: multiply by **π/180**

### Final Tip
- [ ] In numerical problems, **always state the IMO limit, compare numerically, and conclude PASS/FAIL** with the percentage margin used.
